proposals.title: Disentangling Structural and Interactional Drivers of Racial Disparities in Antibiotic Prescribing: A Causal Mediation and Standardized Patient Study
proposals.motivation: Healthcare disparities in infectious diseases, such as antibiotic prescribing, are well-documented but their causal pathways remain entangled. Pre-2026 literature debates the relative contributions of structural factors (e.g., access to care, clinic resources) versus clinician-patient interactional bias, yet studies rarely measure both simultaneously within a causal framework. This limits the development of effective, targeted interventions for equity-focused antimicrobial stewardship.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant portion of observed racial disparities in antibiotic prescribing for acute respiratory infections is mediated by pre-encounter structural variables, but that a residual, direct effect attributable to clinician decision-making during the interaction persists even when these structural factors are controlled.
proposals.method: We will first conduct a retrospective cohort study using electronic health record data, applying causal mediation analysis (using the mediation R package with inverse probability weighting to adjust for confounders) to decompose disparities into direct and indirect (mediated) effects via variables like insurance type and clinic setting. Subsequently, we will deploy a standardized patient (SP) study in primary care clinics, where SPs present with identical scripts and symptoms but vary in perceived race/ethnicity. We will statistically control for clinic-level structural variables measured via survey to isolate the interactional effect using mixed-effects logistic regression.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantified, pathway-specific map of disparity drivers in a common clinical scenario, directly informing the 'Prioritizing Equity in Antimicrobial Stewardship Efforts (EASE)' framework. It will yield concrete evidence for whether interventions should target system navigation, clinical decision support, or implicit bias training, advancing beyond descriptive disparity reports.
proposals.novelty: The novel integration of population-level causal mediation modeling with a controlled, in-vivo SP experiment explicitly designed to separate structural from interactional mechanisms represents a significant methodological advance over purely observational or simulated studies in health disparities research.

proposals.title: Decomposing Disparity: A Causal Attribution Framework for Infectious Disease Outcome Gaps Using Multi-Level Modeling
proposals.motivation: Persistent racial and ethnic disparities in infectious disease outcomes, from sepsis mortality to antimicrobial prescribing, are well-documented but poorly diagnosed. Public health and clinical stewardship efforts, like the Prioritizing Equity in Antimicrobial Stewardship Efforts (EASE) framework, lack standardized, quantitative tools to attribute observed gaps to specific, actionable levels of the healthcare system. This limits the efficient targeting of interventions, whether they aim at clinician behavior, systemic access, or community factors.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying a unified set of disparity metrics (e.g., standardized rate ratios) to multi-level healthcare data will allow for the statistically robust decomposition of observed outcome gaps, revealing that the primary attributable drivers vary significantly by pathogen and clinical context, challenging one-size-fits-all equity interventions.
proposals.method: We will analyze retrospective cohorts from large healthcare systems (e.g., VA, All-Payer Claims Databases) for conditions like sepsis, community-acquired pneumonia, and complicated UTI. Using a pathogen-agnostic disparity metric (e.g., the between-group variance component), we will fit multi-level mixed-effects models with patients nested within clinicians and facilities. We will then employ causal decomposition techniques, specifically three-fold Kitagawa-Oaxaca-Blinder decomposition adapted for non-linear models, to apportion the disparity into components explained by: 1) clinician-level covariates (modeling practice variation), 2) system-level covariates (facility type, geography, insurance), and 3) patient-level covariates (comorbidities, presentation severity).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, generalizable framework that moves from documenting disparities to diagnosing their structural drivers. It will produce a methodological toolkit for health services researchers and deliver concrete, attributed findings to guide stewardship programs, hospital equity officers, and public health departments in prioritizing the most impactful levers for intervention.
proposals.novelty: While disparity measurement and multi-level modeling exist separately, their fusion with formal causal decomposition for outcome attribution in infectious diseases is novel. This approach directly operationalizes the EASE framework's call for actionable equity analysis by statistically apportioning responsibility across clinician, system, and patient factors.

proposals.title: Disentangling Drivers of Disparity: A Comparative Analysis of Clinician Decision-Making and Systemic Access Barriers in Antimicrobial Prescribing
proposals.motivation: Health disparities in infectious diseases outcomes persist, with antimicrobial stewardship (AMS) efforts increasingly prioritizing equity. A critical, unresolved debate centers on the relative contribution of clinician-level cognitive biases versus patient-level systemic access barriers in generating these disparities. Understanding this balance is essential for designing effective, targeted interventions, as solutions for implicit bias differ fundamentally from those for structural inequities in healthcare access.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that quantifiable, community-level systemic barriers (e.g., pharmacy deserts, insurance coverage gaps, transportation access) will be stronger predictors of observed disparities in appropriate antimicrobial prescribing than clinician-level factors derived from practice patterns and decision-making audits.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-site, retrospective cohort study using electronic health record data from 3-5 diverse health systems. The dependent variable will be a composite measure of guideline-concordant antimicrobial prescribing for common infections. We will employ hierarchical linear modeling (HLM) to partition variance in prescribing outcomes. Level-1 predictors will be patient-level access barriers (geocoded to census-tract data on pharmacy density, public transit, and socioeconomic indices). Level-2 predictors will be clinician-level factors, operationalized via audit data on prescribing deviations in standardized clinical vignettes and peer comparison feedback reports. The relative variance explained by each level will be compared.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide empirical evidence to guide the 'Prioritizing Equity in Antimicrobial Stewardship Efforts (EASE)' framework by identifying the primary drivers of disparity. It will move the field from theoretical debate to data-driven strategy, helping AMS programs and medical departments allocate resources toward the most impactful levers for change, whether clinician education or community partnership.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have examined either implicit bias or access barriers in isolation, this study directly compares their explanatory power within a unified statistical model, a novel approach in AMS disparity research. It also innovatively blends social science methods (HLM) with clinical audit data and geospatial analysis.

proposals.title: Within-Clinician Prescribing Disparities: A Quasi-Experimental Study of Intersectional Patient Demographics and Clinician Implicit Associations in Antimicrobial Stewardship
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research documents pervasive racial, ethnic, and gender disparities in antibiotic and other antimicrobial prescribing, but existing ecological or between-clinician studies cannot disentangle interpersonal bias from systemic confounding (e.g., clinic resources, patient case-mix). This methodological gap hinders the development of effective, targeted equity interventions within antimicrobial stewardship programs, a core focus of Dr. Marcelin's prior EASE framework work.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that significant within-clinician prescribing disparities exist for intersectional patient identities (e.g., race-gender) even for similar clinical presentations, and that the magnitude of these disparities is positively associated with clinician implicit bias scores, measured via validated implicit association tests (IATs).
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using electronic health record data from a large health system (2018-2024). For a defined set of infectious syndromes (e.g., UTI, pneumonia), we will use exact and propensity score matching within each clinician to create patient dyads with similar clinical indications but differing on race, ethnicity, and/or gender. The primary outcome will be guideline-discordant or unnecessarily broad-spectrum antibiotic prescribing. We will employ fixed-effects logistic regression models (clinician as the fixed effect) to estimate within-clinician disparity odds ratios. Subsequently, we will link these clinician-specific disparity estimates to their IAT scores (collected via a voluntary survey) using multivariable meta-regression.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first quasi-experimental, causal inference-based evidence directly isolating clinician-level differential treatment as a mechanism for antimicrobial prescribing disparities. It will extend the Prioritizing Equity in Antimicrobial Stewardship Efforts (EASE) framework by empirically testing a core component and identifying clinicians who may benefit most from implicit bias training. The within-clinician design offers a robust model for future health equity research across specialties.
proposals.novelty: The novel application of a within-clinician, matched-patient quasi-experimental design directly separates interpersonal bias from environmental confounding, a key methodological advance over prior disparity studies. Furthermore, integrating clinician-level IAT data with actual prescribing behavior at the patient-dyad level provides a rare and rigorous test of the implicit bias mechanism in a real-world clinical setting.

proposals.title: Evaluating the Impact of an Equity-Aware, EHR-Integrated Clinical Decision Support Tool on Outpatient Antibiotic Prescribing: A Mixed-Methods Study
proposals.motivation: Outpatient antibiotic prescribing is a major driver of antimicrobial resistance, yet existing stewardship interventions often fail to address well-documented disparities in prescribing rates and appropriateness across patient populations. Pre-2026, while EHR-integrated clinical decision support (CDS) tools show promise, they are rarely designed or evaluated through an explicit equity lens, risking the perpetuation of existing biases in automated guidance.
proposals.hypothesis: An EHR-integrated CDS tool, co-designed with clinicians and informed by the Prioritizing Equity in Antimicrobial Stewardship Efforts (EASE) framework, will significantly reduce inappropriate antibiotic prescribing for acute respiratory infections in outpatient settings without exacerbating—and potentially mitigating—existing prescribing disparities across racial, ethnic, and socioeconomic groups.
proposals.method: We will employ a mixed-methods, quasi-experimental design. First, we will co-design the CDS tool with infectious diseases faculty, primary care clinicians, and community members via focus groups, embedding EASE principles (e.g., equity-centered metrics, social risk flagging). The tool will use a rules-based algorithm (e.g., adapting CDC Core Elements guidelines) with natural language processing to extract symptoms from notes. We will then implement the tool in a staggered fashion across clinic sites, comparing prescribing data pre- and post-implementation using interrupted time series analysis, while concurrently conducting social studies (surveys, interviews) to assess clinician adoption and reported barriers.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, implementable model for integrating equity into EHR-driven stewardship at the point of care. It will contribute empirical evidence on whether such tools can improve prescribing quality and equity simultaneously, and generate a framework for 'equity-by-design' in medical CDS development applicable beyond infectious diseases.
proposals.novelty: This proposal uniquely merges the technical development of EHR-CDS with a formal equity framework (EASE) and participatory design involving community stakeholders, moving beyond efficiency-focused alerts to actively interrogate and address social drivers of inappropriate prescribing.

proposals.title: The P-CEST Study: Development and Pilot Validation of a Personalized Cost-Effectiveness Support Toolkit for Equity and Antimicrobial Stewardship in Primary Care
proposals.motivation: Infectious diseases clinicians face dual pressures to advance health equity and practice antimicrobial stewardship (AMS), yet most cost-effectiveness models are designed for institutional policy, not individual practice. Solo practitioners and small clinics lack tools to quantify the financial and operational impact of patient-centered interventions like inclusive scheduling or tailored education, risking clinician burnout and unsustainable initiatives. A scalable, data-driven toolkit is needed to empower clinicians to optimize both equity outcomes and practice viability.
proposals.hypothesis: A modular, practice-data-integrated toolkit can provide valid, actionable cost-effectiveness estimates for clinician-led diversity and stewardship actions, leading to improved clinician adoption and perceived sustainability of these interventions compared to standard guideline dissemination.
proposals.method: We will develop the toolkit using a user-centered design process with infectious diseases and primary care clinicians. The core technical architecture will integrate practice management data (e.g., scheduling, prescriptions, billing) via a lightweight HL7 FHIR interface. A deterministic cost-consequence analysis engine, built on a modular R Shiny/Phyton platform, will allow clinicians to input local parameters for interventions (e.g., time for culturally tailored consultation, generic drug cost). We will pilot-test the toolkit in a pre-post, mixed-methods study across 15-20 small clinics, comparing reported intervention adoption and perceived sustainability against a control cohort using only published AMS/equity guidelines.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first validated, open-source decision-support system enabling individual clinicians to conduct micro-cost-effectiveness analyses for equity and stewardship. It will contribute a novel methodological framework for translating population-level health services research into actionable, personalized practice analytics, directly supporting the 'Prioritizing Equity in Antimicrobial Stewardship Efforts (EASE)' principle.
proposals.novelty: It shifts the unit of analysis from the health system to the individual clinician's practice panel and workflow, applying pragmatic trial and implementation science methods to create a personalized economic tool, a significant departure from traditional one-size-fits-all institutional cost-effectiveness models.

proposals.title: Generalizable vs. Idiosyncratic: A Matched Multi-Tier Cohort Study to Identify Transferable Drivers of Appropriate Antimicrobial Prescribing
proposals.motivation: Antimicrobial stewardship (AMS) programs face persistent challenges in translating successful interventions from high-performing sites to others, as contextual factors like resources and patient mix often confound comparisons. Pre-2026 literature, including frameworks like Prioritizing Equity in Antimicrobial Stewardship Efforts (EASE), highlights the need to disentangle generalizable improvement strategies from unique, non-replicable features of outlier clinics. This gap limits the scalability of effective AMS practices across diverse healthcare settings.
proposals.hypothesis: When clinics are matched on key contextual variables, systematic differences in clinical workflows, communication patterns, and decision-making cultures—rather than idiosyncratic factors—will distinguish high-performing clinics from their low-performing counterparts, revealing transferable drivers of appropriate prescribing.
proposals.method: We will conduct a convergent mixed-methods study. First, we will use electronic health record data from a multi-site healthcare system to calculate condition-specific appropriate prescribing rates (e.g., for community-acquired pneumonia, UTI) and stratify clinics into low, moderate, and high-performance tiers. Clinics will then be matched across tiers using propensity scores based on setting (e.g., academic, community), resources (FTE, EHR tools), and patient case-mix (comorbidity index, demographics). Within matched triads, we will quantitatively analyze workflow metrics (e.g., time-to-antibiotic, guideline adherence alerts) and qualitatively conduct ethnographic observations and semi-structured interviews with key stakeholders (physicians, pharmacists, nurses) to identify convergent and divergent themes.
proposals.contribution: This study will produce a refined framework for AMS intervention design that clearly distinguishes between broadly applicable drivers (e.g., specific pharmacist rounding models, peer comparison feedback structures) and context-bound factors. It will directly inform quality improvement by providing implementable, evidence-based strategies that are more likely to be effective when transferred to new settings, thereby advancing equity in stewardship outcomes.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior studies that compare unmatched high and low performers, this design's rigorous matching across a performance spectrum isolates replicable factors from confounding context, moving beyond descriptive best practices to identify causal, transferable mechanisms of appropriate prescribing.

proposals.title: The Network-Enabled Expectation Management (NEX-M) Trial: A Multi-Center Study to Scale Effective Communication for Outpatient Antibiotic Stewardship
proposals.motivation: Antimicrobial stewardship (AMS) in outpatient settings is hindered by patient expectations for antibiotics, which clinicians often struggle to manage effectively. Current communication strategies are siloed within institutions, and there is no scalable mechanism to identify and propagate the most effective, patient-centered approaches across diverse clinical scenarios and demographics. This limits the cultural shift needed to reduce inappropriate antibiotic prescribing at a population level.
proposals.hypothesis: A centralized, data-driven platform that aggregates and analyzes anonymized communication outcomes from a network of clinics will identify superior communication strategies, and feeding these strategies back to clinicians will improve patient satisfaction and reduce antibiotic prescribing rates compared to standard AMS education.
proposals.method: We will implement a secure, HIPAA-compliant platform across a network of 50+ primary care clinics. The platform will collect structured data on communication techniques (via clinician self-report), patient satisfaction (via post-visit surveys), and clinical decisions (via EHR integration) for acute respiratory infection visits. Using natural language processing (e.g., BERT-based topic modeling) on anonymized clinician notes and patient feedback, combined with multivariate regression and clustering algorithms (e.g., k-means), we will identify communication patterns linked to high satisfaction and appropriate non-prescription. The platform will then provide dynamic, scenario-specific feedback to clinicians via a dashboard.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, scalable framework for AMS communication that moves beyond static guidelines. It will generate the first large-scale, real-world evidence on which communication strategies work best for whom, directly contributing to the 'Prioritizing Equity in Antimicrobial Stewardship Efforts (EASE)' framework. The resulting platform represents a new model for continuous learning and improvement in patient-clinician communication.
proposals.novelty: This approach transforms stewardship from a prescriptive, policy-driven activity into a networked, learning health system for communication. It uniquely applies data science and network effects—common in social media and bibliometric analysis—to the intimate clinical problem of expectation management, creating a feedback loop previously impossible at scale.

proposals.title: Modeling the Long-Term Health Equity Impact of Polygenic Risk Score Integration in Medical Admissions and Clinical Stewardship: A Systems Dynamics Simulation
proposals.motivation: The emergence of polygenic risk scores (PRS) for conditions like severe COVID-19 presents a novel, complex challenge for equity in medicine. Pre-2026, PRS are known to have differential performance across ancestral groups, potentially exacerbating health disparities. Simultaneously, medical education faces pressure to adopt holistic review to diversify the physician workforce, a key social determinant of health. The long-term, systemic consequences of introducing genetic risk data into these high-stakes admissions and subsequent clinical care pathways remain entirely unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the explicit inclusion of PRS in holistic medical admissions will, over decades, alter the demographic and genetic-risk composition of the physician workforce in ways that inadvertently worsen population health equity, whereas a 'PRS-blinded' holistic review policy will better optimize for both a diverse workforce and equitable health outcomes.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel, hybrid systems-dynamics and agent-based model. The model will integrate: 1) Pre-2026 PRS data and known health disparity metrics for infectious and chronic diseases, 2) Published data on holistic review outcomes and physician career trajectories, and 3) Parameters linking clinician demographics to patient trust and guideline adherence (e.g., in antimicrobial stewardship). The simulation will use a discrete-time, compartmental structure to project, over a 50-year horizon, how different admissions policies (PRS-informed, PRS-blinded, traditional) cascade through workforce composition and, via differential clinical care, to national disease burden metrics (DALYs, QALYs) stratified by race/ethnicity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative framework for policymakers and medical school faculty to anticipate the second- and third-order consequences of using genetic data in admissions. It will directly extend equity-focused frameworks like Prioritizing Equity in Antimicrobial Stewardship Efforts (EASE) into the genomic and educational policy domains, offering a rigorous simulation tool to navigate the ethical trade-offs between individual genetic risk and population health equity.
proposals.novelty: This study uniquely fuses genetic epidemiology, medical education policy, and health services research into a single simulation model, moving beyond descriptive bioethics to project quantified, long-term systemic outcomes. It applies a 'systems-dynamics' lens—common in public health but novel here—to the specific, high-stakes context of physician training and genomic integration.

proposals.title: Reconstructing Merit: A Historical-Archival Framework for Developing Equitable Competency Rubrics in Residency Selection
proposals.motivation: Despite growing recognition of structural inequities in medicine, residency selection processes often perpetuate historical disparities by undervaluing competencies forged within marginalized communities. Pre-2026, frameworks like Prioritizing Equity in Antimicrobial Stewardship (EASE) highlight the need for equity-centered clinical protocols, but similar rigor is rarely applied to the foundational act of workforce selection. This gap allows the legacy of discriminatory policies like hospital segregation and redlining to continue shaping medical workforce composition through ostensibly neutral metrics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a systematic historical analysis of specific exclusionary policies will reveal distinct, undervalued applicant competencies (e.g., community health navigation, resilience, systems critique), and that explicitly weighting these within structured interview rubrics will significantly alter the ranking of applicants from marginalized backgrounds compared to traditional rubrics.
proposals.method: We will conduct a focused historical archival analysis of policy documents, institutional records, and oral histories related to redlining and hospital segregation in a defined geographic region. Using qualitative content analysis and thematic coding, we will map identified systemic barriers to potential compensatory competencies. These competencies will be operationalized into behavioral anchors for a structured interview rubric. Using pre-2026 methods from social science and medical education studies, we will employ a comparative simulation: a panel will score de-identified applicant portfolios using both the novel historically-informed rubric and a conventional rubric, with outcomes statistically compared.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, transferable methodology for embedding historical accountability into selection systems, moving beyond bias training to active reparative design. It will contribute a validated, equity-calibrated interview rubric to the medical education toolkit and provide empirical evidence on how definitions of merit impact candidate evaluation, directly supporting stewardship of a more representative physician workforce.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in its direct linkage of specific historical policy analysis to the technical design of evaluative instruments in medical education, moving from general advocacy for equity to a mechanism-based intervention that actively 'reweights' competency based on historical harm.

proposals.title: Closing the Loop: A Dynamic Bias-Audit Feedback System for Adaptive Equity in Pediatric Infectious Diseases Career Advancement
proposals.motivation: Despite widespread adoption of Equity, Diversity, and Inclusion (EDI) frameworks in academic medicine, their impact on tangible career outcomes for underrepresented faculty remains poorly measured. In pediatric infectious diseases, where workforce diversity is critical for addressing health disparities, existing EDI cycles often lack embedded, quantitative mechanisms to assess whether specific interventions (e.g., revised hiring rubrics, mentorship programs) actually reduce bias in hiring and promotion. This creates a 'black box' where implementation proceeds without evidence of efficacy for accelerating career equity.
proposals.hypothesis: Embedding a continuous, quantitative bias-audit feedback loop into the operational stages of an EDI implementation cycle will generate actionable, real-time data that enables institutions to dynamically identify and iteratively refine the most effective interventions for improving career advancement equity for underrepresented pediatric ID professionals.
proposals.method: We will integrate a bias-audit module into the six-step 'Prioritizing Equity in Antimicrobial Stewardship Efforts (EASE)' framework, adapted for career equity. The module will employ pre-2026 natural language processing (e.g., word embedding association tests) to audit bias in job descriptions and promotion dossiers, and interrupted time-series analysis to measure changes in application, interview, and promotion rates by demographic groups. This data will be fed into a structured dashboard linked to specific EDI cycle phases (e.g., 'Assess' and 'Adjust'), creating a closed-loop system where quantitative outcomes directly inform the next cycle's interventions.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, operational model for evidence-based EDI practice in academic medicine, moving from static frameworks to adaptive systems. It will provide pediatric ID divisions with a replicable method to empirically link specific EDI actions to bias reduction and career outcomes, thereby shifting equity efforts from compliance-driven to data-driven stewardship.
proposals.novelty: While bias audits and EDI frameworks exist separately, their fusion into a single, dynamic operational system—where audit data automatically triggers iterative refinement of the EDI cycle itself—is a novel application of continuous quality improvement principles to faculty equity, creating a self-correcting mechanism absent from prior implementations.

proposals.title: Implementation and Impact of a Six-Step Equity, Diversity, and Inclusion (EDI) Cycle in Pediatric Antimicrobial Stewardship Programs: A Multi-Center Mixed-Methods Study
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, healthcare disparities in pediatric infectious diseases, particularly in antimicrobial use and access, are well-documented but inadequately addressed by standard stewardship protocols. The proposed Prioritizing Equity in Antimicrobial Stewardship Efforts (EASE) framework provides a conceptual model, yet its operationalization into a concrete, measurable implementation cycle within real-world pediatric institutions remains untested. This gap limits the ability of clinicians and health systems to systematically advance health equity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the structured implementation of the six-step EDI cycle (Assess, Acknowledge, Act, Analyze, Adjust, Advocate) within pediatric antimicrobial stewardship programs (ASPs) will lead to measurable improvements in equity-focused process metrics and be perceived as feasible and valuable by ASP faculty and members.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, multi-center cohort study with a convergent mixed-methods design. At three pediatric institutions, stewardship teams will implement the six-step cycle over 12 months, guided by a standardized toolkit. Quantitative analysis will compare pre- and post-implementation data on equity-sensitive metrics (e.g., appropriateness of antibiotic prescribing by patient race/ethnicity, interpreter use rates). Concurrently, we will perform qualitative analysis of serial focus groups and interviews with ASP speakers, members, and faculty, using thematic analysis to assess perceived feasibility, barriers, and impact. The two data strands will be integrated to provide a comprehensive evaluation.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical evidence on the real-world implementation of a structured EDI cycle in pediatric stewardship, moving from theory to practice. It will yield a validated implementation toolkit and preliminary data on the cycle's impact on both quantitative equity metrics and the institutional culture of stewardship programs, directly informing national guidelines.
proposals.novelty: While EDI principles are increasingly discussed, this study novelly applies a specific, repeatable operational cycle derived from the EASE framework and tests it using a combined medical-outcome and social-science methodology, comparing implementation across distinct sites to identify transferable lessons.

proposals.title: The Equity Blinding Protocol: A Pilot Study of Double-Blind Review for Promotion and Grant Applications in Health Inequities Research
proposals.motivation: Research on structural drivers of health inequities (e.g., in COVID-19, HIV, maternal mortality) is critical yet often undervalued in academic reward systems. Pre-2026 studies on implicit bias in peer review and bibliometric analyses suggest that interdisciplinary, social justice-oriented scholarship faces systematic devaluation due to author identity, institutional prestige, and disciplinary biases. This threatens both the career advancement of researchers in this field and the robustness of scientific responses to health crises.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a double-blind review protocol for promotion, tenure, and grant committees will significantly reduce the influence of non-merit factors (e.g., author demographics, institutional rank) and increase the perceived methodological rigor and conceptual contribution of health inequities research when evaluated on its own merits.
proposals.method: We will design a standardized blinding protocol to redact author names, institutions, and specific disease mentions from research narratives and CVs, creating 'blinded dossiers'. Using a within-subjects design, we will recruit faculty committee members from medical and social science departments. Participants will evaluate the same set of real, anonymized scholarship dossiers twice: once with traditional, unblinded materials and once with the blinded dossiers, using a validated scoring rubric for scholarly contribution. We will employ mixed-effects regression models to compare scores across conditions, controlling for reviewer characteristics.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, practical blinding framework that institutions can adopt to promote equity in academic evaluation. It will provide the first empirical evidence on how blinding affects the assessment of health equity research specifically, contributing directly to the 'Prioritizing Equity in Antimicrobial Stewardship Efforts (EASE)' framework by extending equity principles to the evaluation of science itself.
proposals.novelty: While double-blind review is known in journal publishing, its application to the high-stakes, multi-faceted evaluation of promotion and grant portfolios for a specific, vulnerable research domain (health inequities) is novel. This fusion of social science methods with academic policy directly tests a structural intervention for bias in a way prior bibliometric or survey studies have not.

proposals.title: Disentangling Antecedent Drivers of Disparity: A Causal Mediation Analysis of Occupation, Housing, and Healthcare Access on Infectious Disease Outcomes
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research has established that social determinants of health drive disparities in infectious disease incidence and mortality, but the relative contribution of specific, modifiable antecedent factors remains poorly quantified. This lack of granularity hinders the development of targeted, equitable interventions in antimicrobial stewardship and public health policy, as seen in frameworks like Prioritizing Equity in Antimicrobial Stewardship Efforts (EASE).
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that occupation (a proxy for exposure risk) and housing instability (a proxy for transmission environment) will have a greater direct effect on infection incidence, while healthcare access will be the primary mediator of disparities in mortality following infection.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using linked electronic health record and census tract-level social vulnerability index data from 2015-2024. We will employ a causal mediation analysis framework, specifically a parametric g-formula, to decompose the total effect of race/ethnicity and socioeconomic status on lab-confirmed infection and 30-day mortality into direct effects and indirect effects mediated through occupation (NAICS codes), housing (crowding, instability metrics), and healthcare access (provider density, insurance status). Sensitivity analyses will assess unmeasured confounding.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, actionable ranking of modifiable antecedent factors contributing to infectious disease disparities, moving beyond association to causal pathway estimation. It will directly inform the EASE framework by identifying the highest-yield social targets for stewardship programs and health system interventions to promote equity.
proposals.novelty: While social determinants are widely acknowledged, applying formal causal mediation methods from epidemiology to disentangle their specific, relative contributions to infectious disease outcomes is a novel integration of medical and social science techniques, offering a more precise tool for equity-focused prioritization.

proposals.title: A Community-Based Participatory Trial of Promotores-Led Education Versus Computerized Decision Support for Improving Antibiotic Prescribing in a Hispanic/Latinx Community
proposals.motivation: Antimicrobial stewardship (AMS) traditionally relies on provider-facing tools like computerized decision support systems (CDSS), which can be limited by alert fatigue and fail to address deep-seated patient expectations and systemic trust barriers, particularly in marginalized communities. Pre-2026, the Prioritizing Equity in Antimicrobial Stewardship Efforts (EASE) framework highlighted the need for equity-centered approaches, yet sustainable, community-driven alternatives to clinic-centric CDSS remain understudied.
proposals.hypothesis: An intensive, culturally grounded education program co-designed with and delivered by lay health workers (promotores) to community members will be non-inferior to a provider-facing CDSS alert in improving rates of guideline-concordant antibiotic prescribing for acute respiratory infections in a Hispanic/Latinx patient population.
proposals.method: Using a community-based participatory research (CBPR) framework, we will partner with a community advisory board to co-design the promotores curriculum and materials. In a pragmatic, cluster-randomized trial, community clusters will receive the promotores-led intervention (repeated group sessions and outreach), while control clinic clusters will have a CDSS alert activated for providers. The primary outcome is the rate of guideline-concordant prescribing for ARIs, analyzed using generalized linear mixed models to account for clustering. Secondary outcomes include patient knowledge, attitudes, and trust, measured via surveys.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide empirical evidence on the comparative effectiveness of a community-driven, equity-focused AMS model versus a technological solution. It contributes a practical, co-designed intervention blueprint and advances the operationalization of the EASE framework by testing a concrete model that shifts stewardship agency from clinicians to the community.
proposals.novelty: This study directly compares a community co-designed, peer-led educational model to a gold-standard technological CDSS, moving beyond simply evaluating 'more education' to testing a fundamentally different, sustainable locus for stewardship intervention that addresses trust and systemic barriers.

proposals.title: Beyond Correlation: A Mixed-Methods, Intersectional Analysis of Interpersonal Mechanisms and Clinic Context in Mediating the Impact of Demographic Differences on Clinical Interaction Quality
proposals.motivation: Persistent health disparities linked to patient-clinician demographic discordance highlight a critical gap: we know correlations but not the specific, modifiable mechanisms that determine whether differences harm or enhance care. This limits the development of effective interventions. Pre-2026, the field lacks granular, longitudinal studies that directly observe real interactions, measure hypothesized mediators like cultural humility, and link them to outcomes within specific clinic contexts that shape these dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: The impact of demographic differences on clinical interaction quality and patient outcomes is not direct but is mediated by measurable interpersonal behaviors (e.g., communication adaptation, expressed cultural humility) and moderated by clinic-level structural factors (e.g., time resources, equity norms). These mechanisms vary significantly across intersectional identity positions.
proposals.method: We propose a 24-month longitudinal, convergent mixed-methods study in infectious diseases clinics. We will recruit a diverse cohort of clinicians and patients, capturing intersectional demographics. Quantitative data: audio-recorded clinical encounters coded using the Roter Interaction Analysis System (RIAS) and the Cultural Humility Scale, linked to patient-reported experience measures (PREMs) and objective adherence/clinical outcomes. Qualitative data: post-encounter semi-structured interviews and clinic ethnography. Analysis will use structural equation modeling to test mediation/moderation and framework analysis for qualitative themes, with integration at the interpretation stage.
proposals.contribution: This work will move the field from describing disparities to identifying trainable clinician skills and actionable clinic-level supports that improve care across demographic differences. It will produce a validated, context-aware framework for "equity-positive" communication, directly informing the EASE (Prioritizing Equity in Antimicrobial Stewardship) model and providing concrete guidance for faculty development and medical education.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior survey-based or single-method studies, this research uniquely combines direct interaction observation, longitudinal outcome linkage, and deep contextual analysis through an explicit intersectional lens to unpack the 'how' and 'under what conditions' of demographic effects in real-world clinical settings.

proposals.title: Longitudinal Impact of Assessment System Redesign on Disability Disclosure and Accommodation Outcomes in Infectious Disease Fellowship Training
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, medical education faces increasing calls to address systemic inequities, including for trainees with disabilities. In infectious diseases (ID), where equity frameworks like Prioritizing Equity in Antimicrobial Stewardship Efforts (EASE) are emerging, there is a critical gap in understanding how structural changes in trainee assessment impact disability inclusion. Current literature evaluates accommodations or bias mitigation in isolation, lacking longitudinal data on how program-level evaluation reforms affect disclosure, accommodation utilization, and career progression.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that ID training programs implementing comprehensive assessment system reforms (e.g., de-emphasizing high-stakes exams in favor of workplace-based assessments) will be associated with earlier trainee disability disclosure, higher rates of approved and utilized accommodations, and improved well-being and performance metrics compared to programs maintaining traditional evaluation structures.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, prospective cohort study of ID fellows from internship through graduation. Using a mixed-methods design, we will quantitatively track assessment system characteristics, accommodation request timing/outcomes, and performance/well-being metrics (e.g., in-training exam scores, Milestones evaluations, burnout surveys). Qualitative interviews will explore trainee and faculty (stewardship) experiences. We will employ difference-in-differences and time-to-event analyses to compare outcomes between cohorts in programs undergoing systemic reform versus those with incremental changes, controlling for confounders identified from pre-2026 social science and medical education studies.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first longitudinal, empirical evidence linking specific assessment system redesigns to equity outcomes for ID trainees with disabilities. It will move the field from conceptual frameworks to actionable data, identifying which structural interventions most effectively foster inclusion. The findings will directly inform accreditation standards and programmatic reforms in ID and other medical specialties.
proposals.novelty: This work is novel in its longitudinal, program-level focus on system reform rather than individual accommodations, and its application of causal inference methods from health services research to a critical medical education equity problem that has not been rigorously studied in the ID specialty context.

proposals.title: Evaluating the Impact of a Structured Equity Framework on Mitigating Diagnostic Test Bias in Infectious Diseases Training and Practice
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 literature highlights pervasive biases in diagnostic testing, where patient demographics like race, gender, or socioeconomic status can influence test ordering and interpretation, leading to disparities in infectious diseases care (e.g., in HIV, sepsis, or COVID-19). While conceptual frameworks for equity in antimicrobial stewardship (like the EASE framework) exist, there is a critical lack of empirical evidence on the effectiveness of specific, actionable strategies to mitigate test bias in real-world clinical and educational settings.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a structured, equity-focused educational and clinical decision-support intervention, based on the Prioritizing Equity in Antimicrobial Stewardship Efforts (EASE) framework, will measurably reduce implicit bias in diagnostic test ordering and improve equity-oriented diagnostic reasoning among infectious diseases trainees and clinicians.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, mixed-methods study comparing intervention and control sites. The intervention will integrate the EASE framework into case-based didactics and clinical workflows via a modified SHEA (Stewardship, Healthcare Epidemiology, and Antimicrobial) curriculum. We will employ pre- and post-intervention surveys (e.g., Implicit Association Test adaptations) and analyze de-identified electronic health record data using interrupted time-series and difference-in-differences statistical models to measure changes in test-ordering patterns by patient demographic groups. Qualitative analysis of focus groups with faculty and members (trainees) will assess perceived impact on diagnostic stewardship.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical data on the efficacy of a defined equity intervention to reduce diagnostic test bias in infectious diseases. It will yield a validated, scalable training module for ID fellowships and generate concrete metrics for health systems to audit and improve diagnostic equity, directly extending stewardship principles from antimicrobials to diagnostics.
proposals.novelty: While bias in AI/algorithmic tools is a recognized problem, this work focuses on mitigating human cognitive bias at the point of clinical decision-making using a proven stewardship framework, and it moves beyond descriptive studies to a comparative, experimental evaluation of a specific mitigation strategy within a defined medical specialty.

proposals.title: Mechanisms of Disparity in Academic Infectious Diseases: An Audit and Ethnographic Study of Bias and Resource Allocation
proposals.motivation: Despite widespread recognition of disparities in healthcare career advancement, interventions often conflate distinct causes. Pre-2026 literature in medical education and social science highlights both unconscious bias and structural inequities as drivers, but their relative contributions and interactions in academic medicine, particularly infectious diseases (ID), remain poorly quantified. This ambiguity hinders the development of targeted, effective reforms in faculty promotion and stewardship roles.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that disparities in ID career advancement are driven more by inequitable distribution of critical career-enhancing resources (structural mechanism) than by evaluators' unconscious bias in assessing identical credentials (cognitive mechanism).
proposals.method: We will employ a sequential mixed-methods design. First, an experimental audit study will measure cognitive bias: identical CVs for a hypothetical ID faculty candidate, varying only name/gender/race, will be evaluated by department chairs for promotion readiness. Second, longitudinal ethnographic observation and archival analysis within three ID divisions will map the real-world allocation of mentorship, high-impact committee assignments, and research leads. We will compare audit results with actual promotion data and resource maps using multivariate regression and social network analysis.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a mechanism-specific attribution of career disparities, moving beyond correlation to identify primary drivers. It will produce a validated framework for diagnosing equity gaps in academic departments and offer clear, distinct evidence to guide interventions, whether focused on debiasing evaluations or restructuring resource allocation.
proposals.novelty: The novel integration of a controlled audit experiment with in-situ ethnographic resource mapping allows for the direct comparison and disentanglement of cognitive versus structural bias mechanisms, a methodological advance over studies examining only one level. Applying this to ID faculty and stewardship roles builds directly on the PI's prior work in equity and dissemination.

proposals.title: Modeling the Academic Niche: A Species Distribution Framework to Quantify Institutional Bias in Infectious Diseases Career Advancement
proposals.motivation: Despite widespread recognition of inequities in academic medicine, quantifying the specific impact of institutional factors on the career advancement of underrepresented professionals remains a challenge. Pre-2026 studies in infectious diseases (ID) have highlighted disparities but often rely on self-reported data or broad correlations, lacking a robust analytical framework to model the complex 'environment' of an institution and its constraining effects. This gap hinders the development of targeted, evidence-based stewardship interventions to promote equity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that Species Distribution Modeling (SDM) techniques can be successfully adapted to model the 'fundamental career niche' for ID professionals, and that the discrepancy between this predicted niche and observed advancement data will quantitatively reveal the magnitude and key drivers of institutional bias.
proposals.method: We will treat promotions to associate/full professor or leadership roles in US ID divisions as 'presence' data, sourced from public faculty directories and professional society listings. Institutional 'environmental variables' will be compiled from AAMC data, climate surveys, and policy audits (e.g., % diverse leadership, formal sponsorship programs, parental leave policies). Using the MaxEnt algorithm, a prevalent SDM method for presence-only data, we will model the probability of advancement as a function of these variables. Model performance will be evaluated via AUC, and variable importance will be assessed through jackknife tests to identify the most significant constraints.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, quantitative metric—the 'niche deviation score'—to benchmark institutional equity in ID. It will move the field from identifying disparities to modeling their systemic causes, offering a data-driven tool for departments to prioritize interventions. The findings will directly inform the 'Prioritizing Equity in Antimicrobial Stewardship Efforts (EASE)' framework by specifying actionable institutional variables.
proposals.novelty: The application of ecological niche modeling to sociological questions of bias is a profound methodological crossover. While bias is often studied qualitatively or with standard regression, this approach uniquely conceptualizes the institution as an ecosystem and advancement as an outcome of habitat suitability, providing a new lens for structural analysis.

proposals.title: Measuring Implicit Bias in Infectious Diseases: Development and Validation of a Specialty-Specific Implicit Association Test for Patient Stereotypes and Clinician Interactions
proposals.motivation: Implicit biases among healthcare providers contribute to health inequities, including in antimicrobial prescribing and patient engagement. In infectious diseases (ID), stereotypes related to conditions like HIV, substance use, or specific cultural groups can negatively impact the clinician-patient relationship and stewardship decisions. Existing general IATs lack the specificity to capture the unique stereotypes and interaction dynamics pertinent to ID practice, creating a measurement gap.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a novel, specialty-tailored Implicit Association Test (IAT) can reliably measure implicit associations specific to infectious disease patient populations and that these implicit biases will correlate with self-reported attitudes and influence simulated clinical interaction judgments.
proposals.method: We will develop and validate the ID-IAT using a multi-phase, mixed-methods approach. First, a Delphi panel of ID faculty and community members will identify key stereotype categories (e.g., 'HIV patient'–'non-adherent'). We will then construct an IAT using the standard seven-block sequential priming paradigm, implemented via platforms like Inquisit or Project Implicit's open-source software. The validation study will involve recruiting ID clinicians and trainees to complete the ID-IAT, explicit attitude surveys, and vignette-based assessments. Psychometric validation will include tests of internal consistency, convergent validity with explicit measures, and known-groups validity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first validated tool to quantify implicit bias within the ID specialty, enabling future studies to rigorously link bias to clinical outcomes. It will directly inform the 'Prioritizing Equity in Antimicrobial Stewardship Efforts (EASE)' framework by offering a measurable component of clinician bias. The methodology can serve as a blueprint for developing IATs in other medical subspecialties.
proposals.novelty: While IATs are used in general medical bias research, creating and validating one for the specific stereotypes and interaction contexts of infectious diseases is novel. This moves beyond generic bias measurement to capture the nuanced associations that directly impact ID care, stewardship, and trust.

proposals.title: The TEM-AID Protocol: A Pilot Study Testing an Aviation-Derived Threat and Error Management Framework to Mitigate Cognitive Bias in Antimicrobial Stewardship Teams
proposals.motivation: Unconscious bias in interprofessional infectious disease (ID) teams can undermine communication, equitable care, and stewardship decisions, leading to suboptimal patient outcomes. Pre-2026, frameworks like Prioritizing Equity in Antimicrobial Stewardship Efforts (EASE) highlight the need for practical tools to address bias, yet few structured, team-based interventions exist. High-reliability fields like aviation have successfully used systematic debriefing to surface cognitive threats, offering a promising but untested model for healthcare.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a structured Threat and Error Management (TEM) protocol for ID team case reviews will increase the team's self-reported identification of cognitive bias threats and improve measured psychological safety and communication clarity compared to standard practice.
proposals.method: We will conduct a mixed-methods, pre-post intervention study at two academic medical centers. First, we will adapt aviation CRM/TEM checklists through focus groups with ID faculty, pharmacists, nurses, and microbiologists to create the TEM-AID (Threat and Error Management for Antimicrobial Infectious Disease) tool. This tool will guide pre-briefing to flag potential biases (e.g., anchoring, stereotyping) and post-case debriefing to analyze errors. We will compare outcomes over 6 months using validated surveys (e.g., TeamSTEPPS Teamwork Perceptions Questionnaire, Edmondson's Psychological Safety Scale) and qualitative analysis of debriefing transcripts, employing thematic analysis to categorize identified threats and errors.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, operational protocol for bias mitigation in ID teamwork and provide initial evidence of its feasibility and impact. It will contribute a concrete method to advance the EASE framework's goals, moving equity in stewardship from principle to practiced team behavior. Findings will offer a transferable model for other high-stakes clinical decision-making teams.
proposals.novelty: While CRM principles are discussed in healthcare, the direct, structured adaptation of aviation's specific Threat and Error Management checklist protocol to explicitly target unconscious bias in ID teams is novel. This approach moves beyond general teamwork training to a replicable cognitive debiasing routine embedded in clinical workflow.

proposals.title: A Cluster-Randomized Trial of EHR-Embedded Nudges to Reduce Diagnostic Bias in Community-Acquired Pneumonia and Sepsis Recognition
proposals.motivation: Diagnostic errors in infectious diseases, such as missed sepsis or atypical pneumonia presentations, are a persistent source of patient harm and are influenced by well-documented cognitive biases like anchoring and representativeness. Pre-2026, while behavioral 'nudges' in EHRs have shown promise in areas like antibiotic stewardship, their empirical, disease-specific application to directly mitigate diagnostic bias remains understudied. This gap is critical, as diagnostic delays disproportionately impact marginalized patient groups, undermining equity in care.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that EHR-embedded, scenario-specific debiasing nudges (e.g., differential diagnosis checklists and altered test-ordering defaults) will significantly improve diagnostic accuracy and reduce time to appropriate therapy for conditions like sepsis and community-acquired pneumonia, with effects most pronounced in patient subgroups historically at risk for diagnostic disparities.
proposals.method: We will conduct a pragmatic, cluster-randomized controlled trial across multiple hospitalist and emergency medicine services. Using the Epic EHR's BestPractice Advisory (BPA) and order-set architecture, we will design nudges triggered by specific clinical data (e.g., SIRS criteria plus altered mental status) to prompt consideration of non-stereotypical infections or suggest procalcitonin ordering by default. Services will be randomized to receive active nudges or usual care. Primary outcomes will be diagnostic accuracy (via chart review) and time-to-appropriate antibiotic, analyzed using mixed-effects models.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate actionable, evidence-based templates for health systems to operationalize diagnostic equity within existing EHR platforms. It will advance the field of behavioral infectious diseases by providing rigorous, clinical outcome data on the efficacy of debiasing interventions, moving beyond theoretical frameworks to implementation science.
proposals.novelty: This study uniquely fuses the precision of behavioral economic interventions with the clinical specificity of infectious diseases diagnostics, empirically testing nudges designed to counteract specific biases (e.g., gender or age-based stereotypes) in high-stakes scenarios, rather than applying generic decision support.

proposals.title: Assessing Stewardship in Action: A Structured Interview Protocol to Reduce Bias in Non-Primary Care Trainee Selection
proposals.motivation: Selection bias in trainee recruitment for complex outpatient settings (e.g., infectious disease, urgent care) persists, undermining workforce equity and quality. Traditional interviews lack standardization and validated, setting-specific content to assess critical competencies like clinical reasoning and resource stewardship. This gap is acute in non-primary care specialties where high-stakes decisions on antimicrobials, diagnostics, and referrals are routine, necessitating tools that predict real-world performance.
proposals.hypothesis: A structured interview protocol, using clinical scenarios and decision-making principles derived from an expanded antimicrobial stewardship framework, will reduce inter-interviewer bias and improve the predictive validity of trainee selection for non-primary care outpatient training programs.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-phase, mixed-methods study. First, a modified Delphi panel of stewardship faculty and organizational psychologists will adapt the Prioritizing Equity in Antimicrobial Stewardship Efforts (EASE) framework into a bank of standardized clinical scenarios and anchored rating scales. Second, we will pilot the protocol in a randomized controlled trial comparing it to traditional interviews for applicant cohorts to infectious diseases fellowship programs. Outcomes will be measured using generalizability theory to quantify bias reduction and correlate interview scores with subsequent faculty evaluations of clinical stewardship during the first training year.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, transferable tool for objective assessment of stewardship aptitude in trainee selection. It will provide empirical evidence on how structured, content-valid interviews can advance equity in medical training admissions. The study will also extend the application of clinical stewardship frameworks beyond patient care into the pedagogical domain of workforce development.
proposals.novelty: This research uniquely fuses organizational psychology's structured interview methods with a clinical content framework (EASE) specifically for selection, moving beyond generic behavioral questions. It innovates by applying stewardship principles—typically used for patient-level decisions—to the human resource challenge of trainee selection.

proposals.title: Mapping the Bias Supply Chain in Sepsis Care: A Causal Framework for Systemic Equity Intervention
proposals.motivation: Despite growing recognition of health inequities, interventions in infectious diseases like sepsis often target individual clinician bias, neglecting the systemic, upstream sources embedded in data and workflows. Pre-2026 work in antimicrobial stewardship and social determinants of health highlights that biased data inputs and institutional protocols can perpetuate disparities in critical care. A proactive, system-level audit of these bias sources is needed to create self-correcting clinical ecosystems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that racial and gender disparities in sepsis recognition and management are driven by identifiable, causal nodes within a 'bias supply chain' spanning EHR documentation, team communication, and institutional guidelines, and that targeted interventions at these nodes will reduce measurable outcome disparities.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-site, retrospective cohort study using EHR data, applying causal directed acyclic graph (DAG) modeling and natural language processing (specifically, named entity recognition and sentiment analysis on clinical notes) to map bias propagation. This will be followed by a mixed-methods phase integrating social network analysis of care teams and qualitative interviews. We will then pilot a pragmatic, stepped-wedge trial of targeted interventions, including structured data entry prompts for social history and a revised sepsis protocol with equity checkpoints.
proposals.contribution: This research will produce a validated, generalizable framework for auditing and disrupting systemic bias in clinical care pathways. It will contribute specific, actionable interventions for health systems to generate less biased data and processes, directly advancing the 'Prioritizing Equity in Antimicrobial Stewardship' (EASE) agenda. The work will also demonstrate the application of causal inference and computational linguistics to health equity research.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in shifting the focus from mitigating bias in final decisions to intercepting it at its systemic sources, conceptualizing clinical data generation as a 'supply chain' amenable to audit and redesign. It uniquely combines causal modeling with sociotechnical analysis to identify root institutional—not just individual—sources of disparity.

proposals.title: Protocols as Shields: A Mixed-Methods Study of Systemic Interventions to Buffer Clinicians from Patient Bias and Promote Equity
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research documents high rates of patient bias and discrimination against clinicians from underrepresented groups, particularly in infectious diseases and antimicrobial stewardship contexts. This bias negatively impacts clinician well-being, contributes to burnout, and may introduce harmful variability into patient care pathways. While individual resilience is often emphasized, there is a critical gap in evidence for institutional-level, system-design solutions that proactively protect clinicians and ensure equitable care delivery.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing structured, team-based clinical protocols (e.g., standardized checklists and mandatory care-team introductions) will reduce the negative impact of patient bias by decreasing bias-driven care variability and lowering associated clinician stress and burnout.
proposals.method: We will conduct a pre-post, mixed-methods study in hospital departments. The intervention comprises two evidence-based protocols: a standardized clinical encounter checklist and a formalized care-team introduction script. Quantitative analysis will compare pre- and post-intervention data on clinician burnout (Maslach Burnout Inventory), patient satisfaction scores, and adherence to clinical care pathways (via EHR audit). Qualitative thematic analysis of semi-structured interviews with participating faculty and staff will elucidate intervention mechanisms and contextual barriers.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide actionable, evidence-based institutional protocols for healthcare systems to mitigate the impact of patient bias. It will contribute directly to the 'Prioritizing Equity in Antimicrobial Stewardship Efforts (EASE)' framework by offering a structural intervention. The findings will advance the field from documenting bias to testing concrete systemic solutions that safeguard clinician welfare and care equity.
proposals.novelty: This work shifts the focus from measuring bias or training clinicians to withstand it, to actively designing and testing system-level 'buffers'. It uniquely integrates equity frameworks from infectious diseases stewardship with operational safety protocols (e.g., checklists) to address a social threat to clinician well-being and care quality.

proposals.title: Assessing and Selecting for Bias Mitigation Competency: A Dual-Purpose Structured Interview to Promote Equity in Residency Selection and Workforce Resilience
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 literature documents pervasive patient bias against clinicians based on gender, race, and other identities, contributing to burnout and attrition. Concurrently, residency programs seek equitable selection tools to reduce interviewer bias. A proactive approach that selects for the specific competency of managing patient bias could build a more resilient workforce and improve patient care.
proposals.hypothesis: Residency candidates selected using a structured interview rubric that explicitly assesses bias recognition and mitigation skills will demonstrate higher self-reported resilience, lower attrition, and better patient-clinician relationship scores in their subsequent training compared to cohorts selected via traditional methods.
proposals.method: We will design a multi-station structured interview using validated behavioral scenarios (e.g., a patient refusing care) and a competency rubric scoring communication, de-escalation, and systems-awareness. This will be implemented in an Infectious Diseases residency match cycle. Using a prospective cohort design, we will compare selected residents to a historical control via longitudinal surveys (resilience, burnout), attrition records, and patient experience scores, analyzed using mixed-effects regression models.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, dual-purpose interview tool for equitable selection and competency assessment. It will provide the first longitudinal evidence linking a specific selection criterion—bias mitigation skill—to downstream workforce sustainability and patient outcomes, informing national residency selection guidelines.
proposals.novelty: It innovatively fuses equity-focused selection (reducing bias IN the process) with competency-based selection (for skills to manage bias FROM patients), creating a proactive pipeline intervention rather than a reactive support system.

proposals.title: A Point-of-Care Diagnostic Platform Integrating Multidrug-Resistant Pathogen Detection with Host Genetic Risk Profiling for Severe Infection
proposals.motivation: Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) is a critical global health threat, yet patient outcomes from resistant infections vary widely. Pre-2026 equity frameworks, like Prioritizing Equity in Antimicrobial Stewardship Efforts (EASE), correctly highlight structural drivers of disparities but lack tools to address biological heterogeneity within populations. A diagnostic that simultaneously identifies the resistant pathogen and a patient's genetic susceptibility would enable more precise, personalized risk stratification at the bedside.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a rapid, multiplexed diagnostic test that concurrently detects bacterial AMR markers and host single-nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) associated with dysregulated immune response (e.g., in TLR or cytokine genes) will identify a high-risk patient phenotype with superior predictive value for severe sepsis or mortality compared to pathogen detection alone.
proposals.method: We will develop a lab-on-a-chip device using isothermal amplification (e.g., RPA/LAMP) for rapid nucleic acid extraction and detection. The assay will target a panel of bacterial AMR genes (e.g., mecA, blaKPC, blaNDM) and host SNPs (e.g., TLR4 Asp299Gly, TNF-α -308G>A) via multiplexed fluorescent probe detection. A companion Bayesian statistical algorithm, integrating microbial and genetic data with basic clinical variables, will generate a composite risk score. Validation will occur via a prospective cohort study comparing the platform's performance to standard culture and PCR in patients with suspected Gram-negative bacteremia.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel diagnostic tool that operationalizes the concept of 'personalized stewardship' by providing immediate, actionable data on both pathogen and host. It will contribute a new methodological framework for studying health disparities that integrates structural and biological factors. The research will yield validated host-pathogen genetic risk profiles to guide earlier, more aggressive intervention in high-risk individuals.
proposals.novelty: While rapid AMR diagnostics exist, none concurrently profile host genetic risk at the point-of-care. This fusion moves beyond attributing outcome differences solely to access or pathogen factors by quantifying individual biological susceptibility within a framework of equity, a novel synthesis of precision medicine and public health principles.

proposals.title: Correlating Wastewater Antimicrobial Resistance Gene Flux with Medical Student Demographics: A Novel Ecological Metric for Admissions Equity
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, medical schools are rapidly de-emphasizing standardized tests (e.g., MCAT) to promote equity, but the long-term public health consequences of these pipeline changes are unknown. Concurrently, wastewater-based epidemiology (WBE) for AMR surveillance has matured, providing a population-level, objective measure of a critical health threat. This creates an urgent need to move beyond institutional metrics like graduation rates and assess how admissions reforms impact the community-level burdens future physicians will face.
proposals.hypothesis: Temporal and geographic shifts in the prevalence of key AMR genes in municipal wastewater will correlate with changes in the academic (e.g., MCAT scores, undergraduate major) and demographic (e.g., geographic origin, socioeconomic background) profiles of matriculating medical students in corresponding regions, indicating that admissions criteria influence the physician pipeline for endemic resistance hotspots.
proposals.method: We will conduct a longitudinal, ecological correlation study. Publicly available AAMC data on matriculant profiles (2015-2025) for all U.S. medical schools will be merged with geocoded, longitudinal wastewater AMR gene data (e.g., for blaCTX-M, mecA, vanA) from the CDC's National Wastewater Surveillance System and academic publications. Using segmented time-series regression and mixed-effects models, we will analyze correlations between annual changes in admissions variables (e.g., mean MCAT percentile) and lagged changes in local AMR gene abundance, controlling for regional antibiotic use and hospital bed density. Spatial analysis will map medical school catchment areas against AMR gene prevalence clusters.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first population-health outcome framework for evaluating medical education equity initiatives, shifting the discourse from individual student success to community health stewardship. It will generate a novel, ecological metric (AMR gene flux) for assessing the long-term impact of admissions policies. The findings will directly inform the Prioritizing Equity in Antimicrobial Stewardship Efforts (EASE) framework by linking physician training pipelines to endemic resistance landscapes.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of wastewater epidemiology with medical education analytics is unprecedented. It proposes a non-obvious, indirect outcome measure (community AMR burden) to evaluate an educational policy intervention, moving far beyond traditional bibliometric or survey-based studies in the field.

proposals.title: Intersectional Heterogeneity and Unintended Consequences: A Mixed-Methods Study of URM Applicant Profiles in the Post-Step 1 Pass/Fail Era
proposals.motivation: The 2022 transition of USMLE Step 1 to pass/fail scoring was intended to reduce bias and support holistic review in medical school admissions. However, this major policy shift, grounded in pre-2026 equity frameworks, risks unintended consequences. It assumes uniform benefit for all Underrepresented in Medicine (URM) applicants, potentially overlooking how the de-emphasis of a key standardized metric may disadvantage specific URM subgroups who leveraged strong scores to counterbalance other application elements.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the URM applicant pool is characterized by significant, measurable heterogeneity in academic profiles and that the removal of a scored Step 1 filter disproportionately disadvantages certain intersectional subgroups (e.g., high-scoring applicants from immigrant communities) within holistic review processes, thereby undermining equity goals.
proposals.method: We will employ a convergent mixed-methods design. Quantitatively, we will analyze de-identified application data (MCAT, GPA, research output) from a multi-institutional cohort (2018-2024), using latent class analysis and regression modeling to identify distinct URM applicant profiles and their post-policy outcomes. Qualitatively, we will conduct semi-structured interviews and focus groups with URM applicants, admissions committee members, and diversity officers, analyzed through thematic analysis. Data strands will be integrated to map profiles onto lived experiences and committee perceptions.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical map of academic heterogeneity within URM pools, directly testing equity assumptions of the Step 1 policy change. It will contribute a novel, data-driven framework—the Prioritizing Equity in Admissions Stewardship (PEAS) framework—to guide admissions committees in implementing nuanced, intersectional holistic review that avoids homogenizing URM applicants.
proposals.novelty: This work moves beyond comparing URM vs. non-URM outcomes to examine intra-group diversity, a critical gap in pre-2026 literature. It uniquely applies an intersectional lens and latent class methods to admissions data, challenging the monolithic treatment of URM applicants in prevailing equity discourses.

proposals.title: Transparency as Trust: A Mixed-Methods Study of Patient-Portal Antibiotic Stewardship Decision Support and Its Impact on Institutional Trust and Vaccine Acceptance
proposals.motivation: Public health institutions face a critical trust deficit, which undermines adherence to guidelines and vaccine uptake. Concurrently, antimicrobial stewardship (AMS) is a core institutional competency, but its processes are often opaque to patients. Integrating patient-facing AMS logic into the EHR portal offers a novel opportunity to demonstrate the healthcare system's commitment to evidence-based, ethical care in a visible, educational manner.
proposals.hypothesis: That providing transparent, patient-accessible antibiotic decision support within the EHR patient portal will significantly increase patient-reported trust in the healthcare institution, and that this increased trust will be positively associated with higher reported intent to accept recommended vaccines.
proposals.method: We will conduct a pragmatic, cluster-randomized trial in primary care clinics. The intervention is an EHR-integrated module (built using SMART on FHIR standards) that displays a simplified, educational version of the institutional antibiotic guideline when an antibiotic is prescribed or withheld. We will measure the primary outcome, institutional trust, using the validated Wake Forest Physician Trust Scale (adapted for the healthcare system) and secondary behavioral intentions via surveys. Analysis will compare pre-post changes between intervention and control clinics using mixed-effects models, with qualitative interviews to explore mechanisms.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical evidence linking a specific EHR-based transparency intervention in AMS to the psychological construct of institutional trust. It will also test a novel synergistic model where a clinical quality improvement tool (AMS) directly serves a public health communication goal, potentially creating a measurable pathway from stewardship to improved vaccine acceptance.
proposals.novelty: While AMS and public health communication are well-studied in isolation, this proposal fuses them, treating the stewardship tool itself as a direct, trust-building communication intervention. It moves beyond measuring prescribing outcomes to measure the tool's impact on the patient-system relationship and downstream public health behaviors.

proposals.title: From Training to Autonomy: A Longitudinal and Comparative Study of System-Level Barriers to Patient-Centered Screening Outcomes in Infectious Diseases
