proposals.motivation: Antimicrobial and diagnostic stewardship programs emphasize patient-centered outcomes, yet most research focuses on test ordering rates rather than the complete care pathway, including diagnosis, linkage to care, and follow-up. Pre-2026 literature identifies barriers in training environments but lacks analysis of how these constraints evolve with career progression. Understanding this evolution is critical for designing effective, equity-focused interventions that support clinicians at the most impactful career phase to improve real patient benefit.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that as physicians transition from residency to independent practice, the primary barriers to achieving patient-centered screening outcomes shift from knowledge and training deficits to system-level constraints (e.g., time, administrative burden, fragmented care coordination), and that this shift diminishes the capacity to ensure patient follow-up despite greater clinical autonomy.
proposals.method: We will employ a convergent mixed-methods design. Quantitatively, we will analyze de-identified electronic health record data from multiple health systems to compare screening cascade metrics (order, result, diagnosis, linkage) for conditions like HIV and HCV across clinicians at different career stages. Qualitatively, we will conduct semi-structured interviews and focus groups with residents, fellows, and early-career attendings, using a framework analysis to map perceived barriers onto the Quantitative Cascade Model. The two strands will be integrated to identify dominant constraint typologies per career stage.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, career-stage-specific framework of barriers to patient-centered stewardship, moving beyond simple test utilization. It will provide actionable evidence for institutional leaders and program designers on whether to target interventions at training, transition, or early independent practice phases to most effectively improve equity in screening outcomes and linkage to care.
proposals.novelty: This study moves beyond measuring test orders to track the entire patient outcome cascade across a critical professional transition, integrating robust digital phenotyping with qualitative insights on evolving constraints, a lens not applied in pre-2026 stewardship or medical education research.

proposals.title: A Two-Tiered, Adaptive Clinical Decision Support System to Reduce Screening Disparities: A Pragmatic Cluster-Randomized Trial
proposals.motivation: Clinical decision support systems (CDSS) often fail to reduce health inequities, as they apply uniform rules that do not address patient-level social risk or clinic-level implementation barriers. This is critical in preventive screening, where disparities in cancer and infectious disease outcomes persist. A dynamic, equity-focused CDSS is needed to allocate intervention intensity where it is most needed, aligning with national calls for equity in antimicrobial and chronic disease stewardship.
proposals.hypothesis: A CDSS that dynamically activates intensive patient prompts based on a real-time risk model (incorporating SDOH and clinical history) and provides tiered clinic support based on organizational readiness will increase recommended screening rates more effectively and equitably than a standard, uniform CDSS.
proposals.method: We will conduct a pragmatic, cluster-randomized trial in primary care clinics. The intervention CDSS uses a real-time risk prediction model (e.g., gradient boosting or a high-dimensional propensity score algorithm) integrating EHR data and area-level social determinants of health (SDOH) to flag high-risk patients for enhanced prompts. Clinics are concurrently assessed using the Organizational Readiness for Implementing Change (ORIC) instrument and historical performance data to receive tiered implementation support (e.g., workflow integration, tailored staff training). Control clinics receive a standard, non-adaptive CDSS. Primary outcome is the change in screening rate disparity (e.g., by race, ethnicity, area deprivation index) over 12 months.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for 'precision implementation' of CDSS, directly addressing equity at patient and system levels. It will generate evidence on the effectiveness of adaptive health IT interventions and offer a scalable model for operationalizing equity in routine clinical informatics, moving beyond one-size-fits-all approaches.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior CDSS studies, this design simultaneously tailors the intervention at two levels—individual patient risk and clinic readiness—using real-time data. It applies an equity-in-design principle from antimicrobial stewardship (EASE framework) to a broader preventive care context, creating a dynamic rather than static system.

proposals.title: Fusing Medical Education and Stigma Reduction: A Mixed-Methods Evaluation of a Workplace Training Program for Managing Drug-Resistant CMV and Disability Disclosure
proposals.motivation: Drug-resistant cytomegalovirus (CMV) poses a significant health risk to immunocompromised employees, requiring complex clinical management. In workplace settings, stigma and fear of disability disclosure often delay symptom reporting, hindering timely accommodations and increasing transmission risk. This creates a critical gap where medical knowledge alone is insufficient without addressing the social and psychological barriers to care.
proposals.hypothesis: An integrated training program for managers and teams that combines evidence-based clinical education on drug-resistant CMV with structured stigma-reduction interventions will significantly increase psychological safety, leading to earlier symptom disclosure and more effective implementation of workplace accommodations compared to clinical education alone.
proposals.method: We will employ a convergent mixed-methods design. First, we will develop the training curriculum using the Prioritizing Equity in Antimicrobial Stewardship Efforts (EASE) framework to structure the clinical content and integrate validated stigma-reduction modules (e.g., contact-based education, social norming). Second, we will conduct a quasi-experimental study comparing intervention and control workplace units, measuring outcomes via pre/post surveys (e.g., stigma scales, knowledge tests) and analyzing reported accommodation requests. Qualitative data from focus groups will be thematically analyzed to understand mechanisms of change.
proposals.contribution: This research will produce a novel, practical framework for merging infectious disease stewardship with disability inclusion in occupational health. It will provide empirical evidence on how reducing stigma acts as a social determinant of health in managing chronic infections, directly contributing to the 'speakers, members, stewardship' themes by empowering workplace leaders as health stewards.
proposals.novelty: While stigma reduction and CMV management are studied separately, this proposal uniquely fuses them into a single operational protocol for the workplace. It applies the equity-focused EASE framework—typically used in clinical antimicrobial stewardship—to the novel context of occupational health and non-pharmaceutical interventions.

proposals.title: An Equity-Focused Implementation Science Protocol for Cytomegalovirus Immunotherapy in Transplant Recipients: The EASE-Immuno Framework
proposals.motivation: Cytomegalovirus (CMV) infection remains a major cause of morbidity and mortality in solid organ and hematopoietic stem cell transplant recipients. While novel adoptive T-cell immunotherapies show clinical promise, pre-2026 evidence indicates that access to advanced transplant therapies is inequitable, with disparities driven by structural, socioeconomic, and healthcare system factors. A proactive, equity-centered implementation strategy is therefore critical to ensure these costly, complex biologics do not exacerbate existing outcome disparities among transplant populations.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying an equity-focused implementation science framework (e.g., the Health Equity Implementation Framework) during the clinical development phase of CMV immunotherapies will enable the identification and proactive mitigation of context-specific barriers, leading to a rollout protocol that improves equitable access, adherence, and clinical outcomes compared to a standard efficacy-then-dissemination approach.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-phase, mixed-methods study. First, we will use the Health Equity Implementation Framework to guide parallel qualitative interviews and focus groups with key stakeholders—including transplant recipients, caregivers, clinicians (infectious diseases, transplant surgery), pharmacists, and hospital administrators—across diverse care settings (academic, community, safety-net). Second, we will analyze this data using a modified grounded theory approach to map barriers and facilitators across socioecological levels. Third, we will employ discrete choice experiments and conjoint analysis with transplant clinicians to model preferences for immunotherapy program design features. These findings will be synthesized into a tailored implementation blueprint.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, generalizable implementation protocol (EASE-Immuno) for equitable deployment of advanced transplant therapies, directly extending the EASE framework for antimicrobial stewardship. It will generate actionable evidence on multi-level barriers specific to CMV immunotherapy and provide a tested model for integrating equity and implementation science early in therapeutic development to preempt disparities.
proposals.novelty: This study moves beyond post-hoc dissemination research by integrating equity-focused implementation science prospectively with therapeutic development, a novel synergy. It also uniquely applies methods from social science and discrete choice modeling, common in health services research, to the specific, high-stakes context of transplant immunotherapy rollout.

proposals.title: Beyond Viral Load: A Mixed-Methods Framework for Formalizing Informal Supports in the Management of Drug-Resistant Cytomegalovirus
proposals.motivation: Drug-resistant cytomegalovirus (CMV) poses a severe threat to immunocompromised patients, particularly transplant recipients. Pre-2026, clinical management focuses on virologic surveillance and limited salvage therapies, yet outcomes vary significantly. This variation suggests that critical, informal supports—like nuanced caregiver communication, flexible appointment scheduling, and patient-led advocacy—play an unmeasured but essential role in adherence and clinical success, creating an equity gap where access to such informal support is uneven.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific categories of informal support (e.g., logistical, communicative, advocacy-based) are consistently reported as critical for managing drug-resistant CMV, and that a subset of these can be systematically operationalized within institutional protocols, thereby reducing outcome disparities linked to support access.
proposals.method: We will employ a convergent mixed-methods design. First, a quantitative analysis of electronic health records and antimicrobial stewardship reports will establish baseline clinical outcomes. Second, qualitative data will be gathered via in-depth interviews and focus groups with three key stakeholder 'speakers': patients, caregivers, and infectious diseases faculty. Using a modified grounded theory approach, we will code transcripts to identify and categorize informal supports. Finally, these categories will be 'compared' against existing stewardship frameworks using a Delphi panel process to determine which supports are candidates for formalization and which inherently resist it.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, holistic 'EASE-for-CMV' framework that integrates virologic surveillance with a taxonomy of human and logistical supports. It will provide infectious diseases clinicians and stewardship programs with actionable strategies to institutionalize key supports, moving beyond purely biomedical management. The study will also delineate the inherent limits of formalization, clarifying where flexible, individualized 'stewardship' remains essential.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have reported on clinical outcomes of resistant CMV, none have systematically analyzed the informal care ecosystem as a core component of effective management. The novelty lies in applying equity-focused stewardship principles, typically used for antibiotics, to the complex sociotechnical system of managing a chronic viral infection, explicitly seeking to formalize the informal.

proposals.title: Integrating Host Pharmacogenomics and Social Determinants to Model Disparities in Cytomegalovirus Treatment Outcomes: A Precision Virology Framework
proposals.motivation: Cytomegalovirus (CMV) disease, particularly in transplant and immunocompromised patients, shows significant disparities in severity and treatment efficacy, often attributed to social determinants of health (SDOH). However, pre-2026 research in HIV has demonstrated that host genetic factors (e.g., in CYP enzymes for drug metabolism or immune genes like UL144) critically influence antiviral resistance and failure, a dimension largely unexplored in CMV. This gap limits our ability to provide equitable, personalized therapy, as biological predispositions may interact with structural barriers to worsen outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that host genetic polymorphisms in drug metabolism (e.g., CYP2C19 for valganciclovir activation) and immune response pathways interact with measured social determinants of health (e.g., medication adherence support, access to therapeutic drug monitoring) to create distinct, predictable risk profiles for CMV treatment failure and the emergence of viral drug resistance.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study of transplant recipients with CMV viremia, comparing patients with treatment failure/resistance to controls. Host DNA will be genotyped via targeted next-generation sequencing panels for candidate genes (e.g., CYP2C19, UL144, TLRs). SDOH will be extracted from electronic health records using validated instruments (e.g., ADI) and patient surveys. We will employ multivariable logistic regression and machine learning (e.g., random forest with SHAP analysis) to model the interaction between genetic risk scores and SDOH variables on the primary outcome of treatment failure. Viral resistance will be confirmed by UL54/97 gene sequencing.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a unified, biopsychosocial model for CMV outcomes that moves beyond describing disparities to explaining mechanistic pathways. It will directly inform the development of more equitable antimicrobial stewardship (EASE) protocols by identifying high-risk patient profiles for targeted intervention. The framework establishes a novel precision virology approach applicable to other opportunistic infections.
proposals.novelty: While viral genotyping for CMV resistance is established, the systematic integration of host pharmacogenomics with granular SDOH data to explain treatment disparities is novel. This approach fuses the principle of rapid resistance genotyping from HIV with Marcelin's expertise in equity-focused stewardship, creating a new explanatory model at the host-virus-society interface.

proposals.title: Blind Audition Protocols in GME: A Controlled Experiment to Quantify Structural vs. Interpersonal Bias in Residency Screening
proposals.motivation: Significant racial, ethnic, and gender disparities persist in Graduate Medical Education (GME) selection, particularly in competitive specialties like Infectious Diseases, impacting career trajectories and workforce diversity. While implicit bias in selection committees is a known concern, the contribution of structural inequities embedded in applicants' CVs—such as differential access to research, prestigious mentors, or high-quality clinical rotations—remains poorly quantified. This study directly addresses the call in pre-2026 equity frameworks, like the Prioritizing Equity in Antimicrobial Stewardship Efforts (EASE) model, to move from recognizing bias to measuring its systemic drivers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that implementing a blinded application review protocol for residency interviews will significantly reduce, but not eliminate, demographic disparities in interview offers. The residual disparity observed under blinded conditions will provide a quantitative measure of the impact of structural inequities on applicant competitiveness.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-institution, randomized controlled trial within a consortium of Infectious Diseases residency programs. Applications will be randomly assigned to either a standard review arm or a blinded arm, where all identifiable demographic data (name, photo, gender-indicating activities) and institution names are algorithmically redacted using a validated text-scrubbing tool. Primary outcomes will be rates of interview offers, stratified by applicant demographics (self-reported URM status, gender) obtained post-review. The analysis will compare disparity metrics (e.g., odds ratios) between the two arms using mixed-effects logistic regression, with the difference quantifying interpersonal bias and the blinded-arm residual quantifying structural drivers.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first direct, experimental quantification of the structural component of bias in GME screening, moving beyond correlation to causation. It will generate actionable evidence to guide interventions, showing whether efforts should focus primarily on committee training (if interpersonal bias is large) or on pipeline programs addressing resource inequities (if structural bias dominates). The findings will directly inform national GME policy and equity initiatives.
proposals.novelty: While blind auditions are studied in orchestras, their application as a controlled experimental tool to disentangle and measure structural versus interpersonal bias in academic medicine is novel. This approach transforms a bias-mitigation technique into a rigorous diagnostic instrument for systemic inequity.

proposals.title: Disaggregating Disparities: A Comparative Analysis of Racial and Socioeconomic Inequities Across CLABSI, CAUTI, and SSI in U.S. Hospitals
proposals.motivation: Healthcare-associated infections (HAIs) are a major source of morbidity and mortality, and pre-2026 evidence consistently shows that racial and socioeconomic disparities exist in HAI incidence. However, most studies and national surveillance programs, like the CDC's NHSN, aggregate HAIs or focus on single types, potentially masking critical heterogeneity. Understanding whether disparities are uniform or vary by infection type is essential for designing targeted, equitable stewardship and prevention interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the magnitude and drivers of racial, ethnic, and socioeconomic disparities in HAI rates are not uniform but differ significantly by infection type (e.g., CLABSI, CAUTI, SSI), reflecting variations in underlying patient risk, device utilization patterns, and care processes.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center retrospective cohort study using linked clinical-administrative data from 2018-2024 from a consortium of U.S. hospitals. The primary exposure will be patient race/ethnicity and area-level socioeconomic status (SES) via the Area Deprivation Index. The primary outcome will be incidence of specific HAIs (CLABSI, CAUTI, SSI), defined by NHSN criteria. We will use multivariable mixed-effects Poisson regression models, with hospital as a random effect, to calculate adjusted incidence rate ratios (aIRRs) for each HAI type. Models will be stratified by HAI type and include interaction terms between patient demographics and infection type to formally test for heterogeneity in disparity effects.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comparative, quantitative assessment of disparities across major HAI types, moving beyond the assumption of uniformity. It will generate infection-specific evidence to inform the Prioritizing Equity in Antimicrobial Stewardship Efforts (EASE) framework by identifying which HAIs and patient populations require the most urgent, tailored intervention. The findings will guide hospitals and policymakers in allocating resources for equity-focused infection prevention.
proposals.novelty: While disparities in aggregated HAIs are documented, a direct, head-to-head comparison of disparities across specific infection types using standardized methodology is novel. This disaggregated approach challenges the implicit assumption that 'an HAI disparity is an HAI disparity' and seeks to uncover the unique epidemiology of inequity for each major infection.

proposals.title: Modeling Structural Disability Barriers as Mediators of Healthcare-Associated Infection Risk: A Mixed-Methods Study of Program Director Training and Unit-Level Compliance
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, healthcare equity research has identified disparities in Healthcare-Associated Infection (HAI) rates but often focuses on socioeconomic or racial determinants, with disability remaining a neglected structural factor. Concurrently, antimicrobial stewardship frameworks like Prioritizing Equity in Antimicrobial Stewardship Efforts (EASE) call for upstream, structural interventions. This study addresses a critical gap by investigating how systemic failures in ADA compliance and clinician training create physical and communication environments that directly elevate infection risk for patients with disabilities.
proposals.hypothesis: Inadequate training of medical program directors on ADA requirements and poor unit-level implementation of accessibility standards mediate increased HAI incidence among patients with disabilities, with specific, measurable environmental barriers (e.g., inaccessible hand hygiene stations) serving as causal pathways.
proposals.method: We will employ a convergent mixed-methods design. First, a national survey of infectious diseases and hospital medicine program directors will assess knowledge, attitudes, and practices regarding ADA compliance, analyzed using structural equation modeling (SEM). Second, structured environmental audits of participating units will quantify accessibility of key infection control points. Third, these data will be linked to electronic health record-derived HAI rates (CDC NHSN definitions) for patients with documented disabilities. A mediation analysis will test whether audit scores statistically mediate the relationship between director knowledge scores and patient-level HAI outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, testable model of disability as a structural determinant of HAI risk, moving beyond documentation of disparities to identify specific, modifiable environmental mediators. It will generate actionable data to inform targeted training for program directors and unit-level stewardship interventions, directly advancing the equity goals of frameworks like EASE.
proposals.novelty: This study uniquely shifts the focus from patient-level disability as a risk factor to institutional compliance failures as a root cause, and innovatively applies mediation analysis from social science to model how structural barriers translate into biological infection risk, a linkage not previously established in the HAI literature.

proposals.title: A Network-Adjusted Equity Performance Metric for Healthcare-Associated Infections: Integrating Contact Dynamics and Social Vulnerability to Incentivize Equitable Stewardship
proposals.motivation: Significant racial and socioeconomic disparities in Healthcare-Associated Infection (HAI) rates persist, yet current national quality metrics inadequately account for the structural and network-based drivers of these inequities. Hospitals serving more vulnerable populations or embedded in dense patient transfer networks face different transmission risks, which are not captured by standard risk-adjustment. This creates a perverse disincentive for these hospitals under purely outcome-based equity metrics, as they may be penalized for factors beyond their immediate control.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating network connectivity data (e.g., inter-facility patient transfers) and community-level social vulnerability indices into HAI equity metrics will produce a more accurate, fair, and actionable performance assessment that better reflects a hospital's opportunity to reduce disparities within its specific epidemiological context.
proposals.method: We will construct a multi-layer network model using pre-2026 Medicare claims data to map inter-facility patient transfers and adjacency-based community contact. This network structure will be integrated with CDC NHSN HAI data and the CDC/ATSDR Social Vulnerability Index (SVI). Using methods from infectious disease modeling (e.g., metapopulation models) and econometrics (e.g., hierarchical Bayesian regression for risk-adjustment), we will develop a composite metric. This metric will compare observed vs. network-predicted HAI disparity outcomes, adjusting for the inherent transmission risk and vulnerability of the served population.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first proof-of-concept for a national HAI equity metric that is explicitly adjusted for structural transmission risks. It will offer a practical framework for policymakers to design more equitable accountability systems. For clinicians and stewardship programs, it will shift the focus from blame to identifying and interrupting specific network pathways that perpetuate inequities.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of epidemiological network science with health equity performance measurement, moving beyond patient-level clinical risk-adjustment to incorporate facility- and community-level structural determinants of transmission risk into a formal accountability metric.

proposals.title: Simulating the Health System Impact of Holistic Residency Selection: A Multi-Platform EHR-Based Modeling Study
proposals.motivation: The 2022 transition of the USMLE Step 1 to pass/fail and the push for holistic review in graduate medical education (GME) represent a seismic policy shift aimed at improving workforce diversity and well-being. However, its downstream effects on patient care quality, health system efficiency, and public health metrics remain entirely theoretical and unquantified. This gap creates uncertainty for policymakers and health system leaders who must steward resources and plan for future clinical needs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a policy shift toward holistic residency selection, modeled using real-world EHR data, will predictably alter the composition and performance of the physician workforce in ways that measurably improve specific health system outcomes, such as adherence to evidence-based screening protocols in underserved populations.
proposals.method: We will use the integrated data warehouse of a large regional health network, spanning multiple EHR platforms (e.g., Epic, Cerner), to create a retrospective cohort of residents and their associated patient panels from 2015-2024. We will employ agent-based modeling to simulate the policy change, where 'agents' (residents) have attributes derived from pre-holistic metrics (e.g., Step 1 scores) and proxies for holistic qualities (e.g., prior community service, research in health equity). The model will link resident cohorts to patient outcomes (e.g., HIV screening rates, diabetes control) and care process metrics. Predictive validity will be tested using machine learning techniques like gradient boosting to compare simulated outcomes against hold-out historical data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirically grounded, quantitative simulation of how a national GME policy change propagates through a health system to affect real patient care. It will offer a novel framework for 'health system impact forecasting' of educational policies, moving beyond trainee-centric outcomes. The findings will directly inform ongoing stewardship efforts by clinical department leaders and GME policymakers by highlighting potential leverage points for maximizing equitable care outcomes.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have compared resident performance metrics or surveyed program directors, none have used multi-platform EHR data to build a causal simulation model that connects selection policy to population health outcomes at the system level. This fusion of medical education research with operational health services data science is a novel approach.

proposals.title: Diagnosing Disparity: An Inverse Problem Analysis of Systemic Barriers to Equitable Enrollment in a Landmark Liver Transplant Prophylaxis Trial
proposals.motivation: Despite the critical need for equitable evidence, clinical trials in transplantation, including pivotal studies on cytomegalovirus prophylaxis, persistently fail to enroll representative patient populations. This limits the generalizability of findings and perpetuates health inequities. A systematic, post-trial investigation is needed to move beyond documenting disparities to actively diagnosing their root causes within trial implementation systems.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying an inverse problem framework to a completed trial's implementation data will reveal specific, modifiable systemic barriers—beyond individual patient factors—that systematically limited equitable enrollment, particularly affecting racially minoritized and socioeconomically disadvantaged groups.
proposals.method: We will conduct a mixed-methods, multi-level analysis of the completed trial's implementation data. This includes quantitative analysis of site-level enrollment demographics, referral pathway mapping, and eligibility criteria adjudication logs. Qualitative components will involve structured interviews with site investigators, coordinators, and a purposive sample of eligible non-enrollees. Data will be integrated using a systems thinking approach, modeled on causal loop diagrams, to identify reinforcing feedback loops and structural bottlenecks.
proposals.contribution: This study will produce a novel, generalizable diagnostic framework for pre-emptively identifying systemic enrollment barriers in transplant trials. It will translate the 'Prioritizing Equity in Antimicrobial Stewardship Efforts (EASE)' principles into actionable trial design and conduct guidance, directly addressing the call for equity in infectious diseases research.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the standard approach to diversity in trials by diagnosing systemic failure points post-hoc, rather than prospectively testing recruitment strategies. It fuses inverse problem analysis from engineering with implementation science and equity frameworks, a novel methodological fusion for clinical trial critique.

proposals.title: A Sequential De-essentialization Intervention to Reduce Defensive Reactance and Improve Equity Outcomes in Antimicrobial Stewardship: A Randomized Controlled Trial
proposals.motivation: Despite widespread implementation, traditional implicit bias training in healthcare often triggers defensiveness and reactance among clinicians, limiting its efficacy in reducing racial disparities in care. Pre-2026 literature in social psychology and medical education highlights that perceived threats to professional identity and objectivity can undermine such interventions. This is particularly critical in infectious diseases and antimicrobial stewardship, where structural inequities, not biological race, drive disparities in infection risk and outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: An educational intervention that first deconstructs the biological essentialism of race (a 'de-essentializing primer') will reduce clinician defensiveness and increase receptivity, leading to greater retention of subsequent bias-mitigation strategies and more equitable antimicrobial prescribing recommendations compared to standard bias training alone.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-site, randomized controlled trial among infectious diseases faculty, fellows, and stewardship members. Participants will be randomized to: 1) Intervention arm: a web-based module first presenting genomic and epidemiological data debunking race as a biological construct, followed by a social-determinants-focused bias mitigation training; or 2) Active control arm: a standard unconscious bias training module of equal duration. Outcomes will be measured pre-, post-, and at 3-month follow-up using validated scales for defensive reactance, bias awareness, and a validated clinical vignette assessment tool measuring equity in proposed management for conditions like community-acquired pneumonia and UTI. Analysis will use mixed-effects models to compare changes between arms.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide empirical evidence for a novel, theory-driven structural intervention to improve the effectiveness of equity training in medicine. It will contribute a practical, scalable educational framework specifically tailored for antimicrobial stewardship programs, directly extending the Prioritizing Equity in Antimicrobial Stewardship Efforts (EASE) framework. Findings will offer a model for sequencing educational content to overcome a key barrier in diversity, equity, and inclusion initiatives.
proposals.novelty: While debunking biological race is established in anthropology, its strategic use as a primer to disarm defensiveness before bias training is a novel fusion of social psychology and medical education. This approach moves beyond merely adding structural competency to training, to actively using it to engineer greater receptivity.

proposals.title: Network-Level Stewardship Against Microaggressions: A Data-Driven Framework for Equity in Pandemic Care
proposals.motivation: The COVID-19 pandemic has starkly revealed and exacerbated health inequities. Pre-2026 evidence indicates clinician bias and microaggressions in clinical encounters contribute to disparities in trust, treatment adherence, and outcomes. Existing antimicrobial stewardship models demonstrate that coordinated, data-driven interventions at the health-system level can effectively change clinician behavior, but this approach has not been systematically applied to steward equity-promoting communication and mitigate discriminatory interactions.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a network-wide, data-driven stewardship program that identifies clinician microaggressions through natural language processing and links them to patient outcomes will reduce measurable disparities in COVID-19 care processes and improve equity in health outcomes across the network.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework integrating: 1) NLP analysis of clinical encounter notes and patient experience surveys using pre-trained transformer models (e.g., BERT) fine-tuned to identify linguistic markers of microaggressions and bias, 2) linkage of these interaction metrics to network-wide COVID-19 outcome data (e.g., time to treatment, ICU admission rates) using multi-level regression models, and 3) a stewardship intervention deploying network-level dashboards, targeted feedback to clinical departments and faculty, and tiered educational modules for clinicians, modeled on successful antimicrobial stewardship programs.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, scalable framework for operationalizing and measuring equity at the point of care within large healthcare systems. It will contribute empirical evidence on the systemic impact of microaggressions on pandemic outcomes and demonstrate the translation of stewardship principles from infectious diseases management to the domain of health equity.
proposals.novelty: This proposal uniquely fuses the technical methodology of NLP bias detection with the established, coordinated implementation science model of antimicrobial stewardship, applying it to the novel problem of mitigating real-time clinician communication harms during a pandemic at a network level.

proposals.title: From Community Resilience to Institutional Stewardship: A Mixed-Methods Framework for Holistic GME Selection
proposals.motivation: The COVID-19 pandemic starkly revealed systemic inequities in healthcare, yet certain racialized communities demonstrated exceptional resilience and achieved equitable outcomes against the odds. Concurrently, Graduate Medical Education (GME) selection remains overly reliant on cognitive metrics, failing to systematically identify applicants with the non-cognitive traits—like adaptive capacity and stewardship—necessary for building resilient, equitable health systems. This research addresses the dual problem of learning from community-level success and translating those lessons into a predictive framework for future physician-leaders.
proposals.hypothesis: That the collective behaviors, leadership styles, and adaptive capacities which predicted community resilience and equity during COVID-19 can be identified, measured, and validly operationalized as non-cognitive predictors for holistic GME selection.
proposals.method: We will employ a sequential mixed-methods design. First, a qualitative comparative case study will analyze 'positive outlier' communities using interviews and archival data to code for resilience competencies. Second, these competencies will inform the adaptation of validated non-cognitive instruments (e.g., situational judgment tests, personality inventories). Third, we will develop and validate a predictive model using machine learning (e.g., random forest or gradient boosting) on a dataset of medical student applicants, incorporating these novel predictors alongside traditional metrics to forecast residency performance and career commitment to equity.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, evidence-based framework for GME selection that prioritizes institutional resilience and health equity. It will contribute a validated set of non-cognitive predictors grounded in real-world community success and provide infectious diseases and medical education leaders with a practical tool for selecting future stewards of the healthcare system.
proposals.novelty: It innovatively treats resilient communities as a predictive training set for institutional selection, fusing social science methods from equity-focused health services research with predictive analytics from admissions science, a synthesis not yet applied in GME.

proposals.title: Auditing Surveillance Bias and Inferring System-Level Vaccine Efficacy: A Cohort Study of Transmission Patterns Across Medical Residency Programs
proposals.motivation: Post-COVID-19, estimating real-world vaccine effectiveness (VE) in healthcare systems remains critical for pandemic preparedness. Traditional cohort studies are confounded by differential exposure risk and, crucially, by systematic under-reporting of infections influenced by workplace culture and stigma. This study addresses the dual challenge of measuring VE while quantifying the surveillance bias that corrupts institutional data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that differential SARS-CoV-2 transmission networks among medical specialty cohorts, when analyzed with a model correcting for specialty-specific reporting probabilities, will yield a more accurate, system-level estimate of vaccine efficacy against infection than standard methods.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective analysis using linked administrative data (vaccination status, residency program, scheduling) and infection control reports from a large academic medical center (2020-2023). We will employ a Bayesian network transmission model to infer transmission probabilities within and between specialty groups (e.g., Emergency Medicine, Psychiatry). Simultaneously, we will use latent class analysis, informed by surveys on stigma and policy, to estimate specialty-specific probabilities of infection reporting. These reporting probabilities will be integrated as weights in a modified Poisson regression model to generate a bias-corrected VE estimate.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for health systems to audit their own infection surveillance data for bias. It will deliver a more robust, operationally relevant estimate of vaccine performance under real-world conditions of heterogeneous exposure and imperfect reporting, directly informing local stewardship and policy decisions.
proposals.novelty: The approach innovatively treats medical specialties as natural cohorts with distinct risk cultures and leverages differential transmission between them to inversely model VE, while directly quantifying and correcting for the social and structural determinants of data quality—a fusion of infectious disease modeling and equity-focused implementation science.

proposals.title: Mapping the Physician Pipeline: A Longitudinal, Mixed-Methods Panel Study of Career Trajectories, Attrition, and Systemic Barriers in Infectious Diseases and General Medicine
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, physician burnout and attrition are recognized crises, threatening healthcare system stability and equity. However, existing studies are largely cross-sectional or retrospective, failing to capture the dynamic, cumulative impact of systemic barriers—such as inequitable resource allocation, lack of mentorship, and hostile work environments—across the career lifespan. This gap limits the ability to design effective, targeted stewardship interventions to retain a diverse clinical workforce.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that career derailment or attrition is not a random event but is systematically predicted by identifiable, cumulative exposures to specific structural and interpersonal barriers, which differ in nature and timing for physicians from underrepresented groups and those in high-burnout specialties like infectious diseases.
proposals.method: We will implement a prospective, multi-decade panel study. We will recruit 5 graduation-year cohorts (e.g., 2025, 2026, 2027) of physicians from U.S. residency programs in infectious diseases and internal medicine. Data integration will link de-identified administrative records (licensing, board certification, employment) with annual validated surveys (measuring burnout, discrimination, resource access) and biennial in-depth interviews. Analysis will employ discrete-time survival models to identify time-varying predictors of attrition/role change, and qualitative comparative analysis (QCA) to map causal pathways. Subgroup comparisons will be made by gender, race/ethnicity, and specialty.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first longitudinal, causal map of systemic barriers driving physician attrition, moving beyond correlation to identify critical intervention points. It will directly inform the 'Prioritizing Equity in Antimicrobial Stewardship Efforts (EASE)' framework by quantifying how workforce equity is a prerequisite for effective clinical stewardship. The proactive tracking method will establish a new standard for studying professional career trajectories.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior snapshot studies, this design treats the career as a longitudinal process, proactively tracking individuals regardless of career path change to avoid survivor bias. It uniquely integrates linked objective administrative data with rich subjective experience data to test systemic rather than individual causes of attrition.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Role of Senior Clinical Staff in Healthcare-Associated Pathogen Transmission: A Genomic-Epidemiologic Network Analysis
proposals.motivation: Healthcare-associated infections (HAIs) remain a major public health burden. While antimicrobial stewardship programs emphasize prescriber roles, the specific contribution of attending physicians and senior staff to direct pathogen transmission is poorly quantified. Understanding role-specific transmission dynamics is critical for moving beyond generic infection control protocols to develop targeted, effective interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that attending physicians and senior clinical staff, when controlling for patient contact frequency, are significant links in HAI transmission networks due to factors like time pressure and cross-ward mobility, and that their contribution is distinct from and potentially greater than that of resident physicians.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using whole-genome sequencing (WGS) of bacterial pathogens (e.g., MRSA, C. difficile) from patients and healthcare workers in a defined hospital unit. Genomic data will be integrated with electronic health record-derived contact networks. Transmission networks will be inferred using established phylogenetic and statistical models (e.g., phylodynamic analysis in BEAST, transmission tree reconstruction with TransPhylo). The contribution of different staff roles (attending, resident, APP, nurse) will be quantified using multivariate regression, controlling for confounders like contact duration and number of patients.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first data-driven, genomic evidence quantifying the transmission role of senior clinicians, directly testing assumptions underlying infection prevention strategies. It will produce a novel framework for integrating genomic epidemiology with staff behavioral data to identify high-impact targets for intervention, advancing the field of precision infection prevention.
proposals.novelty: This work moves beyond characterizing patient-to-patient transmission or general stewardship to explicitly model staff role as a variable in genomic transmission networks, fusing high-resolution pathogen genomics with detailed healthcare worker behavioral epidemiology.

proposals.title: Beyond D+R-: An International Comparative Analysis of Graduate Medical Education System Structures and Cytomegalovirus Serostatus Risk Profiles
proposals.motivation: Current understanding of CMV risk in resident physicians is heavily US-centric, focusing on D+R- serostatus in a single training environment. This limits generalizability and may overlook significant risks for other serostatus groups (e.g., D+R+, D-R+) in different global contexts where seroprevalence and training structures vary widely. A systematic international comparison is needed to inform equitable, evidence-based trainee health protocols and GME policy that account for diverse epidemiological and institutional landscapes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific structural features of national Graduate Medical Education (GME) systems—such as duty hour regulations, patient population seroprevalence, and rotation structures—are significantly associated with differential risks of severe indirect CMV effects across all CMV serostatus groups of resident physicians, not solely the D+R- group.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-country, cross-sectional study using a mixed-methods approach. First, we will perform a systematic review and policy analysis to characterize key GME system variables (duty hours, patient exposure, support systems) in selected high-, medium-, and low-CMV prevalence countries. Second, we will partner with institutions in these countries to collect anonymized, aggregated data on resident CMV serostatus and health outcomes (e.g., seroconversion rates, CMV-related illness). Finally, we will employ multivariable regression modeling and comparative case study analysis to identify associations between GME system structures and risk profiles across serostatus groups.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first internationally comparative framework for assessing CMV risk in medical trainees, moving beyond a single serostatus and a single healthcare system. It will produce actionable, context-specific evidence to guide GME policymakers and infectious diseases clinicians in developing tailored antimicrobial stewardship and occupational health protocols for resident physicians globally.
proposals.novelty: This study shifts the paradigm from a US-focused, D+R- risk model to a holistic, system-level analysis that considers how institutional training environments interact with local epidemiology to create unique risk landscapes for all resident serostatus groups, a perspective absent from pre-2026 literature.

ideas.title: Longitudinal evaluation of test bias mitigation and accommodation outcomes in infectious disease training
ideas.description: This study would track a cohort of residents in infectious disease programs from internship through graduation to longitudinally assess the relationship between implemented test/assessment bias mitigation strategies and the rates and outcomes of disability accommodation requests. It would empirically evaluate if specific mitigation approaches (e.g., shifting from high-stakes exams to workplace-based assessments) are associated with changes in the timing of disclosure, the approval and utilization of accommodations, and resident well-being and performance metrics. The design allows for causal inference about how structural changes in evaluation systems impact equity and inclusion for trainees with disabilities, moving beyond evaluating strategies in isolation to understanding their real-world effect on career-long participation.

ideas.title: Clinician factors outweigh patient factors in disparities
ideas.description: Test if clinician bias is a minor contributor compared to systemic access barriers in creating disparities.
ideas.sourceTitle: Health Equity and Antibiotic Prescribing in the United States: A Systematic Scoping Review.

ideas.title: Inferring Healthcare System-Level Vaccine Efficacy from Differential Transmission Patterns Across Medical Specialties
ideas.description: This research proposes to treat different medical residency programs (e.g., Emergency Medicine vs. Psychiatry) as distinct observational cohorts with inherently different exposure risks and workplace safety cultures. By analyzing the differential patterns of infection transmission within and between these specialty groups among vaccinated and unvaccinated healthcare workers, we can inversely model the system-level vaccine efficacy against infection. Simultaneously, the study will quantify how specialty-specific barriers (e.g., stigma, staffing policies) affect the accuracy of self-reported infection data, which is a critical confounder in transmission models. This approach leverages existing administrative and infection control data to generate population-level efficacy estimates while directly auditing the surveillance bias inherent in the healthcare system.

ideas.title: Test bias mitigation strategies in infectious diseases
ideas.description: Empirically evaluate the described strategies' effectiveness specifically within infectious disease clinical and training settings.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Impact of Unconscious Bias in Healthcare: How to Recognize and Mitigate It.

ideas.title: Network-Level Stewardship Against Microaggressions: A Data-Driven Framework for Equity in Pandemic Care
ideas.description: This research proposes to implement a large-scale, data-driven stewardship program across regional or national healthcare networks specifically designed to identify and mitigate the impact of clinician microaggressions on COVID-19 patient outcomes. By integrating natural language processing of clinical encounter notes and patient feedback with network-wide outcome data, the program will establish system-level metrics for equity of interaction. The synergy enables the network to not only track disparities in care processes but to actively steward clinician behavior and institutional norms, using coordinated policy, feedback, and education to reduce discriminatory interactions and improve health equity at scale.

ideas.title: Genomic epidemiology of healthcare-associated infections: Quantifying the role of attending physicians and senior staff in transmission networks
ideas.description: This research direction applies high-resolution pathogen genome sequencing to investigate healthcare-associated infection (HAI) transmission networks specifically to evaluate the role of attending physicians, advanced practice providers, and other senior clinical staff. By constructing detailed transmission chains from genomic and epidemiological data, it can quantify whether these established professionals are more or less likely than residents to be links in transmission, controlling for patient contact frequency and role. This provides an objective, data-driven method to test if previously identified barriers (e.g., time pressure, hierarchical culture) manifest in measurable transmission outcomes post-residency. The findings could directly inform targeted, role-specific interventions in infection prevention and control programs.

ideas.title: Embedding a Bias-Audit Feedback Loop into the EDI Implementation Cycle to Dynamically Improve Pediatric ID Career Equity
ideas.description: This research embeds a rigorous, quantitative measurement of bias impact on hiring and promotion outcomes directly into the operational steps of a six-step EDI implementation cycle within pediatric infectious diseases divisions. The synergy creates a feedback mechanism: as the EDI cycle is implemented (e.g., policy changes, training), its specific effects on bias metrics and career advancement outcomes for underrepresented professionals are measured in real-time. This data then feeds back into the cycle's assessment and adjustment phases, allowing institutions to empirically identify which interventions are effective and which require iteration. Thus, the project moves beyond merely implementing a framework or measuring bias in isolation, to creating an evidence-based, adaptive system for accelerating equity in career trajectories.

ideas.title: International Comparison of CMV Serostatus Risk Profiles in Graduate Medical Education Systems
ideas.description: This research direction systematically compares how the structure and conditions of graduate medical education (GME) systems in different countries influence the risk landscape for severe indirect CMV effects among all serostatus groups of resident physicians. By moving beyond the US-centric D+R- focus, it will analyze international data to determine if specific training environments (e.g., high-prevalence regions with different serostatus distributions, or systems with varying duty hour protections) unmask significant risks in groups like D+R+ or D-R+. The goal is to generate evidence-based, globally-informed recommendations for GME policy and trainee health protocols that are tailored to diverse epidemiological and institutional contexts.

ideas.title: Develop specialty-specific implicit association tests
ideas.description: Create and validate IATs focused on infectious disease patient stereotypes and clinician interactions.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Impact of Unconscious Bias in Healthcare: How to Recognize and Mitigate It.

ideas.title: Integrate stewardship with electronic health records
ideas.description: Build and evaluate EHR-integrated clinical decision support tools for outpatient antibiotic prescribing.
ideas.sourceTitle: Antimicrobial stewardship in the outpatient setting: A review and proposed framework

ideas.title: Decomposing Disparity: Attributing Infectious Disease Outcome Gaps to Clinician, System, and Patient Factors Using Standardized Metrics
ideas.description: This research develops a unified framework that applies standardized, pathogen-agnostic disparity metrics (like rate ratios or between-group variance) to infectious disease outcomes. It then employs multi-level modeling and causal decomposition techniques to statistically apportion the measured disparity into components attributable to: 1) clinician factors (e.g., variation in testing/treatment by patient race within the same facility), 2) systemic factors (e.g., geographic access to care, insurance), and 3) patient-level factors (e.g., comorbidities, exposure risk). This moves beyond simply documenting disparities to diagnosing their primary drivers, allowing public health officials to distinguish whether a measured racial gap in, for example, sepsis mortality is best addressed by anti-bias training, by building new clinics, or by community outreach programs.

ideas.title: Simulating the population health impact of integrating polygenic risk scores into holistic admissions and clinical care pathways
ideas.description: This research fuses a biomedical investigation of genetic contributors to health disparities with a national policy simulation to create a novel systems-dynamics model. It would simulate the multi-decade impact on the physician workforce and population health outcomes if graduate and medical education programs adopted holistic review that explicitly considered (or blinded itself to) polygenic risk scores for conditions like severe COVID-19. The model would project how these admissions decisions alter the demographic and genetic-risk composition of the future healthcare workforce, and subsequently simulate how that workforce's clinical decision-making affects national disease burden and health equity, thereby testing the long-term interplay between biological risk, structural policy, and systemic outcomes.

ideas.title: SYSTEMS: Institutional protocols to buffer clinician impact and ensure equity from patient bias
ideas.description: This research investigates how healthcare system design can mitigate the negative consequences of patient bias towards clinicians from underrepresented groups. It moves beyond measuring bias to actively testing institutional interventions, such as implementing standardized clinical checklists and integrated care-team introductions, which are designed to reduce bias-driven variability in treatment and clinician stress. The study uses a mixed-methods approach, combining quantitative outcome and burnout measures with qualitative interviews to understand the intervention's mechanisms. The goal is to provide evidence-based, systemic solutions that protect clinician well-being and ensure equitable patient care, regardless of individual patient predispositions.

ideas.title: Personalized Cost-Effectiveness Toolkit for Clinician-Led Diversity and Stewardship
ideas.description: This research develops and validates a scalable, personal toolkit that enables solo practitioners and small clinics to analyze the cost-effectiveness of specific diversity-promoting and resource stewardship actions within their own patient panels and practice workflows. It moves beyond institutional policy evaluation to provide clinicians with data-driven methods to assess the financial and operational impact of interventions like inclusive scheduling, culturally tailored patient education materials, or generic prescribing protocols. The goal is to generate a decision-support system that helps individual clinicians optimize both health equity outcomes and practice sustainability without requiring large administrative resources.

ideas.title: Structured Stewardship Interviews for Bias Reduction in Non-Primary Care Trainee Selection
ideas.description: This research adapts structured interview protocols from organizational psychology and integrates them into an expanded clinical stewardship framework for non-primary care outpatient settings (e.g., specialty clinics, urgent care). The core innovation is using the stewardship framework's defined clinical scenarios and decision-making principles as the structured content for the interviews, moving beyond generic questions. This creates a validated, setting-specific tool to assess a candidate's clinical reasoning, resource stewardship, and communication skills objectively, aiming to reduce selection bias and improve the predictive validity of admissions for these complex training environments.

ideas.title: Stigma-Reduction Training for Managing Drug-Resistant CMV in the Workplace
ideas.description: This research develops and evaluates integrated training programs for workplace managers and teams that simultaneously educate on the clinical management of drug-resistant cytomegalovirus (CMV) as a common scenario and apply evidence-based interventions to reduce stigma and bias against disability. The program's core synergy is that by directly confronting the stigma associated with chronic viral conditions and disability disclosure, it removes a critical barrier to early reporting of symptoms. This enables the timely implementation of non-pharmaceutical accommodations and management strategies essential for drug-resistant cases, such as flexible scheduling for medical appointments or modified duties during periods of immune suppression. Consequently, the approach protects both employee health and organizational productivity by fostering a culture of psychological safety and pragmatic medical preparedness.

ideas.title: Investigating Disability as a Structural Determinant of Healthcare-Associated Infection (HAI) Disparities
ideas.description: This research investigates the hypothesis that inadequate program director training and institutional implementation of ADA compliance creates structural barriers in clinical environments, which in turn function as a root cause of HAI inequities for patients with disabilities. It moves beyond assessing training or quantifying disparities to directly model how specific gaps in disability-competent care—such as inaccessible hand hygiene stations, communication barriers during sterile procedures, or inadequate patient transfer equipment—mediate increased infection risk. The study would employ a mixed-methods approach, correlating program directors' knowledge and unit-level ADA compliance audits with HAI rates among patients with disabilities, thereby identifying modifiable structural interventions to reduce disparities.

ideas.title: Blinding for Equity: A Double-Blind Framework for Evaluating Research on Health Inequities
ideas.description: This research proposes to design and pilot a double-blind review protocol for promotion, tenure, and grant committees specifically applied to scholarship that examines structural drivers of health inequities across crises like COVID-19, HIV, and maternal mortality. The core hypothesis is that implicit biases and disciplinary prejudices may lead to the systematic undervaluation of such interdisciplinary, socially engaged research. By blinding evaluators to the author's identity, institutional prestige, and specific disease focus, the protocol aims to isolate and assess the methodological rigor and conceptual contribution of the research on its own merits. This fusion advances equity in science by protecting both the researchers who do this work and the intellectual legitimacy of the field itself.

ideas.title: Mechanistic and Contextual Analysis of How Demographic Differences Mediate Clinical Interaction Quality
ideas.description: This research moves beyond correlating demographic categories with outcomes to identify the specific interpersonal and systemic mechanisms that determine whether demographic differences hinder or enhance care. It employs a longitudinal, mixed-methods design observing real clinical interactions, measuring mediators like cultural humility and communication adaptation, and linking them to patient-reported and objective health outcomes. The study is explicitly contextual, analyzing how clinic-level resources and norms shape these dynamics, and uses an intersectional framework to avoid reducing identity to single dimensions. The goal is to generate evidence-based, trainable skills and structural supports that help clinicians leverage differences constructively, regardless of demographic composition.

ideas.title: Predicting and Cultivating Institutional Resilience: A Dual-Problem Framework for Holistic GME Selection
ideas.description: This research adapts validated non-cognitive predictors from college admissions to analyze and learn from racialized communities that demonstrated exceptional resilience and equitable outcomes during the COVID-19 pandemic. By treating these communities as 'positive outliers,' the study will identify the specific collective behaviors, leadership styles, and adaptive capacities that predicted success against the odds. These identified traits and community-level practices will then be operationalized into a holistic Graduate Medical Education (GME) selection framework, moving beyond identifying individual merit to selecting for applicants who embody and can propagate the competencies proven to foster institutional resilience and health equity in high-stakes, real-world environments.

ideas.title: Structured Residency Interviews as a Tool to Measure and Mitigate Patient Bias
ideas.description: This research investigates the implementation of structured, competency-based residency interviews with a dual purpose. First, it applies the rubric to reduce interviewer bias and promote equity in resident selection. Second, and innovatively, it designs the interview's scenarios and scoring criteria to evaluate a candidate's preparedness and skill in managing patient bias toward clinicians. The study would then track whether residents selected via this bias-mitigation competency rubric subsequently report higher resilience, better patient outcomes, and lower attrition in challenging clinical environments, creating a proactive pipeline for a more sustainable and effective workforce.

ideas.title: Disentangling Structural and Cognitive Bias Mechanisms in Healthcare Career Advancement
ideas.description: This research employs a mixed-methods, mechanism-focused approach to isolate the specific impact of unconscious bias from broader structural inequalities on career outcomes. It integrates an experimental audit component to directly measure bias in evaluation, with longitudinal ethnographic and archival methods to map the distribution of critical career resources like mentorship and high-visibility assignments. By comparing the audit results with real-world advancement data and resource maps, the study can attribute disparities to specific, actionable bias mechanisms versus systemic resource gaps, providing clear targets for both cognitive debiasing interventions and structural reforms.

ideas.title: Disentangling Bias from Context: A Within-Clinician Quasi-Experimental Analysis of Prescribing Disparities
ideas.description: This study uses a causal inference approach to isolate the intersectional effects of patient and clinician factors on prescribing disparities. By analyzing prescribing decisions made by the same clinician for patients with similar clinical indications but different demographics, it controls for clinician-specific practice style and environmental confounding. The moderating role of clinician characteristics, including measured implicit associations, on the magnitude of these within-clinician disparities is then rigorously tested. This design directly separates interpersonal bias from systemic confounding, providing stronger evidence on the mechanisms driving inequitable care.

ideas.title: Wastewater AMR Surveillance as a Novel Metric for Medical School Admissions Equity
ideas.description: This study proposes using expanded wastewater surveillance for antimicrobial resistance (AMR) genes as an objective, ecological outcome measure to evaluate the long-term public health consequences of changing medical school admissions criteria. By correlating temporal shifts in the geographic prevalence of key AMR genes with the evolving academic and demographic profiles of matriculating medical students in corresponding regions, the research tests whether de-emphasizing standardized tests inadvertently alters the pipeline of physicians trained in areas with high endemic resistance. This provides a novel, population-health-based framework for assessing equity in admissions reforms, moving beyond traditional metrics like match rates to measure impact on the community-level health threats these future doctors will manage.

ideas.title: Network-Informed Equity Performance Metrics for Healthcare-Associated Infection Disparities
ideas.description: This research synthesizes network theory and equity performance metrics to develop a novel framework for a national HAI equity metric. It uses contact network analysis from social science and epidemiology to model how patient demographics, social determinants of health, and inter-facility patient transfers create structurally different risks for infection transmission across communities. The resulting metric would not just penalize or reward hospitals based on disparity reduction, but would be risk-adjusted for the varying network connectivity and vulnerability of the populations they serve. This enables a more equitable and actionable accountability system that incentivizes hospitals to address the specific network-based transmission dynamics perpetuating inequities in their context.

ideas.title: Historical Competency Rubrics for Equitable Medical Workforce Development
ideas.description: This research employs historical archival analysis to identify how specific past discriminatory policies (e.g., redlining, hospital segregation) created systemic barriers that shaped the educational and community health pathways of applicants from marginalized groups. These historically-informed insights are then used to develop and weight the competencies within structured residency interview rubrics, ensuring they value lived experiences and skills developed in response to or despite structural inequities. The resulting protocol does not just standardize interviews but actively recalibrates the definition of 'merit' and 'competency' in medical training selection to counter the legacy of exclusionary policies, aiming to build a physician workforce whose selection process acknowledges and repairs historical harm.

ideas.title: Inverse Problem Analysis of Clinical Trial Implementation: Identifying Systemic Barriers to Equitable Enrollment in Transplant Prophylaxis Studies
ideas.description: This research applies the 'inverse problem' approach to the completed randomized trial comparing universal prophylaxis vs. pre-emptive therapy in D+R- liver transplant recipients. Instead of asking how to improve diversity, it systematically investigates why diversity in enrollment was suboptimal despite the trial's clinical importance and the existence of known effective recruitment interventions. The study will analyze trial implementation data—including site selection, referral pathways, eligibility criteria application, and patient decision-making—to identify the specific, persistent barriers that limited equitable access and representation. The goal is to generate a generalizable framework for pre-emptively diagnosing and mitigating systemic barriers in the design and execution of future transplant clinical trials to ensure they benefit all patient populations.

ideas.title: The Evolution of Patient-Centered Screening Barriers: From Residency to Independent Practice
ideas.description: This research investigates how the barriers and facilitators to achieving patient-centered screening outcomes (e.g., diagnosis rates, linkage to care) evolve as physicians transition from residency into independent attending roles and other healthcare positions. It moves beyond simply measuring test orders to track whether the capacity to ensure follow-up and successful patient linkage changes with greater autonomy, different practice settings, and altered time pressures. The study design would compare outcomes and perceived barriers across career stages, revealing if system-level constraints replace training-level ones, thereby identifying the most impactful career phase for interventions aimed at genuine patient benefit.

ideas.title: Implement and test the six-step EDI cycle
ideas.description: Operationalize the proposed six-step equity, diversity, and inclusion cycle within pediatric institutions and measure its impact.
ideas.sourceTitle: Women in Pediatrics: Progress, Barriers, and Opportunities for Equity, Diversity, and Inclusion

ideas.title: Dynamic, Equity-Focused CDSS Targeting Patient-Level Risk and Clinic-Level Readiness
ideas.description: This redesigned approach deploys the CDSS across all participating clinics but activates its most intensive prompts and support functions only for patients identified as high-risk for not receiving recommended screening, based on a real-time model incorporating social determinants and clinical history. Concurrently, clinics receive tiered implementation support (e.g., workflow integration, staff training) based on an assessment of their organizational readiness and historical performance gaps. This ensures the intervention addresses inequities at both the patient and system level, avoids stigmatizing entire clinics, and allocates support where it is most needed to overcome specific barriers.

ideas.title: Longitudinal Tracking of Career Trajectories and Attrition to Map Systemic Barriers Across the Physician Lifespan
ideas.description: This study implements a multi-decade, mixed-methods panel design following multiple graduation-year cohorts of physicians and other healthcare professionals. It proactively recruits and retains participants regardless of career path changes, using linked administrative data, periodic surveys, and in-depth interviews. The analysis specifically compares the experiences and systemic factors faced by those who persist in their initial clinical roles against those who alter their career course, leave clinical practice, or leave the profession entirely. This design directly quantifies attrition and role transition as key outcomes, identifying the specific barriers most predictive of career derailment and those that persist as burdens even for those who remain in practice.

ideas.title: Quantifying Systemic Bias: Using Blind Audition Protocols to Isolate Structural Drivers of HAI Outcome Disparities
ideas.description: This research applies the anonymized portfolio technique from blind auditions to Graduate Medical Education (GME) screening in a novel way: as a controlled experiment to test Idea A's core hypothesis. By comparing outcomes from anonymized and standard application reviews for residency programs, the study can quantify how much of the observed disparity in interview offers is eliminated by removing identifiable demographic cues. The residual disparity that persists even under blind conditions provides direct evidence of the impact of systemic factors, such as inequitable access to research, prestigious mentors, or high-quality clinical rotations, which are embedded in the CV itself. This moves beyond simply reducing bias to actively measuring the structural drivers of inequity, informing targeted interventions at the pipeline level rather than just the selection level.

ideas.title: Rapid diagnostics for resistant infections integrating host genetic susceptibility markers
ideas.description: This research develops rapid, point-of-care diagnostic tests that simultaneously detect multidrug-resistant bacterial pathogens and key host genetic markers influencing immune response and disease severity. By integrating microbial and human genetic data at the point of diagnosis, the approach moves beyond attributing outcome disparities solely to structural factors to include measurable biological susceptibility within and across racial groups. This enables clinicians to identify patients at highest risk for poor outcomes from a resistant infection due to a combination of pathogen virulence and host genetics, allowing for earlier, more aggressive, and personalized intervention. The synergy lies in creating a unified diagnostic tool that addresses both the external threat (the resistant bug) and the internal vulnerability (the patient's genetic risk), a capability neither original idea could achieve independently.

ideas.title: De-biasing by De-essentializing: A Structural Intervention to Reduce Clinician Defensiveness and Improve Equity
ideas.description: This research proposes to test a novel educational intervention that directly challenges the biological essentialism of race as a foundational step before discussing bias in clinical decision-making. By first rigorously demonstrating that racial health disparities are primarily driven by structural racism and social determinants—not innate biological difference—the intervention aims to disarm the defensive reactions that undermine traditional unconscious bias training. The core hypothesis is that when clinicians understand race as a social, not biological, risk marker, they become more receptive to subsequent training on mitigating cognitive biases in diagnosis and treatment, as the threat to their identity as objective scientists is reduced. The fused idea evaluates whether this 'de-essentializing' primer leads to greater long-term retention of bias-mitigation strategies and more equitable patient outcomes compared to standard bias training alone.

ideas.title: Evaluating EHR-integrated antibiotic stewardship as a trust-building intervention for public health systems
ideas.description: This study investigates whether integrating patient-accessible antibiotic stewardship decision support into electronic health records (EHRs) can function as a trust-building intervention for public health institutions. It posits that by making the rationale for appropriate antibiotic prescribing transparent and educational within the patient portal, healthcare systems can demonstrate competence, benevolence, and integrity. The research would measure changes in patient trust toward the healthcare system and correlate this with subsequent behaviors, such as vaccine acceptance. This creates a synergistic model where a stewardship tool not only improves prescribing but also actively cultivates the trust necessary for broader public health initiatives.

ideas.title: Systemic Bias Interdiction: Mapping and Disrupting the Bias Supply Chain in Critical Infectious Disease Care
ideas.description: This research moves beyond mitigating bias in discrete decisions to proactively intercepting it throughout the clinical system. It develops and validates a framework that continuously audits the 'bias supply chain'—from population data collection and EHR documentation patterns to team communication and institutional guidelines—that feeds into high-stakes decisions like sepsis recognition. The system employs causal modeling to identify root institutional and workflow sources of bias, then pilots targeted interventions (e.g., structured data entry prompts, guideline revisions) that generate less biased data and processes, thereby creating a self-correcting ecosystem for critical care decisions.

ideas.title: Heterogeneity in URM Applicant Profiles and the Unintended Consequences of Holistic Review in Medical School Admissions
ideas.description: This research challenges the dual monolithic assumptions that all URM applicants benefit equally from the de-emphasis of USMLE Step 1 and that BIPOC communities share homogeneous academic profiles.
