It posits that removing this standardized filter may inadvertently disadvantage specific subgroups of URM applicants—such as those from high-achieving, test-strong backgrounds or certain immigrant communities—who used scores to offset other perceived deficiencies in their applications. The study would employ intersectional quantitative and qualitative methods to map the heterogeneity of academic metrics and lived experiences within URM applicant pools. The goal is to develop a more nuanced admissions framework that supports genuine equity without homogenizing the diverse strengths and challenges across underrepresented groups.

ideas.title: Investigating Host Genetic Factors in CMV Drug Resistance and Treatment Disparities
ideas.description: This research direction applies the principle of rapid resistance genotyping from HIV to CMV, but expands it to investigate host genetic factors—such as polymorphisms in drug metabolism or immune response genes—that may predispose patients to treatment failure or accelerated viral resistance. It specifically examines whether these biological factors are unevenly distributed across populations and interact with social determinants of health (e.g., access to care, medication adherence support) to create observed disparities in CMV disease severity and treatment efficacy. The synergy creates a unified model for precision virology that explains disparities not solely through social or viral factors, but through their interaction with host biology, guiding more equitable, personalized antiviral therapy.

ideas.title: Empirical Evaluation of EHR Nudges to Mitigate Diagnostic Bias in Infectious Diseases
ideas.description: This research program empirically tests the effectiveness of specific, pre-designed debiasing nudges embedded within electronic health records (EHRs) to counteract diagnostic biases in infectious disease medicine. It focuses on high-risk scenarios like sepsis recognition, atypical presentations in different patient demographics, and antibiotic stewardship, where cognitive biases can lead to significant harm. By implementing nudges such as differential diagnosis checklists triggered by specific symptoms or altered default options for test ordering in randomized controlled trials, the study measures their direct impact on diagnostic accuracy, time to correct treatment, and clinical outcomes. This approach uniquely combines the precision of behavioral economic interventions with rigorous, disease-specific outcome evaluation to generate actionable evidence for health system implementation.

ideas.title: Network-Enabled Expectation Management: Scaling Patient-Centered Communication for Antibiotic Stewardship
ideas.description: This research develops and implements a data-driven, scalable communication framework for antibiotic stewardship across regional or national healthcare networks. It moves beyond isolated protocols by creating a centralized platform that collects, analyzes, and feeds back anonymized data on communication techniques, patient satisfaction scores, and clinical outcomes from thousands of encounters where antibiotics were appropriately withheld. The system uses this network-wide data to identify and propagate the most effective communication strategies for different clinical scenarios and patient demographics, enabling continuous improvement and local adaptation. This transforms stewardship from a prescriptive policy into a learning system that actively shapes and satisfies patient expectations at scale, creating a new cultural norm for acute visit care.

ideas.title: Modeling the Health System Impact of National GME Policy Change Using Multi-Platform EHR Data
ideas.description: This research would use a large, integrated regional health delivery network—with its diverse patient populations and multiple EHR platforms—as a living laboratory to simulate the system-wide effects of a national policy de-emphasizing Step 1 and adopting holistic review in residency selection. By analyzing resident performance, patient outcomes, and care process data (like HIV screening adherence) across this network, the study can build a predictive model of how such a policy change would ultimately impact public health metrics and healthcare delivery at scale. This moves beyond modeling theoretical educational outcomes to quantifying the policy's tangible effects on patient care and health system efficiency.

ideas.title: Inequities are uniform across all HAI types
ideas.description: Investigate if disparities vary significantly by type of healthcare-associated infection, such as CLABSI versus CAUTI.
ideas.sourceTitle: Racial/Ethnic Inequities in Healthcare-associated Infections Under the Shadow of Structural Racism: Narrative Review and Call to Action

ideas.title: Quantifying Bias in Career Advancement Using Ecological Niche Modeling Techniques
ideas.description: This research adapts the ecological solution of Species Distribution Modeling (SDM) to quantify unconscious bias in hiring and promotion. In ecology, SDMs correlate species presence/absence with environmental variables to map a fundamental niche and identify limiting factors. Analogously, we will treat career advancement events (promotions, hires) as 'presence data' and institutional characteristics (e.g., diversity of leadership, implicit bias training prevalence, sponsorship patterns) as 'environmental variables'. By building a model of the 'fundamental career niche'—the advancement potential in an unbiased environment—and comparing it to observed advancement data for underrepresented professionals in infectious diseases, we can quantify the specific impact and magnitude of institutional biases, identifying which variables most significantly constrain equitable outcomes.

ideas.title: Community-Co-Designed, Lay Health Worker-Led Education as a Sustainable Alternative to Provider-Facing CDSS
ideas.description: This study employs a community-based participatory research (CBPR) framework to co-design and implement an intensive, repeated education program delivered by trained lay health workers (promotores) from the Hispanic/Latinx community. It directly tests whether this community-driven, peer-led model can achieve similar improvements in clinical outcomes (e.g., guideline-concordant prescribing) as a computerized clinical decision support system (CDSS) alert targeting providers. The research moves beyond testing 'more intensive education' in isolation to evaluating a sustainable, culturally grounded intervention model that shifts the locus of change from the clinical encounter to the community, addressing systemic and trust barriers that provider education alone cannot.

ideas.title: Formalizing the Informal: Surveillance and Support Systems for Drug-Resistant CMV Care
ideas.description: This research investigates the management of drug-resistant CMV by examining not only clinical protocols but also the informal, often unrequestable supports—such as caregiver flexibility, patient advocacy nuance, or ad-hoc clinical communication—that are critical for adherence and outcomes. It asks which of these essential supports can be formally institutionalized within surveillance and treatment systems, and which resist formalization, creating inherent gaps. The synergy produces a holistic framework that defines effective CMV care as dependent on both measurable pathogen surveillance and the systematization of key human and logistical accommodations.

ideas.title: Matched Multi-Tier Cohort Analysis to Distinguish Generalizable from Idiosyncratic Drivers of Appropriate Prescribing
ideas.description: This study identifies clinics across a full performance spectrum—low, moderate, and high appropriate prescribing—matched on key contextual variables like setting, resources, and patient case mix. Using a convergent mixed-methods design, it quantitatively analyzes workflows and qualitatively conducts ethnographic observation and interviews within these matched tiers. The direct comparison across performance levels within similar contexts isolates factors that are consistently associated with appropriate use and are potentially transferable. This approach separates generalizable, actionable drivers from unique, non-replicable features of outlier clinics, yielding more robust and implementable findings for quality improvement.

ideas.title: Disentangling Systemic and Interactional Pathways in Healthcare Disparities: A Causal Mediation and Experimental Approach
ideas.description: This study moves beyond weighing broad factors to map the specific causal pathways through which disparities manifest in a clinical outcome. It first establishes the total observed disparity using real-world data, then employs causal mediation modeling to quantify the portion mediated by pre-encounter systemic and access-related variables. Crucially, it then uses a standardized patient (SP) methodology within actual clinical settings, where SPs present identical clinical symptoms but differ in perceived race/ethnicity, while researchers rigorously control for and measure access variables (e.g., clinic type, resource availability). This design isolates the unique effect of the clinician-patient interaction, providing direct evidence of bias mechanisms separate from, and interacting with, structural barriers.

ideas.title: Applying equity-focused implementation science to optimize and equitably deploy CMV immunotherapies in transplant populations
ideas.description: This research program integrates equity-focused implementation science frameworks with the development and deployment of CMV immunotherapies for transplant recipients. It moves beyond evaluating clinical efficacy to explicitly design the rollout strategy using models like the Health Equity Implementation Framework to identify context-specific barriers across diverse patient subgroups and care settings. The synergy enables the proactive tailoring of immunotherapy protocols, support systems, and monitoring pathways to ensure equitable access, adherence, and outcomes, particularly for populations disproportionately burdened by transplant complications. Thus, the research generates both a therapeutic strategy and an implementation blueprint that jointly advance equity in transplant care.

ideas.title: Adapting Aviation's Threat and Error Management to Mitigate Bias in Infectious Disease Teams
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from aviation safety to address unconscious bias in interprofessional infectious disease teams. The analogous field is Aviation Safety, specifically Crew Resource Management (CRM), where structured debriefings and Threat and Error Management checklists are used to surface pilots' and crew's unconscious biases and assumptions, improving team communication and safety. The adapted idea is to implement and study a similar structured protocol for infectious disease teams. This involves creating and testing a pre-briefing/debriefing tool that guides diverse professionals (physicians, pharmacists, nurses, microbiologists) to explicitly flag potential biases as 'threats' prior to case review and to analyze communication breakdowns as 'errors' afterward, aiming to mitigate bias's impact on teamwork and patient management decisions.

ideas.title: Quantify specific antecedent factors' contributions to disparities
ideas.description: Disentangle and measure the relative impact of factors like occupation, housing, and healthcare access on infection and mortality rates.
ideas.sourceTitle: COVID-19 Pandemic: Disparate Health Impact on the Hispanic/Latinx Population in the United States

ideas.title: Comparative Longitudinal Study of Institutional and Community Mediators of Healthcare Trust Dynamics Post-COVID-19
ideas.description: This study employs a multi-cohort longitudinal design to track healthcare system trust across diverse racial, ethnic, and socioeconomic groups before, during, and after the COVID-19 pandemic. It moves beyond documenting inequities to test how specific institutional policies (e.g., vaccine rollout equity, language access improvements) and community-led resources mediate long-term trust outcomes. By comparing trust trajectories between groups and analyzing within-group variation, the research identifies which systemic interventions and grassroots assets most effectively mitigate distrust stemming from pandemic-era inequities, providing an actionable evidence base for trust repair.

ideas.title: Mapping Systemic vs. Contextual Accommodation Barriers Across High-Stakes Clinical Rotations
ideas.description: This study uses a multi-site, comparative case study design within a single academic medical center to investigate accommodation barriers across three distinct high-stakes clinical rotations (e.g., surgery, ICU, emergency medicine). By applying identical mixed-methods—including longitudinal trainee surveys, ethnographic observation, and interviews with key stakeholders (trainees, program directors, attending physicians)—across these cases, the research isolates barriers inherent to the institutional system (e.g., generic policy, pervasive culture) from those specific to a rotation's unique demands. The resulting framework identifies actionable leverage points for institutional reform while still providing nuanced guidance for rotation-specific adaptations.

ideas.title: Equity Audits for Guideline Development: Prescribing Appropriate *and* Equitable Care
ideas.description: This research system moves beyond assuming guideline-concordant care is inherently equitable. It develops and applies a formal 'equity audit' framework to clinical practice guidelines themselves, evaluating the demographic representativeness of their underlying evidence and identifying implicit biases in their recommendations. The system then uses simulation modeling to project the impact of strict guideline adherence on health outcomes across diverse subpopulations, highlighting where blind adherence may worsen disparities. The final output is a set of validated, equity-informed prescribing protocols that modify or supplement existing guidelines to ensure 'appropriate care' is explicitly defined to advance health equity, not just clinical efficiency.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Niche Modeling to Map Implicit Bias Landscapes in Infectious Disease
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from ecological niche modeling to the problem of measuring implicit bias. In ecology, Species Distribution Models (SDMs) predict where a species is likely to be found by analyzing its relationship to environmental variables. Analogously, this project will develop and validate specialty-specific Implicit Association Tests (IATs) for infectious disease, but will extend the methodology by using the IAT response data as 'occurrence points' for bias. These will be statistically correlated with a suite of predefined, specialty-specific contextual 'variables' (e.g., stigma associated with the disease, acuity of presentation, perceived behavioral risk factors). The output will be a predictive model—a 'bias susceptibility landscape'—that maps the conditions under which specific implicit stereotypes are most likely to be activated and influence clinician interactions. This model can then be tested and refined using independent data from simulated or real clinical encounters, providing a more nuanced, context-aware tool for understanding and ultimately mitigating bias in infectious disease care.

ideas.title: Design interventions for the four gatekeeper groups
ideas.description: Develop and evaluate targeted strategic interventions for academic centers, societies, journals, and funding agencies.
ideas.sourceTitle: Women in Pediatrics: Progress, Barriers, and Opportunities for Equity, Diversity, and Inclusion

ideas.title: Embedded, Context-Aware Stewardship Assistant for Point-of-Care Decision Support
ideas.description: This system moves beyond a standalone toolkit by integrating a lightweight software assistant directly into the clinician's existing electronic health record or prescribing platform. It analyzes the practice's own historical data, local resistance patterns (when available), and current patient context to generate concise, evidence-based alerts and suggestions only when most relevant, such as during antibiotic prescription entry. The design prioritizes minimal clicks, seamless workflow integration, and adaptive learning from clinician feedback to avoid alert fatigue, thereby making stewardship an inherent, low-effort part of the clinical decision-making process for solo and small-group practitioners.

ideas.title: Nudging Toward Equity: Using EHR Choice Architecture to Mitigate Structural Drivers of COVID-19 Disparities
ideas.description: This research develops and tests a suite of EHR-embedded nudges designed to counteract the specific structural and cognitive drivers of COVID-19 outcome disparities. The intervention goes beyond generic clinical decision support by using behavioral economics principles to architect choices related to testing prioritization, treatment eligibility assessment, vaccine outreach, and social risk documentation, all tailored to equity metrics. It evaluates whether this automated, low-touch approach can systematically reduce biased clinical decisions and improve equitable resource allocation within existing healthcare workflows, providing a measurable tool for health systems to operationalize structural competency.

ideas.title: Inverse Prevention: Identifying and Scaling Protective Practices Across Multi-Organ Transplant Centers
ideas.description: This research applies an 'inverse problem' methodology to antimicrobial resistance (AMR) prevention in multi-organ transplantation. Instead of only comparing pre-defined prevention strategies in heart, lung, and kidney transplant recipients, it first identifies transplant centers or regions with unexpectedly low post-transplant infection and AMR rates across these organ groups. Using mixed-methods analysis, it uncovers the specific clinical practices, environmental controls, stewardship policies, and social or administrative factors driving this protection. The core innovation is then to prospectively scale and adapt these identified, real-world protective 'bundles' into a comparative effectiveness trial tailored to the distinct immunological and infectious risks of each transplant organ population.

ideas.title: Scale down to specific high-stakes decisions
ideas.description: Focus bias mitigation on critical infectious disease decisions like sepsis recognition or antibiotic stewardship.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Impact of Unconscious Bias in Healthcare: How to Recognize and Mitigate It.

ideas.title: Equity-Focused Community Surveillance of Vaccine Transmission Benefits
ideas.description: This research tests the hypothesis that vaccines always reduce transmission by implementing a community-embedded, equity-focused surveillance network within small, private pediatric practices. Using a tailored EDI (Equity, Diversity, Inclusion) cycle, practices will systematically recruit and longitudinally follow families from historically marginalized groups to measure household secondary attack rates and viral dynamics in vaccinated versus unvaccinated children. This approach uniquely investigates whether sociostructural factors or specific community conditions, rather than just viral variants, can erode the transmission benefit of vaccination, generating actionable, practice-level data to guide hyper-localized public health communication and intervention.

ideas.title: Implement audit and feedback from quality improvement
ideas.description: Adapt structured audit and feedback cycles from QI to review outpatient antibiotic prescriptions.
ideas.sourceTitle: Antimicrobial stewardship in the outpatient setting: A review and proposed framework

ideas.title: Restorative Data Dialogues: Co-Interpreting Unit-Level Equity Dashboards to Drive Systemic Change
ideas.description: This research develops and tests a model where real-time, unit-level dashboards tracking HAI rates stratified by race/ethnicity are used as the central artifact within facilitated restorative justice circles. These 'Restorative Data Dialogues' bring together frontline clinical staff, hospital leadership, and community representatives from affected BIPOC populations to collectively interpret the data, share lived experiences, and identify the systemic root causes of disparities. The process moves beyond simple feedback to repair trust and co-produce actionable, unit-specific interventions, thereby embedding community voice and restorative accountability directly into the quality improvement cycle for health equity.

ideas.title: Co-Investigating Novel Disparities: A CBPR Framework to Distinguish Amplification from New Mechanisms
ideas.description: This research program employs a longitudinal, community-based participatory research (CBPR) design to rigorously investigate whether pandemic impacts were solely due to the amplification of pre-existing disparities or involved novel mechanisms. Community co-researchers from disproportionately affected groups are engaged from the outset to define the relevant disparities, identify potential novel mechanisms (e.g., digital exclusion in healthcare, stigma of 'essential' work), and co-design mixed-methods tools to detect them. The synergy lies in using CBPR's deep engagement to move beyond administrative data, capturing nuanced, community-identified experiences of disadvantage that may be invisible to standard metrics, thereby producing a more valid and actionable test of the core hypothesis.

ideas.title: Long-term sustainability of equity-focused CDSS alerts: Applying diversity analytics to predict and maintain equitable screening outcomes
ideas.description: This study investigates the long-term sustainability of Clinical Decision Support System (CDSS) alerts specifically through an equity lens. It adapts predictive analytics and benchmarking methodologies from corporate diversity software to analyze pediatric workforce data (e.g., clinician demographics, specialty, experience) alongside longitudinal patient screening data. The core research question is whether and how the equitable distribution of screening improvements across different patient demographic groups decays over multiple years post-intervention, and which clinician and team factors predict sustained equity. This moves beyond measuring overall alert durability to modeling the durability of equitable care outcomes.

ideas.title: Assessing the sustainability of institutional health equity gains against evolving systemic pressures
ideas.description: This research applies epidemiological modeling concepts to institutional diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI) outcomes. It investigates whether gains in URM representation (akin to an immune response) are durable or 'waning' when challenged by new 'variants'—such as changes in leadership, economic downturns, policy shifts, or evolving public discourse. The study would systematically track cohorts of URM matriculants or hires over multiple cycles, measuring how their retention and advancement rates are affected by these external and internal pressures, thereby predicting the long-term efficacy and necessary adaptations of DEI interventions.

ideas.title: SDOH-Adjusted Composite Outcome Index for Screening Program Evaluation
ideas.description: This research proposes a multi-component, adjusted outcome measure for evaluating screening programs. The core innovation is a composite index that includes screening uptake, diagnosis yield, and linkage to care, but critically adjusts the linkage component using a pre-specified, validated adjustment for community-level SDOH and patient-reported access barriers. This prevents penalizing programs for engaging marginalized populations while maintaining a patient-centered focus. The adjustment model is developed transparently with stakeholder input to ensure it accounts for external systemic factors without masking poor program performance on factors within its control.

ideas.title: Study the dual: programs that successfully grant accommodations
ideas.description: Identify programs with high disclosure rates and analyze their enabling factors.
ideas.sourceTitle: Barriers to Disclosure of Disability and Request for Accommodations Among First-Year Resident Physicians in the US

ideas.title: Study inverse: settings with minimal HAI disparity
ideas.description: Identify and study hospitals or health systems that have successfully achieved equitable HAI outcomes to extract best practices.
ideas.sourceTitle: Racial/Ethnic Inequities in Healthcare-associated Infections Under the Shadow of Structural Racism: Narrative Review and Call to Action

ideas.title: Demographic mismatch inherently causes negative bias
ideas.description: Explore if patient-clinician demographic differences can sometimes enhance care through complementary perspectives.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Impact of Unconscious Bias in Healthcare: How to Recognize and Mitigate It.

ideas.title: Modeling the Ecological Succession of a Medical Workforce: Tracing Diversity Interventions Through Career Trajectories
ideas.description: This research adapts methodologies from ecology, specifically the study of ecological succession, to quantify the long-term impact of residency diversity interventions. In ecology, scientists seed plots with specific species and track the community's composition over decades to see if the initial change persists and influences subsequent generations. The adapted solution is to model medical career pathways as a form of professional succession. By treating residency programs as distinct 'plots' and using longitudinal career data to 'track' marked individuals, the research will measure how the 'seeding' event (the diversity intervention) affects not just immediate diversity but subsequent career advancement stages, specialty representation, and the diversity of mentorship networks 10-20 years later, identifying critical transition points where the impact is sustained or lost.

ideas.title: Context-Aware, Disposition-Integrated Screening for High-Risk Patients in Emergency Settings
ideas.description: This system moves screening prompts from the chaotic acute assessment phase to the disposition and discharge planning phase of an emergency department or urgent care visit. It activates only for stable patients being discharged to the community, using a minimal-data, high-specificity rule to identify patients at severe short-term risk from a missed screening (e.g., a patient with HIV not on antiretrovirals flagged for opportunistic infection screening). The recommendation is presented as a prioritized item in the after-visit summary and a prompt for a tailored referral, aligning with the ED's transitional care role without disrupting acute care workflows.

ideas.title: Assessing team bias in the dynamic evaluation of vaccine protection against severe disease
ideas.description: This research investigates how unconscious bias among infectious disease professionals (e.g., epidemiologists, clinicians, public health officials) impacts the collective assessment and communication of vaccine protection against severe illness as viruses evolve and immunity wanes. It examines whether team composition and dynamics lead to systematic over- or under-estimation of vaccine impact when analyzing ambiguous or emerging real-world evidence. The study aims to identify bias patterns that could distort risk assessments and public health guidance, thereby improving the reliability of team-based evaluations during evolving health crises.

ideas.title: Data-Driven Segmentation for Precision Public Health: Targeting Interventions by Quantified Risk Profiles
ideas.description: This research fuses marketing segmentation with causal disparity analysis to develop a precision public health framework. It first uses advanced modeling to quantify the relative contribution of antecedent factors (e.g., occupation, transportation access, medical distrust) to COVID-19 outcomes within BIPOC communities. These quantified risk drivers are then used as the primary variables to cluster the population into distinct, actionable segments. The result is a segmentation map where each group is defined not just by demographics, but by its specific, evidence-based combination of risk factors, enabling the design of communication campaigns and resource allocation strategies that are precisely tailored to mitigate the most salient drivers of disparity for each segment.

ideas.title: Challenge need for antiviral prophylaxis in all
ideas.description: Investigate if a subset of patients can avoid prophylaxis without increased risk.
ideas.sourceTitle: Cytomegalovirus infection in liver transplant recipients: updates on clinical management.

ideas.title: Longitudinal Assessment of Structural Competency Interventions Using Standardized Behavioral Interviews
ideas.description: This research fuses longitudinal policy evaluation with structured interview methodology to rigorously assess the long-term effectiveness of structural interventions in healthcare education and practice. It adapts organizational psychology's structured behavioral interviews to create standardized, longitudinal assessments of healthcare trainees' and professionals' structural competency—their ability to recognize and respond to structural drivers of health disparity. By administering these interviews at multiple time points before and after structural interventions (e.g., revised curricula, policy changes in admissions or care delivery), the study tracks the evolution of bias-resistant, structural thinking in clinical reasoning. This allows for a direct, causal linkage between specific educational or policy interventions, the development of individual structural competency in the workforce, and downstream population health outcomes over time.

ideas.title: Assessing BIPOC Healthcare Workers as Trusted Messengers for Vaccine Confidence in High-Risk Non-Household Transmission Settings
ideas.description: This study shifts the focus from households to high-risk non-household transmission settings, such as workplaces, factories, and communal living facilities, and evaluates the specific role of BIPOC healthcare workers as trusted messengers within these contexts. It investigates whether vaccine confidence and uptake interventions led by trusted BIPOC clinicians and community health workers are more effective when delivered on-site in these high-exposure environments compared to general community or household-based outreach. The research aims to identify optimal strategies for integrating trusted messengers into workplace safety and public health programs to directly mitigate transmission where it is most likely to occur, addressing a critical gap in both transmission science and health equity practice.

ideas.title: Modeling the Systemic-Individual Feedback Loop in HAI Outcome Disparities
ideas.description: This research tests the hypothesis that healthcare-associated infection (HAI) outcome disparities are driven by a reinforcing feedback loop between systemic resource allocation and individual clinical decisions, rather than by either factor in isolation. The study uses a hybrid methodology combining agent-based simulation of provider behavior under varying resource constraints with causal discovery analysis on electronic health record and hospital resource data. This approach quantifies how systemic factors (e.g., nurse-to-patient ratios, supply access) constrain and bias individual decisions, and how the aggregation of those decisions perpetuates or amplifies systemic inequities. The outcome is a validated, dynamic model that identifies critical nodes within the loop for targeted, multi-level interventions.

ideas.title: A Causal Process Diagnostic Framework for Equity in HAI Prevention
ideas.description: This research develops and validates a dynamic framework that moves beyond documenting outcome disparities to diagnosing their systemic causes. It decomposes the HAI prevention pathway into sequential clinical and operational processes, embedding equity-sensitive metrics at each step to identify specific failure points for different patient groups. The framework is designed to link observed disparities directly to actionable institutional factors, such as protocol adherence variability, staffing models, or device utilization patterns. This enables targeted, evidence-based interventions rather than just broader disparity reporting.

ideas.title: Mapping the differential influence of gatekeeper groups on confidence versus access barriers to vaccine uptake
ideas.description: This research tests the hypothesis that the relative necessity and optimal sequence of engaging the four gatekeeper groups (e.g., policymakers, healthcare providers, community leaders, media) depends fundamentally on whether the primary barrier to vaccine uptake in a given context is confidence or access/logistics. It moves beyond asking if all groups are equally critical to asking which group is pivotal for shifting confidence-specific beliefs versus which is essential for solving logistical bottlenecks. The synergy creates a diagnostic framework to design context-specific, barrier-tailored gatekeeper engagement strategies, rather than applying a uniform sequence of interventions regardless of the underlying cause of low uptake.

ideas.title: Longitudinal Assessment of Structural Interventions via Psychological Safety as a Mediating Mechanism
ideas.description: This research program longitudinally tracks the health outcomes of resident physicians (and, by extension, their patient panels) following the implementation of structural interventions designed to address systemic disparities in medical education and healthcare delivery. Crucially, it employs repeated, validated psychological safety climate surveys within residency programs as a key mediating variable to measure the intervention's immediate impact on the organizational environment. This design allows researchers to determine not just if a structural intervention improves long-term health, but how—by testing whether changes in psychological safety serve as the mechanism that explains improved clinician well-being, reduced burnout, enhanced team performance, and downstream patient health equity outcomes over time.

ideas.title: A Pre-Deployment Bias Stress-Testing Framework for Infectious Disease Diagnostic Development
ideas.description: This research designs and validates a proactive framework to identify and mitigate biases in infectious disease diagnostics during their development phase, prior to clinical trials. The system uses synthetic data generation and adversarial simulation to model diverse epidemiological scenarios, host factors, and healthcare access conditions, stress-testing assay performance across subpopulations. It integrates causal discovery methods to pinpoint the source of performance disparities (e.g., in training data, biomarker selection, or analytical thresholds). The output is a validated protocol and software tool for developers to preemptively correct bias vulnerabilities, moving bias mitigation upstream from clinical evaluation to the design stage.

ideas.title: Scale down to high-risk subpopulations
ideas.description: Focus the CDSS intervention only on clinics serving populations with historically low screening rates.
ideas.sourceTitle: Assessment and improvement of HIV screening rates in a Midwest primary care practice using an electronic clinical decision support system: a quality improvement study

ideas.title: Equity-Focused AMR Sentinel Networks in Community Pediatric Practices
ideas.description: This research tests a model where small, private pediatric practices implement a modified six-step EDI cycle to integrate AMR and syndromic pathogen surveillance into their daily workflow. The intervention focuses on ensuring equitable testing, culturally competent patient engagement, and tailored reporting back to the community. By doing so, it builds a decentralized, trust-based surveillance network that can detect localized outbreaks and resistance patterns early, while directly using the data to address care disparities within the practice's own patient population, making public health action locally relevant and responsive.

ideas.title: Process Control for Structural Bias: Monitoring Systemic Drivers of Health Disparities
ideas.description: This research applies industrial statistical process control (SPC) charts not just to monitor equity metrics, but to investigate the core debate from Idea B: whether structural factors or individual unconscious bias is the primary driver of observed disparities. By treating a health system's equity outcomes as a production process, SPC charts can detect 'special cause variation'—significant deviations from the baseline—in infection rates, vaccination uptake, or treatment access across demographic groups. Analyzing the timing, scale, and pattern of these statistical alarms in relation to specific policy changes, resource allocations, or training interventions allows researchers to test if shifts correlate more strongly with structural changes (e.g., clinic closures, eligibility rule changes) or with initiatives targeting individual bias (e.g., implicit bias training rollouts). This provides an evidence-based framework to attribute drivers of inequity and prioritize the most effective intervention types.

ideas.title: Validated Equity in HAI Outcomes: A Multi-Method Funnel to Identify and Audit True High-Performers
ideas.description: This study employs a sequential validation model to distinguish genuinely equitable HAI outcomes from statistical artifacts. Phase 1 uses quantitative analysis of national data to flag candidate institutions, applying rigorous adjustment for surveillance intensity, risk-adjustment, and reporting completeness. Phase 2 involves in-depth, on-site mixed-methods evaluation of short-listed sites, including chart reviews, staff interviews, and process observation, to audit and confirm equitable practices. Only sites passing both stages contribute to a robust repository of validated strategies for achieving equitable infection prevention.

ideas.title: Structural Racism in Global Health Governance: A Critical Scenario Analysis of US Leadership for AMR Equity
ideas.description: This research investigates the hypothesis that structural racism is a primary, under-examined driver shaping the United States' approach to global antimicrobial resistance (AMR) governance and financing. It employs counterfactual scenario analysis to model global AMR progress without US leadership, specifically examining whether the resulting governance and funding gaps would interact with and exacerbate existing global structures of racial and economic inequality. The study moves beyond assessing general efficacy to critically evaluate whose health is prioritized or neglected in current and alternative global AMR regimes, thereby fusing a critical race theory lens with geopolitical scenario planning for global health security.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Niche Modeling to Estimate Causal Policy Effects on Healthcare Disparities
ideas.description: This idea adapts a causal inference solution from ecology—specifically, the quasi-experimental methods used in species distribution modeling to assess the impact of environmental shocks. In ecology, researchers model species presence as an outcome of environmental variables and use comparisons between treated and carefully matched control sites over time to estimate causal effects. Adapted to health policy, we would model HAI rates as an outcome determined by policy exposure, hospital ecology, and patient factors. The core methodological adaptation is a matching-enhanced difference-in-differences design. Prior to applying DiD, hospitals exposed to a payment reform are matched with unexposed controls using a propensity score or multivariate matching based on a broad set of observed confounders that define the hospital's 'ecological niche' (e.g., teaching status, nurse-to-patient ratios, geographic region, historical infection rates, and patient demographic profiles). This creates a more balanced comparison group, reducing bias. The causal effect of the reform is then estimated via DiD on the matched sample, with a specific focus on how the effect differs across racial, ethnic, or socioeconomic groups to quantify the policy's impact on disparities.

ideas.title: A Public Accountability Platform for Predictive Pediatric Workforce Equity
ideas.description: This research develops an open platform where pediatric healthcare institutions publicly report workforce diversity data, enhanced by predictive analytics adapted from corporate HR tech. The platform would not only show current demographics but also use predictive models to forecast future workforce composition under current hiring and retention practices, benchmarked against peer institutions and regional population data. This creates a transparent, forward-looking accountability mechanism, allowing the public, accrediting bodies, and policymakers to see if an institution's trajectory aligns with stated equity goals. The synergy transforms internal analytics into a driver of public accountability and proactive, data-driven policy change.

ideas.title: Applying a Validated Intervention Protocol to Untested Graduate Medical Education Contexts via a Modular Implementation Framework
ideas.description: This idea proposes using analogical reasoning from conservation biology to structure the future work of testing diversity interventions in new GME contexts. In ecological restoration, a validated protocol for one ecosystem is adapted for another via a modular framework that preserves core principles while swapping context-specific components. The analogous solution is adapted by first deconstructing the identified combination of effective diversity interventions into their core mechanisms and variable elements. For each new residency or fellowship program (the 'untested ecosystem'), researchers would systematically analyze key parameters—such as specialty-specific norms, applicant demographics, and institutional resources—to tailor the variable elements of the intervention package. This creates a principled yet flexible methodology for piloting and evaluating the adapted interventions, increasing the likelihood of successful implementation and meaningful comparison across different GME contexts.

ideas.title: Embedding evidence-based vaccine equity messaging within gatekeeper-controlled scientific communication channels
ideas.description: This research designs and evaluates interventions that embed culturally tailored, evidence-based vaccine confidence content from national BIPOC media campaigns directly into the communication outputs of academic gatekeepers. It transforms how academic centers, societies, journals, and funding agencies communicate science by integrating proven public health messaging into their press releases, policy statements, journal editorials, and funded research dissemination. This approach leverages the institutional trust and reach of the scientific establishment to amplify and legitimize public health campaigns, while simultaneously holding these gatekeepers accountable for advancing health equity through their core communication functions.

ideas.title: National Policy Simulation of Holistic Admissions with Geospatial Health Equity Mapping
ideas.description: This research fuses environmental justice mapping techniques with national policy simulation to model the population health and equity consequences of a systemic shift to holistic review in graduate medical education. It uses GIS to layer pre-pandemic social vulnerability indices, environmental exposure data, and COVID-19 morbidity/mortality to create a high-resolution, place-based 'health equity risk' profile for applicant populations. This geospatial risk framework is then integrated into a national simulation model to project how de-emphasizing Step 1 would differentially affect the pipeline of future physicians from communities with high cumulative environmental and health burdens, thereby evaluating the policy's impact on long-term structural health equity.

ideas.title: Auditing the Efficacy Gap: Public Reporting on Committee Diversity, Bias Training, and Outcome Equity
ideas.description: This research establishes a framework where institutions publicly report disaggregated data on committee composition, the presence and type of implicit bias mitigation training, and the equity outcomes of committee decisions (e.g., hiring, funding, promotions). By mandating this transparent reporting, it enables a large-scale, cross-institutional analysis to empirically test whether diverse committees without proven bias training perpetuate inequitable outcomes. The synergy creates a feedback loop where public accountability drives participation in the study, and the study's findings, visible to all, create evidence-based pressure for adopting effective bias interventions, moving beyond symbolic diversity toward audited procedural justice.

ideas.title: Targeted Nudges for Intra-Group Equity: Reducing Bias Against Hispanic/Latinx Subgroups
ideas.description: This research synthesizes nudge theory with a critical analysis of intra-group diversity to develop precision behavioral interventions for hiring, promotion, and peer review. It challenges the one-size-fits-all approach to bias reduction by designing nudges that specifically disrupt monolithic stereotypes of the Hispanic/Latinx population, such as assumptions linking nationality to skill sets or immigration status to commitment. The project will empirically test whether nudges tailored to highlight individual professional attributes, while countering subgroup stereotypes, are more effective at reducing bias than standard diversity nudges. This creates a novel framework for equity initiatives that acknowledges and addresses the complex, layered nature of social perception.

ideas.title: Positive Deviance Analysis of COVID-19 Outcomes in African American Communities
ideas.description: This research adapts the 'Positive Deviance' framework from public health nutrition to study COVID-19 disparities. The analogous solution involved identifying well-nourished children in high-risk, resource-poor communities and using ethnographic inquiry to discover the specific, uncommon caregiving and feeding practices responsible for their positive outcomes. Adapted to this context, the study will systematically identify African American communities that acted as 'positive deviants' by achieving better-than-expected COVID-19 outcomes. Researchers will then employ community-based participatory research, including surveys, focus groups, and social network analysis, to uncover the existing but potentially overlooked protective factors—such as specific forms of social cohesion, trusted local leadership, innovative information dissemination, or collective adherence to protective measures—that contributed to this resilience. The goal is to move beyond deficit models and discover actionable, community-grown solutions that can be validated and scaled.

ideas.title: Audit programmatic accessibility outcomes and systemic barriers for trainees with disabilities
ideas.description: This study moves beyond assessing program directors' knowledge to directly evaluate the accessibility outcomes and systemic barriers within residency or fellowship programs. It employs a three-pronged method: an environmental audit of digital and physical resources, confidential surveys of current trainees (with and without disabilities) on inclusion experiences, and a knowledge/attitude assessment of program leadership. By correlating director knowledge with objective audit results and trainee-reported outcomes, the research identifies specific, actionable disconnects between policy awareness and practical implementation, providing a robust blueprint for systemic intervention.

ideas.title: Mapping Structural Drivers to High-Stakes Decision Pathways in Infectious Disease Management
ideas.description: This research investigates how specific structural and systemic drivers of inequity—such as differential access to diagnostic resources, time pressures in under-resourced settings, and algorithmic bias in clinical decision support tools—directly manifest as cognitive biases and workflow distortions in high-stakes infectious disease decisions. By mapping these drivers onto discrete decision points in sepsis recognition and antibiotic stewardship, the study aims to identify the precise mechanisms by which structural factors become operationalized as clinical risk. This mechanistic understanding will inform the design of decision-support interventions that are not just clinically accurate but also structurally aware, capable of mitigating the specific inequities introduced at each critical juncture in patient care.

ideas.title: Quantifying the impact of clinician bias on public trust and vaccine effectiveness for emerging variants
ideas.description: This research investigates the hypothesis that patient unconscious bias against clinicians from underrepresented groups erodes trust in public health messaging, which in turn reduces vaccine uptake and adherence to non-pharmaceutical interventions. The study would quantitatively measure this erosion of trust and its behavioral consequences, then model how this social factor attenuates the population-level transmission reduction expected from vaccines against new SARS-CoV-2 variants. The result is an integrated biosocial model that predicts outbreak trajectories by fusing virological vaccine efficacy data with sociologically measured bias impacts, identifying critical points for bias mitigation to preserve intervention effectiveness.

ideas.title: Stewardship as Ecosystem Health: Measuring Success by Functional Resilience, Not Just Input Reduction
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that stewardship success is synonymous with prescription reduction. By drawing an analogy from sustainable agriculture's shift from input reduction to ecosystem health metrics, we propose a parallel solution for clinical stewardship. Success should be redefined using a multidimensional 'Clinical Ecosystem Health Index' that integrates microbiological, clinical, and patient-centered outcomes. This adapted framework measures the functional resilience and overall health of the clinical environment, ensuring stewardship programs are evaluated on their ultimate goal of sustaining patient and community well-being, not just on a single, potentially misleading process metric.

ideas.title: Embedded Behavioral Nudges and Covert Auditing for Objective HAI Prevention Equity
ideas.description: This research develops and evaluates a systems-level intervention that embeds bias-mitigating tools directly into the healthcare workflow to objectively change behavior, not just awareness. It employs electronic health record prompts, standardized risk-assessment checklists, and environmental redesign to guide equitable infection control decisions at the point of care, minimizing reliance on individual vigilance. The system's efficacy is rigorously measured using covert, randomized audits with standardized patient actors presenting identical clinical scenarios across different demographic profiles, providing an objective, behavioral outcome metric for equity in HAI prevention practices. This approach directly targets the knowing-doing gap and provides robust evidence of real-world impact.

ideas.title: Predictive Nudge Systems: Structuring Holistic Review to Mitigate Bias in GME Selection
ideas.description: This research synthesizes validated non-cognitive predictors from undergraduate admissions (e.g., grit, teamwork, leadership) with principles of behavioral economics to redesign Graduate Medical Education (GME) application review systems. The core innovation is to use the predictors as a framework to structure the evaluation interface and workflow, embedding evidence-based nudges—such as structured scoring rubrics for each predictor, pre-commitment prompts, and blinded review of specific competencies—that actively guide selection committees. This moves beyond simply adding predictive scores to applications and instead engineers the decision environment to promote consistent, equitable consideration of holistic attributes. The result is a scalable, process-embedded intervention aimed at mitigating unconscious gender and racial bias during the critical assessment phase, potentially improving both the fairness and predictive validity of GME selections.

ideas.title: Modeling the Structural-Cognitive Feedback Loop in Infectious Disease Inequity
ideas.description: This research investigates the recursive relationship between individual bias and structural factors as a dynamic system driving health disparities. It uses computational social science methods, including agent-based models calibrated with empirical data on implicit bias, healthcare access, and housing policy, to simulate how biases influence policy creation and institutional practice. The system then measures how these newly shaped structures feedback to alter population-level exposure, susceptibility, and treatment outcomes for infectious diseases over time. The goal is to identify high-leverage intervention points within this feedback loop, moving beyond attributing primary causality to one domain.

ideas.title: Use peer comparison from social psychology
ideas.description: Provide clinicians with data on their prescribing rates compared to anonymized peers.
ideas.sourceTitle: Antimicrobial stewardship in the outpatient setting: A review and proposed framework

ideas.title: Adapting the PDSA Cycle from Quality Improvement Science to Implement and Test the Six-Step EDI Cycle
ideas.description: This research idea adapts the solution to a parallel problem in Healthcare Quality Improvement—implementing systemic change through iterative testing—to the context of operationalizing a six-step Equity, Diversity, and Inclusion (EDI) cycle in pediatric institutions. The analogous solution is the Plan-Do-Study-Act (PDSA) cycle, a proven method for testing changes on a small scale before full implementation. The adapted idea proposes to implement the six-step EDI cycle not as a monolithic program, but through a series of structured PDSA cycles within pilot units. This approach allows researchers to measure the impact of each EDI step iteratively, using rapid feedback loops to refine the process, identify contextual barriers, and build evidence for effectiveness based on measurable intermediate outcomes (like team climate surveys or recruitment pipeline data) before institution-wide adoption.

ideas.title: Adaptive, Real-Time Feedback for Antibiotic Prescribing Using EHR-Embedded Ecological Models
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from precision agriculture to clinical antibiotic stewardship. In agriculture, sensor data and predictive models are integrated into management software for real-time, ecologically-informed decisions on resource application (e.g., water, fertilizer). The analogous solution is to build and evaluate an EHR-embedded clinical decision support (CDS) tool that functions similarly for outpatient antibiotic prescribing. This adaptive CDS would analyze a dashboard of patient-specific factors (comorbidities, allergies, renal function), local and real-time antimicrobial resistance patterns, and infection details to generate dynamic, evidence-based recommendations for the optimal antibiotic agent, dose, and duration. The goal is to provide clinicians with precision feedback that balances individual patient treatment needs with the broader ecological goal of minimizing unnecessary antibiotic exposure and curbing resistance, effectively 'cultivating' better prescribing practices.

ideas.title: Co-designing and evaluating community-governed trust architectures to improve vaccine equity
ideas.description: This research employs participatory action research (PAR) to partner with BIPOC community organizations in designing and implementing shared governance structures for local health initiatives. Instead of testing pre-defined institutional actions, the study establishes community oversight boards with real decision-making power over vaccine program design, messaging, and delivery. The impact is evaluated through a mixed-methods framework that prioritizes longitudinal relationship quality metrics, shifts in narrative and discourse within the community, and changes in perceived legitimacy of health institutions, with vaccine uptake data serving as one secondary outcome among several measures of restored agency and health equity.

ideas.title: Structured, competency-based frameworks for personalizing post-transplant prophylaxis duration
ideas.description: This research applies the principles of structured, competency-based interview rubrics from corporate HR to the clinical decision-making process for determining antiviral prophylaxis duration in post-transplant patients. Instead of relying on subjective clinician judgment or a one-size-fits-all approach, it develops a standardized assessment framework that defines and scores key 'competencies' of immune reconstitution (e.g., T-cell repertoire diversity speed, antigen-specific response robustness). This structured scoring system allows for reproducible, bias-minimized personalization of prophylaxis, turning a complex biological decision into an objective, auditable clinical protocol that improves equity and consistency in patient outcomes.

ideas.title: Systemic Inversion for Global Health Governance: Applying Gender Equity Frameworks to AMR Policy Design
ideas.description: This research inverts the typical approach to Antimicrobial Resistance (AMR) policy, which often focuses on changing behaviors of prescribers and patients. Instead, it applies the core feminist principle of fixing systemic structures—not the individuals within them—to the design of global AMR governance. It critically analyzes and proposes to redesign the promotion, compensation, and power-sharing systems within global health institutions, pharmaceutical R&D, and agricultural policy that currently incentivize antibiotic overuse and disincentivize sustainable innovation. The result is a novel governance model that transplants the long-term, binding framework logic from climate policy but fundamentally rewires its operational systems to align rewards with equity, stewardship, and planetary health outcomes.

ideas.title: A Resource-Equity Framework for Testing Stewardship Delivery Models
ideas.description: This study moves beyond a simple head-to-head comparison of stewardship models by first deconstructing stewardship into its core functional components. It then implements these components through centralized, peer-led, and individual clinician models, but critically, ensures each model is provided equitable baseline resources (e.g., protected time, data infrastructure, educational materials) to execute its assigned functions. The research measures not just traditional outcome metrics (e.g., antibiotic use), but also implementation outcomes like feasibility, cost-per-component, and model adaptability to local context. The goal is to identify which delivery architecture most efficiently deploys specific stewardship functions, enabling a modular, context-aware approach rather than a one-size-fits-all mandate.

ideas.title: Prospective RCT to Validate a Biomarker-Defined Low-Risk Cohort for Safe Antiviral Prophylaxis Withdrawal
ideas.description: This study prospectively tests the hypothesis that a clearly defined subset of patients, identified by a validated biomarker profile or clinical risk score, can safely forgo antiviral prophylaxis. The core design is a multicenter, randomized, controlled, non-inferiority trial where eligible, low-risk patients are randomized to either standard prophylaxis or placebo/no prophylaxis. The primary endpoint is the incidence of clinically significant infection, with rigorous safety monitoring. The goal is to generate Level I evidence for a personalized prophylaxis strategy that reduces unnecessary medication use without increasing morbidity.

ideas.title: Develop and test community-specific vaccine access models
ideas.description: Design and evaluate tailored models for vaccine delivery that address unique access barriers in different BIPOC populations.
ideas.sourceTitle: Addressing and Inspiring Vaccine Confidence in Black, Indigenous, and People of Color During the Coronavirus Disease 2019 Pandemic

ideas.title: A Multi-Method, Contextual Framework for Modeling Clinician Bias in Prescribing Decisions
ideas.description: This research system moves beyond standalone implicit bias testing by integrating it into a comprehensive model of clinical decision-making. It combines the Implicit Association Test with explicit bias surveys, standardized clinical vignettes featuring patient actors of varied demographics, and analysis of real-world electronic health record prescribing data. Crucially, it incorporates contextual moderators like clinic workflow pressures and diagnostic certainty to test when and how implicit associations are most likely to manifest in prescribing disparities. This approach isolates the specific, actionable mechanisms by which bias may operate in practice, rather than relying on a single psychological metric of uncertain clinical relevance.

ideas.title: Patient-Adjusted, Evidence-Based Prescribing Benchmark Feedback
ideas.description: This system provides clinicians with feedback on their prescribing rates compared to a personalized, evidence-based benchmark, not to peers. For each clinician, an algorithm analyzes their de-identified patient panel data to generate an expected prescribing rate based on clinical guidelines and the specific morbidity profile of their patients. The feedback report highlights the gap between their actual prescribing and this patient-adjusted ideal, accompanied by actionable, guideline-concordant reasons for potential variance. This method decouples feedback from social norms and grounds it directly in clinical evidence and patient need.

ideas.title: Equity-Audited Vaccine Impact Synthesis with Stratified Policy Simulation
ideas.description: This system integrates data on infection, illness, and transmission with a mandatory, pre-decision equity audit to inform public health policies. It automatically disaggregates all synthesized data across demographic and clinical strata to identify heterogeneous vaccine effects. Using this stratified evidence, it runs policy simulations to project differential outcomes across population subgroups, explicitly highlighting trade-offs and risks of exacerbating disparities. The final output is a set of policy options accompanied by transparent, stratified impact assessments, ensuring equity considerations are quantitatively central to the decision-making process.

ideas.title: Mechanistic Tracing of Structural Drivers into Clinical Pathways of HAI Risk
ideas.description: This research employs a causal-mechanistic framework to identify and quantify the precise clinical and operational pathways through which structural inequities manifest as differential HAI risk. It combines advanced causal inference on large-scale electronic health record and hospital operations data with in-depth, real-time ethnographic and sensor-based observation of care delivery for high-risk procedures. The goal is to pinpoint critical, intervenable junctures—such as variations in environmental cleaning, device insertion protocols, or antibiotic stewardship—that disproportionately affect vulnerable populations, thereby providing a direct blueprint for equity-focused quality improvement.

ideas.title: Adaptive Benchmarking and Feedback: Using Positive Outlier Insights to Drive Audit Cycles
ideas.description: This research fuses audit and feedback (A&F) with positive deviance methods to create an adaptive, evidence-informed intervention. First, qualitative and quantitative methods are used to identify the specific structural, cultural, and behavioral drivers of appropriate prescribing in outlier clinics with low inappropriate use. These identified enabling factors are then systematically embedded into the design of the A&F cycles, tailoring feedback reports to highlight not just gaps, but locally relevant, proven strategies for improvement. The approach transforms static A&F into a learning system that disseminates peer-generated best practices, thereby increasing the credibility and potential effectiveness of the intervention.

ideas.title: A Multi-Method Assessment of Implicit Bias and Clinical Behavior in Infectious Disease Management
ideas.description: This research develops and validates a multi-method assessment system to link implicit biases directly to clinical actions. It combines a validated infectious disease stereotype IAT with high-fidelity clinical simulations featuring standardized patients from varied demographic backgrounds. Objective behavioral coding of clinician performance in the simulations provides concrete outcome measures of diagnostic reasoning, communication, and treatment decisions. The integrated analysis determines if and how implicit biases, as measured by the IAT, manifest in observable clinical behaviors under realistic conditions.

ideas.title: Model the Long-Term Economic and Public Health Value of Outpatient Stewardship Using Multi-Perspective Dynamic Simulation
ideas.description: This research employs a dynamic simulation modeling approach to conduct a comprehensive economic evaluation of outpatient antibiotic stewardship programs. The analysis integrates healthcare payer, societal, and public health perspectives to capture both immediate costs and long-term benefits, such as reduced antibiotic resistance transmission and avoided complications of resistant infections. The model projects outcomes over a multi-year timeframe, quantifying the economic value of preserving antibiotic effectiveness for the population. This provides a more robust and policy-relevant assessment of stewardship's true return on investment than a short-term cost-accounting study.

ideas.title: Inverse problem: Identify drivers of appropriate use
ideas.description: Study clinics with exceptionally low inappropriate prescribing to identify enabling factors.
ideas.sourceTitle: Antimicrobial stewardship in the outpatient setting: A review and proposed framework

ideas.title: A Randomized, Partner-Driven Evaluation of the Six-Step EDI Cycle's Impact on Pre-Specified Equity Outcomes in Pediatric Care
ideas.description: This study implements the six-step EDI cycle within a consortium of pediatric institutions using a stepped-wedge cluster randomized controlled trial design. Prior to implementation, community partners and researchers co-define a core set of quantifiable primary outcome measures focused on health equity and inclusion. The intervention's rollout is sequentially randomized across sites, allowing for a rigorous comparison of outcomes between control and intervention periods within each site. The integrated mixed-methods evaluation assesses both the process fidelity of the cycle and its causal impact on the pre-specified, partner-endorsed equity metrics.

ideas.title: Digital Biomonitoring for Auditing Implicit Bias in Real-World Committee Decisions
ideas.description: This research fuses digital epidemiology's tools for passive, continuous biometric data collection (e.g., via wearables measuring heart rate variability, electrodermal activity) with the critical hypothesis that committee diversity alone is insufficient for equity. It proposes deploying these tools in real-world committees (e.g., hiring, grant review) to collect synchronized, objective physiological data from participants during deliberations. By analyzing this biometric stream alongside speech patterns and decision outcomes, the study can test for systematic differences in stress, engagement, or cognitive load correlated with a member's demographic identity, providing direct evidence of whether implicit bias persists despite diverse representation. This moves audit research from assessing outcomes and composition to quantifying the subjective experience of the decision-making process itself.

ideas.title: Dual problem: Manage patient expectations without antibiotics
ideas.description: Develop communication protocols for satisfying patient visits that do not end in antibiotic prescriptions.
ideas.sourceTitle: Antimicrobial stewardship in the outpatient setting: A review and proposed framework

ideas.title: Equity-Driven Clinical Decision Support: From Alert Suppression to Guideline-Concordant Access
ideas.description: This research develops and validates a clinical decision support system (CDSS) that inverts the traditional alert paradigm. Instead of merely suppressing 'unnecessary' HIV screening alerts, the system uses suppression logic as a diagnostic tool to identify populations for whom guideline-concordant screening is being systematically withheld or overlooked. By analyzing patterns of 'appropriate' alert suppression (e.g., for recently tested patients) versus 'inappropriate' non-alerts (e.g., missed screenings in marginalized groups), the system can generate equity-focused dashboards and targeted prompts. The goal is to transform CDSS from a blunt instrument for reducing alert fatigue into an adaptive engine that actively equalizes access to appropriate, evidence-based care, thereby aligning operational efficiency with health equity.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Niche Modeling to Map Bias Risk in Clinical Environments
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from Ecology—Species Distribution Modeling (SDM)—to the problem of clinician bias in prescribing. In ecology, SDMs predict species presence by modeling correlations between occurrence data and environmental layers. Analogously, we can model 'prescribing disparity occurrence' against layers of clinical environment data (e.g., caseload, staffing ratios, local socioeconomic indices, implicit bias scores aggregated by unit). The output is a predictive spatial-temporal map of 'bias risk niches' within a hospital or health network. This shifts the focus from solely measuring individual clinician bias via IATs to identifying and proactively mitigating the systemic environmental conditions that foster and amplify such biases, enabling more efficient, targeted interventions at the unit or institutional level.

ideas.title: Emergency Department Syndromic Surveillance for Structural Racism Fingerprinting
ideas.description: This research direction proposes using high-volume, non-primary care clinical settings—specifically emergency departments and urgent care centers—as a novel data source for the inverse problem of identifying racist policies. By analyzing real-time patterns of health disparities, missed preventive screenings, and acute care presentations for preventable conditions in these settings, the system can generate a dynamic 'policy fingerprint' of the structural determinants affecting the local population. This approach captures the acute, downstream consequences of policies in near real-time, offering a more immediate and geographically specific diagnostic tool than retrospective analysis of primary care records. The resulting surveillance system could identify which specific policy levers (e.g., housing, transportation, policing) are most actively driving health inequities in a community at a given time.

ideas.title: Dynamic CDSS for Risk-Adaptive Screening and Vaccination in a Post-Pandemic Era
ideas.description: This research develops and evaluates a next-generation clinical decision support system (CDSS) that unifies the management of preventive screenings and vaccination. The system uses a dynamic risk engine that incorporates data on individual vaccination history, time since last dose, circulating viral variants, and population-level evidence on waning protection against severe outcomes. It then generates personalized, prioritized alerts not only for guideline-recommended screenings (e.g., hepatitis C) but also for booster vaccinations when a patient's calculated risk of severe disease from vaccine-preventable illnesses rises above a threshold. This creates a holistic, adaptive prevention platform that responds to the evolving landscape of both chronic infectious diseases and acute respiratory threats.

ideas.title: Longitudinal inference of workplace accommodation efficacy from observed career progression patterns
ideas.description: This study applies an inverse problem framework, akin to methods used in epidemiology, to the longitudinal tracking of medical residents. Instead of directly tracking accommodation requests and disclosures, the research would analyze observed, anonymized career progression patterns—such as attrition rates, milestone completion times, and board pass rates across cohorts and programs. By modeling these patterns as the outcome of an underlying 'support efficacy', the study aims to indirectly infer the real-world effectiveness of accommodation systems and institutional climate in mitigating career attrition, without relying solely on imperfect self-report data. This provides a novel, systemic measure of how well support structures function to 'protect' career trajectories from the risk of attrition due to disability or illness.

ideas.title: Assessing bias in household transmission study design and analysis committees
ideas.description: This research develops a method to audit the design and analysis phases of household transmission studies for implicit bias. It applies the core question from Idea A—whether diversity without bias training is sufficient—to the technical arena of Idea B, such as choices in defining index cases, adjusting for household structure, or interpreting vaccine effectiveness signals. By simulating household transmission data with known parameters and convening diverse committees (with and without bias mitigation training) to design studies and analyze the simulated data, we can quantify if and how untrained committees produce methodological choices that systematically skew results for different sub-populations, thereby perpetuating inequities in the foundational evidence base for vaccine policy.

ideas.title: Healthcare system is the primary intervention point
ideas.description: Test if direct economic or housing interventions reduce health disparities more effectively than healthcare reforms.
ideas.sourceTitle: Racial Disparity of Coronavirus Disease 2019 in African American Communities

ideas.title: Proactive Equity as a Causal Mechanism: Testing Accommodation-Solicitation Models to Overcome Gender Equity Stagnation
ideas.description: This research investigates the hypothesis that stalled gender equity progress, particularly in fields like medicine or STEM, is causally linked to the persistent institutional reliance on individual disclosure of needs or barriers. The study would employ a comparative, mixed-methods design to analyze programs that proactively solicit and offer accommodations (e.g., flexible scheduling, parental support, equipment) without requiring disclosure, versus traditional disclosure-based models. By treating the 'proactive solicitation' model as an independent variable, the research aims to isolate its causal impact on key equity metrics—retention, promotion, and satisfaction—thereby identifying a concrete systemic intervention to disrupt stagnation. This moves root-cause analysis from identifying correlational barriers to experimentally testing a systemic solution.

ideas.title: Modeling the Institutional Drivers of Sustained Diversity and Equity in Graduate Education
ideas.description: This study moves beyond tracking recruitment numbers to build a causal model of the factors that determine whether diversity gains are sustained and lead to equitable outcomes. It longitudinally tracks URM student cohorts from matriculation through degree completion and into early career stages, while concurrently collecting time-series data on institutional variables like funding, mentorship networks, departmental climate, and support program participation. The analysis employs mixed methods to identify which institutional investments and policy changes are most strongly associated with not only maintaining but also deepening diversity, equity, and inclusion across multiple cycles. The goal is to provide actionable evidence on how to transform short-term recruitment successes into lasting structural change.

ideas.title: Longitudinal study of structural interventions on health
ideas.description: Track health outcomes over time after implementing policies to address root structural causes of disparity.
ideas.sourceTitle: Racial Disparity of Coronavirus Disease 2019 in African American Communities

ideas.title: A Comparative Ecology of Disclosure: Mapping Accommodation Barriers Across Medical Training Environments
ideas.description: This research adapts methods from community ecology to investigate differences in disclosure rates and accommodation barriers across medical specialties. In ecology, the distribution of species is studied by comparing how different habitats (e.g., forests, grasslands) present unique combinations of barriers and resources. Analogously, residency programs (e.g., surgical, psychiatric, primary care) will be treated as distinct professional habitats. The solution involves deploying a standardized, cross-specialty survey instrument (akin to ecological quadrat sampling) to uniformly measure environmental pressures (workload, culture, physical/cognitive demands) and institutional resources. Using multivariate statistical techniques common in ecology, the analysis will model how these habitat-specific factors predict trainee disclosure behavior and perceived barriers, thereby identifying the key programmatic 'environmental' features that facilitate or inhibit access to accommodations.

ideas.title: Apply community-based participatory research
ideas.description: Engage affected communities directly to co-design studies on the drivers and solutions for prescribing inequities.
ideas.sourceTitle: Health Equity and Antibiotic Prescribing in the United States: A Systematic Scoping Review.

ideas.title: Risk-Stratified Antiviral Prophylaxis Using Biomarker Profiling
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that all patients require antiviral prophylaxis by adapting a solution from oncology: risk-stratified management. In fields like prostate cancer, not all patients receive immediate treatment; instead, biomarkers identify low-risk individuals for active surveillance. The adapted idea is to develop a multifactorial risk score for patients vulnerable to viral infection (e.g., transplant recipients, those on immunosuppressants). By combining host immune biomarkers, viral exposure metrics, and clinical data, researchers can define a low-risk subset eligible for a deferred prophylaxis strategy with enhanced monitoring, thereby testing the hypothesis that prophylaxis can be safely avoided in this group without increased clinical risk.

ideas.title: Hyper-local Clinical Trial Site Optimization Under Equity Constraints
ideas.description: This research develops a computational model to optimize the selection of clinical trial sites and participants under a mandatory minimum representation constraint for underrepresented groups.
