It integrates hyper-local maps of COVID-19 (or other disease) disparities and structural factors (e.g., poverty, access to care) at the census tract level to quantify and predict neighborhood-specific risk and recruitment feasibility. The model then solves a dual-objective optimization problem: maximizing the aggregate predicted probability of trial success (merit) based on participant profiles, while mandating minimum enrollment from high-disparity tracts identified by the mapping. This enables proactive, geographically-precise equity in trial design rather than relying on broad demographic quotas.

ideas.title: Inverse problem: From prescribing to inferring bias
ideas.description: Develop models to infer latent clinician bias or system factors from observed prescription patterns across groups.
ideas.sourceTitle: Health Equity and Antibiotic Prescribing in the United States: A Systematic Scoping Review.

ideas.title: Decomposing Variance in Disparities: A Variance Partitioning Approach Adapted from Ecology
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from community ecology to challenge the assumption about the primary drivers of healthcare disparities. In ecology, variance partitioning is used to disentangle the effects of environmental filters from dispersal limits on species communities. The analogous solution is applied here by designing an observational study using large-scale clinical encounter data. The outcome (e.g., treatment decision, diagnostic delay, pain management score) is modeled. Using hierarchical linear modeling or similar techniques, the total variance is partitioned into components: variance explained uniquely by clinician factors (bias, training), uniquely by patient factors (clinical and demographic), uniquely by systemic factors (access, resources), and their overlaps. This provides a direct, quantitative test of whether clinician bias is a minor or major independent contributor relative to other domains, moving beyond correlation to apportion explained variance.

ideas.title: Causal Inference of Implicit Bias Recognition as an Instrument for Health Equity
ideas.description: This research program applies econometric causal inference frameworks, such as instrumental variables or difference-in-differences, to rigorously test the hypothesis that automatic bias recognition is a sufficient intervention for behavioral correction. It would design natural or quasi-experiments where the 'assignment' to bias recognition (e.g., via an implicit association test) serves as a potential instrument, and the 'treatment' is the subsequent reduction in biased behavior by professionals (e.g., clinicians, teachers, employers). The ultimate outcome is a population-level health metric, allowing for the estimation of whether bias recognition causally disrupts the pathway from structural racism to health disparities, without requiring additional structured interventions.

ideas.title: Pragmatic cluster-randomized trial with embedded implementation science comparing systems for CMV prevention in high-risk liver transplant recipients
ideas.description: This redesigned study conducts a hybrid effectiveness-implementation trial comparing universal prophylaxis versus pre-emptive therapy for CMV prevention in D+R- liver transplant recipients. The trial employs cluster randomization at the transplant center level to evaluate real-world delivery systems, incorporating varying monitoring protocols and treatment initiation pathways. An adaptive enrichment design identifies patient subgroups that differentially benefit from each strategy based on clinical characteristics and center capabilities. The primary outcome combines clinical efficacy with implementation fidelity metrics, providing both biological and operational evidence to guide protocol adoption across diverse healthcare settings.

ideas.title: Causal Inference for Micro-Scale Community Health Worker Program Evaluation
ideas.description: This research employs causal inference methods, such as difference-in-differences or regression discontinuity, to evaluate the impact of micro-scale, neighborhood-based community health worker (CHW) programs on specific health outcomes like vaccine uptake or infection rates. By treating the staggered rollout of these hyper-local programs across different city blocks or housing complexes as a natural experiment, it isolates their causal effect from confounding factors. This allows for the precise measurement of how trusted local interventions reduce health equity gaps, providing rigorous, granular evidence for optimizing CHW training and resource allocation. The approach moves beyond assessing broad payment policies to directly test and refine the mechanisms of community-level trust-building.

ideas.title: Community-Validated, Anonymized Portfolio Review for GME Selection
ideas.description: This research applies community-based participatory research (CBPR) methods to first co-design, with local URM communities, the holistic criteria and contextual portfolio elements (e.g., service, leadership narratives) that signify potential for becoming a community-responsive physician. These co-designed metrics then directly inform the construction of a structured, anonymized application portfolio for initial screening, akin to a blind audition. This ensures the anonymization process does not strip away valued lived experiences but rather refocuses evaluators on community-validated evidence of competency, fundamentally aligning equity-oriented program goals with bias-reduced technical implementation.

ideas.title: Inverse problem: from health outcomes to policy failure
ideas.description: Infer the most impactful historical policy failures by analyzing the spatial and demographic patterns of disease outcomes.
ideas.sourceTitle: Racial Disparity of Coronavirus Disease 2019 in African American Communities

ideas.title: Closed-loop diagnostic and therapeutic guidance system for multidrug-resistant infections
ideas.description: This system develops rapid, culture-free diagnostics that not only identify multidrug-resistant bacterial pathogens but also characterize their resistance profile to a range of available antibiotics. The diagnostic device is linked to a dynamic, locally customizable database containing information on local antibiotic availability, cost, and institutional or national guidelines. Upon generating a result, the system provides ranked, actionable treatment recommendations, closing the loop between detection and effective intervention. This integrated approach ensures diagnostic utility translates directly into improved patient outcomes and supports antimicrobial stewardship in diverse clinical settings.

ideas.title: Develop and test specific behavioral interventions
ideas.description: Design interventions targeting clinician and patient behaviors that drive inappropriate outpatient prescribing.
ideas.sourceTitle: Antimicrobial stewardship in the outpatient setting: A review and proposed framework

ideas.title: Implement a Socio-Ecological Systems (SES) Intervention Framework for COVID-19 Disparities
ideas.description: This research adapts the holistic, multi-tiered intervention framework from ecosystem restoration ecology to address structural inequities driving COVID-19 disparities. In ecology, restoring a degraded ecosystem requires simultaneous interventions at the abiotic, biotic, and landscape levels to create a resilient, self-sustaining system. The adapted approach involves developing and testing a comprehensive, multi-pronged strategy that concurrently targets: 1) the 'abiotic' structural and policy environment (e.g., implementing policies for paid sick leave, improving housing and transportation access); 2) the 'biotic' community health organism network (e.g., deploying and supporting community health workers, strengthening local clinic and testing infrastructure); and 3) the overarching 'landscape' of systemic racism and bias (e.g., implementing and evaluating structural competency training for providers, creating equitable data collection and resource allocation systems). The study would evaluate how these synergistic, level-specific interventions interact to create positive feedback loops, thereby producing a more durable and equitable public health infrastructure resilient to future shocks.

ideas.title: A Cross-National Discrete Choice Framework for Quantifying and Comparing Resident Physician Utility in Graduate Medical Education
ideas.description: This research applies discrete choice experiments (DCEs) simultaneously across multiple, structurally different international graduate medical education systems (e.g., US, UK, Canada, Germany, Japan). By employing a harmonized DCE design, it quantifies and directly compares the trade-offs residents in each system make between attributes like work hours, supervision, salary, training quality, and geographic location. The core innovation is using the resulting preference weights to model counterfactual scenarios, such as applying the US duty-hour regime to the UK system or the German compensation model to Canada, to predict impacts on applicant utility and choice. This generates a robust evidence base for cross-national learning and systemic reform.

ideas.title: Inverse Governance: Designing Resilient AMR Policy by Learning from High-Performing Communities
ideas.description: This research inverts the standard policy design process by first identifying Hispanic/Latinx communities that demonstrated unexpectedly low health disparities, including during health crises like AMR-related outbreaks, to understand their protective social, cultural, and institutional factors. It then uses these community-derived models of resilience—such as trusted communication networks, collective health norms, or adaptive local leadership—as the foundational blueprints for designing formal governance structures aimed at sustaining political and public focus on Antimicrobial Resistance (AMR) in the post-acute phase of a pandemic. This approach ensures policies are pre-validated by real-world success, culturally grounded, and more likely to maintain engagement because they are modeled on existing, organic systems of community endurance rather than top-down theoretical frameworks.

ideas.title: The Pediatric Equity Integrity System: A Triangulated Audit for Program Culture and Outcomes
ideas.description: This system moves beyond a static dashboard to an integrated audit platform that actively monitors for performative equity. It confidentially collects longitudinal trainee experience data, rigorously audits program policies and resource allocation, and cross-references these with public outcome metrics. An independent oversight body uses analyzed data to identify programs with significant gaps between their reported equity efforts and the lived experiences or outcomes of their trainees and faculty, triggering structured interventions and, if needed, confidential reporting to accreditors while protecting individual anonymity.

ideas.title: Test in diverse practice settings
ideas.description: Replicate the study in non-academic, rural, or multi-site primary care practices to assess generalizability.
ideas.sourceTitle: Assessment and improvement of HIV screening rates in a Midwest primary care practice using an electronic clinical decision support system: a quality improvement study

ideas.title: Design and evaluate an accountable, safety-guaranteed accommodation system with anonymized needs assessment and third-party advocacy
ideas.description: This research designs and tests an institutional accommodation system that first anonymously assesses the prevalence and types of unmet accommodation needs within a residency cohort to gauge the true scale of the problem. It then implements a mandatory, independent ombudsperson model where all accommodation requests and negotiations are facilitated by a third party not employed by the training program, severing the direct power link between requester and program leadership. The study longitudinally evaluates the system's effectiveness by tracking usage rates, resident psychological safety metrics, and objective career outcomes (e.g., milestone evaluations, attrition) compared to traditional models, ensuring the process doesn't just exist but is actively safe and effective to use.

ideas.title: The Compliance Burden Paradox: When Disability Awareness Training Impairs Program Director Judgment
ideas.description: This study investigates the conditions under which mandated ADA compliance training for graduate medical education program directors leads to counterproductive outcomes, such as increased stereotyping of trainees with disabilities or overly rigid, less individualized accommodation decisions. It applies the 'inverse problem' lens from bias research to measure how the cognitive load and anxiety associated with heightened legal and bias awareness might impair clinical educators' judgment and problem-solving flexibility. The research would employ mixed methods, assessing not just knowledge gaps but also performance under simulated accommodation scenarios to identify training elements that maximize both compliance and effective, trainee-centered support.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Niche Modeling to Design Community-Specific Pandemic Care Systems
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from Ecology and Conservation Biology, this research adapts the solution of 'Ecological Niche Modeling' to the challenge of designing community-specific pandemic care. In ecology, niche modeling predicts species survival by analyzing specific local environmental and biotic factors to create tailored conservation plans. The adapted idea applies this framework to design healthcare delivery for African American communities during infectious disease outbreaks. We propose to model a community's 'healthcare niche' by quantitatively and qualitatively mapping its unique social determinants of health, cultural communication patterns, historical trust in institutions, and existing community asset networks. This data-driven model will then be used to predict and design the most effective, culturally congruent points of intervention—such as leveraging trusted local venues for testing or tailoring communication through specific community influencers—thereby moving beyond generic public health approaches to create resilient, community-optimized care systems for pandemic response.

ideas.title: Micro-communication analysis of clinician-patient discussions on variant-specific vaccine protection
ideas.description: This research fuses dyad-level analysis with variant-specific vaccine evaluation by studying audio-recorded clinician-patient conversations about COVID-19 boosters or new variant-specific vaccines. It systematically analyzes how clinicians explain evolving variant risks and vaccine protection, and then links these communication elements (e.g., clarity, personalization of risk, addressing misinformation) to individual patient decisions and their subsequent real-world infection outcomes, stratified by circulating variant. This reveals not just if protection wanes by variant, but how clinician communication acts as a behavioral mediator of that protection at the individual level.

ideas.title: Standard HAI metrics adequately capture disparities
ideas.description: Develop new metrics that specifically capture equity in HAI prevention processes and outcomes across groups.
ideas.sourceTitle: Racial/Ethnic Inequities in Healthcare-associated Infections Under the Shadow of Structural Racism: Narrative Review and Call to Action

ideas.title: Demographic Complementarity as a Resource: Adapting Biodiversity-Stability Principles to Clinical Dyads
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that patient-clinician demographic mismatch inherently causes negative bias by exploring the potential for complementary perspectives to enhance care. It uses an analogy from ecology and ecosystem science, specifically the biodiversity-stability hypothesis and portfolio effect, where systems with diverse functional traits are more resilient. The analogous solution involves analyzing ecosystems not just for species count but for complementary functional roles and response diversity that buffer against stress. Adapted to the clinical context, the research idea reconceptualizes the demographic-mismatched dyad as a diverse micro-system. It proposes to identify and map the specific, complementary 'functional traits'—such as different forms of knowledge, problem-solving approaches, and community insights—that arise from demographic differences. The goal is to develop a framework showing how these complementary traits, when effectively integrated, can create a more adaptive, robust, and effective clinical interaction, thereby turning potential mismatch into a strategic resource for care resilience.

ideas.title: Participatory Action Research to Map and Disrupt Racism's Uniform Impact on the Disability Care Continuum
ideas.description: This project employs a participatory action research (PAR) framework where disabled residents, particularly from racialized communities, are co-researchers to investigate the hypothesis that racism uniformly impacts all stages of the disability care continuum. The co-designed study will generate granular, experiential data to test the uniformity claim, moving beyond aggregate statistics to reveal how uniform systemic forces manifest in uniquely personal and structural barriers. The synergy enables the development of community-validated metrics and interventions that target the specific, yet uniformly oppressive, mechanisms identified across the continuum, from diagnosis to long-term outcomes. This transforms an abstract theoretical challenge into a concrete, actionable mapping and dismantling project led by those most affected.

ideas.title: Hyper-Local Causal Mapping for Targeted Health Worker Interventions
ideas.description: This research integrates causal inference methods with hyper-local implementation to create a precision public health model. Community health workers deploy tailored vaccine confidence interventions at the neighborhood level while simultaneously collecting granular, real-world data on occupation, housing, and healthcare access factors. By analyzing the differential outcomes of these micro-interventions across neighborhoods, the study can quantify the specific, local contribution of each antecedent factor to disparities in vaccine uptake and health outcomes. This enables a closed-loop system where intervention strategies are continuously optimized based on empirical, localized causal evidence.

ideas.title: Geospatially-Targeted National Media Campaign for Vaccine Equity
ideas.description: This research fuses geospatial epidemiology and a national BIPOC media campaign to create a precision public health intervention. Geospatial hot-spot analysis of prescribing and vaccine uptake data identifies specific neighborhoods and communities experiencing the greatest inequities. These high-priority geographic and demographic targets then direct the placement and messaging of the national media campaign across partnered outlets. Crucially, the fusion enables measurement of campaign impact in those same target areas using spatial regression, creating a feedback loop to iteratively refine media strategy and resource allocation for closing equity gaps.

ideas.title: Pre-existing disparities fully explain pandemic impact
ideas.description: Test if the pandemic created novel, unique mechanisms of disadvantage beyond the amplification of known pre-existing disparities.
ideas.sourceTitle: COVID-19 Pandemic: Disparate Health Impact on the Hispanic/Latinx Population in the United States

ideas.title: Dynamic Risk-Adaptive CDSS: A Stepped-Wedge Trial to Transition High-Risk Clinics Toward Universal Screening
ideas.description: This research proposes a stepped-wedge, cluster-randomized trial that fuses targeted implementation with a universal goal. It tests a Clinical Decision Support System (CDSS) that begins by targeting patients with dynamic risk factors within clinics serving populations with historically low screening rates. The core hypothesis is that this initial, focused targeting increases clinic-level efficiency and protocol familiarity, creating a foundation to systematically 'scale up' the CDSS's recommendation logic over time within the same clinics toward universal screening for all eligible patients. The study will measure whether this adaptive, risk-informed pathway is more effective at achieving and sustaining high universal screening rates compared to a static approach of either immediate universal prompts or indefinite targeted screening.

ideas.title: Generative Equity Design: Building Optimal Pediatric Systems from Disaggregated Disparity Data
ideas.description: This research develops a computational framework that first applies high-resolution disaggregation (e.g., by nationality, language, immigration status, socioeconomic strata within broad racial/ethnic groups) to pediatric health outcome data, such as from COVID-19, to identify the most precise failure points of current systems. It then uses these identified, granular inequity patterns as the foundational input parameters for an inverse generative model. The model's core task is to algorithmically design the blueprint of a theoretically optimal pediatric care department—including its physical layout, staffing models, service protocols, and digital infrastructure—where the discovered disparity drivers are structurally impossible or minimized by design from the outset.

ideas.title: Designing Transparent Institutional Processes to Amplify Community-Derived Protective Factors
ideas.description: This research identifies African American communities with positive COVID-19 outcomes to uncover protective social, cultural, or organizational factors. It then uses the principles of transparent procedural design from disability accommodation systems to develop and test standardized institutional processes that formally integrate these community-derived protective factors. The goal is to create scalable, equitable institutional protocols—for example, in healthcare or education—that actively leverage community strengths rather than imposing external solutions, thereby building systemic resilience from the ground up.

ideas.title: Micro-mechanistic modeling of AMR spread by linking clinician-patient interactions to population-level forecasts
ideas.description: This research direction integrates the two scales by using rich data from clinician-patient interactions—such as audio recordings or simulated consultations—to identify and quantify the specific behavioral, cognitive, and contextual factors that lead to antibiotic prescriptions. These quantified 'micro-mechanisms' are then formalized as parameters (e.g., transmission probabilities, intervention efficacies) within adapted pandemic forecasting models. This allows for the creation of next-generation, mechanism-based AMR spread models that can predict geographic and temporal trends of resistance emergence based on real-world human interaction patterns, and can test the population-level impact of communication-focused interventions with unprecedented specificity.

ideas.title: Improve diagnostics for undetected resistant infections
ideas.description: Develop rapid, accessible tests to identify multidrug-resistant bacterial infections early.
ideas.sourceTitle: Confronting antimicrobial resistance beyond the COVID-19 pandemic and the 2020 US election

ideas.title: Targeted Conservation: Applying Conservation Biology's Triage Logic to Clinical Decision Support
ideas.description: This idea adapts the 'triage' principle from conservation biology, where limited resources are focused on populations at the highest risk of irreversible loss, to the scaling of a Clinical Decision Support System (CDSS) for cancer screening. The analogous solution involves prioritizing intervention on species or ecosystems most at risk of extinction. Adapted to the research context, this means focusing the CDSS intervention only on clinics serving populations identified through a triage analysis as having the most critically low historical screening rates combined with high disease burden—the clinical equivalent of high extinction risk. The CDSS would be specifically calibrated for the socio-cultural and access barriers in these priority subpopulations, creating a targeted, resource-efficient implementation strategy aimed at preventing the most severe negative health outcomes.

ideas.title: Inverse problem: from health data to policy fingerprint
ideas.description: Infer the specific racist policies present by analyzing the pattern of health disparities.
ideas.sourceTitle: Racism, Not Race, Drives Inequity Across the COVID-19 Continuum.

ideas.title: Modeling the legacy of structural inequities on clinical decision-making during public health crises
ideas.description: This research investigates how the non-linear, threshold effects of historical policies (e.g., redlining, segregation, underinvestment) shape present-day clinical decision-making during public health emergencies like pandemics. It moves beyond analyzing patient-clinician demographic pairings in a vacuum by modeling how these interactions are conditioned and intensified by the legacy burden of structural inequities embedded in specific communities. The approach would use multi-level modeling to examine how historical policy thresholds interact with intersectional clinician-patient factors to produce extreme disparities in treatment access and outcomes during crises, identifying critical junctures where systemic historical factors amplify bias in clinical encounters.

ideas.title: Automated Bias Recognition and Feedback to Scale and Personalize Prevention in Multi-Organ Transplant Care
ideas.description: This study integrates a technology-driven bias recognition system into a large-scale, multi-organ transplant prevention trial. The system uses natural language processing and clinical data analysis to automatically detect potential cognitive biases in real-time clinician decision-making regarding infection prophylaxis for heart, lung, and kidney recipients. It then tests whether delivering immediate, contextualized feedback on these detected biases to clinicians is sufficient to improve adherence to and personalization of prevention protocols, without adding structured de-biasing training. This creates a novel experimental platform to simultaneously scale prevention research and directly investigate the causal pathway from automated bias recognition to mitigation in a high-stakes, heterogeneous clinical setting.

ideas.title: Multi-Level Causal Modeling of COVID-19 Disparities: Integrating Individual Risk and Neighborhood Context
ideas.description: This research moves beyond simple disparity mapping by constructing a causal modeling pipeline that disentangles individual from neighborhood-level drivers of COVID-19 outcomes. It links de-identified electronic health records or case surveillance data to tract-level structural variables, employing multi-level models to quantify the independent contribution of neighborhood context while controlling for individual demographics and comorbidities. The system outputs include maps of the predicted neighborhood-level residual risk (the contextual effect) and estimates of cross-level interactions, such as how the effect of poverty varies across different age groups. This approach provides actionable insights for targeting interventions to both high-risk places and the specific sub-populations within them most vulnerable to contextual factors.

ideas.title: Network-Structured Heterogeneity: Modeling Intra-Group Transmission Dynamics in Hispanic/Latinx Populations
ideas.description: This research fuses network theory with a critical challenge to monolithic risk grouping to develop a heterogeneous contact network model for disease or information transmission within Hispanic/Latinx populations. The model explicitly structures nodes and ties based on intra-group diversity factors such as nationality, immigration status (e.g., 1st vs. 2nd generation), and geographic context (e.g., established enclave vs. new destination), which influence mixing patterns, contact intensity, and resource access. This allows for the simulation of differential outbreak trajectories, peak timing, and intervention efficacy across subgroups that are masked in aggregate models. The result is a precision public health tool that identifies which structural facets of diversity most critically drive transmission disparities, enabling targeted, culturally congruent interventions.

ideas.title: Hispanic/Latinx population is a monolithic risk group
ideas.description: Challenge the assumption of uniform risk by analyzing intra-group diversity in nationality, immigration status, and regional context.
ideas.sourceTitle: COVID-19 Pandemic: Disparate Health Impact on the Hispanic/Latinx Population in the United States

ideas.title: Prescribing differences are not always inequities
ideas.description: Challenge the assumption that all prescribing variation is inequitable; some may reflect appropriate, patient-centered care.
ideas.sourceTitle: Health Equity and Antibiotic Prescribing in the United States: A Systematic Scoping Review.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Health Indices to Create a Pediatric Career Equity Index (PCEI)
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from ecology, where scientists created standardized composite indices (like the Ocean Health Index) to assess the complex, multi-dimensional health of ecosystems, this research adapts that solution to the problem of tracking gender equity in pediatrics. The analogous solution involved defining core 'goals' (e.g., biodiversity, livelihoods), identifying measurable indicators for each, and developing a framework to weight and combine them into a comparable index score. Adapted to this context, the research will: 1) Define core equity dimensions for pediatric careers (e.g., pipeline, promotion, pay parity, professional satisfaction). 2) For each dimension and career stage (trainee, early, mid, late, emeritus), identify and validate specific, abstract metrics that are generalizable across settings. 3) Develop a weighting and aggregation framework to combine these metrics into a single, standardized Pediatric Career Equity Index (PCEI) score. This will create a powerful, comparable tool for institutions and the field to benchmark progress, identify systemic bottlenecks, and evaluate the impact of equity interventions over time.

ideas.title: From Equal Outcomes to Equal Access: A Precision Ecology Framework for Prescribing
ideas.description: This idea inverts the goal from equalizing prescription rates to equalizing access to appropriate, guideline-concordant care. Drawing an analogy from Ecosystem Management, where success metrics evolved from equal species counts to ensuring each habitat could support its appropriate species mix via suitability models, we adapt this solution. The adapted research would develop 'clinical suitability models' that define appropriate, guideline-concordant care for specific patient-clinical context pairs. Equity would then be measured and managed by minimizing disparities in the gap between current care and this model-defined appropriate care, fundamentally reframing the assumption that equal prescribing rates are inherently equitable.

ideas.title: The Contextual Information Gap: How Diagnostic Uncertainty and Information Flow Dynamics Drive Inappropriate Antimicrobial Use
ideas.description: This research synthesizes the drivers of inappropriate use (time pressure, diagnostic uncertainty, habit) with the AMR-as-information-crisis frame to investigate a 'contextual information gap.' It posits that prescribing decisions are not driven by a lack of general knowledge, but by the failure of the right diagnostic information to arrive in a usable, actionable form at the critical decision point under real-world clinical constraints. The novel direction is to empirically map how information flow bottlenecks interact with specific contextual pressures to create predictable 'failure pathways' for appropriate prescribing. This enables the shift from broad education campaigns to the design of intelligent systems that adapt information delivery to the prescriber's immediate cognitive and workflow context.

ideas.title: Community-Governed Causal Modeling for Structural Vaccine Equity
ideas.description: This research establishes community governance boards with budgetary and methodological authority to guide a multi-phase study. First, community-based system dynamics groups map the local historical and structural drivers of health inequity and vaccine access. Second, these causal models are used to co-design and stress-test potential intervention models in simulation. Finally, only the community-endorsed models are implemented and evaluated, with success measured against community-defined metrics of structural change and autonomy, not just vaccination rates.

ideas.title: Geospatially-Informed Bias Mitigation Training for HAI Equity
ideas.description: This research develops and evaluates a novel training intervention for healthcare workers that integrates real-time geospatial mapping of HAI hotspots, hospital resource allocation, and community vulnerability. The maps serve as a core pedagogical tool to visually demonstrate the link between systemic inequities, implicit bias, and infection outcomes, making abstract biases concrete. Training then focuses on recognizing how these biases may manifest in local resource use and patient interactions, and teaches specific, map-informed mitigation actions to disrupt inequitable practices. This creates a closed-loop system where GIS data both reveals disparities and drives personalized bias mitigation, moving beyond general awareness to spatially-targeted behavioral change.

ideas.title: A Participatory Action Framework for Co-Designing and Validating Context-Sensitive Pediatric Equity Metrics
ideas.description: This project establishes a multi-institutional participatory action research (PAR) network to develop and validate pediatric career equity metrics. Instead of imposing top-down standardized measures, it forms local co-design teams of diverse pediatricians and leaders to first map structural and cultural barriers via narrative methods. These teams then iteratively create and pilot mixed-method metrics (quantitative and qualitative) that are sensitive to local context yet allow for cross-institutional comparison. The final validated metric sets are explicitly linked to mandated, resourced action plans, ensuring measurement is intrinsically connected to intervention and accountability.

ideas.title: Analyze cost-effectiveness of diversity interventions
ideas.description: Evaluate the resource requirements and financial implications of implementing holistic review processes.
ideas.sourceTitle: Interventions Associated With Racial and Ethnic Diversity in US Graduate Medical Education

ideas.title: Comparative Network Analysis of Vaccine Discourse and Decision-Making Across Racialized Social Ecologies
ideas.description: This research applies network science to comparatively map the structure of peer influence and information flow within both BIPOC communities and white majority groups regarding vaccines. By analyzing these parallel networks, it seeks to identify not only key influencers but also the distinct relational mechanisms—such as trust pathways, information gatekeeping, and normative reinforcement—that drive either high uptake or hesitancy. This dual-frame approach moves beyond cataloging demographic differences to model how social ecologies, shaped by historical inequities and contemporary structural factors, produce divergent public health outcomes. The resulting framework can inform tailored advocacy strategies that are ecologically valid for specific community networks while identifying transferable principles of effective science communication.

ideas.title: Modeling Biological Embedding of Social Inequity: A Causal Framework for COVID-19 Severity Disparities
ideas.description: This research employs a causal inference and systems epidemiology approach to rigorously test how social determinants of health (e.g., structural racism, economic deprivation, environmental stress) become biologically embedded to influence COVID-19 severity. It investigates specific, hypothesized physiological pathways (e.g., chronic inflammation, immune senescence, vascular dysfunction) and their potential molecular correlates as mediators linking social exposures to clinical outcomes. The design pre-registers social-contextual variables as primary causal factors, uses methods like Mendelian randomization and mediation analysis with sensitivity testing to guard against genetic determinism, and explicitly models intersectionality. The goal is to identify points for intervention within the biological consequences of social inequity, without reifying biological difference as a primary cause.

ideas.title: Multi-Level Resilience Audit: Disaggregating Protective Factors Within Low-Disparity Hispanic/Latinx Communities
ideas.description: This study first identifies Hispanic/Latinx communities with lower-than-predicted health or socioeconomic disparities using aggregate data. Its core innovation is a subsequent 'resilience audit' that disaggregates outcomes and potential protective factors within these communities using linked individual-level data. The analysis explicitly tests for heterogeneity by key intersecting social positions (e.g., immigration generation, income, Indigenous ancestry) to determine if aggregate community resilience masks internal inequities. The final output is a stratified map of resilience strategies, identifying which are broadly communal and which are specific to, or absent for, particular at-risk subgroups.

ideas.title: Community-Led Diagnostic and Adaptive Micro-Intervention System for Structural Health Equity
ideas.description: This research establishes a system where affected communities lead a deep diagnostic process to map the specific local structures and power dynamics causing COVID-19 disparities, moving beyond broad categories like 'access' or 'trust'. Based on this map, community-research teams co-design and implement a set of precise, targeted micro-interventions aimed at identified leverage points within healthcare, social, and economic systems. The impact of these micro-interventions is rigorously tested using adaptive, rapid-cycle trial designs that allow for continuous iteration and optimization based on community feedback and outcome data, ensuring the strategy is dynamically tailored to local realities and sustains community agency.

ideas.title: Inverse problem: Inferring protective factors from pathogen transmission network anomalies
ideas.description: This research direction develops an inverse modeling framework that analyzes observed patterns in pathogen transmission networks—such as unexpected breaks in chains, slowed propagation, or reduced cluster sizes—to infer the presence and combined efficacy of underlying protective factors. By applying tools from vaccine efficacy estimation to the problem of low AMR regions, the method can disentangle the effects of unmeasured or complex interventions, like antibiotic stewardship or infection control protocols, from natural epidemiological variation. This allows for the identification of synergistic policy and practice bundles that are actually effective in real-world, heterogeneous settings, rather than studying factors in isolation. The outcome is a data-driven approach to reverse-engineer optimal, context-specific public health strategies from successful outcomes evident in transmission data.

ideas.title: Testing a decentralized, peer-network model for antimicrobial stewardship inspired by open-source software development
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that effective antimicrobial stewardship requires centralized, formal programs by testing if decentralized, peer-led initiatives can be equally effective. Drawing an analogy from open-source software development—where complex projects like the Linux kernel are maintained successfully through decentralized, peer-led networks with modular design and community-driven review—the adapted idea proposes to test a decentralized stewardship model. Clinicians would form a peer network using a dedicated platform to collaboratively propose, debate, and implement local stewardship interventions, governed by clear protocols and reputation systems. The study would compare this network's outcomes (e.g., guideline adherence, antibiotic consumption, resistance rates) against those of traditional centralized programs to determine if a distributed, community-driven approach can achieve comparable stewardship effectiveness.

ideas.title: Participatory Agent-Based Modeling to Ground and Test Theories of Systemic Discrimination in Pandemic Health Outcomes
ideas.description: This research employs a participatory agent-based modeling (ABM) framework to rigorously investigate how structural discrimination generates health inequities during a pandemic. The core innovation is the embedded co-design process, where the model's rules for social structures, resource allocation, and agent interactions are derived and validated through collaborative workshops with subject matter experts and community partners. This ensures the simulation mechanisms are empirically grounded and reflect complex, real-world dynamics of power and exclusion. The resulting model serves as a computational testbed for stakeholders to evaluate the potential equity impacts of different policy choices before implementation, transforming the ABM from a closed predictive tool into an open platform for structured, evidence-based dialogue on systemic change.

ideas.title: A Multi-Phase Hybrid Trial to Assess Robustness, Scalability, and Sustainability of the Outpatient Stewardship Framework
ideas.description: This research employs a phased, pragmatic approach to move beyond simple efficacy testing. First, a feasibility and acceptability study refines the framework in a small set of clinics. Second, a stepped-wedge cluster randomized trial in a diverse network of community practices measures comparative effectiveness and identifies contextual moderators. The final phase is a large-scale Type 2 hybrid implementation-effectiveness trial designed explicitly to test scalability and sustainability, measuring framework adaptation, cost, long-term clinician adherence, and patient outcomes across a stratified sample of high-, medium-, and low-resource primary care settings.

ideas.title: Scale down to clinician-patient dyad analysis
ideas.description: Study prescribing decisions at the individual clinician-patient interaction level using audio recordings or simulations.
ideas.sourceTitle: Health Equity and Antibiotic Prescribing in the United States: A Systematic Scoping Review.

ideas.title: Causal Disentanglement: Robustly Identifying Clinician and System Factors in Prescription Disparities
ideas.description: This research develops a causal inference framework to rigorously separate latent clinician or system factors from patient-level clinical heterogeneity in observed prescription patterns. It leverages quasi-experimental variation, such as differential clinician exposure to training or changes in formulary policies, to create natural experiments that help identify bias-related signals. The approach explicitly quantifies the uncertainty and model dependence of any inferred bias through extensive sensitivity analyses and partial identification techniques. The output is a robust, bounded estimate of systemic factors contributing to disparities, moving beyond mere correlation to support more defensible interventions.

ideas.title: Operationalizing EDI to manage political attention scarcity in concurrent health crises
ideas.description: This research tests the hypothesis that implementing the six-step EDI cycle within health governance and advocacy institutions can strategically overcome political attention scarcity during concurrent crises. By systematically identifying, engaging, and amplifying the voices of communities disproportionately affected by both antimicrobial resistance (AMR) and pandemic threats, the EDI process generates compelling, equity-centered narratives. This reframes concurrent crises not as competing priorities, but as interconnected challenges requiring integrated policy responses, thereby making them harder for political systems to ignore. The impact is measured both in terms of institutional EDI outcomes and shifts in policy attention and resource allocation for concurrent health threats.

ideas.title: Questioning if all four gatekeeper groups are equally critical
ideas.description: Test the relative influence and necessary sequencing of interventions across the four identified gatekeeper groups.
ideas.sourceTitle: Women in Pediatrics: Progress, Barriers, and Opportunities for Equity, Diversity, and Inclusion

ideas.title: Targeted Interventions for Systemic Bias: A Systems Biology Approach to Policy Design
ideas.description: This research inverts the assumption that race-neutral policies can effectively address racism-driven inequities by drawing an analogy from systems biology and precision medicine. In that field, the solution to complex diseases moved from non-specific, broadly toxic treatments to therapies that map and target the specific dysregulated biological pathways causing the disease. Analogously, this project proposes that effective 'colorblind' policy must move beyond broad, one-size-fits-all interventions. Instead, it should employ a 'precision policy' approach: first, rigorously map the specific, historically-rooted socio-economic and institutional 'pathways' that generate and perpetuate racial disparities in a given domain (e.g., the precise appraisal algorithms, zoning legacies, or wealth-accumulation mechanisms). Then, design policy instruments that are formally race-neutral but are precisely calibrated to disrupt those identified pathways, using highly specific, causal proxies (e.g., targeting neighborhoods based on historical redlining maps and subsequent disinvestment metrics, rather than current racial demographics). The outcome is a framework for developing structurally targeted, yet legally colorblind, policy solutions.

ideas.title: Analyze effectiveness of public health messaging
ideas.description: Evaluate how culturally tailored communication influences preventive behaviors and vaccine uptake in Hispanic/Latinx communities.
ideas.sourceTitle: COVID-19 Pandemic: Disparate Health Impact on the Hispanic/Latinx Population in the United States

ideas.title: Counteracting Bias Through Structured Interaction Protocols
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from experimental psychology—specifically the structured awareness and feedback mechanisms of Implicit Association Testing (IAT)—to mitigate patient unconscious bias towards clinicians from underrepresented groups. The analogous problem of measuring and reducing implicit social biases in lab settings was addressed by creating controlled tasks that expose and challenge automatic associations. The adapted idea involves designing and evaluating brief, pre-consultation digital interventions for patients. These modules would use reaction-time tasks and feedback to positively link clinician competence with diverse identities, aiming to disrupt biased cognitive schemas before the clinical interaction, thereby scaling a dual-sided solution that addresses patient bias directly to improve equity in care delivery.

ideas.title: Invert focus from resident disclosure to program solicitation
ideas.description: Study programs that proactively offer accommodations without requiring disclosure.
ideas.sourceTitle: Barriers to Disclosure of Disability and Request for Accommodations Among First-Year Resident Physicians in the US

ideas.title: An equity-adaptive digital epidemiology system for generalizable population health insights
ideas.description: This system is designed to overcome the inherent selection bias of purely passive digital data collection by integrating multiple, inclusive data streams from its inception. It employs a dynamic sampling framework that monitors real-time dataset composition against census-level benchmarks, automatically activating equitable recruitment strategies and alternative low-tech data channels when representation falters. The core analytics engine is explicitly trained and validated on these intentionally diverse data, weighting and adjusting for residual biases to produce models that are robust across subpopulations. The result is a digital epidemiology platform whose insights are both timely and generalizable, actively mitigating rather than amplifying existing health disparities.

ideas.title: Assess cost-effectiveness of outpatient stewardship programs
ideas.description: Conduct economic analyses to determine the financial impact of implementing outpatient stewardship.
ideas.sourceTitle: Antimicrobial stewardship in the outpatient setting: A review and proposed framework

ideas.title: From Information to Action: Closing the Loop Between AMR Diagnosis and Feasible Treatment
ideas.description: This approach reframes the AMR crisis not just as an information failure but as a systemic failure to convert diagnostic information into a viable patient outcome. It proposes a research and implementation system that couples rapid diagnostic data with a dynamic, locality-specific registry of antimicrobial access, cost, and stewardship rules. The system's core function is to identify and alert when a diagnostic result reveals a critical gap between known resistance and locally available treatment options, thereby directing policy and supply chain interventions to the most urgent points of failure. This creates a closed loop where information flow is designed to trigger concrete system-level responses, not just clinical decisions.

ideas.title: Applying Pharmacokinetic Modeling to Personalize and Destigmatize Disability Accommodation Interventions
ideas.description: This research synthesizes concepts from pharmacometrics and disability stigma reduction to develop a predictive, model-based framework for anti-bias training. Just as PK/PD modeling predicts drug concentration and effect over time, this approach would model the 'exposure-response' relationship of different training interventions (e.g., duration, modality, content) on measurable outcomes like implicit bias reduction and supportive behavior change in clinical trainees. The goal is to create optimized, personalized training regimens that account for individual learner 'phenotypes' (e.g., baseline attitudes, clinical role) to maximize and sustain the therapeutic effect of reducing bias, thereby fostering environments where disability disclosure is met with competent, supportive care. This transforms stigma reduction from a qualitative educational effort into a quantitative, engineering-informed behavioral intervention science.

ideas.title: Policy-Sensitive Intervention Design for Knowledge Gatekeepers
ideas.description: This research develops and evaluates strategic interventions for academic centers, societies, journals, and funding agencies that are specifically designed to shape the implementation of national stewardship policies. It integrates computational modeling to predict how different policy pathways, influenced by these gatekeepers, will differentially affect researcher subpopulations and equity outcomes. The core innovation is using these equity impact forecasts to iteratively redesign the gatekeeper interventions before they are deployed, creating a proactive, evidence-based framework for aligning institutional actions with equitable national policy goals.

ideas.title: Applying Population Genetics Methods to Decouple Genetic Ancestry from Socioeconomic Variables in COVID-19 Outcomes
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that disparities are solely due to structural factors by proposing to investigate potential genetic or biological contributors to COVID-19 outcomes. Using an analogy from population genetics, the study adapts methods used to disentangle genetic ancestry from environmental confounders in complex trait analysis. The analogous solution involves using genetic data to control for population structure to find true genetic associations. Adapted to the COVID-19 context, the research would recruit a diverse cohort, genotype participants for ancestry-informative markers, and collect detailed data on structural factors (e.g., zip code-based exposure risk, occupation, healthcare access). Statistical models would then test for an independent association between genetic ancestry (modeled as principal components) and disease severity or mortality, after rigorously controlling for the structural covariates. This design directly addresses the core challenge by attempting to isolate a potential genetic signal from the overwhelmingly correlated structural variables that define racial disparities.

ideas.title: Adapting Conservation Biology's 'Effective Population Size' to Measure Screening Program Success
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that screening program success is best measured by test order volume. Drawing an analogy from conservation biology, where the key metric shifted from animals processed to 'effective population size' (genetic contributors), we propose adapting this solution to clinical screening. The adapted research idea is to develop and validate a new primary outcome metric called 'Effective Screening Yield.' This patient-centered composite metric would not simply count screenings performed, but would weight each screened individual by their progression through subsequent, crucial steps: definitive diagnosis, linkage to appropriate care, and initiation of evidence-based treatment. This refocuses evaluation on the meaningful pathway to improved health outcomes, incentivizing system improvements that ensure screening translates into actual patient benefit, just as conservation biology incentivizes practices that ensure released animals successfully reproduce.

ideas.title: Longitudinal Value Modeling of Holistic Admissions: Integrating Fiscal Cost with Institutional and Societal Return
ideas.description: This research moves beyond simple cost accounting to develop a comprehensive value model for holistic review processes. It integrates traditional financial cost data with longitudinal quantitative metrics of institutional and societal benefit, such as research productivity, graduation equity, and post-graduation success of cohorts. The study employs comparative case studies and quasi-experimental designs to isolate the impact of these interventions from other factors. The final output is a dynamic evaluation framework that allows institutions to project the multi-dimensional return on investment of diversity-focused admissions reforms over extended timeframes.

ideas.title: Adapt Aviation Safety Reporting Systems to Measure Psychological Safety in Residency Programs
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from Aviation Safety and Human Factors, specifically the use of confidential, non-punitive safety reporting systems (e.g., NASA's ASRS) designed to capture near-misses and system flaws by removing fear of reprisal. The analogous solution is adapted by creating a 'Residency Psychological Safety Reporting System' for medical education. This system would collect anonymous, narrative reports from residents about specific instances where psychological safety was lacking (e.g., fear of asking a question, admitting an error, or challenging a superior). By analyzing these de-identified reports, program leadership can identify recurring patterns, high-risk situations, and specific systemic or interpersonal factors that validated survey scales might not capture, enabling more targeted and effective interventions to improve the training climate.

ideas.title: Community-Driven Environmental Justice Mapping for Pandemic Response
ideas.description: This research employs a community-based participatory research (CBPR) framework to co-produce environmental justice-style exposure maps for COVID-19 and future pandemic risks. Community members are engaged as co-researchers in defining relevant risk layers (e.g., air pollution, crowded housing, transit access, essential worker density), gathering local data, and interpreting GIS model outputs. This process generates maps that reflect lived experience and local knowledge, moving beyond standard administrative data. The resulting community-validated spatial models then serve as a foundational tool for co-designing and prioritizing place-based health interventions that address the root environmental and social drivers of inequitable disease burden.

ideas.title: Adapting Contact Hypothesis Interventions from Intergroup Relations to Reduce Disability Stigma
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from social psychology's intergroup relations research, specifically the Contact Hypothesis, this idea adapts proven prejudice-reduction frameworks to the context of disability stigma in medical residency. The analogous solution involves creating structured, cooperative contact under optimal conditions to reduce bias. The adapted research idea involves designing and assessing a training program for medical residents that moves beyond passive awareness lectures to implement active, 'Jigsaw Classroom'-style cooperative learning modules. In these modules, disabled and non-disabled residents would work as interdependent teams on clinical simulations, fostering equal-status interaction toward a common goal. The study would rigorously evaluate this intervention's efficacy in reducing both implicit and explicit bias, thereby fostering a more supportive environment for disability disclosure in medical training programs.

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Niche Theory to Compare Disease Disparity Patterns
ideas.description: This research idea challenges the assumption that observed health disparity patterns during the COVID-19 pandemic are identical to those for other infectious diseases like influenza or HIV. Drawing an analogy from community ecology—specifically the solution to determining if two species occupy identical ecological niches—the adapted approach applies Hutchinson's n-dimensional hypervolume concept. In ecology, researchers compare species across multiple resource axes using multivariate statistics to quantify niche overlap. Adapted here, we define a 'disparity hypervolume' for each disease across axes such as demographic, socioeconomic, geographic, and clinical risk factors. The core method involves using principal component analysis (PCA) or discriminant analysis on large-scale surveillance and cohort data to formally test for statistically significant differences in the multidimensional structure and magnitude of disparities, moving beyond anecdotal or single-variable comparisons to a rigorous, quantitative assessment of whether COVID-19's disparity signature is unique.

ideas.title: Primary outcome should be screening rate
ideas.description: Shift focus to patient-centered outcomes like linkage to care or diagnosis rates, not just test orders.
ideas.sourceTitle: Assessment and improvement of HIV screening rates in a Midwest primary care practice using an electronic clinical decision support system: a quality improvement study

ideas.title: Adapting the 'Planetary Protection Protocol' Validation Model for Outpatient Stewardship
ideas.description: This idea adapts the validation model for planetary protection protocols from space science to empirically evaluate a novel outpatient stewardship framework. In planetary science, protocols to prevent biological contamination are validated through a staged process: laboratory simulation followed by field testing in Earth-based analog environments (e.g., extreme deserts) before spaceflight. Adapted for primary care research, this involves a two-phase empirical evaluation. First, the framework is tested in a controlled, simulated 'digital twin' of a primary care setting. Second, it is deployed in a real-world 'clinical analog environment'—a carefully chosen pilot clinic that serves as a high-fidelity testbed mirroring the intended setting's complexities. This tiered approach allows for iterative refinement and de-risking of the framework before a broader, more costly multi-site implementation study.

ideas.title: Context-Generalizable Pilots via Multi-Site, Heterogeneous Program Testing
ideas.description: This approach tests a single intervention across a small, strategically diverse set of programs rather than just one. Researchers select pilot sites to maximize variation on key contextual factors believed to influence implementation, such as program size, institutional resources, or faculty governance culture. Using a harmonized evaluation protocol, the study measures both outcomes and contextual variables at each site. This design explicitly tests for generalizability, identifies boundary conditions for the intervention's effectiveness, and generates an implementation playbook tailored to different program contexts before any system-wide rollout.

ideas.title: Scale to non-pandemic chronic disease disparities
ideas.description: Apply the same structural inequity framework to analyze disparities in diabetes or hypertension outcomes.
ideas.sourceTitle: Racial Disparity of Coronavirus Disease 2019 in African American Communities

ideas.title: Apply Mendelian Randomization from Genetic Epidemiology to Identify Causal Drivers of Prescribing Disparities
ideas.description: This idea adapts the core methodological solution from genetic epidemiology—Mendelian Randomization (MR)—to the problem of identifying causal drivers of prescribing disparities. In MR, genetic variants serve as instrumental variables to establish causality from observational data by mimicking randomization. The adapted research idea proposes using quasi-randomly assigned, genetically-influenced patient biomarkers (e.g., metabolomic signatures or polygenic risk scores for specific traits) as novel instrumental variables in an econometric analysis of prescribing data. This approach can isolate the causal impact of a patient's biological characteristics on treatment decisions, helping to distinguish true clinical drivers of disparities from the effects of confounding social or systemic factors, thereby offering a more rigorous test for biological bias in clinical decision-making.

ideas.title: Institutions must lead trust-building efforts
ideas.description: Invert the assumption that healthcare institutions are the primary agents for rebuilding trust in BIPOC communities.
ideas.sourceTitle: Addressing and Inspiring Vaccine Confidence in Black, Indigenous, and People of Color During the Coronavirus Disease 2019 Pandemic

ideas.title: Longitudinal Equity Process Control for the Medical Education Pipeline
ideas.description: This research applies industrial statistical process control (SPC) methods to create a longitudinal monitoring system for equity metrics across the entire medical education pipeline, from pre-medical programs through graduate medical education. By integrating SPC charts with linked, pipeline-wide data, the system can distinguish between normal variation and statistically significant regressions in diversity outcomes at each critical transition point. This allows institutions and accrediting bodies to move from reactive, siloed interventions to a proactive, systems-engineering approach, dynamically allocating resources to shore up the pipeline where it is most vulnerable to equity losses.

ideas.title: Equity-Efficient Screening: Optimizing Pandemic Surveillance Under Non-Linear Historical Legacies
ideas.description: This research investigates the optimal design of pandemic screening programs by integrating the efficiency question of universal versus targeted screening with a sophisticated model of how historical policies (e.g., redlining, underinvestment) create non-linear, threshold-based vulnerabilities in present-day populations. It moves beyond comparing static efficiency to dynamically model scenarios where targeted screening, based on contemporary risk factors, catastrophically fails because it misses sub-populations pushed past a vulnerability threshold by compounding historical inequities. The outcome is a decision-support model that identifies the conditions—defined by pathogen characteristics and the geometry of historical inequity—under which universal screening is not just a moral imperative for equity but a strict necessity for effective pandemic containment.

ideas.title: A Dedicated AMR Coordination Hub with Primary Care Sentinel Integration
ideas.description: This research tests the hypothesis that a specialized, centralized AMR Coordination Hub, supported by a sentinel network of primary care practices, is more effective than diffuse integration. The Hub would provide 24/7 expert consultation, manage regional antibiogram data, coordinate rapid diagnostic support, and implement stewardship protocols, while primary care acts as a crucial early detection and reporting layer. The study would compare outcomes—like appropriate prescribing rates, time to effective therapy, and resistance trend detection—between this hub-and-spoke model and a fully integrated model.

ideas.title: Longitudinal Ecological Succession Model of Disability Disclosure and Accommodation in Residency
ideas.description: This study adapts the methodology of longitudinal ecological succession research to track disability disclosure and accommodation requests across medical residency. Just as ecologists monitor how plant and animal communities change in a landscape over time in response to environmental factors, this research would treat the residency training environment as a dynamic habitat. It would track the 'colonization' (initial disclosure), 'establishment' (formal request), and 'persistence' (ongoing use) of accommodations from intern year through graduation. Using repeated, standardized surveys and institutional data, the study would model how different 'environmental stressors'—such as rotation type, workload, and institutional culture—influence the rates and success of these processes, identifying critical windows for intervention to support resident success and program resilience.

ideas.title: Optimizing Holistic Review Intervention Cocktails via Factorial Design and Response Surface Methodology
ideas.description: This research adapts solution methods from pharmaceutical drug development, specifically factorial design and Response Surface Methodology (RSM), to the problem of optimizing combinations of holistic review and complementary admissions strategies. In the analogous field, these techniques are used to find the most effective and synergistic multi-drug cocktails by systematically varying component doses and modeling the outcome landscape. The adapted idea applies this framework to educational interventions, treating elements like holistic review rubric design, companion outreach programs, and policy changes as combinable factors. By constructing designed experiments and modeling outcomes such as diversity metrics and student performance, the research will identify the specific combinations and 'dosages' of strategies that produce the optimal results, moving beyond simple A/B testing to a sophisticated optimization of the entire intervention system.

ideas.title: Pilot-to-Public: Embedding Real-Time Equity Data Transparency in Small-Scale Program Pilots
ideas.description: This research proposes embedding a transparent, public reporting system directly into the design of a small-scale pilot study for an equity intervention, such as committee training. The pilot program would not only test the intervention's efficacy but also serve as a live testbed for developing and refining the data collection, analysis, and public reporting mechanisms required for system-wide accountability. This approach allows researchers to identify and solve practical challenges in data transparency—like privacy, metric validity, and stakeholder communication—in a low-stakes environment before scaling. The outcome is a co-validated package: an evidence-based intervention paired with a proven, piloted reporting framework ready for broader institutional adoption.

ideas.title: Disentangling Integration from Placement: A Three-Arm Trial of Novel, Standard, and Redesigned HIV Alert Systems
ideas.description: This study challenges the assumption that poor performance of CDSS alerts is due solely to suboptimal placement within an existing tool. It tests whether the benefits of a standalone prompt are due to its novelty or its placement by comparing three interventions: a standard integrated alert, a novel standalone prompt, and a redesigned, deeply integrated alert within the existing system. The primary outcome is clinician adherence to the screening recommendation, with secondary outcomes measuring workflow disruption and alert fatigue. This design isolates the specific effects of alert placement, integration depth, and intervention novelty on effectiveness.

ideas.title: Causal Triangulation and Robustness Testing for Policy Impact on Healthcare Disparities
ideas.description: This research employs a multi-method causal inference framework to rigorously estimate the impact of payment reforms on healthcare-associated infection (HAI) disparities. It moves beyond reliance on a single method by simultaneously applying difference-in-differences, instrumental variable, and synthetic control designs to the same policy evaluation. The core innovation is a formal causal triangulation and robustness testing protocol, which assesses the consistency of estimates across methods, diagnoses assumption violations, and uses ensemble techniques to derive a more credible and assumption-robust causal conclusion regarding the policy's effect on equity.

ideas.title: Implement blinded peer review from academia
ideas.description: Apply double-blind review processes to clinical case conferences and promotion committees.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Impact of Unconscious Bias in Healthcare: How to Recognize and Mitigate It.

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Succession Theory to Assess Structural Equity in Pediatrics
ideas.description: This research seed uses an analogy from ecology to challenge the assumption that a female-majority workforce in pediatrics inherently creates equity. Drawing from the field's move beyond the 'diversity-stability hypothesis,' where surface-level species counts were found to mask unequal resource flows and historical constraints, this study proposes a similar structural analysis. It adapts the ecological solution by investigating pediatrics as a professional ecosystem, mapping the distribution of key resources (leadership roles, sub-specialty prestige, research funding, compensation) and analyzing historical policy legacies to determine if persistent structural biases exist beneath the demographic majority. The aim is to test whether equitable outcomes in influence, pay, and advancement have genuinely followed the demographic shift, or if the workforce composition masks a deeper, unchallenged inequity in the specialty's foundational structures.

ideas.title: Compare universal prophylaxis vs pre-emptive therapy in D+R-
ideas.description: Complete and analyze the randomized trial comparing the two main prevention strategies in high-risk liver transplant recipients.
ideas.sourceTitle: Cytomegalovirus infection in liver transplant recipients: updates on clinical management.

ideas.title: The Placebo Protocol: Using Expectation Management and Positive Rituals to Satisfy Patients Without Antibiotics
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from placebo effect research in psychology and neuroscience, where robust clinical outcomes are achieved without active drugs through expectation management. The analogous solution involves using authoritative communication, therapeutic ritual, and a plausible treatment narrative to satisfy the patient's need for care. Adapted to the antibiotic stewardship context, the research would develop and test structured communication protocols that replace the expectation of a pill with a compelling, positive clinical experience. This includes scripts that authoritatively explain why antibiotics are not needed, coupled with a definitive 'prescription' for a non-drug treatment ritual (e.g., a specific saline rinse, hydration schedule, or monitoring plan), designed to meet the patient's psychological need for a tangible intervention and thereby increase satisfaction with the visit outcome.

ideas.title: Trust as Earned Outcome: A Framework for Mandatory Institutional Pre-Certification Before Community Engagement
ideas.description: This research rejects the premise that institutions can lead trust-building while remaining unexamined. It designs a 'pre-certification' model where healthcare institutions must first pass a community-co-designed audit of concrete, structural reforms. The system measures tangible changes in power, policy, and resource allocation—not sentiment or communication—as the prerequisite for engagement. This shifts the focus from performative outreach to mandatory institutional transformation, making trust a potential consequence of demonstrated accountability, rather than a promotional objective.

ideas.title: Transfer behavioral nudges from public health campaigns
ideas.description: Apply techniques from vaccination or smoking cessation to promote antibiotic stewardship.
ideas.sourceTitle: Confronting antimicrobial resistance beyond the COVID-19 pandemic and the 2020 US election

ideas.title: Equity-constrained ML for risk prediction to ensure fair allocation of screening alerts
ideas.description: This research develops a machine learning system for screening prioritization that explicitly optimizes for both predictive performance and fairness. The core innovation is a multi-objective optimization framework that actively constrains disparity metrics across protected demographic groups, preventing the amplification of existing biases. It integrates causal adjustment for access-related confounders and employs adversarial neural networks to learn risk representations that are invariant to sensitive attributes. The final system outputs prioritized alerts that are both clinically effective and equitably distributed, validated across diverse subpopulations.

ideas.title: Apply community-based participatory research frameworks
ideas.description: Employ CBPR principles to co-design and implement interventions with Hispanic/Latinx communities, not just for them.
ideas.sourceTitle: COVID-19 Pandemic: Disparate Health Impact on the Hispanic/Latinx Population in the United States

ideas.title: Apply epidemiological outbreak detection to equity intervention monitoring
ideas.description: This idea adapts solutions from the field of Public Health Epidemiology, specifically statistical outbreak detection systems used for disease surveillance (e.g., EARS methods like CUSUM). In epidemiology, these tools continuously analyze health data streams to identify unusual patterns signaling potential outbreaks. The adapted research idea applies these algorithmic approaches to equity data. Instead of detecting disease clusters, the system would be configured to detect 'clusters of inequity'—such as unexpected localized regressions in hiring diversity, spikes in attrition within specific demographic groups, or emerging geographic or departmental disparities in promotion rates. This enables proactive, targeted intervention by shifting equity monitoring from passive tracking of organization-wide averages to an active surveillance system that hunts for and flags nascent problems before they become widespread.

ideas.title: Hybrid Cognitive Support: Testing Education Augmented by On-Demand Decision Support
ideas.description: This study evaluates whether a combined intervention of targeted provider education and an accessible, low-burden clinical decision support system (CDSS) can sustainably improve practice without relying on interruptive alerts. The education component focuses on underlying principles and guideline rationale, while the parallel CDSS provides a quick-reference, patient-specific checklist or calculator that providers can choose to consult during patient encounters. The primary outcome is adherence to the desired clinical practice, with secondary analyses separating the effects of education alone from voluntary CDSS use, thereby identifying the complementary roles of knowledge retention and just-in-time cognitive aid.

ideas.title: Institutional Policy Forensics: Using Disparity Data to Audit and Assign Accountability
ideas.description: This research inverts the trust-building paradigm by proposing that healthcare institutions must first undergo a forensic audit using their own patient data. The method applies inverse problem-solving to health disparity patterns to infer the specific racist policies—both historical and contemporary—that created them, then explicitly maps those policies to the institutions responsible for their enactment or perpetuation. This generates a concrete, evidence-based 'accountability profile' for each institution, moving beyond generic apologies to specific, actionable redress. The core innovation is that the institution-led trust-building process is initiated and structured by this objective, data-driven assignment of historical and policy responsibility.

ideas.title: Cost-effectiveness of CDSS interventions accounting for clinician defensive reactivity to bias mitigation
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel cost-effectiveness analysis framework for CDSS interventions (like HIV screening prompts) that explicitly incorporates the potential for the intervention's bias-mitigation logic to induce clinician defensiveness. Instead of assuming a direct, positive link between awareness and behavior, the model would quantify how defensive reactions—such as alert overrides, workarounds, or reduced engagement—erode the intervention's clinical effectiveness and increase downstream costs (e.g., missed diagnoses, subsequent care). The result is a more realistic economic evaluation that treats clinician psychological response as a critical moderating variable, identifying intervention designs and implementation strategies that maximize cost-effectiveness by minimizing defensive backlash.

ideas.title: The Signal-to-Noise Framework for Prescribing Variation: Distinguishing Clinical Rationale from Systemic Bias
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that all prescribing variation is inequitable by adapting a solution from astrophysics/cosmology. In cosmology, scientists use component separation techniques to distinguish the fundamental Cosmic Microwave Background signal from foreground contamination. Analogously, we propose developing a 'signal-to-noise' framework for prescribing data. The 'signal' represents variation justified by patient-centered factors (comorbidity, genetics, informed preference, treatment response), while the 'noise' represents variation driven by inequities (bias, unequal access, non-clinical physician characteristics).
