By creating and validating statistical filters and decomposition algorithms, this research aims to provide a nuanced tool that can identify true inequities for intervention while preserving and understanding the appropriate, individualized variation that constitutes high-quality care.

ideas.title: Scale down to micro-aggressions in clinical encounters
ideas.description: Study how routine discriminatory interactions affect individual patient outcomes during COVID-19 care.
ideas.sourceTitle: Racism, Not Race, Drives Inequity Across the COVID-19 Continuum.

ideas.title: Dual problem: excess resource use from inequities
ideas.description: Calculate the excess healthcare costs and bed-days attributable to racial/ethnic disparities in HAIs.
ideas.sourceTitle: Racial/Ethnic Inequities in Healthcare-associated Infections Under the Shadow of Structural Racism: Narrative Review and Call to Action

ideas.title: Stewardship requires centralized, formal programs
ideas.description: Test if decentralized, peer-led or individual clinician initiatives can be equally effective.
ideas.sourceTitle: Antimicrobial stewardship in the outpatient setting: A review and proposed framework

ideas.title: Scale down to unit-level equity dashboards
ideas.description: Develop real-time, unit-level dashboards tracking HAI rates and adherence stratified by patient race/ethnicity for immediate feedback.
ideas.sourceTitle: Racial/Ethnic Inequities in Healthcare-associated Infections Under the Shadow of Structural Racism: Narrative Review and Call to Action

ideas.title: Disentangling Amplification from Emergence: Mapping Novel Disadvantage Pathways Forged by Pandemic-Predisposition Interactions
ideas.description: This research moves beyond a binary test to map the novel causal architecture of pandemic disadvantage. It first catalogs the unique shocks (e.g., lockdowns, school closures, specific aid policies) and then employs longitudinal data with causal mediation analysis to decompose effects into pure amplification, pure novel shock, and—critically—interaction effects. Concurrent qualitative work traces the mechanisms behind significant interaction terms, identifying and validating new pathways (e.g., how pre-existing digital divides interacted with remote work policies to create unique career penalties) that are irreducible to either pre-existing disparities or the pandemic context alone.

ideas.title: Mitigating Diagnostic Test Bias in Infectious Diseases via Calibration Transfer from Computer Vision
ideas.description: This research seed expands via analogy to computer vision's domain adaptation solutions. The analogous problem is model performance bias when machine learning models for image recognition are deployed in new environments with different data characteristics (e.g., new lighting or camera sensors), solved through calibration transfer techniques using small, targeted validation sets. The adapted idea is to empirically evaluate calibration transfer as a test bias mitigation strategy in infectious diseases. Specifically, the research would assess whether using a small, diverse sample from a new clinical or training setting to recalibrate diagnostic algorithms (for diseases like malaria, tuberculosis, or sepsis) can effectively correct for systematic performance biases across different patient demographics or healthcare environments. The study would measure the strategy's effectiveness by comparing diagnostic accuracy, fairness, and equity metrics before and after the calibration intervention in both simulated data and real-world clinical pilot studies.

ideas.title: Inferring Structural and Vaccine-Driven Health Inequities from Transmission Dynamics Using Agent-Based Models
ideas.description: This research develops an agent-based modeling (ABM) platform where an artificial society with programmable discriminatory rules (e.g., in healthcare access, occupational risk, or mobility) experiences a simulated pandemic. The core innovation is an inverse problem methodology applied to the ABM's output transmission chains: by fitting the simulated outbreak data to real-world observed transmission patterns, the model jointly infers the most likely values for both the vaccine's biological efficacy against infection and the strength of the social-structural inequities shaping transmission. This allows researchers to quantify how much of observed disparities in infection rates are attributable to vaccine performance versus systemic bias, providing a powerful tool for equitable vaccine evaluation and policy design.

ideas.title: Adaptive Institutional Architecture: Continuous Feedback Loops for Bias Mitigation
ideas.description: This approach redesigns hospital systems as learning environments that actively detect and respond to bias. It integrates unobtrusive sensors and routine data collection (e.g., anonymized patient flow, decision timestamps, resource allocation) with a secure, non-punitive staff reporting mechanism for potential bias incidents. A multidisciplinary team, including clinicians, data scientists, and architects, analyzes this aggregated data to identify disparity patterns and pilots targeted, reversible modifications to policy or physical layout. The impact of each intervention is measured against pre-defined equity metrics, creating a cycle of continuous refinement where only proven-effective changes become permanent.

ideas.title: Attributable Excess Burden: Adapting Environmental Impact Accounting to Quantify Disparity-Driven Healthcare Waste
ideas.description: This research adapts the attributional modeling approach from Industrial Ecology's Life Cycle Assessment to quantify the excess resource burden of healthcare disparities. In LCA, the total environmental impact of a system is partitioned and attributed to specific inputs or processes. Analogously, we propose to treat the healthcare system as an industrial process with an output of 'resource consumption' (costs, bed-days). The method involves establishing an equitable benchmark for Hospital-Acquired Infection (HAI) rates and associated resource use, then using population-attributable fraction calculations within a counterfactual modeling framework to allocate the 'excess' national healthcare costs and bed-days specifically to racial/ethnic inequities in HAI incidence and outcomes. This provides a rigorous, systems-based accounting of the wasteful resource drain caused by disparities, framing it as an inefficiency to be eliminated.

ideas.title: Applying Opt-Out Architecture from Organ Donation Systems to Increase HIV Screening
ideas.description: This idea applies an analogous solution from public health policy—specifically organ donation registration systems—to the problem of increasing HIV screening rates. In several European countries, 'presumed consent' or 'opt-out' policies have successfully increased organ donor registration by making donation the default choice, requiring active refusal. We adapt this by designing the EHR's clinical decision support to set HIV screening as the default action for all eligible patients during applicable visits (e.g., annual physicals, new patient intake). The system would automatically generate a screening order, and the clinician must actively dismiss it with a documented reason (e.g., patient refusal, recent test). This shifts the cognitive and procedural burden from initiating a test to justifying its omission, leveraging the power of defaults within the digital choice architecture to nudge behavior toward higher screening compliance.

ideas.title: Calibrated Dual Optimization: Jointly Maximizing Calibrated Merit and Diversity
ideas.description: This system addresses the inherent bias in predictive metrics by integrating an audit-and-calibration loop into the selection process. It begins by quantifying the subgroup-specific performance and potential bias of the initial 'clinical success' predictor. The core optimization then selects a cohort that maximizes a fairness-corrected aggregate success score, subject to a diversity constraint, while simultaneously using the selection data to iteratively recalibrate the predictive model. This creates a robust, self-improving system where the definition of merit becomes more equitable through use, directly breaking the feedback loop of historical bias.

ideas.title: Adapting Conservation Biology's Adaptive Management for Equity-Focused HAI Bundle Implementation
ideas.description: This idea applies an analogical solution from conservation biology to the challenge of equity-focused implementation of HAI prevention bundles. In conservation, 'Adaptive Management' frameworks are used to implement complex ecological interventions in landscapes with historical social inequities, relying on structured stakeholder engagement and iterative learning based on dual ecological and social metrics. The adapted research idea transplants this approach by establishing a participatory, co-design process for HAI bundle rollout that explicitly maps and addresses systemic inequities in the healthcare setting. The core of the method is to treat the implementation strategy itself as a testable intervention, using rapid-cycle data on both clinical outcomes (HAI rates) and equity outcomes (disparity indices) to continuously adapt and improve the implementation process, ensuring it reduces rather than exacerbates health inequities.

ideas.title: Bias-Audited, Competency-Based Scoring for Anonymized GME Applications
ideas.description: This system moves beyond simple anonymization by implementing a two-stage screening process for initial GME reviews. First, applications are anonymized and evaluated using a standardized, evidence-based competency rubric focused on specific clinical, academic, and professional skills. Second, the anonymized text and data are analyzed by an algorithm trained to detect and discount the influence of privileged-opportunity signals, such as certain institution names or high-cost experiences, that can bias human reviewers even without explicit identifiers. The final composite score prioritizes demonstrated competencies while actively mitigating the hidden socioeconomic and institutional biases embedded within application narratives.

ideas.title: Real-time genomic surveillance and transmission alerting in acute care settings
ideas.description: This research direction proposes implementing rapid, on-site viral genome sequencing for specific pathogens (e.g., influenza, RSV, emerging threats) within emergency departments and urgent care centers. By analyzing sequences from these high-throughput, high-acuity settings—which capture patients missed by routine screening—the system can dynamically link geographically dispersed cases into probable transmission clusters in near-real-time. This enables the generation of automated alerts to infection prevention and public health teams about active, previously undetected community transmission chains. The approach moves beyond retrospective outbreak investigation to proactive intervention, allowing for targeted containment measures in specific populations or locations identified through the acute care genomic surveillance network.

ideas.title: Use historical archival analysis for policy impact
ideas.description: Employ historical research methods to trace specific discriminatory policies to current community health infrastructure gaps.
ideas.sourceTitle: Racial Disparity of Coronavirus Disease 2019 in African American Communities

ideas.title: FLEX-EQI: Flexible, Low-Burden Equity Intervention Bundles for Small Pediatric Practices
ideas.description: This redesigned study abandons the one-size-fits-all six-step EDI cycle in favor of a tailored, capacity-sensitive implementation strategy for small private practices. It begins with a rapid practice assessment to diagnose readiness and pinpoint specific, contextual equity gaps, then matches the practice to a short menu of pre-validated, minimal-disruption intervention modules (e.g., standardized social needs screening script, bias-aware visit note template). Implementation support is provided through virtual practice coaching and ultra-simplified toolkits, with adherence and impact measured via pragmatic, EHR-embedded audits to minimize data collection burden and ensure real-world viability.

ideas.title: Shifting from Prescriber to Patient: A Demand-Side Intervention Model from Conservation Science
ideas.description: This idea challenges the core assumption that prescriber behavior is the primary lever for change in pharmaceutical use. Drawing an analogy from environmental conservation, where reducing consumer demand (e.g., for ivory or unsustainably harvested fish) often proves more effective than solely regulating suppliers, this research adapts that solution. It proposes to investigate if a primary focus on shifting patient demand and expectations—through targeted education, public messaging, and social norm interventions—creates a more impactful and sustainable 'pull' for appropriate prescribing than traditional prescriber-focused interventions, fundamentally reframing the problem as a market dynamic driven by patient-side factors.

ideas.title: Applying Boomerang Effect Solutions from Public Health to Diversity Messaging
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original idea by drawing an analogy from public health communication, specifically the study of 'boomerang effects' where well-intentioned messages trigger backlash and opposite behaviors. In public health, solutions involved reframing messages to reduce psychological reactance by emphasizing autonomy, using descriptive norms, and employing gain-framed messaging. Adapted to organizational diversity messaging, this research would investigate whether similar reframing techniques can mitigate the deterrent effect on majority-group applicants. The study would experimentally test different framings of diversity commitments (e.g., innovation-enhancement framing, descriptive norm framing, autonomy-supportive framing) against traditional explicit diversity statements, measuring effects on applicant pool size, diversity, and perceived quality across majority and minority groups.

ideas.title: Inverting the focus from fixing women to fixing systems
ideas.description: Shift research from women's adaptation strategies to redesigning promotion, compensation, and leadership systems.
ideas.sourceTitle: Women in Pediatrics: Progress, Barriers, and Opportunities for Equity, Diversity, and Inclusion

ideas.title: Causal Evaluation of Human-Centered Workflow Interventions in Healthcare
ideas.description: This research applies econometric causal inference methods, such as difference-in-differences or regression discontinuity, to evaluate the health effects of human factors engineering interventions in clinical settings. By treating the phased rollout of a redesigned, human-centered screening workflow as a natural experiment, the study can isolate the causal effect of the workflow change on downstream health outcomes from confounding factors. This moves past traditional pre-post usability assessments to provide robust, quasi-experimental evidence on how specific design principles directly influence clinical effectiveness. The result is a powerful framework for prioritizing and iterating workflow interventions based on their proven causal impact on patient health.

ideas.title: Longitudinal study of COVID inequities' effect on health trust
ideas.description: Track how experiencing pandemic-related health inequities influences long-term trust in healthcare systems among BIPOC individuals.
ideas.sourceTitle: Addressing and Inspiring Vaccine Confidence in Black, Indigenous, and People of Color During the Coronavirus Disease 2019 Pandemic

ideas.title: Applying Double-Blind Peer Review Protocols to GME Application Screening
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to bias from the field of Academic Scientific Publishing—specifically, the double-blind peer review system used to judge research manuscripts—to the problem of bias in Graduate Medical Education (GME) screening. The analogous solution involves concealing both author and reviewer identities to force evaluation based purely on content merit. The adapted research idea implements a structured, two-stage anonymization protocol: a dedicated redaction team first removes all personally identifiable information from application portfolios (e.g., names, photos, institutional identifiers). These blinded portfolios are then evaluated by a separate screening committee using competency-based rubrics, with procedural walls ensuring the evaluators cannot access the redacted data. This methodologically enforces a 'double-blind' review of GME candidates, aiming to reduce initial screening bias based on pedigree, gender, or ethnicity.

ideas.title: Cascade Interventions in Medical Education: A Systems Ecology Model
ideas.description: This research adapts a cascading systems model from river restoration ecology to address scaling diversity across the medical education pipeline. In ecology, successful restoration requires linking upstream interventions (e.g., riparian management) to downstream outcomes (e.g., fish populations), ensuring effects propagate through the entire system. The adapted idea applies this by designing GME diversity interventions not as isolated endpoints, but as measurable consequences of specific, earlier reforms in medical school and pre-med admissions. The research would establish causal or correlational links between upstream 'habitat' changes (e.g., holistic review, pipeline programs) and downstream 'population' outcomes (e.g., resident diversity, retention), creating a scalable, integrated model where interventions at each stage are explicitly designed to reinforce and amplify the next.

ideas.title: Decentralized Stewardship as a Natural Experiment: Testing if Synthesized Observational Data Can Replace Formal Trials for Policy
ideas.description: This research direction challenges two assumptions simultaneously: the necessity of centralized programs for effective stewardship and the necessity of new causal inference frameworks for robust policy. It proposes treating the emergent, patchwork landscape of peer-led and individual clinician stewardship initiatives as a large-scale natural experiment. The core question is whether synthesizing the vast, heterogeneous observational data generated by this decentralized ecosystem—using existing, advanced synthesis methods but not novel causal frameworks—can produce policy-relevant causal conclusions about effectiveness that are as reliable as those from formal, centralized program evaluations. This tests the real-world policy utility of big data synthesis against the gold standard of controlled intervention research in a complex, adaptive healthcare environment.

ideas.title: Network-Enabled Cascade: Using Social Contagion Maps to Drive Patient-Centered Screening Outcomes
ideas.description: This research integrates social network analysis of disclosure patterns with a primary outcome focus on actual screening rates and linkage to care. It maps how disclosure and care-seeking behaviors propagate through resident cohorts to identify key 'influencer' patients whose successful screening cascades positively impact peers. The novel synergy lies in using the network map to design and test targeted peer-support interventions aimed at these influencers, with the explicit goal of maximizing the network's overall rate of completed screenings and successful care linkages, rather than merely measuring test orders or disclosure spread.

ideas.title: Applying the 'Lumpers vs. Splitters' Resolution from Taxonomy to Race in Biomedicine
ideas.description: This idea inverts the assumption that race has a biological basis in health by drawing an analogy from biological taxonomy. In taxonomy, the conflict between 'lumpers' and 'splitters' was resolved by adopting a phylogenetic framework that prioritized evolutionary relationships over mutable phenotypic traits. Adapting this solution, the research treats racial categories as a socially motivated form of 'splitting' that misrepresents human genetic continuity. By shifting the analytical framework to focus on specific genetic ancestry, local environmental exposures, and measurable socio-economic factors—akin to using a phylogenetic tree—the research would demonstrate that the concept of race itself is biologically irrelevant for explaining health disparities, serving only as a crude and often harmful proxy for these more precise determinants.

ideas.title: Contextual Mechanisms of Institutional Trust: A Causal Pathway Analysis of Vaccine Delivery Modalities
ideas.description: This study investigates the specific components of vaccine access programs (e.g., privacy guarantees, scheduling autonomy, community-embedded vs. clinical delivery) that differentially impact trust in healthcare institutions. Using a longitudinal, multi-site experimental design with embedded qualitative interviews, it maps the causal pathways—including potential negative ones—through which access features influence trust. The analysis employs causal mediation models to identify the necessary and sufficient conditions for trust-building, separating the effects of access from confounding factors like pre-existing attitudes or political messaging. The outcome is a mechanistic model for designing trust-sensitive access interventions, rather than a simple test of correlation.

ideas.title: Multi-level geospatial modeling integrating patient-level clinical data to isolate systemic prescribing inequities
ideas.description: This research employs a multi-level, case-control framework within a geospatial context to move beyond simple hot-spot mapping. It integrates anonymized patient-level EHR data (including diagnoses and prior utilization) with area-level covariates and precise measures of healthcare access. Spatial regression is then applied to model residual geographic variation in prescribing after rigorously adjusting for clinical appropriateness and patient factors at the individual level. This design directly isolates and quantifies the systemic, place-based drivers of inequity, mitigating the ecological fallacy and providing actionable evidence for targeted interventions.

ideas.title: Use behavioral economics nudge theory for bias reduction
ideas.description: Design nudges in hiring, promotion, and peer review to mitigate unconscious gender bias.
ideas.sourceTitle: Women in Pediatrics: Progress, Barriers, and Opportunities for Equity, Diversity, and Inclusion

ideas.title: Adopt Cognitive Bias Mitigation from Cybersecurity Penetration Testing
ideas.description: This idea adapts the 'red team/blue team' exercise from cybersecurity penetration testing to mitigate bias in patient-clinician interactions. In cybersecurity, this method uses structured adversarial role-play (attackers vs. defenders) to overcome defensive bias and reveal system vulnerabilities. Adapted to a clinical context, the solution involves creating guided simulations where the clinician (blue team) must defend their clinical reasoning, while a trained 'red team' participant actively embodies the perspective, priorities, and potential misunderstandings of a patient from a specific marginalized background (e.g., based on race, gender identity, disability, or health literacy). This active, challenge-based framework is designed to move beyond simple role-reversal, forcing clinicians to confront and correct cognitive and communicative blind spots in a controlled, reflective environment.

ideas.title: Causal isolation of bias training impact in diverse committees via randomized field experiment
ideas.description: This research uses a randomized controlled trial design to causally test whether implicit bias training changes decision-making processes and outcomes in demographically diverse committees, moving beyond correlation. Committees across multiple institutions are randomly assigned to receive validated mitigation training or not, then evaluate standardized materials where candidate quality is independently known. The study measures not only final equity of decisions but also analyzes deliberation transcripts for bias markers and tracks changes in members' implicit associations pre- and post-intervention to isolate the training's mechanistic effect.

ideas.title: Implement Orchestral Audition-Style Blinded Review for Clinical and Promotion Decisions
ideas.description: This idea adapts the highly successful blind audition process from classical music orchestras to reduce bias in medical decision-making forums. In orchestral auditions, a physical screen ensures selection is based purely on musical merit, dramatically increasing diversity. The adapted solution involves creating procedural 'screens' for two key areas: 1) Clinical Case Conferences: Present cases with anonymized patient and clinician details, using standardized case formats and potentially text-based or voice-moderated discussion to conceal presenter identity during the diagnostic and management debate. 2) Promotion Committees: Implement a two-stage review where initial evaluation is based on fully redacted dossiers—achievements, publication titles (with author names removed), grant amounts (without PI name), and anonymized excerpts from peer letters focusing on the work's quality. Only after initial scoring would the committee unblind the candidate's identity for final deliberation, mirroring the practice of revealing the musician only after the audition is scored. This forces evaluation on the substance of the clinical reasoning or academic contribution, rather than the reputation, gender, ethnicity, or institutional affiliation of the individual involved.

ideas.title: A Functional Architecture Analysis of International Graduate Medical Education: Comparing Core Processes Across Systems
ideas.description: This research moves beyond direct country-to-country comparison by developing and applying a functional framework to analyze graduate medical education (GME) systems. It identifies and isolates universal core processes—such as competency progression mechanisms, feedback loop structures, and the integration of service with education—across diverse international systems like the US, UK, Netherlands, and Singapore. The study then conducts controlled, context-sensitive comparisons within these aligned functional modules to identify transferable principles and effective practices. This approach generates insights that are both generalizable and actionable for system reform, as they are decoupled from the idiosyncrasies of any single national structure.

ideas.title: Strategic Non-Disclosure and Network Resilience in Health Advocacy
ideas.description: This research fuses network science with the strategic non-disclosure concept to investigate how BIPOC community network structures influence and are influenced by rational decisions to not disclose health status or request accommodations. It maps not just information flow for advocacy, but also the flow of protective silence, identifying network positions and conditions where non-disclosure is a normative, resilient strategy against stigma or systemic bias. This allows for the design of advocacy interventions that are sensitive to these protective network dynamics, rather than inadvertently penalizing them.

ideas.title: Transfer community-based participatory research methods
ideas.description: Engage local URM communities in co-designing residency selection criteria and outreach.
ideas.sourceTitle: Interventions Associated With Racial and Ethnic Diversity in US Graduate Medical Education

ideas.title: Testing Upstream Economic Interventions as Primary Lever for Health Equity
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that the healthcare system is the primary intervention point for reducing health disparities. Drawing an analogy from Ecosystem Ecology, where restoring a polluted lake requires addressing upstream nutrient sources rather than just treating in-lake symptoms, the research adapts this solution framework. It proposes a quasi-experimental study to test if direct economic or housing interventions (the 'upstream' lever) reduce a composite health disparity index more effectively and sustainably than healthcare reforms (the 'in-system' treatment), measuring outcomes over a multi-year period to determine the most impactful primary intervention point.

ideas.title: Expand community resources addressing HAI determinants
ideas.description: Research the effect of expanding community-based social and structural support services on HAI disparities.
ideas.sourceTitle: Racial/Ethnic Inequities in Healthcare-associated Infections Under the Shadow of Structural Racism: Narrative Review and Call to Action

ideas.title: Mechanistic Comparison of Structural Racism's Pathways in Health Crises: A Domain-Specific Historical Analysis
ideas.description: This research systematically maps and compares the specific institutional mechanisms through which structural racism produced population health inequities across distinct crises like COVID-19, HIV/AIDS, and maternal mortality. Instead of comparing broad outcomes, it analyzes parallel historical narratives within defined policy domains (e.g., housing segregation, insurance design, public health messaging) to identify which mechanisms are crisis-specific, which are recurrent, and how their interactions differ. The goal is to generate a transferable framework for predicting and intervening in the pathways of inequity in future public health emergencies, moving beyond description to mechanistic explanation.

ideas.title: Forensic Policy Audit for High-Stakes Clinical Decision Algorithms
ideas.description: This research applies the structured, evidence-compelling techniques of legal discovery—such as interrogatories, requests for production, and depositions—to audit the specific institutional policies and clinical decision-support algorithms governing high-stakes infectious disease scenarios like sepsis protocols or antibiotic approval systems. The approach systematically traces discriminatory outcomes (e.g., delayed sepsis recognition in specific patient groups) back to their root causes in written guidelines, training materials, or software logic, uncovering both explicit discriminatory intent and implicit discriminatory effects. This enables not just bias mitigation but the construction of a legally and ethically actionable audit trail, holding both human policymakers and algorithmic systems accountable for harms in life-critical decisions.

ideas.title: Scale down to neighborhood-level disparity mapping
ideas.description: Map COVID-19 disparities and structural factors at the census tract or block group level for hyper-local analysis.
ideas.sourceTitle: Racial Disparity of Coronavirus Disease 2019 in African American Communities

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Niche Modeling to Pediatric Workforce Diversity
ideas.description: This research adapts the methodological framework of Ecological Niche Modeling (ENM) or Species Distribution Modeling from conservation biology to pediatric workforce diversity analytics. In ecology, SDMs predict a species' potential geographic range based on environmental covariates and compare it to observed occurrences to guide conservation. Analogously, we propose developing a Pediatrician Suitability Distribution Model (PSDM). This model would use institutional and regional covariates (e.g., NIH funding per capita, presence of pipeline programs, community socioeconomic indices, departmental diversity climate scores) to predict 'high-suitability environments' for the recruitment and retention of pediatricians from underrepresented groups. The core analytical step is to benchmark real-world workforce data against these model-generated 'potential' distributions. This identifies not just where diversity is low, but where it is unexpectedly low relative to supportive conditions—pinpointing specific institutional or regional barriers—and where supportive conditions are underutilized, highlighting strategic opportunities for intervention. This shifts the methodology from descriptive HR analytics to a predictive, gap-analysis framework rooted in ecological theory.

ideas.title: Medical-Virtue-Based Structured Interviews (MVSI) for Admissions
ideas.description: This approach creates structured interviews explicitly grounded in the virtues and competencies of medical practice, rather than importing corporate models. It begins with a consensus-driven definition of essential medical virtues (e.g., compassion, integrity, diagnostic humility) and core non-academic competencies. Interview protocols are then engineered de novo to elicit evidence of these traits, using situational and behavioral questions derived from real clinical and ethical scenarios. The system includes rater training focused on medical context and evaluates predictive validity against downstream performance in clinical rotations and residency.

ideas.title: Universal screening is always the optimal goal
ideas.description: Investigate whether targeted screening based on dynamic risk factors is more efficient than universal screening.
ideas.sourceTitle: Assessment and improvement of HIV screening rates in a Midwest primary care practice using an electronic clinical decision support system: a quality improvement study

ideas.title: Identifying Hidden Resistances: A Systems Ecology Approach to Diversity Barriers
ideas.description: This research adapts the 'biotic resistance' concept from Systems Ecology to the inverse problem of identifying barriers to diversity. In ecology, the failure of a species to thrive in a seemingly suitable habitat is often due to hidden inhibitory interactions from the resident community. Analogously, programs with persistently low diversity despite implementing known effective interventions may be experiencing 'social-structural resistance.' The adapted solution involves treating the program as a complex social ecosystem. Researchers would systematically map the informal networks, cultural norms, and implicit resource flows that constitute the 'resident community.' Using methods analogous to ecological perturbation studies and interaction network analysis—such as organizational ethnography, social network analysis, and process tracing of career advancement—the study would aim to identify the specific, often informal, mechanisms (e.g., micro-exclusions, gatekeeping patterns, cultural mismatches in evaluation) that actively neutralize formal diversity initiatives. The outcome is a diagnostic framework for pinpointing the hidden sources of resistance that scaling efforts must overcome.

ideas.title: Mapping Systemic Contradictions: When 'Inappropriate Use' is an Inevitable Outcome of Competing System Demands
ideas.description: This research investigates the hypothesis that a significant portion of clinical activity labeled as 'inappropriate use' stems not from individual knowledge deficits or cognitive pressures, but from unavoidable conflicts between competing system priorities, guidelines, and metrics. The study will first deconstruct the concept of 'appropriateness' through document analysis and interviews with stakeholders to reveal conflicting standards. It will then use in-situ observation and cognitive interviews to identify real-world clinical decision points where these conflicts force practitioners into actions that violate one standard to satisfy another. The goal is to generate a framework for differentiating knowledge-based errors from systems-based 'double-binds,' leading to more effective, systemic interventions rather than blame-oriented education.

ideas.title: The Paradox of Disclosure: When Bias-Awareness and Strategic Non-Disclosure Interact to Affect Clinical Decision-Making
ideas.description: This research investigates the clinical performance consequences that emerge at the intersection of clinician bias-awareness and patient strategic non-disclosure. It examines how a clinician's cognitive load or performance impairment from hyper-vigilance to bias may be perceptible to a patient, influencing that patient's rational calculation to withhold information or decline accommodations. Conversely, it studies how a patient's strategic non-disclosure, when detected or suspected by a bias-aware clinician, may further amplify the clinician's cognitive burden, creating a detrimental feedback loop that undermines diagnostic accuracy and therapeutic alliance. The goal is to model these interactions to identify conditions where 'best practice' ideals for both parties conflict, producing suboptimal systemic outcomes.

ideas.title: Invert risk focus from D+R- only
ideas.description: Study if other serostatus groups have underappreciated risks for severe indirect CMV effects.
ideas.sourceTitle: Cytomegalovirus infection in liver transplant recipients: updates on clinical management.

ideas.title: Adapting Clinical Trial Blinding and Block Randomization for Alert Design Testing
ideas.description: This idea adapts the rigorous experimental framework of clinical trials to the problem of determining the most effective clinical alert design. The analogous field is clinical trials in medical research, which solve the problem of empirically testing interventions using randomized controlled trials (RCTs) with techniques like blinding and block randomization. The adapted research idea applies this by randomizing providers to different alert designs using block randomization to ensure balanced groups across key confounders like specialty and experience. Furthermore, it implements a single-blind design where providers are not informed of the specific study comparison to reduce performance bias, thereby yielding a more valid empirical measure of which alert design most effectively changes clinical behavior or improves patient outcomes.

ideas.title: Benchmarking Graduate Medical Education Systems Using Ecological Niche Modeling
ideas.description: This research adapts the ecological concept of niche modeling to the scaling of medical education research. Just as ecologists model a species' niche in one region to predict its distribution in others, we model the 'training niche' of US residency programs using structural and process variables (supervision, workload, curriculum). This model is then used as a benchmark to systematically compare and map international GME systems. The output identifies countries with highly analogous training environments (where US findings might directly scale) versus divergent ones (where findings require significant contextual adaptation), providing a data-driven framework for international comparison and transfer of educational best practices.

ideas.title: Lower uptake is primarily due to confidence
ideas.description: Relax the assumption that lower vaccine uptake is mainly driven by confidence, not access or logistics.
ideas.sourceTitle: Addressing and Inspiring Vaccine Confidence in Black, Indigenous, and People of Color During the Coronavirus Disease 2019 Pandemic

ideas.title: Compare effectiveness of intervention combinations
ideas.description: Determine which specific combinations of holistic review and other strategies are most effective.
ideas.sourceTitle: Interventions Associated With Racial and Ethnic Diversity in US Graduate Medical Education

ideas.title: Modeling the Prescriber-Patient Feedback Loop for Synergistic Intervention Design
ideas.description: This research investigates the dynamic system of antibiotic prescribing, rejecting a primary-lever framework in favor of analyzing the reinforcing feedback between patient expectations and prescriber behavior. Using computational simulation (e.g., agent-based modeling), it maps how interventions on patient demand (e.g., public awareness campaigns) alter prescriber decision-making incentives and norms, and vice-versa. The goal is to identify and empirically test combined intervention packages that leverage these interactions, aiming for a total effect greater than the sum of targeting either domain in isolation.

ideas.title: Inverse Design of Pediatric Stewardship Systems with Outcome-Optimized Metrics
ideas.description: This research proposes to mathematically model an optimal pediatric department from first principles, where the system architecture and its key performance indicators are co-designed to maximize equitable health outcomes and patient/family satisfaction. Instead of grafting stewardship programs onto existing structures, the model defines antimicrobial or intervention 'stewardship' as the system's inherent, self-correcting logic, where any reduction in prescriptions is only valued as a success if it demonstrably contributes to predefined, equitable outcome targets. This generates a testable, theoretical blueprint for a healthcare system where efficiency metrics cannot be gamed or pursued in isolation, as they are algorithmically subordinated to higher-order ethical and clinical objectives.

ideas.title: Relaxing the assumption that bias is always overt
ideas.description: Study how subtle, systemic biases in workflow and evaluation cause more harm than overt discrimination.
ideas.sourceTitle: Women in Pediatrics: Progress, Barriers, and Opportunities for Equity, Diversity, and Inclusion

ideas.title: Expand to other guideline-recommended screenings
ideas.description: Apply the same CDSS alert methodology to improve rates for other preventive services like hepatitis C screening.
ideas.sourceTitle: Assessment and improvement of HIV screening rates in a Midwest primary care practice using an electronic clinical decision support system: a quality improvement study

ideas.title: Adapting Epidemic Modeling to Map and Strategize Peer Influence for Vaccine Advocacy
ideas.description: This idea adapts solutions from epidemiology and infectious disease modeling to the context of vaccine advocacy in BIPOC communities. In the analogous field, network-based contagion models (e.g., SIR models) are used to simulate disease spread, identify superspreader individuals in a contact network, and test the efficacy of targeted interventions to contain outbreaks. The adapted research idea applies this framework to social influence, modeling the flow of vaccine-related attitudes as a contagion process on a community social network. By identifying key influencer 'nodes' and simulating different advocacy 'interventions' on the network, the method will provide a strategic, evidence-based map for deploying peer influence resources to most efficiently increase vaccine confidence, directly analogous to public health strategies for curbing an epidemic.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Niche Modeling to Map Intersectional Prescribing Landscapes
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from Ecological Niche Modeling (ENM) in ecology to the problem of understanding prescribing disparities. In ENM, the complex interaction of multiple environmental factors defines a habitat niche for a species. Analogously, we propose modeling the 'disparity niche' where specific combinations of patient demographics (e.g., race, gender, socioeconomic status) and clinician characteristics (e.g., specialty, training background, workload) interact to create a high-risk context for inequitable prescribing. By employing analogous multivariate or machine learning techniques used in ENM, this study would identify and map these critical intersectional combinations, moving beyond single-factor analyses to reveal the complex, interactive landscapes where interventions could be most precisely targeted.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Feedback Loops for Antibiotic Stewardship
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from ecosystem management, this idea adapts the structured audit and feedback cycles used in ecological restoration to the problem of outpatient antibiotic overuse. In ecology, key health indicators are monitored, analyzed, and fed back to prompt adaptive interventions. The adapted research idea applies this by defining antibiotic prescribing quality indicators, implementing regular audit cycles to collect data, and creating a feedback system that provides prescribers with comparative data and specific, actionable guidance to correct deviations, thereby establishing a self-correcting, adaptive loop for improving prescription practices.

ideas.title: Path Analysis and Structural Equation Modeling for Disentangling Disparity Pathways
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from ecology and evolutionary biology—path analysis and structural equation modeling (SEM)—to the problem of quantifying specific antecedent factors' contributions to health disparities. In ecology, SEM is used to disentangle the direct and indirect effects of interconnected environmental drivers on population outcomes. Adapted to public health, the research would involve building a causal network model where factors like occupation, housing, and healthcare access are linked as predictors and mediators to infection and mortality rates. By fitting this model to demographic and epidemiological data, we can measure the relative impact of each factor, partitioning its total effect into direct and indirect pathways, thereby providing a nuanced, quantitative map of the drivers of disparity.

ideas.title: Invert focus from applicant to institutional change
ideas.description: Prioritize reforming program culture over selecting diverse applicants to fit existing structures.
ideas.sourceTitle: Interventions Associated With Racial and Ethnic Diversity in US Graduate Medical Education

ideas.title: The Accommodation-Bias Paradox: When Procedural Fairness Undermines Substantive Fairness and Performance
ideas.description: This research investigates the paradoxical intersection where procedural mechanisms for fairness—specifically, accommodation request protocols and bias-awareness interventions—collide to create adverse outcomes. It examines scenarios in high-stakes, time-pressured environments (e.g., clinical settings, academic testing) where the cognitive load of navigating accommodation requests without clear need, combined with heightened vigilance about bias, impairs professional judgment and performance. The study aims to identify the specific conditions under which these processes trigger stereotype threat in decision-makers, encourage defensive or performative compliance, and ultimately compromise substantive fairness for all parties involved, including the individual requesting accommodation.

ideas.title: Model Clinical Microsystems to Design and Test Integrated Behavioral-Structural Interventions for Outpatient Prescribing
ideas.description: This research begins by using systems engineering methods to map the clinical microsystem—including clinicians, patients, EHRs, workflows, and organizational policies—to identify the root causes and key leverage points for inappropriate prescribing. Researchers then collaborate with frontline stakeholders to codesign interventions that combine targeted behavioral strategies (e.g., nudges, communication training) with precise modifications to local system structures, such as EHR decision support, clinic protocols, or feedback mechanisms. These integrated intervention packages are tested using rapid-cycle, pragmatic designs to evaluate their feasibility, impact on prescribing outcomes, and sustainability within the real-world clinical environment.

ideas.title: A Publicly Accountable CRT-GIS Dashboard for Structural Health Equity
ideas.description: This research develops and implements a public dashboard that integrates CRT-informed spatial analysis (e.g., layering historical redlining, pollution sources, and disinvestment maps with real-time health outcome data like COVID-19 rates) with a mandated, transparent reporting system for institutional equity actions. Local governments, health systems, and other anchor institutions in mapped equity priority zones are required to publicly report their specific investments, policy changes, and programmatic outcomes into the same platform. This creates a feedback loop where the structural analysis identifies 'where' accountability is needed, and the reporting system forces answers on 'what is being done,' enabling continuous public monitoring of whether interventions are effectively targeting the root causes of disparity.

ideas.title: Co-governance Model for Residency Selection: Embedding Community Authority and Accountability
ideas.description: This research establishes a formal co-governance structure where local URM community representatives and program leadership jointly hold decision-making power over residency selection. The system co-designs not only criteria but also the voting or ranking procedures, with community partners constituting a required bloc on the final selection committee. An independent, community-led audit committee is established to annually review selection data, assess fidelity to the co-designed process, and publicly report on demographic and outcome metrics. This model institutionalizes shared authority and transparent accountability, moving beyond advisory input to ensure community priorities directly shape final selections.

ideas.title: Diagnostic Framework for Context-Specific Drivers of Vaccine Uptake
ideas.description: This research proposes a diagnostic system to move beyond generic assumptions about vaccine uptake drivers. It first uses quantitative surveys and geospatial mapping to measure and visualize the distribution of confidence, access, and logistical barriers within a target community. Then, it employs experimental methods like conjoint analysis to quantify the relative influence of each barrier on individual vaccination decisions. The output is a locally validated, ranked set of barriers, enabling public health officials to design and deploy precisely targeted, efficient interventions rather than relying on broad assumptions.

ideas.title: Scale down to individual clinician level
ideas.description: Create a personal stewardship toolkit for solo practitioners or small practices without resources.
ideas.sourceTitle: Antimicrobial stewardship in the outpatient setting: A review and proposed framework

ideas.title: Scale down AMR surveillance to community clinics
ideas.description: Implement decentralized, point-of-care AMR testing and data reporting in local health centers.
ideas.sourceTitle: Confronting antimicrobial resistance beyond the COVID-19 pandemic and the 2020 US election

ideas.title: Adapt equity-focused implementation science frameworks
ideas.description: Apply equity-focused implementation science models to design and evaluate the rollout of HAI prevention bundles.
ideas.sourceTitle: Racial/Ethnic Inequities in Healthcare-associated Infections Under the Shadow of Structural Racism: Narrative Review and Call to Action

ideas.title: Adapt machine learning for risk prediction
ideas.description: Use predictive analytics to identify patients most likely to benefit from screening, prioritizing alerts.
ideas.sourceTitle: Assessment and improvement of HIV screening rates in a Midwest primary care practice using an electronic clinical decision support system: a quality improvement study

ideas.title: Primary focus is on prescriber behavior
ideas.description: Investigate if targeting patient demand and expectations is a more impactful primary lever.
ideas.sourceTitle: Antimicrobial stewardship in the outpatient setting: A review and proposed framework

ideas.title: Scale up: National pediatric society equity dashboard
ideas.description: Create a public, national dashboard aggregating equity metrics from all pediatric training programs and departments.
ideas.sourceTitle: Women in Pediatrics: Progress, Barriers, and Opportunities for Equity, Diversity, and Inclusion

ideas.title: Diagnostic Information Gaps as a Financial Determinant: Modeling Policy Impacts on AMR Detection and HAI Outcomes in Safety-Net Hospitals
ideas.description: This research investigates the hypothesis that financial policies (e.g., value-based purchasing, HAC penalties) create systematic diagnostic information gaps for antimicrobial resistance (AMR) in safety-net hospitals, which in turn drive reported hospital-acquired infection (HAI) rates. It frames the lack of rapid, comprehensive AMR diagnostics not just as a technical failure, but as a direct consequence of constrained capital and operational budgets shaped by policy. The study would employ a mixed-methods approach to model how investments in diagnostic information flow could alter both AMR detection and financial penalty trajectories, proposing policy levers that specifically fund information infrastructure to break the cycle of under-detection, poor outcomes, and resource deprivation.

ideas.title: Adapt diversity analytics from corporate HR tech
ideas.description: Apply predictive analytics and benchmarking tools from corporate diversity software to pediatric workforce data.
ideas.sourceTitle: Women in Pediatrics: Progress, Barriers, and Opportunities for Equity, Diversity, and Inclusion

ideas.title: A Distributed Observational Network for Ecological Validation of Clinical Interventions
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from ecology, where distributed observational networks are used to test the generalizability of models across diverse environments. The analogous solution involves establishing standardized, coordinated data collection across a stratified sample of sites. Adapted to clinical research, this means moving beyond a simple replication to actively construct a network of diverse, non-academic primary care practices (rural, multi-site, etc.) that implement a common, lean intervention protocol. The core of the study becomes a comparative analysis across this network, using harmonized data to quantify variation in outcomes and identify specific contextual factors (e.g., practice size, resources, patient population) that moderate effectiveness, thereby providing a map of generalizability rather than a binary pass/fail test.

ideas.title: Applying Epidemiological Causal Inference Models to Diagnose Stalled Equity Progress
ideas.description: This research seed adapts causal inference methodologies from epidemiology to investigate the root causes of stalled or regressing gender equity progress. In epidemiology, complex models are used to disentangle the web of causation behind why a disease persists or re-emerges despite public health campaigns, focusing on specific risk factors, environmental interactions, and system failures. By analogizing stalled equity to a persistent public health issue, this study would employ directed acyclic graphs (DAGs) to map theoretical causal relationships and apply counterfactual and quasi-experimental analysis to real-world equity data. The goal is to move beyond correlation, rigorously testing which specific interventions have failed, which new negative factors have emerged, and how their interactions within specific socio-economic contexts act as causal mechanisms halting progress, thereby identifying precise, high-leverage points for intervention.

ideas.title: Agent-Based Modeling of Holistic Admissions to Simulate Unintended Consequences for URM Applicants
ideas.description: This research uses computational sociology's agent-based modeling (ABM) to simulate the medical residency match process under different admissions policy regimes. It creates artificial agents (applicants, program directors) embedded within socially structured networks that reflect mentorship, institutional prestige, and implicit bias. The model tests whether de-emphasizing USMLE Step 1, when combined with the real-world network structures that influence holistic review components like research and letters, inadvertently reshapes opportunity pathways in ways that disadvantage some URM applicants who would have benefited from a standardized metric. This allows for virtual policy experimentation, forecasting second-order effects of systemic reform before real-world implementation.

ideas.title: Mechanistic and Trade-off Explicit National Policy Simulation for Holistic Review Adoption
ideas.description: This research develops a national-scale, agent-based simulation that explicitly models the decision-making algorithms of medical schools and the strategic behaviors of applicants under a shift to holistic review. It incorporates competing policy priorities as explicit, tunable parameters to visualize trade-off frontiers, rather than optimizing for a single outcome. The system is designed to generate falsifiable, intermediate predictions about changes in application patterns and interview distributions, which can be validated against early-adopter data, ensuring the model's mechanisms are transparent and grounded in observable behavior.

ideas.title: Observed Micro-Behaviors and Immediate Patient Responses in Clinical Care: A Direct Observation Study
ideas.description: This study employs direct, structured observation of video/audio-recorded clinical encounters during COVID-19 care, using a validated coding scheme to objectively identify specific clinician micro-behaviors with discriminatory potential. These observed behaviors are then quantitatively linked to patients' immediate physiological responses (e.g., vocal pitch stress) and in-visit behaviors (e.g., question-asking). The design further tracks short-term, objective outcomes like follow-up appointment adherence and medication pickup to connect the interaction to concrete care processes, moving beyond subjective recall to establish an evidence chain from observed behavior to measurable consequence.

ideas.title: Multi-Objective Immunosuppression Balancing to Navigate the CMV-Rejection Risk Trade-off
ideas.description: This research develops a computational decision-support system that reframes immunosuppression management as a dual-risk optimization problem. It employs quantitative models, informed by real-world transplant data, to predict patient-specific risk curves for both CMV infection and acute rejection based on drug exposure levels. The system's goal is to identify the regimen that keeps a patient within a personalized safety window, minimizing the combined probability of these competing adverse events. This approach moves beyond primary CMV prevention to holistically manage the fundamental risk trade-off in transplant care.

ideas.title: Organ-Tailored Master Protocol for Prevention in Thoracic vs. Abdominal Transplant Recipients
ideas.description: This research system moves beyond a one-size-fits-all comparison by implementing a master protocol with distinct, organ-specific cohorts for heart, lung, and kidney transplant recipients. Each cohort evaluates prevention strategies optimized for the unique immunology and dominant complication profiles of that organ, using tailored primary endpoints. The design enables efficient, parallel trials within a unified infrastructure while a pre-specified cross-organ analysis focuses on comparing mechanistic biomarker responses to the interventions, providing generalizable biological insights without the confounding of clinical heterogeneity.

ideas.title: Testing the Unintended Consequences of De-emphasizing a Standardized Metric on Underrepresented High Performers
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that de-emphasizing USMLE Step 1 scores universally benefits URM applicants by testing if it inadvertently disadvantages a specific subgroup: URM applicants with strong test scores. Drawing an analogy from ecology, where removing a single conservation indicator can harm other protected species, the solution is to adapt the method of a 'biodiversity audit.' The adapted research would conduct a longitudinal audit of the medical residency application 'ecosystem' before and after Step 1 became pass/fail. It would specifically track outcomes for URM applicants with high Step 1 scores to determine if the policy shift reduced their competitiveness by removing a key, objective differentiator, thereby testing for an unintended negative consequence within the population the policy aimed to help.

ideas.title: Scale down to hyper-local community health worker programs
ideas.description: Implement micro-scale, neighborhood-based vaccine confidence programs led by trusted local health workers.
ideas.sourceTitle: Addressing and Inspiring Vaccine Confidence in Black, Indigenous, and People of Color During the Coronavirus Disease 2019 Pandemic

ideas.title: Positive Deviance in Public Health: A Resilience Engineering Framework
ideas.description: This research adapts the 'Resilience Engineering' framework from high-risk systems safety (e.g., aviation, nuclear power) to public health. In those fields, studying 'positive outliers'—teams that avoid accidents under extreme pressure—reveals hidden capacities and adaptive practices. The analogous solution involves in-depth, systemic analysis of these successes to extract generalizable principles. Adapted to the original seed idea, the research would treat racialized communities with equitable COVID-19 outcomes as resilient systems. By employing methods from resilience engineering (like cognitive task analysis and systems modeling) alongside public health mixed methods, the study would aim to uncover and formally model the specific community-driven structures, adaptive actions, and resource networks that successfully countered the dual threats of racism and the pandemic. The output would be a framework of actionable 'community resilience mechanisms' to inform equitable public health policy and practice.

ideas.title: Dynamic Adaptive Screening: A Resource-Optimization Framework for Public Health
ideas.description: This research develops and validates a computational framework to determine the optimal screening strategy as a dynamic function of available resources and real-time population health data. The system moves beyond the universal versus targeted debate by treating screening as an adaptive resource allocation problem, where the goal is to maximize a composite health outcome metric (e.g., QALYs saved, cases averted). It employs agent-based simulation and optimization techniques to identify the precise conditions—including prevalence, resource levels, and risk-factor accuracy—under which universal, targeted, or mixed screening protocols are optimal, providing a practical decision tool for public health planners.

ideas.title: Historical inequities have a linear, direct impact
ideas.description: Model the non-linear and threshold effects of historical policies on present-day pandemic vulnerability.
ideas.sourceTitle: Racial Disparity of Coronavirus Disease 2019 in African American Communities

ideas.title: Context-Revalidated Non-Cognitive Construct Modeling for GME Selection
ideas.description: This research deconstructs generic non-cognitive predictors from undergraduate admissions into their core psychological constructs and then systematically re-validates them for the GME environment. It employs longitudinal cohort studies within active residency programs to test which constructs, and their specific behavioral manifestations in clinical settings, predict critical outcomes like clinical competency, professionalism, and well-being. The final output is a calibrated assessment toolkit of context-specific behavioral indicators and validated situational judgment tests, moving beyond direct adaptation to purpose-built, evidence-based GME selection metrics.

ideas.title: Quasi-Experimental Stepped-Wedge Study of Structural Interventions with Synthetic Controls for Causal Inference
ideas.description: This research employs a staggered, multi-site stepped-wedge design to rigorously assess the causal impact of structural interventions on population health. Jurisdictions are randomly assigned to sequential implementation waves, allowing each to serve as a control for others at different times. A synthetic control methodology is used to construct counterfactual health outcome trajectories for each treated unit based on a weighted combination of non-treated units, accounting for pre-existing trends. The analysis isolates the effect of the policy change from secular trends by comparing observed post-intervention outcomes to the synthetic control's projected path, providing robust evidence on whether the structural intervention directly altered health disparities.

ideas.title: Assessing how organizational value signaling interacts with identity to shape applicant attraction and pool quality
ideas.description: This research investigates how explicit commitments to diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI) and other organizational values (e.g., meritocracy, innovation) function as contextual signals that differentially affect applicant attraction across demographic groups. Using experimental vignettes and audit studies, it tests how message framing (moral, business, fairness-based) interacts with applicant social identity and beliefs to influence application intentions and perceptions of organizational competence and trustworthiness. The design moves beyond a simple 'backlash' model by concurrently measuring effects on underrepresented minority applicants and assessing the net impact on the quality and diversity of the applicant pool, while controlling for perceptions of existing organizational diversity.

ideas.title: Adapting Standardized Ecological Survey Protocols to Residency Interviews
ideas.description: This idea applies the solution to a parallel problem in ecology—the need for objective, comparable biodiversity assessments—to the context of residency interviews. In ecology, standardized survey protocols with trained observers and clear metrics are used to minimize bias and enable valid comparisons across different sites. Adapted to medical residency selection, this involves creating a highly structured interview 'field protocol' that standardizes the physical and procedural setting, uses a fixed sequence of competency-based questions, and employs a detailed rubric with behavioral anchors for scoring. Interviewers undergo specific training to function as 'standardized observers,' reducing inter-interviewer variability and subjective bias. This method generates objective, comparable data on candidate competencies, directly transferring the rigor of ecological field methods to improve the fairness and predictive validity of residency admissions.

ideas.title: Causal Attribution of Population Health Disparities: A Counterfactual Framework for Policy Evaluation
ideas.description: This research system moves beyond simple pattern-matching to establish causal links between policies and health outcomes. It constructs detailed counterfactual scenarios for what health outcomes would have been in the absence of a specific policy, using a comprehensive set of demographic, economic, and environmental pre-policy data. By applying quasi-experimental methods to compare observed outcomes against these counterfactuals, it quantifies the causal effect attributable to the policy. This allows for the rigorous identification of policies that caused significant, measurable harm, distinguishing true policy failure from mere correlation with underlying disadvantage.

ideas.title: Apply HIV resistance genotyping to CMV
ideas.description: Use rapid genotypic resistance testing methods from HIV management for guiding CMV therapy.
ideas.sourceTitle: Cytomegalovirus infection in liver transplant recipients: updates on clinical management.

ideas.title: Adapting Conversation Analysis from Linguistics to Map Clinician-Patient Prescribing Micro-dynamics
ideas.description: This research adapts the methodological solution from Linguistics, specifically Conversation Analysis (CA), to the problem of studying prescribing decisions at the clinician-patient dyad level. In CA, researchers use fine-grained, sequential analysis of audio/video recordings to decompose natural talk into turn-by-turn sequences, identifying how specific conversational structures accomplish social actions. The adapted idea applies this analytical lens to audio recordings or simulated clinical consultations. The goal is to move beyond self-reported decision factors and instead map the observable, micro-interactional patterns—such as the phrasing of patient cues, the timing and framing of clinical recommendations, and the structure of negotiation sequences—that systematically precede or preclude a prescribing event. This provides a mechanistic, interaction-based model of how prescribing decisions are collaboratively built in real-time dialogue.

ideas.title: Causal and Mechanistic Disentanglement of CMV Serostatus on Indirect Morbidity Risks
ideas.description: This study employs a causal inference design to determine whether CMV serostatus (D+/R-, D+/R+, D-/R+, D-/R-) has a causal effect on risks for non-CMV morbidity, such as sepsis, graft failure, or age-related diseases, in transplant and general aging cohorts. It integrates longitudinal clinical data with multi-omic profiling (e.g., epigenomic clocks, T-cell repertoire sequencing, inflammatory proteomics) to identify and quantify specific biological pathways mediating any observed effects. The approach directly tests competing hypotheses, such as whether risks are driven by chronic viral reactivation, accelerated immune aging, or a persistent inflammatory milieu, thereby moving from observation to mechanistic understanding. This design yields actionable insights for risk stratification and identifies potential targets for intervention beyond antiviral prophylaxis.

ideas.title: Dual: Maximize merit under diversity constraint
ideas.description: Optimize selection for predicted clinical success given a mandatory minimum URM representation.
ideas.sourceTitle: Interventions Associated With Racial and Ethnic Diversity in US Graduate Medical Education

ideas.title: Adapting Climate Change Risk Modeling Frameworks for AMR Impact Projection
ideas.description: This research adapts solution frameworks from climate change risk modeling to the problem of projecting and planning for Antimicrobial Resistance (AMR) impacts. In climate science, Integrated Assessment Models (IAMs) are used to combine socioeconomic narratives (Shared Socioeconomic Pathways - SSPs) with geophysical projections (Representative Concentration Pathways - RCPs) to quantify long-term risks and evaluate policy interventions. The adapted idea is to develop an 'AMR Impact Assessment Model' that similarly integrates future scenarios of global demographics, travel, agriculture, and healthcare access with biological models of resistance gene flow and pathogen evolution. This computational framework would project the health and economic burden of AMR under different 'futures' and allow for the stress-testing of combined intervention portfolios—such as stewardship programs, diagnostic rollout, vaccine development, and sanitation investments—providing a rigorous, scenario-based tool for global health strategy and preparedness funding decisions, mirroring how climate IAMs inform emission targets and adaptation planning.

ideas.title: Closed-Loop Safety Climate Assessment and Co-Design Intervention System for Residency Programs
ideas.description: This system integrates validated psychological safety surveys with a mandatory, structured response mechanism to prevent passive data collection. Following each survey cycle, results are immediately shared with all residents and faculty, and a facilitated workshop convenes stakeholders to interpret findings and co-design specific program improvements. The system then tracks the implementation of these interventions and measures their impact on subsequent survey scores, creating a dynamic cycle of measurement, transparent feedback, collaborative action, and outcome evaluation to genuinely improve program supportiveness.

ideas.title: Human-Centered Micro-Pilot: Using a single-program pilot as a living lab for iterative workflow redesign
ideas.description: This research proposes to conduct a small-scale pilot of a specific intervention (e.g., committee training) within a single residency program, but frames the pilot itself through a human factors engineering lens. The program's clinical workflow becomes a 'living lab,' where researchers systematically observe, model, and analyze the integration process using human-centered design methods like cognitive task analysis and rapid prototyping. This approach enables the iterative redesign of both the intervention and its surrounding workflow in real-time based on user feedback and performance data, creating a highly contextualized and optimized model. The outcome is a dual deliverable: a refined, tested intervention and a replicable micro-pilot methodology for human-centered implementation that can inform scalable, system-wide rollout.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Tipping Point Analysis to Model Healthcare Disparity Systems
ideas.description: This research adapts the systems dynamics approach used in ecology to model regime shifts and tipping points (e.g., clear lakes turning turbid) to the problem of structural healthcare inequity. The analogous ecological solution involves mapping critical reinforcing and balancing feedback loops with delays to predict interventions that can prevent an irreversible system collapse. Adapted to the healthcare context, the research will model the healthcare disparity system to identify key feedback loops—such as the cycle linking distrust in medical systems, avoidance of care, and worsening health outcomes—and the delays within them (e.g., between policy implementation and population health impact). The goal is to simulate various policy interventions to discover which leverage points could fundamentally shift the system from a persistent state of disparity to a sustainable state of equity, thereby providing a predictive tool for designing more effective, systemic health equity policies.

ideas.title: Scale down: Equity micro-interventions in small practices
ideas.description: Test the six-step EDI cycle in small, private pediatric practices instead of large academic centers.
ideas.sourceTitle: Women in Pediatrics: Progress, Barriers, and Opportunities for Equity, Diversity, and Inclusion

ideas.title: Challenge that stigma is primarily about disability
ideas.description: Investigate if stigma is more about requesting help than the disability itself.
ideas.sourceTitle: Barriers to Disclosure of Disability and Request for Accommodations Among First-Year Resident Physicians in the US

ideas.title: Applying Conservation Psychology's 'Nudge' Frameworks to Antibiotic Demand Campaigns
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from Environmental Conservation Psychology, where 'nudge theory' and behavioral insights are successfully used to shift public behavior (e.g., reducing resource use) by reframing choices, leveraging social norms, and providing immediate feedback. The analogous solution moves beyond information campaigns to subtly alter the choice architecture. Adapted to public health, this research would design antibiotic demand campaigns that apply these principles: making watchful waiting or alternative treatments the default, easier path in clinical decision support tools; employing descriptive social norms in messaging (e.g., 'Most patients with your symptoms recover without antibiotics'); and providing immediate, tangible positive reinforcement for appropriate behavior, such as digital recognition in patient health records, to create new social scripts and reduce inappropriate antibiotic demand.

ideas.title: Equity means equal, not appropriate, prescribing
ideas.description: Invert the goal from equalizing prescription rates to equalizing access to appropriate, guideline-concordant care.
ideas.sourceTitle: Health Equity and Antibiotic Prescribing in the United States: A Systematic Scoping Review.

ideas.title: Disentangling Novel Pandemic Mechanisms from Amplified Disparities via Causal Decomposition
ideas.description: This research adapts the methodological framework of extreme event attribution from climate science to challenge the assumption that pandemic impacts were solely amplifications of pre-existing disparities. In climate science, the causal contribution of climate change to a specific event is isolated by comparing real-world simulations to counterfactual models without anthropogenic forcing. Similarly, here, researchers would develop a high-fidelity model of societal outcomes (e.g., mortality, job loss, learning loss) using pre-pandemic data on known disparities (socioeconomic, racial, health, etc.) and their historical rates of change. This model would generate a counterfactual 'disparities-only' projection for pandemic-era outcomes. By rigorously comparing observed pandemic impacts to this counterfactual baseline, the study can quantify the portion of the impact explained by amplified pre-existing inequities versus a residual portion that may be attributable to novel, pandemic-specific mechanisms of disadvantage (e.g., unique virological risk factors, unprecedented policy responses, or new digital divides). This moves the debate from assumption to evidence-based decomposition.

ideas.title: Assess program director training on disability compliance
ideas.description: Evaluate program directors' knowledge and implementation of ADA best practices.
ideas.sourceTitle: Barriers to Disclosure of Disability and Request for Accommodations Among First-Year Resident Physicians in the US

ideas.title: Unconscious bias is primary driver of inequities
ideas.description: Test if systemic resource allocation, not individual bias, is the dominant factor in HAI outcome disparities.
ideas.sourceTitle: Racial/Ethnic Inequities in Healthcare-associated Infections Under the Shadow of Structural Racism: Narrative Review and Call to Action

ideas.title: Causal inference analysis of USMLE Step 1 score de-emphasis on URM applicant outcomes using quasi-experimental methods
ideas.description: This study uses a quasi-experimental, difference-in-differences design to isolate the causal effect of Step 1 de-emphasis on URM applicants. It compares match outcomes for URM and non-URM applicants before and after the policy change in de-emphasizing specialties against a control group of non-de-emphasizing specialties, while rigorously controlling for concurrent shifts in application strategies, letters, and research. The analysis specifically tests whether high-scoring URM applicants lose a previously reliable signal of competence, leading to worse outcomes, or if holistic review effectively captures their strengths. This method moves beyond correlation to provide stronger evidence for or against a causal disadvantage.

ideas.title: Adapt Portfolio Optimization's Efficient Frontier for Clinical Risk Stratification
ideas.description: This idea adapts a core solution from financial portfolio management—the Efficient Frontier from Modern Portfolio Theory—to the problem of clinical risk prediction for screening. In finance, the Efficient Frontier is used to identify the set of optimal portfolios that offer the highest expected return for a defined level of risk, based on the covariance between assets. Adapted to healthcare, the research would develop a predictive analytics framework that constructs a similar frontier for patient screening. The model would not output a single binary 'high-risk' classification but would instead map the trade-off curve between key clinical objectives, such as the predicted yield of true positive cases and the predicted burden of false positives or resource utilization.
