This would transform risk prediction from a unidimensional alert system into a multi-objective decision-support tool, allowing healthcare systems to explicitly choose a screening prioritization strategy along an efficient frontier of benefit-risk trade-offs, tailored to their specific capacity and clinical priorities.

ideas.title: A Causal Inference Framework for Disentangling Environmental and Social Pathways to Disease Disparities
ideas.description: This research integrates high-resolution GIS mapping with causal inference methodologies to move beyond spatial correlation and identify the mechanisms driving disease disparities. It constructs a longitudinal dataset linking historical redlining, dynamic pollution exposure, pre-existing health conditions, and COVID-19 outcomes, while employing techniques like propensity score matching to control for confounding socioeconomic factors. The analysis tests specific causal pathways, such as whether long-term air pollution exposure increased community-level vulnerability by elevating rates of respiratory disease prior to the pandemic. The result is a robust, evidence-based model that pinpoints actionable environmental and social intervention points, rather than just mapping coincident hotspots.

ideas.title: Architectural Defaults and Feedback Loops for Systemic Bias Reduction
ideas.description: This research designs and tests systemic interventions that restructure hiring, promotion, and review processes to make unbiased evaluation the default. It replaces optional nudges with integrated architectural changes, such as blinded skill assessments and mandatory, criterion-based scoring before any demographic information is revealed. The system is augmented by transparent feedback loops that provide individual decision-makers with data on their own evaluation patterns compared to anonymized aggregate benchmarks, fostering accountability and deliberate correction rather than passive compliance.

ideas.title: Precision Antiviral Stewardship: Integrating Rapid Resistance Testing to Guide CMV Management
ideas.description: This research designs and validates a clinical management system where the assumption of common resistance triggers immediate, specific testing, not a blanket change in therapy. It focuses on developing and deploying rapid assays for CMV resistance mutations or drug susceptibility directly from clinical samples. These results are fed into a staged treatment algorithm that escalates therapy only upon confirmation of resistance, ensuring patients with susceptible virus receive standard, less toxic care. The system aims to minimize unnecessary drug exposure, contain costs, and strategically deploy advanced therapies to preserve their long-term efficacy.

ideas.title: Adapting Environmental Remediation Models to Trace Historical Policy 'Toxins' in COVID-19 Health Disparities
ideas.description: This research adapts methods from environmental remediation and toxicology to empirically measure how specific historical policies directly influence COVID-19 severity and mortality in African American communities. The analogous problem—tracing a current environmental contaminant to its historical sources—is solved using source apportionment and fate/transport modeling. The adapted idea treats health disparities as the 'contamination' and uses spatial epidemiology, historical GIS data, and advanced statistical modeling to quantify the contribution of discrete historical inequities (e.g., redlining's impact on comorbidities, hospital segregation's impact on access) to the observed COVID-19 burden, moving beyond correlation to model causal pathways from policy legacy to disease outcome.

ideas.title: Multi-Node Transmission Networks: From Hospital to Community as Drivers of Racial Inequity in HAIs
ideas.description: This research challenges the sufficiency of hospital-level analysis and the household-centric transmission model by proposing a unified 'multi-node transmission network' framework. It investigates how racial inequities in HAIs are systemically produced through the interaction of patient-level care pathways (e.g., inter-facility transfers to specific post-acute facilities) and community-level transmission dynamics in workplaces, congregate living, or public transit. The core innovation is tracing how disparities initiated in one node (e.g., a hospital) are amplified or mitigated as individuals move through subsequent nodes in the network, revealing structural drivers of inequity that are invisible to single-setting studies.

ideas.title: Modeling and Mitigating Threshold Effects of Historical Inequities in Institutional Accommodation Systems
ideas.description: This research develops and tests institutional accommodation processes (e.g., for disability, family status, or religious observance) that are explicitly designed to account for the non-linear and threshold effects of historical inequities. It models how past policies (like redlining, educational segregation, or discriminatory hiring) create compounded vulnerabilities that affect an individual's ability to successfully navigate even transparent procedures. The outcome is a framework for 'equity-resilient' process design that anticipates and disrupts these historical feedback loops, moving beyond standardization to actively mitigate inherited systemic disadvantage within institutional systems.

ideas.title: Apply Aviation Crew Resource Management (CRM) principles to clinical screening workflow design
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from Aviation Human Factors, specifically Crew Resource Management (CRM), to the clinical context. The analogous problem in aviation was integrating complex new safety technologies and checks into a high-pressure, team-based cockpit workflow without increasing cognitive load or error. The solution was CRM, which systematically redesigned communication, leadership, and procedure protocols. Adapted to clinical screening, the research would involve using CRM principles to evaluate and redesign the clinical team's workflow. This includes defining roles for screening data handling, establishing standardized communication channels for results, and implementing structured cross-checking mechanisms to ensure the new screening process is reliably and safely embedded into the existing clinical routine, thereby improving integration and reducing the risk of missed or misinterpreted screenings.

ideas.title: Scale to non-primary care settings
ideas.description: Implement similar alerts in emergency departments or urgent care centers to capture missed screening opportunities.
ideas.sourceTitle: Assessment and improvement of HIV screening rates in a Midwest primary care practice using an electronic clinical decision support system: a quality improvement study

ideas.title: Empathy-Driven Choice Architecture for HIV Screening
ideas.description: This research synthesizes nudge theory with perspective-taking exercises to develop an EHR-integrated intervention that first primes clinicians with brief, digital role-reversal simulations focused on patient barriers to HIV testing. The clinician's completed exercise then informs the EHR system to present contextually appropriate, empathy-shaped nudges—such as personalized default options or script suggestions—during the patient encounter. This approach aims to increase screening acceptance by making the nudge more psychologically attuned to the individual patient's perspective, as interpreted by the clinician, thereby reducing bias and strengthening therapeutic alliance while leveraging efficient choice architecture.

ideas.title: Use geospatial analysis from epidemiology
ideas.description: Apply hot-spot mapping and spatial regression to analyze geographic prescribing inequities mentioned in the review.
ideas.sourceTitle: Health Equity and Antibiotic Prescribing in the United States: A Systematic Scoping Review.

ideas.title: Geospatial Analysis of Structural and Cultural Enablers for HAI Disclosure and Accommodation
ideas.description: This research uses GIS mapping to layer HAI hotspot data, hospital resource metrics, and community socioeconomic vulnerability with the geographic locations of healthcare institutions known to have high rates of HAI disclosure and successful accommodation programs. By spatially analyzing these layers, the study aims to identify if specific environmental or structural conditions (e.g., high vulnerability but adequate resources) correlate with the presence of enabling program factors. This creates a predictive spatial model to pinpoint where targeted interventions to build disclosure-supportive cultures are most critically needed and most likely to succeed, based on replicable local conditions.

ideas.title: Apply econometric causal inference methods
ideas.description: Use instrumental variables or difference-in-differences to estimate racism's causal effect on health.
ideas.sourceTitle: Racism, Not Race, Drives Inequity Across the COVID-19 Continuum.

ideas.title: Network-Responsive Cultural Tailoring: A Dynamic Framework for Public Health Messaging in Hispanic/Latinx Communities
ideas.description: This research integrates network science with cultural tailoring to move beyond static message evaluation. It will map social and communication networks within specific Hispanic/Latinx communities to identify key influencers and information pathways, then deploy culturally tailored health messages through these identified channels. Crucially, it will track how the messages propagate, mutate, and are reinforced through network ties, using this data to dynamically adapt message content and delivery strategy. This creates a closed-loop system where messaging is continuously optimized for both cultural resonance and network-driven amplification, leading to more effective and sustainable behavior change.

ideas.title: Dual-Spectrum Prescribing Analysis: A Thermodynamic Equilibrium Model
ideas.description: This research investigates inequities in antibiotic prescribing by adapting a solution from thermodynamics. In thermodynamics, system states are analyzed by modeling deviations from an optimal equilibrium point, rather than treating excess and deficit as separate problems. Analogously, this study will first define an 'optimal prescribing equilibrium' for antibiotics based on clinical guidelines and patient factors. All prescribing decisions will then be mapped as deviations from this equilibrium, creating a continuous spectrum from severe under-prescribing to severe over-prescribing. The core innovation is the development of a unified 'prescribing entropy' metric that captures the disorder and inefficiency of the entire prescribing system. This approach allows researchers to move beyond binary classifications and instead identify the common systemic factors—such as diagnostic uncertainty, time pressure, or implicit bias—that drive prescribing away from the clinical optimum in both directions, revealing deeper structural inequities in care delivery.

ideas.title: Geospatial Analysis of Socio-Environmental and Genetic Interactions in COVID-19 Severity
ideas.description: This research integrates geospatial epidemiology with genetic epidemiology to investigate the interplay between social determinants and potential biological factors in COVID-19 outcomes. It employs hot-spot analysis and spatial regression to identify clusters of severe disease, then layers these maps with data on population genetic structure (e.g., polygenic risk scores, allele frequencies for candidate genes) and high-resolution data on social and environmental stressors. The core novel aim is to test for statistically significant spatial interaction effects, determining whether the association between genetic factors and severe COVID-19 is modified by the local socio-environmental context, thereby revealing place-based biological vulnerabilities.

ideas.title: Inverse problem: reducing unnecessary alerts
ideas.description: Develop a system to suppress HIV screening alerts when they are clinically inappropriate or redundant.
ideas.sourceTitle: Assessment and improvement of HIV screening rates in a Midwest primary care practice using an electronic clinical decision support system: a quality improvement study

ideas.title: Epidemiological 'Cold Spot' Analysis: Identifying Protective Factors in Low AMR Regions
ideas.description: This research adapts the 'cold spot' analysis approach from cancer epidemiology to solve the inverse problem of identifying drivers of low Antimicrobial Resistance (AMR). The analogous solution involves studying populations with unexpectedly low disease incidence to uncover protective factors. The adapted idea is to systematically identify global 'AMR cold spots'—regions with documented low AMR prevalence despite conditions that typically foster resistance. Through a multi-scale, comparative case-control methodology, contrasting these cold spots with matched high-AMR regions, the research will isolate and validate the specific protective practices, policies, environmental conditions, or microbial community structures that act as natural barriers to resistance development and transmission, offering novel targets for intervention.

ideas.title: Institutional Inversion: A Systems-Level Framework for Equity by Redesigning Core Operational Logics
ideas.description: This research synthesizes the shifts from 'fixing the applicant' to 'fixing the program' and from 'fixing women' to 'fixing systems' into a unified, higher-order inversion: from adapting individuals to fit systems, to adapting systems to produce equitable outcomes as a core function. It posits that equity initiatives fail when layered onto unaltered institutional logics—the deep-seated principles governing resource allocation, promotion, and what constitutes 'merit.' The fused framework develops methodologies to audit these logics across academic, corporate, and public sectors and provides blueprints for their redesign, ensuring that equity is engineered into the operational core rather than managed as an additive compliance task.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Niche Modeling to Simulate Structural Health Inequities
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from ecology's Species Distribution Modeling (SDM) to the research context of pandemic health inequities. In ecology, SDMs simulate how environmental niche constraints (e.g., temperature, habitat) determine a species' geographic range and viability under change. Here, we reconceptualize discriminatory social rules and structures (in healthcare, housing, employment) as the 'niche constraints' on different population groups. An agent-based model is built where agents have traits and behaviors, but their health outcomes and movement during a pandemic are filtered through this structurally defined social niche. This allows us to simulate how specific, modifiable constraints (analogous to altering an environmental variable in an SDM) propagate inequities in infection rates, mortality, and access to care, providing a powerful tool for testing policy interventions aimed at reshaping the structural niche to promote health equity.

ideas.title: Scale up to institutional policy and architecture
ideas.description: Design hospital policies and physical spaces to inherently reduce opportunities for biased decision-making.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Impact of Unconscious Bias in Healthcare: How to Recognize and Mitigate It.

ideas.title: Applying Behavioral Nudges and Feedback Loops from Environmental Psychology to Reduce Inappropriate Prescribing
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from environmental psychology's success in promoting pro-environmental behaviors, this research adapts the solution of using comparative feedback and social norms. The analogous field uses reports comparing an individual's resource consumption to their neighbors' to motivate conservation. The adapted idea is to design and test a behavioral intervention for clinicians involving periodic, anonymized feedback reports. These reports would compare a clinician's outpatient prescribing patterns for target drug classes to aggregated, de-identified data from peer clinicians in similar practice settings, alongside clear evidence-based benchmarks. By leveraging descriptive social norms and creating a continuous feedback loop, this intervention aims to nudge clinician behavior and reduce rates of inappropriate prescribing.

ideas.title: Applying the 'Missing Heritability' Paradigm to CMV Serostatus Risk
ideas.description: This idea applies the conceptual solution to the 'missing heritability' problem in complex disease genetics to CMV research. In genetics, the initial focus on major, high-effect risk alleles (analogous to the D+R- serostatus for direct CMV disease) was expanded to uncover significant contributions from numerous other genetic and epigenetic factors in wider populations. By analogically adapting this solution, the research would invert the risk focus from D+R- only. It would systematically study whether other serostatus groups (D+R+, D-R+, D-R-) harbor underappreciated risks for severe indirect CMV effects (e.g., immunosenescence, cardiovascular sequelae). This involves searching for 'missing morbidity' driven by mechanisms like viral strain-specific immune responses, host genetic variation in non-canonical pathways, or subclinical reactivation dynamics, potentially revealing a broader landscape of CMV-associated pathology.

ideas.title: Adapting Participatory Design Sprints from Software Development for CBPR Intervention Co-Design
ideas.description: This idea adapts the participatory design sprint methodology from software development and HCI to enhance community-based participatory research (CBPR) with Hispanic/Latinx communities. The analogous solution involves time-boxed, intensive workshops where end-users and developers collaboratively prototype and validate software concepts. Adapted to this research context, the method involves organizing structured, multi-day co-design sprints where community members, alongside researchers and practitioners, rapidly cycle through stages of problem definition, ideation, prototyping, and testing of health intervention concepts. This approach provides a concrete, accelerated framework for genuine co-creation, moving beyond traditional meeting structures to generate tangible, community-validated intervention prototypes ready for pilot implementation.

ideas.title: Bias training may increase clinician defensiveness
ideas.description: Challenge the assumption that raising awareness of unconscious bias always leads to positive behavioral change.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Impact of Unconscious Bias in Healthcare: How to Recognize and Mitigate It.

ideas.title: Embedded Equity Action Cycles: Unit-Level Dashboards Coupled with Structured Improvement Protocols
ideas.description: This system integrates real-time, unit-level equity dashboards tracking HAI rates and adherence with a mandatory, structured action protocol. When the dashboard signals a disparity, it automatically initiates a predefined Equity QI Cycle for the unit, requiring a multidisciplinary team to conduct a structured root-cause analysis using provided equity-focused frameworks. The system then suggests a menu of validated, context-specific interventions from an embedded library (e.g., standardized procedure checklists, health literacy aids, implicit bias mitigation prompts) and tracks the implementation and impact of the chosen action, closing the feedback loop from measurement to improvement.

ideas.title: Closed-Loop Empathic Priming with Behavioral Feedback for Clinical Encounters
ideas.description: This system integrates brief, targeted perspective-taking prompts into the clinical workflow using a digital platform. Before a visit, the clinician receives a 60-second guided reflection based on the specific patient's demographic or clinical background. After the encounter, the system analyzes visit audio for predefined linguistic and paralinguistic markers of empathy and provides private, data-linked feedback comparing the clinician's behavior to their own goals and group norms. This approach moves beyond one-time training to create a reinforcing cycle of priming, action, and reflection that is directly tied to measurable performance in actual patient interactions.

ideas.title: Equity Micro-Interventions in Small Practices: A Precision Agriculture Model
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from precision agriculture, where large-scale farming principles are successfully scaled down for small, heterogeneous plots using low-cost, hyper-local sensing and micro-interventions. The analogous agricultural solution involves using simple tools to assess micro-variations in conditions (like soil quality) on a plot-by-plot basis and applying tailored, minimal interventions (like localized drip irrigation) rather than uniform, resource-intensive approaches. Adapted to the research context, this means testing a modified version of the six-step EDI cycle in small, private pediatric practices. The adapted approach would first use low-burden, practice-specific 'probes' (e.g., short surveys, demographic audits) to diagnose the 1-2 most pressing equity gaps unique to that practice's patient population and workflow. The intervention then becomes a targeted 'micro-patch'—a single, concrete change such as adjusting appointment reminder language or implementing a specific screening question—designed to address the identified gap. This allows the equity intervention to be precisely fitted to the practice's specific context and capacity, increasing feasibility and sustainability compared to attempting to implement the full EDI cycle.

ideas.title: A Hybrid Endemic-Pandemic Modeling Framework for AMR Threat Preparedness and Response
ideas.description: This research develops a novel modeling system that integrates the dynamics of acute pandemic threats with the persistent drivers of endemic disease to accurately represent AMR. It defines distinct 'spark' (rapid clonal/plasmid spread) and 'pressure' (background selection from use and pollution) components, each with tailored detection and response protocols. The framework generates scenarios that specify when and how to switch from sustained stewardship programs to emergency containment measures, creating a more robust and operationally relevant preparedness tool.

ideas.title: Platform-Agnostic Vaccine Development: Reforming Institutional Culture for Agile Technology Transfer
ideas.description: This research direction inverts the focus from simply applying a specific new vaccine platform (like mRNA) to CMV, to instead reforming the institutional culture, incentives, and decision-making processes that determine *how* platform technologies are selected and transferred. It investigates the structural barriers—such as risk-averse funding, siloed expertise, and entrenched intellectual property norms—that prevent agile, equitable adoption of platform technologies across disease areas. The goal is to develop and test interventions that reshape institutional environments to be inherently receptive to cross-disease technology transfer, thereby creating a sustainable pipeline for vaccine innovation that outlasts any single technological trend.

ideas.title: Auditing and Incentivizing Structural Power Shifts in Institutional Reform
ideas.description: This research moves beyond assessing cultural narratives to directly audit and incentivize changes in who holds power and control over resources within an institution. It develops and validates a set of concrete, observable metrics—such as the distribution of budgetary authority, composition of promotion committees, and allocation of protected research time—to track structural reform. The system then creates explicit, high-stakes linkages between these audited outcomes and institutional rewards (e.g., grant eligibility, accreditation status). This approach ensures the focus on institutional change is rigorous, accountable, and forces the redistribution of power necessary for genuine inclusivity.

ideas.title: Micro-Practice Systems Modeling for Adaptive Equity Interventions
ideas.description: This research applies systems dynamics modeling to the specific context of small, private pediatric practices implementing equity micro-interventions. By treating each practice as a unique 'micro-system,' the project will instrument the six-step EDI cycle to collect data on feedback loops, delays, and unintended consequences. This granular, real-world data will then be used to build and refine a computational systems model, moving it from a theoretical framework to an empirically grounded tool. The validated model can subsequently guide adaptive intervention design, allowing practices to simulate the potential systemic impact of different equity strategies before implementation.

ideas.title: A Causal Mediation Analysis Disentangling the Pathways from Historical Redlining to Contemporary COVID-19 Inequities
ideas.description: This study employs a formal causal mediation analysis within a counterfactual framework to quantify the specific, contemporary mechanisms through which historical racist housing policies (like redlining) cause COVID-19 outcome disparities. Using spatially-linked, individual-level health records, census, and policy data, it models neighborhood resource deprivation, healthcare facility density, and household crowding as key mediators, testing their proportional contribution to the observed racial inequity. The design explicitly separates the distal effect of historical policy from proximal, measurable mediating factors, moving beyond ecological correlation to provide actionable evidence on which contemporary intervention points would most effectively disrupt the causal chain.

ideas.title: Causal Identification of Structural Drivers in Health Disparities Using Quasi-Experimental Shocks
ideas.description: This research moves beyond correlation to identify causal effects of structural factors on infection and mortality disparities. By leveraging natural experiments—such as sudden industry closures affecting occupation, housing voucher lotteries, or Medicaid expansions—the design treats changes to specific antecedents as quasi-random shocks. It then measures the differential impact on health outcomes in affected versus control populations, using causal inference methods to isolate the effect of each factor while rigorously accounting for confounding. The result is a robust, actionable quantification of which structural interventions would most effectively reduce observed disparities.

ideas.title: Interrogate root causes of HAI inequities
ideas.description: Conduct research to identify and quantify the specific structural and socioeconomic factors driving HAI disparities.
ideas.sourceTitle: Racial/Ethnic Inequities in Healthcare-associated Infections Under the Shadow of Structural Racism: Narrative Review and Call to Action

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Niche Theory to Deconstruct Monolithic BIPOC Health Models
ideas.description: This idea challenges the monolithic treatment of BIPOC communities in public health by drawing an analogy from community ecology. In ecology, the outdated view of a species as a uniform entity was solved by niche theory, which recognizes subpopulations adapted to specific environmental conditions. We adapt this solution by proposing research that maps BIPOC subpopulations according to their distinct 'social-ecological niches'—defined by intersecting factors like specific historical trauma, immigration status, linguistic isolation, religious beliefs, and local policy environments. The adapted research would identify the key dimensions of these niches and test the hypothesis that tailored, niche-specific intervention models predict vaccine uptake more accurately than models assuming homogeneity across the broad BIPOC category, thereby providing a framework for precision public health.

ideas.title: Community-Embedded Trust Infrastructure for Vaccine Confidence
ideas.description: This redesigned system establishes a distributed network of micro-grants and participatory design hubs embedded within diverse BIPOC communities to identify and support local trusted messengers and cultural narrative creators. Instead of a single national campaign, it generates a portfolio of hyperlocal, community-co-designed health communication initiatives tailored to specific contexts and concerns. A central coordinating entity, informed by continuous local feedback, then facilitates the strategic amplification of the most resonant local content through the national media partnership network, ensuring scale is achieved through authenticity rather than uniformity.

ideas.title: Dual problem: Study unvaccinated impact on vaccinated transmission
ideas.description: Analyze how transmission among vaccinated individuals is affected by the surrounding unvaccinated population.
ideas.sourceTitle: COVID-19 Vaccines and SARS-CoV-2 Transmission in the Era of New Variants: A Review and Perspective

ideas.title: Geospatial Agent-Based Modeling for Simulating and Targeting HAI Reduction Interventions
ideas.description: This research integrates high-resolution GIS data on HAI hotspots, hospital resources, and community vulnerability into an agent-based simulation model (ABM). The ABM simulates patient flow, pathogen transmission, and healthcare interactions within the mapped geospatial and socioeconomic context. This computational laboratory allows for the testing and optimization of hypothetical, targeted interventions—such as reallocating infection control personnel or deploying specific community health resources—by predicting their impact on reducing HAI incidence and disparities. The outcome is a dynamic, evidence-based planning tool that moves beyond identifying problems to evaluating solutions.

ideas.title: Structured De-identification with Post-Hoc Bias Audit for Clinical Peer Review
ideas.description: This system enhances traditional double-blind review for clinical settings by formally separating the anonymization process from evaluation and adding a verification layer. A dedicated pre-processing committee rigorously de-identifies all case conference submissions and promotion portfolios, using standardized templates to mask institutional and personal identifiers. The anonymized materials then undergo standard blinded review by the relevant committee. Crucially, a final, separate audit committee compares the blinded review scores against the original materials using statistical methods to detect scoring anomalies suggestive of residual bias, allowing for calibrated adjustments to final decisions.

ideas.title: Contextually-Informed, Hybrid Implementation Trial Across Diverse Practice Ecosystems
ideas.description: This study moves beyond simple replication to prospectively identify and test how practice context influences intervention effectiveness and implementation. Using a hybrid type 2 design, it recruits a stratified sample of practices from diverse ecosystems (e.g., independent rural, FQHC, suburban multi-site) and employs a mixed-methods process evaluation grounded in an implementation science framework. The approach quantitatively tests for moderation of outcomes by pre-measured contextual variables while collecting qualitative data to explain success or failure, generating a generalizable model for which components work, for whom, and under what conditions.

ideas.title: Mechanistic Deconstruction of Bias Training: Identifying Causal Drivers of Clinician Defensiveness and Ineffective Performance
ideas.description: This research moves beyond observing a correlation to experimentally testing why and how bias training can increase defensiveness. Using a randomized controlled trial, it deconstructs standard training into core components to manipulate variables like message framing, accountability structures, and skill-building focus. The study measures immediate psychological reactions (defensiveness, motivation), implicit and explicit bias, and, crucially, objective behavioral performance in high-fidelity clinical simulations. The goal is to produce a causal model identifying the specific features of training that mitigate or exacerbate defensiveness, directly informing the design of more effective, evidence-based interventions.

ideas.title: Adapting Phylogenetic Linkage Methods for Household Transmission Chains
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from evolutionary biology—phylogenetic analysis used to reconstruct pathogen transmission trees—to the problem of linking infections to vaccine status within households. The analogous solution uses genetic sequence data to build 'family trees' of a virus to identify transmission pathways. Adapted to household transmission studies, the method would involve collecting high-resolution pathogen genome sequences from all infected household members, combined with precise metadata on vaccine status, dose dates, and symptom timelines. By analyzing the genetic relatedness of the sequenced viruses within the statistical framework of a household study, researchers can more robustly infer the direction of transmission (who was the likely source) and directly attribute secondary infection risk to the specific vaccine status and timing of exposure from the index case, moving beyond simple temporal association.

ideas.title: Relax assumption that committee diversity suffices
ideas.description: Test if diverse committees without trained implicit bias mitigation perpetuate inequities.
ideas.sourceTitle: Interventions Associated With Racial and Ethnic Diversity in US Graduate Medical Education

ideas.title: Use social marketing techniques for public campaigns
ideas.description: Design public health campaigns using commercial marketing principles to change antibiotic demand.
ideas.sourceTitle: Antimicrobial stewardship in the outpatient setting: A review and proposed framework

ideas.title: Inverse Problem in Prescribing: A Geophysical Tomography Approach to Mapping Bias
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution to inverse problems from geophysical tomography to develop models for inferring latent clinician bias or system factors from observed prescription patterns. In geophysics, tomography solves for subsurface structure from surface data using forward models and regularized inversion. Here, we construct a forward model that simulates prescription decisions as an output of both clinical factors (the 'signal') and latent bias/system factors (the 'noise' or structured distortion). By treating aggregated prescription rates across demographic groups as the observed 'surface data,' we apply inverse methods to solve for the underlying spatial and demographic distribution of bias that best explains the disparities. This provides a quantitative, model-based map of potential bias, moving from simply describing prescription patterns to inferring their hidden structural causes.

ideas.title: Challenging the Uniform Impact of Racism with a Systems Resilience Model
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that racism impacts all stages of the care continuum (testing, treatment, outcomes) with equal magnitude by drawing an analogy from ecosystem ecology. In ecology, the assumption of uniform stress impact was solved by analyzing differential system resilience, identifying critical failure points. Adapted to healthcare, this research would model the care continuum as a complex system under the stressor of structural racism. It would map how racism differentially permeates subsystems and measure their variable resilience to identify which stage (e.g., initial access, diagnostic trust, treatment fidelity, post-care support) is the most critical or vulnerable point of failure, thereby providing a more nuanced, systems-based understanding of inequity propagation.

ideas.title: Stewardship success is measured by prescription reduction
ideas.description: Define success as improved patient outcomes or satisfaction, not just fewer prescriptions.
ideas.sourceTitle: Antimicrobial stewardship in the outpatient setting: A review and proposed framework

ideas.title: Co-Designing Accommodation Processes via Empathy-Driven Simulation
ideas.description: This research synthesizes perspective-taking exercises with institutional design to develop and evaluate disability accommodation processes in residency programs. It employs guided role-reversal simulations where program directors, faculty, and non-disabled residents experience the procedural and interpersonal barriers of navigating accommodation requests from the applicant's or resident's perspective. These experiential insights directly inform the iterative co-design of clear, standardized procedures, ensuring they are not only transparent but also psychologically safe and equitable. The outcome is a validated process that reduces bias, builds institutional empathy, and increases the legitimacy and effectiveness of accommodation systems.

ideas.title: Socio-Technical Durability of CDSS: Measuring Adaptive Clinician Response and System Evolution Over Time
ideas.description: This study investigates the long-term sustainability of CDSS screening improvements by moving beyond simple rate measurement to model the dynamic interaction between the alert system and its clinical users. It employs a mixed-methods longitudinal design that quantitatively tracks alert firing, acceptance, and override rates alongside qualitative assessments of clinician perception and workflow adaptation. The core innovation is the integration of these data streams to identify tipping points where usability issues trigger workarounds or systemic deactivation, distinguishing sustainable integration from mere temporary compliance. The final output is a predictive framework for alert durability that accounts for both technological and human factors, guiding the design of more resilient clinical decision support.

ideas.title: Integrate AMR surveillance with pandemic response systems
ideas.description: Develop unified public health systems that monitor AMR and pandemic threats simultaneously.
ideas.sourceTitle: Confronting antimicrobial resistance beyond the COVID-19 pandemic and the 2020 US election

ideas.title: A Precision CDSS Framework for Preventive Services: Tailoring Alert Design to Service-Specific Determinants of Success
ideas.description: This research develops and validates a taxonomy-based framework for designing clinical decision support (CDSS) alerts for diverse preventive screenings. Instead of applying a uniform alert methodology, the system first classifies target services (e.g., hepatitis C, lung cancer, abdominal aortic aneurysm screening) based on critical factors like recommendation strength, eligibility complexity, and workflow burden. For each category, it then generates and tests specifically tailored alert designs—varying in logic, information presentation, and action pathways—to optimize effectiveness while minimizing alert fatigue. The goal is to move from a generic 'alert-all' approach to a precision-engineered CDSS strategy that matches the intervention to the specific clinical and operational context of each preventive service.

ideas.title: Testing Standalone vs. Integrated Alerts Using Ecological Interface Design Principles
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that adding to an existing CDSS is optimal by testing a standalone HIV screening prompt, drawing an analogy from human factors engineering in aviation cockpit design. In that field, the analogous problem of alert and information overload in integrated primary displays was solved by applying ecological interface design principles, creating dedicated, separate displays for critical functions to reduce cognitive load and improve attention capture. The adapted research idea is to design an HIV screening prompt as a separate, context-aware application that launches based on EHR triggers but operates in its own visual space, rather than as another integrated alert. A study would then compare this standalone, ecologically designed prompt against the traditional integrated CDSS alert, measuring metrics such as alert adherence, screening completion rates, and clinician task-switching efficiency to determine if functional separation outperforms integration.

ideas.title: Scale down to specific high-stakes clinical rotations
ideas.description: Study accommodation barriers within specific, demanding rotations like surgery or ICU.
ideas.sourceTitle: Barriers to Disclosure of Disability and Request for Accommodations Among First-Year Resident Physicians in the US

ideas.title: Equity Implementation Pathways: Mapping the Political Economy from Idealized Model to Feasible Transition
ideas.description: This research begins by constructing an idealized, optimally equitable pediatric department model to define the target state. Its core, however, is a reverse-engineering process that identifies the specific political, financial, and institutional barriers preventing its realization in a given context. The output is not just a blueprint but a strategic 'pathway map' detailing sequenced interventions, necessary coalitions, and policy leverage points to navigate from the current system toward greater equity. This approach grounds utopian ideals in actionable transition strategies, making the theoretical model a tool for practical systemic change.

ideas.title: Design and test a modular, user-configurable accommodation request system for medical residency
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from Human-Computer Interaction (HCI) and Accessible Software Design, where 'user preference panels' or 'accessibility wizards' successfully guide diverse users in configuring software settings through modular, step-by-step interfaces. The analogous solution presents options in clear language and allows for saving profiles. Adapted to the context of disability accommodations in residency, the idea is to design and test a modular, user-configurable digital process. This system would function as an interactive guide, helping residents identify and request accommodations based on functional needs rather than diagnostic labels, generating a standardized request for the institution. The research would evaluate this adapted solution's impact on transparency, user experience, administrative efficiency, and the perceived fairness of the accommodation process.

ideas.title: Adapting Environmental Cap-and-Trade to a National HAI Equity Performance Market
ideas.description: This idea proposes adapting the environmental cap-and-trade solution to scale up a national HAI equity performance metric. In environmental economics, cap-and-trade systems successfully control aggregate pollution by creating a market for emission permits, financially rewarding entities that reduce emissions most efficiently. Adapted to healthcare, a national regulatory body would set a maximum allowable disparity (the 'cap') for specific healthcare-associated infections (HAIs). Hospital systems would receive disparity permits based on their served population's risk profile. Hospitals that reduce outcome disparities below their permitted level generate 'equity credits,' which can be traded to hospitals that exceed their disparity allowance. This market mechanism directly ties financial penalty/reward to disparity reduction, incentivizes the sharing of best practices between institutions, and ensures that the national system achieves a net reduction in HAI inequities, scaling the accountability for equity from the hospital to the national system level.

ideas.title: Implement double-blind review from other sciences
ideas.description: Extend rigorous double-blind review processes from grant agencies and journals to promotion and tenure committees.
ideas.sourceTitle: Women in Pediatrics: Progress, Barriers, and Opportunities for Equity, Diversity, and Inclusion

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Niche Modeling to Map Structural Drivers of Infectious Disease Disparities
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that unconscious individual bias is the primary driver of health inequities by proposing that structural factors are more significant. Using analogical reasoning from Ecology, we adapt the solution of Ecological Niche Modeling (ENM). In ecology, ENM shifted focus from individual organism traits to the environmental conditions that determine species distribution. Similarly, we propose to model infectious disease disparity 'hotspots' not as a direct result of biased individual actions, but as emerging from a 'structural niche.' By applying spatial statistical modeling (akin to MaxEnt or GARP algorithms) to correlate disease burden data with multi-layer geospatial data on structural determinants (e.g., healthcare infrastructure, zoning laws, pollution sources, public transit access, and economic investment), the research will identify and rank the key structural drivers. This methodologically shifts the investigation from measuring individual prejudice to mapping and quantifying the systemic landscape that creates and sustains health disparities, potentially revealing that structural configurations overshadow individual bias as the primary explanatory factor.

ideas.title: Colorblind Social Marketing for Public Policy Campaigns
ideas.description: This research direction investigates whether public policy campaigns (e.g., for health, education, or economic participation) can be designed using sophisticated social marketing techniques to be formally colorblind yet effectively reduce racial inequities. It applies commercial marketing principles—like deep psychographic segmentation, message framing, and channel targeting—to address the root behavioral or perceptual drivers of disparate outcomes without explicit racial categorization in the campaign creative. The core inquiry tests if such campaigns can circumvent political resistance to race-targeted programs while still producing equitable results, offering a pragmatic tool for policymakers operating within legal or social constraints on race-conscious policy.

ideas.title: From Static Solicitation to Dynamic Needs-Sensing: A Barrier-Removal Framework for Residency Programs
ideas.description: This study investigates residency programs that implement a continuous, data-informed process to identify and remove systemic barriers, rather than waiting for disclosure or offering a pre-set menu of accommodations. The research system would analyze programs that use anonymized environmental sensing and structured, opt-in resident feedback mechanisms to detect patterns of friction across workload, scheduling, physical environment, and communication. The core metric of success is the measurable reduction or elimination of identified barriers through program redesign, assessed via longitudinal well-being and performance outcomes. This approach aims to create inherently accessible structures that minimize the need for individual reactive accommodations.

ideas.title: Modeling the Long-Term AMR Impact of Pandemic-Driven Resource Reallocation in Healthcare Networks
ideas.description: This research employs computational sociology's agent-based modeling (ABM) to simulate the complex, long-term trade-offs between pandemic response and antimicrobial resistance (AMR) surveillance. The model represents key agents—hospitals, public health labs, funding bodies, and policymakers—within a socially structured network, dynamically reallocating resources in response to a simulated COVID-19-like shock. By running simulations over extended time horizons, it can identify specific conditions (e.g., pre-existing network cohesion, parallel diagnostic advancements) under which pandemic-driven disruptions inadvertently strengthen AMR surveillance capacity, rather than depleting it, offering evidence-based policy designs for resilient health systems.

ideas.title: Household Transmission Forensics for Policy Attribution
ideas.description: This research develops a novel forensic epidemiology framework that uses designed household transmission studies as a core analytical unit. By rigorously linking infections to individual-level policy exposures (e.g., vaccine eligibility dates, school/work mandates) within households, it creates standardized, comparable 'policy failure detection units' across diverse regions and demographics. Aggregating these units allows for the inverse reconstruction of which specific policy parameters (like delayed second doses or narrow eligibility criteria) most directly caused excess transmission and poor health outcomes. This turns households into natural experiments to pinpoint the mechanistic pathways of policy failure, offering direct causal evidence for historical analysis and real-time policy evaluation.

ideas.title: A Coordinated Intervention Framework for Gatekeeper Groups Using a 'Keystone Species' Analogy
ideas.description: This research seed expands via analogy to ecological restoration. The parallel problem is restoring a degraded ecosystem, where solutions focus on keystone species to catalyze broad, systemic recovery. The analogous solution involves designing interventions for the keystone species that trigger positive feedback loops for the whole community. Adapted to the original idea, this means developing and evaluating a coordinated intervention framework for the four academic gatekeeper groups (centers, societies, journals, funders). Instead of four separate designs, the research would identify a pivotal 'keystone' group (e.g., a consortium of major funding agencies) and craft a strategic intervention for it (like a new grant requirement) explicitly engineered to incentivize and compel aligned changes in the practices of academic centers, societies, and journals. The evaluation would measure the cascading effects and the emergence of a self-sustaining shift in the ecosystem.

ideas.title: Bias-Audited, Criteria-First Evaluation for Promotion and Tenure
ideas.description: This system replaces the flawed double-blind model with a transparent, criteria-anchored process designed to mitigate bias where it actually occurs. Reviewers must first establish and agree upon specific, weighted evaluation metrics for research, teaching, and service before receiving any candidate dossiers. Candidate portfolios are then presented in a standardized, non-narrative format to limit persuasive but irrelevant framing. The final and critical stage is a structured 'bias audit' where evaluators must explicitly justify any outlier assessments that deviate from the pre-agreed criteria, creating accountability and forcing reflection on the sources of subjective judgment. This approach makes bias a documented part of the deliberation rather than an invisible factor hiding behind performative anonymity.

ideas.title: Dynamic Structural Exposure Modeling for Lifecourse Disparities in Chronic Disease
ideas.description: This research moves beyond a static framework transfer by building a dynamic model of how structural inequities accumulate and interact over the lifecourse to shape chronic disease outcomes. It explicitly compares and adapts pandemic-derived inequity pathways to the prolonged timelines and complex care ecosystems of conditions like hypertension and diabetes. The system uses simulation to test how interventions at different life stages (e.g., childhood environment, early-care access, chronic care management) can mitigate or exacerbate disparities, pinpointing targeted leverage points for policy.

ideas.title: Adapting Participatory Action Research (PAR) from Critical Pedagogy for CBPR Intervention Co-Design
ideas.description: This idea adapts the Participatory Action Research (PAR) model from the field of critical pedagogy/education to enhance community-based participatory research (CBPR) designs in social work. In education, PAR is used to tackle systemic inequities by engaging students and educators as equal co-researchers in iterative cycles of investigation, action, and reflection. This solution is adapted by importing its structured, cyclical methodology—community-led problem diagnosis, collective analysis, collaborative intervention design, joint implementation, and shared reflection—into CBPR for intervention co-design and evaluation. The adaptation emphasizes formalizing the community's role as co-researchers through specific participatory tools (e.g., participatory diagramming, community data forums), ensuring the research process actively flattens power dynamics and produces interventions that are deeply rooted in and owned by the affected community.

ideas.title: Applying Path Analysis from Environmental Epidemiology to Model Policy-Driven Health Inequities
ideas.description: This research adapts a core methodological approach from environmental epidemiology—path analysis and structural equation modeling (SEM)—to the study of structural racism and health inequities. In environmental epidemiology, these techniques are used to decompose the total effect of an upstream environmental hazard (like a toxic waste site) into the specific effects mediated by distinct exposure pathways (e.g., air, water, soil). Analogously, this project would operationalize specific racist policies (redlining, punitive policing, inequitable school funding) as upstream causal 'exposures.' The research would then empirically model and quantify how these policies propagate through specific, hypothesized intermediary pathways—such as residential crowding (from housing policy), frequency of police encounters, educational attainment, and allostatic load (a measure of chronic stress)—to create racial inequities in COVID-19 testing rates, time to diagnosis, hospitalization risk, and treatment outcomes. The goal is to move beyond correlation to estimate the specific causal contribution of each policy via each pathway, identifying precise intervention points within the complex system of structural racism.

ideas.title: A Federated Learning Approach to Antimicrobial Stewardship Network Optimization
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original idea of scaling stewardship to regional/national networks by drawing an analogy from distributed machine learning. The analogous problem in federated learning is training a robust model across multiple data silos without centralizing sensitive data. That problem is solved by having participants train local models and share only model parameter updates with a central aggregator. Adapted to the healthcare context, the research idea is to implement a coordinated, large-scale antimicrobial stewardship program using a federated architecture. Independent healthcare systems would participate in a network where they retain full control of their local patient data but contribute de-identified, aggregated insights on stewardship outcomes, local resistance trends, and intervention effectiveness. A central coordinating body would synthesize these inputs to continuously refine and broadcast a set of optimized, context-aware stewardship protocols, decision support rules, and early warning signals for emerging resistance, creating a learning health system for antimicrobial use without compromising data privacy or institutional autonomy.

ideas.title: Adapting Participatory Design from Environmental Justice to Co-Create Vaccine Communication Hubs
ideas.description: This idea adapts participatory design and spatial planning methods from the field of environmental justice to the challenge of building vaccine confidence in BIPOC communities. In environmental justice, communities often use participatory mapping and community design charrettes to visually document local assets and hazards, then collaboratively design physical interventions like parks or monitoring systems. This solution is adapted by applying these co-design techniques to create 'Vaccine Confidence Hubs.' Researchers would facilitate sessions where community members map trusted locations (e.g., churches, barber shops, community centers) and information flow networks, then jointly design the structure, placement, and operation of hubs for vaccine education and dialogue, ensuring the intervention is spatially and culturally grounded in the community's own geography and social fabric.

ideas.title: Apply Aviation Checklist Protocols to EHRs to Mitigate Cognitive Bias
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from aviation safety—the use of standardized, interruptive checklists to mitigate human cognitive bias and error—to the problem of diagnostic bias in medicine. In aviation, checklists counteract biases like confirmation bias by forcing a systematic review. Adapted to electronic health records (EHRs), this would involve embedding 'diagnostic time-out' nudges. When a clinician selects a common or potentially biased diagnosis, the system triggers a mandatory, brief checklist that requires active consideration and documentation of high-risk alternative diagnoses, thereby using choice architecture to interrupt automatic cognitive processes and promote more deliberate, systematic differential diagnosis.

ideas.title: Hyper-Local Outcome Forensics: Using Micro-Scale Health Worker Data to Diagnose Macro-Scale Policy Failures
ideas.description: This research direction proposes deploying hyper-local, community health worker-led monitoring programs to collect granular, real-time data on vaccine confidence, uptake, and health outcomes at the neighborhood scale. This high-resolution data is then used as the primary input for an inverse problem analysis, moving backward from the observed spatial and demographic patterns to infer the specific historical policy failures that most likely caused them. The synergy transforms community health workers from localized intervention agents into a distributed sensor network for policy diagnostics, enabling a precise, evidence-based mapping between macro-level policy decisions and their micro-level human consequences. This creates a closed-loop system where local data directly informs the identification and correction of systemic policy flaws.

ideas.title: Policy solutions can be effectively colorblind
ideas.description: Invert the assumption that race-neutral policies can effectively address racism-driven inequities.
ideas.sourceTitle: Racism, Not Race, Drives Inequity Across the COVID-19 Continuum.

ideas.title: Integrated CMV Resistome Profiling and Phenotypic-Guided Therapy Prediction
ideas.description: This system moves beyond simple genotyping by combining rapid, high-throughput deep sequencing of all known CMV resistance-associated genes with a concurrent, same-sample phenotypic drug susceptibility assay. The genotypic and phenotypic data are integrated via a clinical decision support algorithm trained on historical patient outcomes to interpret complex mutation patterns and their clinical significance. This approach provides a comprehensive resistance profile that accounts for polygenic interactions and compartment-specific resistance, enabling personalized antiviral selection. The goal is to generate a actionable, patient-specific report within a clinically relevant timeframe to guide first-line and salvage therapy in transplant and immunocompromised patients.

ideas.title: Apply human factors engineering principles
ideas.description: Systematically evaluate and redesign the clinical workflow using human-centered design to integrate screening.
ideas.sourceTitle: Assessment and improvement of HIV screening rates in a Midwest primary care practice using an electronic clinical decision support system: a quality improvement study

ideas.title: Use structured interviews from organizational psychology
ideas.description: Adapt structured interview protocols from corporate hiring to reduce bias in medical training admissions.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Impact of Unconscious Bias in Healthcare: How to Recognize and Mitigate It.

ideas.title: Scale up prevention to multi-organ transplants
ideas.description: Extend the comparative trial of prevention strategies to heart, lung, and kidney transplant recipients.
ideas.sourceTitle: Cytomegalovirus infection in liver transplant recipients: updates on clinical management.

ideas.title: Disentangling Primary Causal Drivers: A Path Analysis from Epidemiology
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from epidemiology, where disentangling correlated causal factors is a core challenge. The analogous problem—determining whether a disease is driven primarily by one risk factor or a network of interconnected ones—is solved using path analysis and structural equation modeling to partition direct and indirect effects. Adapted to the social science context, the research would formally test the assumption that structural racism is the sole primary driver of racial inequity by modeling it alongside other structural factors (e.g., class, geography) as latent variables in an SEM framework. Using large-scale data, the analysis would quantify the unique variance explained by each factor and their interactions, revealing whether racism operates independently as the primary cause, is mediated by other structures, or is part of a syndemic of mutually constitutive forces.

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Niche Analysis to Accommodation Programs
ideas.description: This research adapts the 'safe site' concept from community ecology to study programs with high accommodation disclosure rates. In ecology, a safe site is a specific microhabitat where conditions like soil chemistry and moisture allow a species to establish despite a generally hostile landscape. The analogous solution involves identifying these sites and analyzing the precise combination of enabling factors. Adapted to the research context, the study would first identify organizational 'niches'—specific combinations of department, team structure, leadership, and policy—that function as safe sites for disclosure and accommodation. The core analysis would then dissect these niches to determine the exact cultural, structural, and procedural 'abiotic and biotic factors' (e.g., psychological safety metrics, specific workflow adjustments, manager training protocols, peer support dynamics) that interact to create a successfully enabling environment, providing a granular blueprint for replicating these conditions elsewhere.

ideas.title: Applying Portfolio Theory to Evaluate the Cost-Effectiveness of Holistic Review
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from Financial Economics—Modern Portfolio Theory (MPT)—to the problem of evaluating the cost-effectiveness of holistic review and other diversity interventions. In finance, MPT solves capital allocation by optimizing a portfolio's risk-return profile, considering how assets interact. Analogously, we treat diversity initiatives as investments in an institutional portfolio. The adapted idea is to develop a quantitative model that assesses the resource requirements and outcomes of interventions like holistic review not in isolation, but based on their contribution to and interaction within a portfolio of diversity strategies. This allows for the identification of the optimal, most cost-effective mix of interventions to achieve specific institutional diversity and excellence goals under real-world budget and operational constraints.

ideas.title: Neighborhood-level serostatus mapping of indirect CMV morbidity to identify hyper-local risk microclimates
ideas.description: This study integrates hyper-local transmission modeling with a broadened serostatus risk framework to investigate if specific neighborhoods exhibit unique 'risk microclimates' where particular CMV serostatus groups (e.g., D-R+, D+R+) face severe indirect health effects. By modeling outbreak dynamics at the block or household-cluster level within high-density Hispanic/Latinx communities, the research will test whether localized factors—such as kinship networks, childcare sharing patterns, or housing density—interact with host sero-immunology to create pockets of elevated risk previously obscured in city- or cohort-level analyses. The outcome is a fine-grained, mechanistic map of how place modifies individual serostatus risk, identifying specific, intervenable neighborhood features that disproportionately harm underappreciated serogroups.

ideas.title: Comparative analysis of enabling and disabling factors in workplace accommodation disclosure across matched organizational pairs
ideas.description: This research identifies pairs of similar organizations or programs—one with high rates of accommodation disclosure and use, and a matched counterpart with low rates—within the same sector or demographic profile. Using mixed methods, it conducts a comparative analysis to isolate the specific structural, cultural, and procedural factors that differentiate successful environments from struggling ones. The design controls for confounding variables like industry or workforce composition, moving beyond success stories to directly contrast enabling conditions with active barriers. This yields a robust, actionable framework for transforming disabling environments into enabling ones.

ideas.title: Auditable Alert Funnel: Context-Aware Triage and Escalation for HIV Screening Notifications
ideas.description: This system addresses the risk of silent failures by implementing a transparent, multi-stage triage process for HIV screening alerts. Instead of simply suppressing alerts, it classifies each potential alert using clinical context and guidelines into distinct pathways: silent log-only for documented redundancy, a non-interruptive clinical inbox message for scenarios requiring clinician awareness but not immediate action, or a high-priority interruptive alert for situations demanding immediate attention. All decisions are logged with explicit rationale, creating an audit trail for safety monitoring and model refinement, thereby balancing alert fatigue reduction with patient safety.

ideas.title: Community-Sovereign Research Partnership with Binding Governance for Vaccine Trust
ideas.description: This research system establishes a formal partnership structure where a Community Governance Council (CGC), independently formed and led by BIPOC community representatives, holds co-equal and binding authority with the academic institution. The CGC controls a significant portion of the project budget, participates in hiring key staff, and holds veto power over research questions, methods, interpretation of findings, and intervention design. The partnership operates under a pre-negotiated and legally cognizable agreement that defines these shared powers, ensuring the research process cannot proceed without authentic community consent at each phase, thereby preventing tokenistic participation and placing genuine agency in community hands.

ideas.title: Evaluating the risk-benefit paradigm of pre-emptive therapy: A framework to determine when early intervention provides net clinical benefit
ideas.description: This research moves beyond questioning monitoring tools to fundamentally test whether pre-emptive therapeutic strategies provide net clinical benefit across different disease contexts. It establishes a framework that compares personalized, risk-stratified intervention timing against standard pre-emptive approaches, explicitly weighing the benefits of early treatment against the harms of overtreatment and unnecessary intervention. The study employs adaptive trial designs and real-world data to identify conditions and patient subgroups where pre-emptive therapy is justified versus those where watchful waiting or later intervention is superior. The outcome is an evidence-based decision matrix for clinicians, moving the field from a blanket pre-emptive paradigm to a precision-timed intervention strategy.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Niche Modeling to Map and Target Cultural-Communication Niches for Public Health Messaging
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from conservation biology's use of Species Distribution Models (SDMs) to target conservation efforts, this research proposes a novel method for public health messaging. In ecology, SDMs analyze environmental variables to predict where a species will succeed. Adapted here, the research would identify and model the cultural, linguistic, media-consumption, and trust-network 'variables' that define distinct sub-group niches within Hispanic/Latinx communities. By developing a predictive 'cultural-communication niche' model, the study aims to systematically evaluate and enhance the effectiveness of preventive health messaging by precisely matching message content and delivery channels to the specific socio-cultural conditions where they are most likely to succeed, thereby improving vaccine uptake and preventive behaviors.

ideas.title: Adapting Multi-Hazard Risk Mitigation Frameworks to Multi-Organ Transplant Prevention Trials
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by using an analogy from disaster risk reduction. The analogous field is Civil Engineering and Disaster Risk Reduction, where the 'multi-hazard' solution created integrated mitigation strategies for earthquakes, floods, and landslides by targeting common structural vulnerabilities. The adapted research idea applies this framework to scaling up prevention trials for heart, lung, and kidney transplant recipients. It proposes moving beyond organ-specific trials to design a unified, modular prevention protocol that first addresses shared immunological and clinical risks (e.g., initial immunosuppression response, cytomegalovirus prophylaxis) across all three organ types. This core protocol would then be supplemented with organ-specific modules (e.g., lung-specific anti-fungal strategies, kidney-specific BK virus monitoring). A single, adaptive comparative trial could then efficiently evaluate this integrated strategy's effectiveness across the different transplant populations, accelerating the development of broadly applicable prevention standards.

ideas.title: Assume drug resistance will become common
ideas.description: Design management strategies predicated on drug-resistant CMV being the norm, not the exception.
ideas.sourceTitle: Cytomegalovirus infection in liver transplant recipients: updates on clinical management.

ideas.title: Agent-Based Modeling of Co-Evolving Prescriber Behavior and Patient Demand in Pandemic Triage
ideas.description: This research uses agent-based modeling to simulate an artificial healthcare system during a pandemic, where prescriber agents (e.g., physicians, triage officers) operate under formal or informal discriminatory rules for resource allocation. Crucially, patient agents are endowed with adaptive expectations and demand, which evolve based on their observed outcomes and perceived equity. The model investigates how targeting interventions to shape patient expectations (Idea A) interacts with and potentially amplifies or mitigates the structural inequities propagated by prescriber behavior and rules (Idea B). This allows for testing whether demand-side interventions can create feedback loops that pressure prescribers to alter discriminatory practices, or conversely, lead to the entrenchment of inequitable norms.

ideas.title: Comparative Phylogenetic Analysis of Structural Drivers in Global Racial Health Disparities
ideas.description: This research adapts phylogenetic comparative methods from evolutionary biology to compare the structural drivers of COVID-19 disparities among African diaspora populations across different nations. The analogous solution involves constructing phylogenetic trees to model relationships and control for shared ancestry when comparing traits across species. Adapted to this context, researchers would build a 'structural phylogeny' of nations based on shared historical nodes (e.g., colonial empires, major slave trade routes, patterns of post-colonial migration) and contemporary structural similarities (e.g., welfare regime type, healthcare system architecture). Statistical models (e.g., phylogenetic generalized least squares) would then be applied to disparity outcome data, controlling for this shared 'ancestry' to disentangle the effects of deep historical-structural legacies from convergent, independent policy choices. This methodologically rigorous scaling enables a global comparison that identifies whether observed health disparities are primarily 'phylogenetic signals' of entrenched historical pathways or 'convergent adaptations' to similar modern pressures, pinpointing which structural drivers are most amenable to intervention across different national contexts.

ideas.title: Applying Aerospace Crew Resource Management to High-Stakes Clinical Rotations
ideas.description: This research adapts the problem-solving approach of Aerospace Crew Resource Management (CRM) to the study of accommodation barriers in high-stakes clinical rotations. In aviation, CRM successfully addressed human error by zooming in on the specific, high-pressure phases of flight operations (like landing) rather than treating 'cockpit work' as monolithic. Analogously, this study will scale down to dissect specific, demanding clinical rotations (e.g., surgery, ICU) into their constituent high-stakes 'phases' or workflows (e.g., patient sign-out, sterile field maintenance, crisis management). By treating each phase as a unique operational environment with distinct task loads, communication patterns, and physical/cognitive demands, the research will identify phase-specific accommodation barriers and design targeted, context-sensitive solutions, moving beyond one-size-fits-all accommodation frameworks.

ideas.title: Adaptive, Risk-Informed Monitoring: Using Machine Learning to Dynamically Target and Validate Pre-emptive Therapy Surveillance
ideas.description: This research proposes a paradigm shift from questioning monitoring tools in general to actively optimizing their use. It develops machine learning models that perform two key functions: first, predicting which patients are at highest risk for a clinical event to prioritize monitoring resources. Second, and more critically, the models will analyze real-world monitoring data to quantitatively assess the sensitivity and timeliness of different tools for specific patient subpopulations and clinical contexts. This creates an adaptive framework where the pre-emptive therapy strategy is continuously informed by a predictive evaluation of which monitoring modality is most likely to succeed for a given patient, thereby validating or challenging the reliance on specific tools with empirical, personalized evidence.

ideas.title: Employ digital epidemiology for real-time data linkage
ideas.description: Leverage digital tools and wearables for passive, accurate exposure and symptom data collection.
ideas.sourceTitle: COVID-19 Vaccines and SARS-CoV-2 Transmission in the Era of New Variants: A Review and Perspective

ideas.title: Historical Analysis of Stigma as a Policy Mechanism: Tracing the Penalization of Help-Seeking in Health Infrastructure
ideas.description: This research employs historical archival analysis to investigate whether stigma has functioned less as a reaction to disability per se and more as a societal and policy-level penalty for requesting help or resources. It traces how specific historical policies (e.g., poor laws, settlement acts, institutionalization statutes) formally discouraged or punished public assistance requests, and then maps how the infrastructure gaps created by those policies persist in modern community health systems. The synergy produces a structural and historical test of a social-psychological hypothesis, revealing how the stigma of help-seeking was operationalized into law and physically built into environments, creating enduring barriers to care.

ideas.title: Architectural Feedback Loops: Embedding Hyper-Local Trust Data into Institutional Policy Design
ideas.description: This research designs a system where hyper-local community health workers (CHWs) act as embedded sensors within hospital architecture and policy workflows. CHWs collect structured, anonymized data on trust barriers and perceived bias from neighborhood interactions, which is then fed into a centralized institutional dashboard. Hospital administrators and architects use this real-time community intelligence to iteratively redesign physical spaces (e.g., signage, clinic layouts) and revise policies (e.g., vaccine scheduling protocols, staff training) to preemptively mitigate identified biases. This creates a continuous feedback loop, ensuring top-down structural designs are perpetually calibrated and legitimized by bottom-up, trust-based community insight.

ideas.title: Closed-Loop System Engineering for Screening Integration: Combining Human Factors with Control Theory
ideas.description: This approach integrates human-centered workflow design with principles from control systems engineering to create a resilient screening integration system. It implements continuous monitoring of the entire screening pathway, from clinician order to patient completion, using real-time data. The system automatically detects failures or delays and triggers corrective actions, such as patient navigation support or resource reallocation, to maintain throughput. This ensures that the usability gains for clinicians directly translate into measurable improvements in population-level screening outcomes by closing the loop between workflow design and public health performance.

ideas.title: Adapting Agricultural Pathogen Resistance Monitoring for CMV Genotypic Testing
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from agricultural plant pathology, where rapid, field-deployable PCR assays are used to detect fungicide-resistant strains of crop pathogens by targeting known resistance-conferring mutations. The analogous solution involves targeted amplification to guide chemical treatment decisions within a critical, short timeframe. Adapted to the CMV context, this would involve developing a point-of-care, multiplex PCR panel that targets a defined set of known resistance mutations in CMV genes (like UL54 and UL97). This would provide clinicians with a same-day genotypic resistance profile from patient samples, enabling immediate adjustment of antiviral therapy (ganciclovir, foscarnet, cidofovir) for immunocompromised patients, mirroring the rapid decision-making cycle used in agricultural pathogen management.

ideas.title: Scale up to national equity performance metric
ideas.description: Advocate for a national HAI equity performance metric that penalizes or rewards hospitals based on disparity reduction.
ideas.sourceTitle: Racial/Ethnic Inequities in Healthcare-associated Infections Under the Shadow of Structural Racism: Narrative Review and Call to Action

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Restoration 'Disturbance Buffers' as Nudges to Mitigate Bias Spillover
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from ecological restoration, where buffer zones are used to protect restored habitats from invasive species and pollutants, this idea adapts the 'disturbance buffer' as a behavioral nudge for bias reduction. The analogous solution involves creating transitional areas that filter negative influences. Adapted to the research context, the idea is to design procedural 'bias buffers'—structured, low-cost interventions deployed immediately before critical evaluation moments in hiring, promotion, and peer review.
