These buffers, such as a mandatory reflection prompt or an equity-priming task, act as cognitive filters to mitigate the spillover of unconscious gender bias from prior events or discussions, thereby creating a protected decision-making environment that nudges evaluators toward more equitable outcomes.

ideas.title: Use causal inference for observational vaccine studies
ideas.description: Apply methods like propensity score matching to estimate vaccine effects on transmission.
ideas.sourceTitle: COVID-19 Vaccines and SARS-CoV-2 Transmission in the Era of New Variants: A Review and Perspective

ideas.title: Inverse Epidemiology: Deconvolving Health Disparity Signatures to Identify Policy Origins
ideas.description: This research adapts the inverse problem methodology from cosmology, specifically the analysis of the cosmic microwave background, to social epidemiology. In cosmology, the observed CMB pattern is a consequence of initial conditions and physical laws; scientists work backwards from the pattern to infer those origins. Analogously, the spatial and demographic pattern of a health disparity (the observed 'map') is a consequence of underlying social and policy conditions. The adapted idea is to build a computational framework that treats policies as 'input parameters' with known mechanistic effects on social determinants of health. By running an inverse model—statistically deconvolving the observed health disparity pattern through this policy-mechanism simulator—the research aims to identify the specific combination and relative weighting of racist policies (e.g., historical redlining, contemporary policing practices, disinvestment) whose simulated fingerprint provides the best statistical fit to the real-world health data, thereby moving from correlation to causal attribution of policy origins.

ideas.title: Inferring Vaccine Efficacy via Phylogenetic Comparative Methods
ideas.description: This idea adapts a core solution from evolutionary phylogenetics to estimate vaccine efficacy indirectly from transmission data. In phylogenetics, the strength of selection on a trait is inferred by comparing its observed distribution across a known evolutionary tree to models of trait evolution. Here, we treat an observed transmission chain or a partially resolved transmission tree (from genomic or contact tracing data) as the analogous 'phylogeny'. The 'trait' is the infection status (susceptible/infected) and vaccine status of each individual. We develop a probabilistic transmission model where the likelihood of an infection event along a link in the transmission tree is a function of the vaccine-derived protection in the susceptible contact. Using statistical inference (e.g., maximum likelihood or Bayesian approaches) on the observed tree structure and node states, we can estimate the key parameter: the per-exposure reduction in transmission risk, which defines vaccine efficacy against infection. This provides an alternative to direct cohort studies, especially useful when individual infection outcomes are uncertain but transmission patterns are observable.

ideas.title: Inverse problem: When does bias awareness harm?
ideas.description: Study conditions where heightened focus on bias impairs clinical performance or increases cognitive load.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Impact of Unconscious Bias in Healthcare: How to Recognize and Mitigate It.

ideas.title: Causal Identification and Intervention for Subtle Systemic Bias in Organizational Workflows
ideas.description: This research program moves beyond documenting subtle bias to causally identifying its most harmful mechanisms and testing targeted interventions. It employs a triangulated methodology: first, leveraging natural experiments and instrumental variables in longitudinal organizational data to suggest causal pathways; second, conducting RCTs that manipulate specific, hypothesized bias-laden workflow elements (e.g., task assignment algorithms, feedback phrasing, meeting structures) to measure their discrete effects on equity and performance outcomes; and third, using agent-based models to explore the emergent, systemic consequences of these micro-mechanisms and to pre-test intervention strategies. The outcome is a validated toolkit of concrete, evidence-based modifications to workflows and evaluation systems that demonstrably reduce systemic bias without relying on overt discrimination as a benchmark.

ideas.title: Adapting Phylogenetic Comparative Methods for Policy Lineage Audits
ideas.description: This research adapts phylogenetic comparative methods from evolutionary biology to systematically audit healthcare policies for discriminatory intent or effect. The analogous solution involves reconstructing evolutionary lineages and testing hypotheses about trait evolution. Here, we model a policy's development over time as a phylogenetic tree, where nodes represent policy versions and branches represent amendments or influences. By coding discrete and continuous 'traits' from policy texts and contextual data, we can apply statistical comparative methods to test for correlations between the 'evolution' of restrictive policy features and demographic or political variables. This provides a rigorous, data-driven audit of a policy's historical lineage to uncover systemic biases embedded through its amendment history, moving beyond a static analysis of its final form.

ideas.title: Assess policy impacts on safety-net hospital HAI rates
ideas.description: Analyze how reimbursement policies and financial penalties affect HAI prevention resources and outcomes in safety-net hospitals.
ideas.sourceTitle: Racial/Ethnic Inequities in Healthcare-associated Infections Under the Shadow of Structural Racism: Narrative Review and Call to Action

ideas.title: Modeling the Systemic Feedback Dynamics of Gender Equity to Identify High-Leverage Intervention Points
ideas.description: This research moves beyond descriptive root-cause analysis to build dynamic simulation models of gender equity systems within specific organizational or sectoral contexts. By integrating data and stakeholder input to map causal feedback loops—such as those linking representation, mentorship, bias, and policy compliance—the model identifies which factors are dominant drivers of stagnation or regression. The system is then used to computationally test and rank potential interventions based on their predicted ability to create sustainable progress while minimizing unintended negative consequences. This approach provides a predictive, strategic tool for designing robust equity initiatives that account for complex system behavior.

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Succession to Institutional Culture Reform
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from ecology to challenge the assumption that diversity must be achieved primarily through applicant selection. In ecological restoration, the analogous problem is reintroducing species into a degraded habitat where they fail to thrive. The solution is not to keep sourcing hardier individuals but to change the habitat itself through processes like modifying soil chemistry, hydrology, or pioneer species—a concept known as altering the state of the system. Adapted to institutional change, this means shifting research focus from selecting 'resilient' diverse applicants to fit an existing culture, to instead identifying and reforming the core, self-reinforcing elements of program culture (e.g., mentorship models, evaluation criteria, social infrastructure). The adapted research idea is to design and study interventions that alter these foundational conditions to create a new, stable institutional 'state' that naturally recruits, retains, and benefits from diversity, making it an outcome of the environment rather than a perpetual selection challenge.

ideas.title: Adapt supply chain resilience for antibiotic access
ideas.description: Use lessons from COVID-19 PPE supply chains to secure essential antibiotic supplies.
ideas.sourceTitle: Confronting antimicrobial resistance beyond the COVID-19 pandemic and the 2020 US election

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Tipping Point Analysis to Policy Impacts on HAI Prevention
ideas.description: This research adapts methods from ecology, specifically the study of ecosystem tipping points under cumulative stress, to analyze policy impacts on hospital-acquired infection (HAI) rates in safety-net hospitals. The analogous ecological solution involves modeling system resilience and using time-series data to detect non-linear thresholds and early warning signals before a regime shift. Adapted to this context, the study would analyze longitudinal data on reimbursement policies, financial penalties, HAI prevention resources, and HAI outcomes. The goal is to determine if there are identifiable financial or policy 'tipping points' where the cumulative strain of penalties causes a disproportionate, non-linear collapse in prevention capacity and a sharp increase in HAI rates, thereby providing evidence for more nuanced, resilience-focused policy design.

ideas.title: Combine CDSS with patient-facing tools
ideas.description: Integrate the provider alert with patient portal prompts to create a multi-modal screening intervention.
ideas.sourceTitle: Assessment and improvement of HIV screening rates in a Midwest primary care practice using an electronic clinical decision support system: a quality improvement study

ideas.title: Adapting Pharmacokinetic Compartmental Modeling for National Policy Equity Impact Forecasting
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from pharmacokinetics, where multi-compartment models predict differential drug effects in biological subsystems, to the context of national policy impact modeling. The analogous solution uses mathematically defined compartments with transfer rates to model how a substance disperses and acts differently across a heterogeneous system. Adapted to stewardship policies, the research would develop a national-scale computational model where societal subpopulations are represented as compartments with quantified parameters for policy uptake, benefit conversion, and impact dissipation. By simulating policy interventions as inputs to this system, the model can predict the equity impacts—identifying which subpopulations experience amplified, diminished, or delayed benefits/burdens—and inform the design of mitigating policy mechanisms, much as pharmacokinetic models guide tailored dosing regimens.

ideas.title: Provider education alone is insufficient
ideas.description: Test if a more intensive, repeated education program could achieve similar results without a CDSS alert.
ideas.sourceTitle: Assessment and improvement of HIV screening rates in a Midwest primary care practice using an electronic clinical decision support system: a quality improvement study

ideas.title: Adaptive Micro-Messaging for Vaccine Confidence via Community Signal Repeaters
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from wireless mesh network engineering to the challenge of scaling vaccine confidence. In mesh networks, signal repeaters are deployed to receive, authenticate, and adaptively rebroadcast signals locally to ensure integrity and coverage. Adapted here, hyper-local community health workers function as 'social signal repeaters.' They are trained with a core curriculum but empowered to authenticate public health information and adapt its delivery—language, narratives, examples—to their immediate neighborhood's specific social and cultural fabric. These workers form a peer-supported, decentralized network that can identify localized misinformation and respond with trusted, contextualized messaging, creating a resilient and self-reinforcing system for building vaccine confidence at the micro-scale.

ideas.title: Modeling Disclosure as a Contagion Process Using Epidemiological Compartmental Models
ideas.description: This idea adapts a classic epidemiological solution to the problem of tracking social influence. The analogous problem in epidemiology is modeling how a disease spreads through contact networks, solved using compartmental models like SIR. By mapping disclosure patterns in resident cohorts onto states of Susceptible, Infected, and Recovered, we can apply SIR differential equations or agent-based simulations on the observed social network. This allows for quantifying the rate of disclosure transmission per social tie, predicting the ultimate reach within a cohort, and simulating the effects of interventions (e.g., confidential reporting systems) that might alter the network's 'transmission' or 'recovery' parameters.

ideas.title: Community-Governed Participatory Research with Binding Power-Sharing Protocols
ideas.description: This system establishes a community-led research governance board with contractual authority over key study decisions, including veto power on design, analysis, and publication. It employs a structured 'community equipoise' framework, agreed upon before the study begins, to guide conflict resolution when community and academic perspectives diverge. The model ensures resource control is shared, with a portion of funding directly managed by the community board for capacity building and independent analysis. This creates a binding partnership that structurally prevents tokenism and centers community sovereignty in defining both the problems and solutions in prescribing equity research.

ideas.title: Adapting Community-Based Participatory Conservation for Disability Research
ideas.description: This idea adapts the methodology of Community-Based Participatory Conservation, where local communities are essential research and management partners, to the context of disability research. The analogous solution uses structured participatory tools (e.g., resource mapping, seasonal calendars) to integrate local knowledge with scientific aims. Adapted for disability research, disabled residents would be engaged as expert partners using analogous co-assessment tools—such as accessibility audits mapped by residents and 'daily routine calendars' highlighting points of systemic friction—to collaboratively diagnose problems and design interventions. This transfer ensures the research process itself is empowering, grounds solutions in lived experience, and builds sustainable community capacity for ongoing evaluation and adaptation, mirroring the long-term stewardship goals of conservation projects.

ideas.title: Adapting Crime Pattern Analysis to Map and Predict Prescribing Hotspots
ideas.description: This idea adapts spatial analysis methods from criminology and predictive policing to the problem of geographic prescribing inequities. In criminology, kernel density estimation is used to map crime hotspots, and Geographically Weighted Regression (GWR) models how the influence of risk factors (like poverty or land use) changes from one neighborhood to another, enabling targeted interventions. Adapted to healthcare, this approach would first map prescribing hotspots for specific drug classes. Then, GWR would be used to analyze how the strength of association between potential drivers (e.g., clinic density, median income, racial composition) and prescribing rates varies across the geographic landscape. This reveals not just *where* inequities exist, but *why* they might be more pronounced in specific regions, allowing for locally tailored policy responses rather than one-size-fits-all solutions.

ideas.title: Inverse problem: Identify drivers of low AMR regions
ideas.description: Study areas with unexpectedly low AMR to identify protective practices and policies.
ideas.sourceTitle: Confronting antimicrobial resistance beyond the COVID-19 pandemic and the 2020 US election

ideas.title: Scale down to individual program pilot studies
ideas.description: Test a single intervention, like committee training, in a small program before system-wide rollout.
ideas.sourceTitle: Interventions Associated With Racial and Ethnic Diversity in US Graduate Medical Education

ideas.title: Disentangling Transmission Contributions Across Interconnected Daily Networks
ideas.description: This research moves beyond the isolated study of any single transmission setting to model how infections propagate through interconnected daily networks. By prospectively following individuals and mapping their contacts across all frequented environments—household, workplace, transport, and communal spaces—the study captures the complete exposure landscape. Integrated data from diaries, proximity sensors, and pathogen sampling enables the use of network inference models to attribute transmission risk to specific settings and their interactions. This approach reveals the critical bridging nodes and contextual interdependencies that truly drive transmission dynamics, providing a robust evidence base for targeted, multi-pronged interventions.

ideas.title: Apply causal inference methods from economics
ideas.description: Use econometric techniques like instrumental variables to identify causal drivers of prescribing disparities.
ideas.sourceTitle: Health Equity and Antibiotic Prescribing in the United States: A Systematic Scoping Review.

ideas.title: Scale up to national policy simulation
ideas.description: Model the system-wide impact if all programs adopted holistic review and de-emphasized Step 1.
ideas.sourceTitle: Interventions Associated With Racial and Ethnic Diversity in US Graduate Medical Education

ideas.title: Adaptive Geospatial Risk Modeling for HAI and Resource Disparity
ideas.description: This idea adapts the methodological framework of Species Distribution Modeling (SDM) from conservation ecology to the problem of healthcare-associated infections (HAI) and resource disparity. In ecology, SDM uses GIS to statistically model species presence as a function of multiple environmental layers. The adapted solution applies this to create a predictive, multi-layer geospatial risk model. It would analyze the spatial correlation and interaction between three key data layers: HAI incidence hotspots (the 'species occurrence' data), hospital resource capacity, and community socioeconomic vulnerability indices. By running statistical analyses (e.g., MaxEnt or regression-based approaches) on this spatial overlap, the model generates predictive risk surfaces. These surfaces identify high-probability 'outbreak habitats'—areas where low resources and high community vulnerability converge with existing HAI cases. This moves beyond static visualization to dynamic, predictive modeling, allowing public health officials to proactively target resource allocation, infection control audits, and community health interventions to the areas of greatest predicted risk.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Niche Modeling to Predict Trainee Success in GME
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from conservation biology—ecological niche modeling (ENM)—to the problem of holistic GME review. In ENM, ecologists integrate multiple, non-linear environmental variables to predict a species' probability of survival in a given habitat. Analogously, we can adapt this methodological framework to create a 'training niche suitability' model for medical residency. The adapted research idea involves developing a predictive model that integrates validated non-cognitive success predictors (adapted from undergraduate admissions) with critical, program-specific contextual variables (e.g., institutional support, patient population, learning environment). This composite model would generate a holistic match score, moving beyond checklist scoring to predict a candidate's likelihood of thriving within the specific 'ecosystem' of a given residency program.

ideas.title: Assume diversity messaging can cause backlash
ideas.description: Investigate if explicit diversity commitments deter majority-group applicants, affecting overall applicant pool.
ideas.sourceTitle: Interventions Associated With Racial and Ethnic Diversity in US Graduate Medical Education

ideas.title: Modeling the co-evolutionary production of syndemic vulnerability: A process-based historical systems approach
ideas.description: This research models how pandemic vulnerability is not a direct output of past policies but is co-produced over time through the dynamic interaction of structural conditions and social narratives. It uses a computational historical systems framework to simulate the feedback loops between factors like racial segregation, healthcare disinvestment, and the erosion of public trust. The approach identifies non-linear thresholds and synergistic ('syndemic') traps that emerge from these interactions, providing a testbed for evaluating which sequences of interventions could have altered present-day vulnerability trajectories.

ideas.title: A Structurally-Enforced Co-Governance Model for Disability Research
ideas.description: This research system establishes a legally-framed partnership charter from the outset, granting disabled co-researcher teams shared signatory authority over budgets and project milestones. It begins with a participatory Power and Resource Audit to identify and dismantle institutional barriers to equitable collaboration. The core methodology integrates a blinded 'veto and redesign' stage, where co-researchers can anonymously challenge and reconfigure study findings or intervention plans before public release. This structure ensures that participatory action research moves beyond consultation to enforceable co-governance, redistributing power and guaranteeing that outcomes are directly accountable to the disabled community.

ideas.title: Implement participatory action research with disabled residents
ideas.description: Co-design interventions with disabled residents as research partners.
ideas.sourceTitle: Barriers to Disclosure of Disability and Request for Accommodations Among First-Year Resident Physicians in the US

ideas.title: Systemic Analysis of Accommodation Ecosystems: Mismatches, Barriers, and Participatory Redesign
ideas.description: This research investigates the systemic factors within accommodation ecosystems that create friction, inefficiency, or unmet needs for all stakeholders. Instead of judging individual requests, it analyzes anonymized, aggregated data on accommodation types, utilization rates, and user feedback to identify patterns of mismatch between provision and need. The core methodology is participatory, involving disability communities, accommodation providers, and policymakers in co-designing studies to uncover barriers in policy, communication, technology, or process design. The goal is to generate evidence for creating more effective, equitable, and streamlined accommodation systems that better serve their intended purpose.

ideas.title: Inverse problem: Model equitable systems from scratch
ideas.description: Design a theoretical, optimally equitable pediatric department without the constraints of existing structures.
ideas.sourceTitle: Women in Pediatrics: Progress, Barriers, and Opportunities for Equity, Diversity, and Inclusion

ideas.title: Use agent-based modeling from social sciences
ideas.description: Simulate how discriminatory rules in artificial societies propagate health inequities during a pandemic.
ideas.sourceTitle: Racism, Not Race, Drives Inequity Across the COVID-19 Continuum.

ideas.title: Individual Mucosal Immunity Profiling via Microfluidics and Single-Cell Transcriptomics
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from precision oncology to the problem of measuring individual mucosal immunity. In oncology, microfluidics and single-cell transcriptomics are used to profile the heterogeneous immune and tumor cell landscape from rare patient samples at single-cell resolution, enabling personalized diagnostics. By applying this technological solution, the research would develop a platform to capture and sequence individual immune cells from human mucosal samples post-vaccination. This would create a detailed, individual-level profile of the vaccine-induced mucosal immune state (including cell types, receptor repertoires, and functional states), which can then be directly correlated with individual transmission reduction outcomes in controlled studies, scaling down the measurement of population-level mucosal immunity to a precise, personalized readout.

ideas.title: Adapting Climate Change Mitigation Frameworks to Sustain AMR Governance Post-Crisis
ideas.description: This research idea adapts a proven governance solution from climate science—specifically the model of independent oversight bodies like the UK's Committee on Climate Change—to the challenge of sustaining political focus on Antimicrobial Resistance (AMR) after acute pandemics subside. The analogous problem in climate policy was maintaining momentum for long-term mitigation after the acute crisis of a natural disaster faded from headlines, which was solved by creating statutory, science-based institutions with mandated reporting cycles. The adapted idea is to design a policy framework for establishing permanent, independent 'AMR Governance Committees' via national legislation. These committees would be empowered to set binding national AMR action targets, audit government and sectoral progress against these targets, and issue public reports at regular intervals. This institutionalizes a mechanism for continuous scrutiny and political accountability, ensuring AMR remains a priority on the political agenda through structured, legally-required review processes, thus preventing the 'attention deficit' that typically follows a crisis.

ideas.title: Applying Microfracture Analysis to Micro-aggressions in Clinical Encounters
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from materials science for studying microfractures to investigate micro-aggressions in clinical encounters during COVID-19. In materials science, catastrophic failure is understood by detecting and mapping tiny, cumulative microfractures using tools like acoustic emission and microscopy. Analogously, this study will treat routine discriminatory interactions as 'social microfractures.' It will employ a mixed-methods approach: using real-time patient sensing (like brief surveys or biometric wearables) as 'acoustic emission' detectors for stress events, combined with detailed qualitative 'microscopy' of clinical dialogue transcripts. The goal is to rigorously map how these micro-events propagate under the stress of a pandemic, cumulatively damaging the therapeutic relationship and leading to measurable failures in patient trust, treatment adherence, and clinical outcomes.

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Resilience Theory to Committee Bias: Diversity Without Intervention as a Fragile System
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that demographic diversity alone suffices for equitable committee decisions by drawing an analogy from ecology. In conservation, high biodiversity does not automatically create a resilient, functioning ecosystem; it requires active management for functional roles and specific interventions. The adapted research idea tests if diverse committees, without trained implicit bias mitigation, perpetuate inequities because they lack the 'intervention structures' to build bias resilience. It proposes an experiment comparing diverse committees using standard procedures against those implementing structured bias-mitigation protocols (inspired by ecological management techniques) to measure differences in equitable outcomes, shifting the focus from compositional diversity to functional, managed system design.

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Resilience Models to Study Healthcare Trust Erosion and Recovery
ideas.description: This research adapts longitudinal disturbance-recovery models from ecosystem ecology to study how pandemic-related health inequities affect long-term trust in healthcare systems among BIPOC individuals. Analogous to how ecologists track an ecosystem's resilience after a major fire or flood, this study would treat systemic healthcare inequities exposed during COVID-19 as a profound social-system disturbance. It would longitudinally measure key trust metrics (e.g., institutional confidence, preventative care utilization, compliance with public health guidance) to model the 'resilience' of the trust ecosystem—identifying critical thresholds past which trust collapses and mapping potential pathways for recovery or stabilization, thereby providing a dynamic systems model to guide restorative policy and communication interventions.

ideas.title: Modeling the Structural Determinants of Disability Disclosure: From Individual Choice to Systemic Intervention
ideas.description: This research moves beyond cataloging individual reasons for non-disclosure to empirically model the organizational and systemic factors that constitute the 'disclosure ecosystem.' It employs multi-level modeling and policy analysis to quantify how specific structures—such as accommodation request bureaucracy, managerial discretion, and resource allocation—create rational incentives for concealment. The goal is to identify high-leverage points for structural reform that reduce the costs and risks of disclosure, shifting the focus from accommodating individual choice to redesigning equitable systems.

ideas.title: Adopt perspective-taking exercises from conflict resolution
ideas.description: Use guided role-reversal simulations to build empathy and reduce bias in patient-clinician interactions.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Impact of Unconscious Bias in Healthcare: How to Recognize and Mitigate It.

ideas.title: A Federated Standard for Multi-Platform HIV Screening Alerts
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from distributed computing and internet protocol design, this idea adapts the solution of creating lightweight, interoperable standards to solve the problem of scaling an HIV screening alert across a regional health system with multiple, disparate EHR platforms. The analogous field is Distributed Computing/Internet Protocol Design, where the IETF solves interoperability between diverse systems via standards like TCP/IP. The analogous solution involves defining protocol standards that specify data formats and rules for exchange, enabling independent implementation. Adapted to the research context, the idea is to develop and implement a federated 'Health Alert Protocol' (HAP) standard for HIV screening. This standard would allow each EHR vendor in the integrated delivery network to build a conformant alert module locally. A central service would only handle patient consent management and de-identified reporting aggregates, not patient data, ensuring scalability, vendor buy-in, and privacy while achieving consistent alert functionality across the entire regional network.

ideas.title: Adapting Precision Agriculture's Hyperlocal Soil Models to Develop Hyperlocal Vaccine Access Models
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from precision agriculture's success with hyperlocal soil management, this research proposes to develop and test 'Hyperlocal Vaccine Access Models.' In precision agriculture, solutions are tailored to small, specific zones within a field based on detailed sensor data, rather than applying uniform treatments. Adapted to public health, this project will use community-engaged, fine-grained data collection (e.g., spatial ethnography, local asset mapping, micro-demographic surveys) to map unique vaccine access barriers at the neighborhood or block level within broader BIPOC populations. The core research activity involves designing, implementing, and evaluating a suite of micro-targeted delivery protocols—each customized for a specific 'access zone'—to determine if this hyperlocal tailoring proves more effective than community-wide models in overcoming deep-seated structural and cultural barriers to vaccination.

ideas.title: Adaptive, Closed-Loop Debiasing System for Clinical Decision Support
ideas.description: This research designs and tests a machine learning-driven system integrated into the electronic health record that provides personalized, context-sensitive debiasing interventions. Instead of applying uniform nudges, the system continuously analyzes individual clinician decision patterns, patient outcomes, and prompt effectiveness to tailor and adjust its feedback. It employs a reinforcement learning framework to optimize intervention timing and content, minimizing alert fatigue while maximizing diagnostic accuracy. The closed-loop design allows the system to learn which debiasing strategies work for whom and in what clinical situations, creating a continuously improving and robust safeguard against cognitive bias.

ideas.title: Assess COVID-19's impact on AMR using ecological disturbance-recovery models
ideas.description: This research adapts methods from ecosystem ecology to quantify how the COVID-19 pandemic altered antibiotic use and resistance patterns worldwide. By drawing a direct analogy to how ecologists model major disturbances (like wildfires), the pandemic is treated as a global shock to the healthcare ecosystem. The analogous solution involves analyzing longitudinal, global data on antibiotic consumption and resistance rates pre- and post-pandemic, applying ecological models (e.g., state-space models, resilience metrics, network analyses of resistance gene flow) to characterize the disturbance's impact, map altered selection pressures, and distinguish between temporary deviations and permanent regime shifts in the global AMR landscape, thereby predicting future trajectories and informing mitigation policy.

ideas.title: Apply systems dynamics modeling to structural inequity
ideas.description: Model the healthcare disparity system using feedback loops and delays from systems science.
ideas.sourceTitle: Racial Disparity of Coronavirus Disease 2019 in African American Communities

ideas.title: Inverse problem: Infer vaccine efficacy from transmission data
ideas.description: Estimate vaccine protection against infection indirectly from observed transmission chain patterns.
ideas.sourceTitle: COVID-19 Vaccines and SARS-CoV-2 Transmission in the Era of New Variants: A Review and Perspective

ideas.title: A Multi-Driver, Gene-Centric Network Model for Predicting the Spread of Antimicrobial Resistance Determinants
ideas.description: This research develops a novel modeling framework to predict the geographic spread of antimicrobial resistance (AMR) by focusing on the transmission of resistance genes and mobile genetic elements, rather than applying pandemic models directly to bacteria. It integrates disparate data streams—including genomic surveillance, antibiotic consumption, environmental contamination, and host mobility—into a unified network model that captures the complex ecology of AMR spread. The system is designed to identify high-risk pathways for gene flow and predict the emergence of resistant strains in specific geographic regions under different intervention scenarios.

ideas.title: A Multi-Threat Public Health 'Early Warning and Attribution System' (EWAS) for AMR and Pandemics
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from integrated forecasting in meteorology, this research proposes developing a Multi-Threat Public Health 'Early Warning and Attribution System' (EWAS). Just as meteorological systems use shared sensor data and unified models to monitor and attribute risks from diverse atmospheric threats (e.g., hurricanes, heatwaves, long-term climate shifts), the EWAS would integrate AMR and pandemic pathogen surveillance into a single framework. It would utilize a common infrastructure of genomic sequencing, clinical reporting, and environmental sampling. The core innovation is a unified data assimilation and modeling platform capable of analyzing this stream to differentiate between emerging novel pathogens (pandemic threat) and shifts in antimicrobial resistance profiles or multi-drug resistant organism transmission (AMR threat). The system would produce a fused risk dashboard with attributed alerts, allowing public health agencies to launch specific, threat-appropriate responses—such as novel pathogen containment protocols versus AMR stewardship or infection control interventions—from a single, coordinated intelligence source.

ideas.title: Network-Integrated Mucosal Immunity Mapping for Transmission Block Prediction
ideas.description: This study moves beyond correlating individual mucosal immunity with personal protection to directly model its impact on transmission. We measure vaccine-induced mucosal immunity (e.g., IgA, neutralizing antibodies) in all participants of a densely sampled community cohort while simultaneously quantifying their close-contact networks over time. By integrating high-resolution immunological data with empirical contact networks into stochastic transmission models, we can identify which individuals, based on both their immunity level and network position, are most critical for blocking transmission chains. This approach predicts the real-world transmission-reducing effect of a mucosal vaccine and identifies key immunological and behavioral targets for maximizing herd protection.

ideas.title: Apply nudge theory from behavioral economics
ideas.description: Use choice architecture and defaults in EHRs to nudge clinicians toward appropriate prescribing.
ideas.sourceTitle: Antimicrobial stewardship in the outpatient setting: A review and proposed framework

ideas.title: Portfolio Optimization for Clinical Trial Cohorts: Balancing Expected Return and Mandatory Diversity
ideas.description: This research adapts the constrained optimization framework of Modern Portfolio Theory from mathematical finance to the problem of clinical trial cohort selection under a dual mandate. The analogous problem is constructing an investment portfolio that must maximize expected financial return subject to a mandatory minimum allocation to a specific asset class. Here, individual candidate therapies or trial arms are treated as assets with quantified estimates of predicted clinical success (expected return) and associated uncertainty (risk). The mandatory minimum URM representation acts as the hard compositional constraint. The solution involves formulating and solving a constrained optimization problem—potentially using Lagrange multipliers or quadratic programming techniques—to select the cohort that lies on the 'efficient frontier,' maximizing aggregate predicted merit for any achievable level of the diversity constraint. This provides a rigorous, quantitative methodology for the Dual scaling challenge.

ideas.title: Governance-First Co-Design: Embedding Formal Power-Sharing and Resource Control in CBPR with Hispanic/Latinx Communities
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond participatory principles to institutionalize equitable partnership through a pre-negotiated governance framework. It establishes a joint steering committee with equal representation and decisive authority over all project phases, including shared control of the budget and resources. The research explicitly measures and validates shifts in community capacity, leadership, and ownership of the intervention process as primary outcomes. This ensures the co-designed intervention is not only culturally tailored but is also genuinely community-owned and sustainable beyond the research timeline.

ideas.title: Mechanisms for Political Attention Allocation to Concurrent Health Crises
ideas.description: This research program systematically identifies and tests the mechanisms that determine how political systems allocate attention across concurrent, slow-burn (AMR) and acute (COVID-19) crises. It combines comparative policy analysis with quantitative text analysis of legislative and executive communications to measure attention shifts. The core design tests hypotheses linking specific mechanisms—such as crisis framing synergy, bureaucratic entrepreneurship, and cross-issue advocacy coalitions—to observed outcomes in policy agenda space and resource allocation, moving beyond assertion to explain the conditions under which concurrent focus is possible.

ideas.title: Investigate causality behind stalled equity progress
ideas.description: Conduct root-cause analyses to understand why gender equity progress is slow or regressing in specific areas.
ideas.sourceTitle: Women in Pediatrics: Progress, Barriers, and Opportunities for Equity, Diversity, and Inclusion

ideas.title: Use marketing segmentation for tailored messaging
ideas.description: Apply consumer marketing segmentation techniques to identify subgroups within BIPOC populations for targeted vaccine communication.
ideas.sourceTitle: Addressing and Inspiring Vaccine Confidence in Black, Indigenous, and People of Color During the Coronavirus Disease 2019 Pandemic

ideas.title: Inverse problem: when are accommodations requested unnecessarily?
ideas.description: Examine cases where accommodations are requested without a clear disability need.
ideas.sourceTitle: Barriers to Disclosure of Disability and Request for Accommodations Among First-Year Resident Physicians in the US

ideas.title: Apply Phylogenetic Comparative Methods to Map Historical Redlining onto Modern COVID-19 Disparities
ideas.description: This idea adapts phylogenetic comparative methods from evolutionary biology to the study of structural racism and health disparities. In biology, PCMs use a phylogenetic tree to control for shared ancestry when correlating traits. Here, we construct an analogous 'policy lineage tree' for urban neighborhoods, mapping the historical sequence and intensity of discriminatory policies like redlining onto their geographic boundaries. This tree is integrated as a random effect or covariance structure within a Bayesian spatial model analyzing modern COVID-19 hotspot data. The adapted method allows researchers to formally test the hypothesis that neighborhoods sharing a history of severe, sequential policy discrimination exhibit correlated modern health outcomes, while controlling for the non-independence of data points caused by shared historical-geographic pathways. This provides a rigorous, quantifiable framework for CRT-informed spatial analysis.

ideas.title: Leveraging Deliberate Practice and Feedback Loops to Replace Passive Education
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that the failure of provider education is merely a problem of dosage or repetition. Drawing an analogy from skill acquisition in fields like cognitive psychology and sports science, where passive instruction fails to build reliable performance, the adapted solution shifts from passive education to active, deliberate practice. The research would test if a simulation-based training platform, which forces repeated clinical decision-making with immediate corrective feedback, can embed correct prescribing habits as effectively as an interruptive CDSS alert, thereby potentially achieving similar outcomes without the need for the alert system itself.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Niche Modeling to Map the 'Behavioral Niche' of Inappropriate Use
ideas.description: This research idea challenges the assumption that inappropriate use of a clinical intervention is primarily a knowledge deficit by adapting a solution from ecology. In ecology, Species Distribution Models (SDMs) are used to predict species presence based on the interplay of multiple environmental variables, moving beyond explanations based solely on species biology. We adapt this to model the 'occurrence' of inappropriate clinical practice. The research would involve collecting data on episodes of care, measuring contextual variables (time pressure, diagnostic uncertainty, habit strength via behavioral cues), and controlling for practitioner knowledge. Using multivariate statistical or machine learning modeling akin to SDMs, we can map the 'behavioral niche' where inappropriate use thrives and quantify the relative contribution of each contextual driver versus knowledge, directly testing which are the strongest predictors.

ideas.title: Structural racism is the sole primary driver
ideas.description: Test if other structural factors (e.g., class, geography) interact equally or more strongly.
ideas.sourceTitle: Racism, Not Race, Drives Inequity Across the COVID-19 Continuum.

ideas.title: Mapping the Legibility Frontier: The Political Construction of Requestable Accommodations
ideas.description: This research investigates the political and administrative processes that determine which human needs can be translated into a formal accommodation request within institutional systems. It posits that the 'unrequestable' is not a natural category but is produced by boundaries of legibility enforced by policy, technology, and social norms. The study will combine institutional ethnography with design workshops to expose these boundary-making mechanisms and prototype alternative governance models that expand the sphere of legible need. The goal is to shift the focus from cataloging inaccessible supports to redesigning the systems that define access itself.

ideas.title: Community-verified equity audits with algorithmic action-tracking
ideas.description: This system requires institutions to develop their key equity metrics and reporting frameworks in partnership with representative community stakeholders, ensuring relevance and preventing metric manipulation. All reported data is then subjected to independent algorithmic audits that cross-reference public claims with internal policy changes, budget allocations, and outcome data to detect performative reporting. The audit results and the institution's responsive actions are published in a standardized, machine-readable registry, enabling watchdogs, researchers, and the public to track consistency and follow-through over time. This creates a closed-loop system where reporting is intrinsically linked to verified action and community-defined accountability.

ideas.title: Long-term sustainability of CDSS alerts
ideas.description: Study the durability of screening rate improvements over multiple years after the initial CDSS intervention.
ideas.sourceTitle: Assessment and improvement of HIV screening rates in a Midwest primary care practice using an electronic clinical decision support system: a quality improvement study

ideas.title: Establish transparent reporting systems for equity data
ideas.description: Create mechanisms for institutions to publicly share equity data and progress reports.
ideas.sourceTitle: Women in Pediatrics: Progress, Barriers, and Opportunities for Equity, Diversity, and Inclusion

ideas.title: Neighborhood-Level AMR Governance: Translating Micro-Dynamics into Global Policy Frameworks
ideas.description: This research synthesizes hyper-local modeling of antimicrobial resistance (AMR) transmission within specific high-density neighborhoods with the structural frameworks of global climate governance. It treats neighborhood-level outbreak dynamics and resistance gene flow as the critical, granular data source—analogous to local greenhouse gas emissions inventories—to inform and calibrate long-term, binding AMR policy instruments like regional 'cap-and-trade' for antibiotic use or community-adaptive stewardship targets. This approach grounds top-down global governance in empirically observed micro-mechanisms of spread, ensuring interventions are equitable, context-specific, and capable of dynamically responding to local socio-ecological drivers of resistance.

ideas.title: The AMR Bond: A Self-Funding Mechanism to Lock in Political and Financial Commitment
ideas.description: This research designs and models a mandatory financial instrument, the 'AMR Bond', that automatically generates sustained funding for antimicrobial resistance (AMR) work. The mechanism legislatively requires that a fixed percentage (e.g., 0.5-1.5%) of any future government appropriation for pandemic or acute infectious disease crisis response is channeled into a protected, transparent AMR fund. This creates a powerful political-economic feedback loop: every act of crisis spending reinforces the infrastructure to prevent the next, more complex AMR crisis. The system addresses the fatal flaw of static structures by making sustained AMR investment an automatic, growing, and politically salient byproduct of the crisis response cycle itself.

ideas.title: A Prospective Sentinel System to Forecast Vaccine Protection Against Severe Disease
ideas.description: This research establishes a continuous, multi-site sentinel network to prospectively monitor and model vaccine effectiveness (VE) against hospitalization and death. It integrates high-frequency data on hospital admissions, vaccination and booster status, breakthrough infections, and viral genomic sequences into a dynamic modeling framework. The system is designed to detect signals of waning protection and variant-driven immune escape in near-real time, generating forecasts of VE trends. This enables proactive public health guidance and vaccine update recommendations before significant losses in protection against severe illness accumulate in the population.

ideas.title: Co-optimization of Immunosuppression and Antiviral Prophylaxis via Integrated PK/PD Modeling for CMV Prevention
ideas.description: This research direction develops an integrated pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic (PK/PD) model that jointly simulates the effects of immunosuppressive drugs (e.g., tacrolimus, mycophenolate) on host immunity and the pharmacokinetics of prophylactic antivirals (e.g., letermovir, valganciclovir) against CMV. By modeling the interaction between drug-induced immunosuppression (the driver of risk) and antiviral exposure (the preventive agent), the framework can identify regimen pairs that maintain sufficient immunosuppression for graft survival while keeping CMV replication probability below a threshold, all within defined drug exposure safety margins. This allows for the creation of personalized, adaptive dosing protocols that proactively adjust both immunosuppressive and antiviral doses based on biomarkers, moving beyond sequential optimization to a unified prevention strategy.

ideas.title: Vaccines always reduce transmission compared to unvaccinated
ideas.description: Test if certain variants or conditions eliminate the transmission reduction benefit of vaccination.
ideas.sourceTitle: COVID-19 Vaccines and SARS-CoV-2 Transmission in the Era of New Variants: A Review and Perspective

ideas.title: A Context-Aware, Adaptive Alerting System to Preserve Performance Under Cognitive Load
ideas.description: This research designs and evaluates a dynamic clinical alert system that modulates its behavior based on real-time assessment of the clinician's cognitive load and clinical context. Instead of testing static alert designs, the system uses inputs from the EHR, workload management software, and ambient sensors to infer context and adjust alert modality, timing, and information density. The study measures the system's impact on both routine alert fatigue and, crucially, on performance and error rates during high-fidelity simulations of complex, high-acuity patient scenarios. This approach ensures robustness by explicitly testing the intervention under the most demanding conditions it will face in practice.

ideas.title: Synthesized Multi-Modal Screening with Unified Alert Logic and Closed-Loop Patient Feedback
ideas.description: This system integrates patient portal prompts and clinical data feeds into a single decision engine that synthesizes all inputs to generate one prioritized, staged alert for the provider, eliminating contradictory signals. The logic employs a severity-weighted algorithm to resolve discrepancies between patient-reported symptoms and objective clinical data, presenting a clear clinical rationale. Simultaneously, the patient portal provides tailored feedback, acknowledging their input and explaining its role in the care process, thereby reinforcing engagement. This closed-loop, synthesized approach reduces provider cognitive load and alert fatigue while maintaining patient involvement and transparency.

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Niche Modeling Methods to Estimate Causal Effects of Racism on Health
ideas.description: This idea adapts causal inference methods from Ecology and Conservation Biology to estimate racism's effect on health. Ecologists use species distribution models (SDMs) with quasi-experimental designs, leveraging exogenous 'natural experiments' (like sudden habitat fragmentation) as instrumental variables to isolate the causal impact of specific environmental factors on species. We adapt this by identifying analogous exogenous, policy-driven shocks that alter exposure to structural racism (e.g., changes in housing, policing, or education policies) and using them as instruments in econometric models. This approach treats health outcomes as a niche shaped by racism exposure, allowing us to model and isolate its causal effect from confounding variables, similar to how ecologists disentangle the effects of environmental drivers on species distributions.

ideas.title: Strategic Non-Disclosure as an Optimal Signaling Equilibrium in Disability Accommodation
ideas.description: This research idea adapts solutions from evolutionary biology and game theory, specifically the modeling of evolutionarily stable strategies (ESS) and costly signaling, to the context of disability accommodation disclosure. The analogous problem in biology is understanding when an organism benefits from conspicuous signaling (like bright coloration) versus cryptic concealment. Biologists solved this by modeling the environmental conditions and payoff matrices that make each strategy stable. Adapting this, the research would formally model the organizational and social conditions—such as the magnitude of perceived stigma, the true utility of available accommodations, and the career risk/reward structure—under which choosing not to disclose a disability and not request accommodations constitutes a rational, optimal strategy for the individual. This challenges the prevailing assumption that non-disclosure is inherently problematic or a failure of inclusion systems, reframing it as a potential strategic equilibrium in a complex signaling game.

ideas.title: Apply crop growth and pest control modeling to optimize antiviral dosing
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from agricultural science, where sophisticated models optimize the timing and dosage of interventions like pesticides based on dynamic systems of crop growth and pest populations. The analogous solution uses predictive modeling of biological and environmental variables to schedule interventions for maximum effect and minimal collateral damage. Adapted to CMV drug development, this approach involves creating a PK/PD model that frames the host-virus interaction as a competitive population dynamic. The model would integrate patient-specific factors as 'environmental variables' to simulate and predict optimal, personalized dosing regimens for new CMV antivirals, aiming to enhance efficacy, reduce toxicity, and delay resistance by strategically timing drug exposure relative to the predicted dynamics of viral replication and immune response.

ideas.title: Agent-based modeling of transmission in competing social microstructures
ideas.description: This research uses agent-based models (ABMs) from computational sociology to simulate disease transmission across multiple, interconnected social microstructures—specifically comparing households, workplaces, and communal settings like gyms or cafes. The model explicitly represents the distinct contact patterns, duration, and environmental factors of each setting, allowing them to compete as transmission venues under varying intervention scenarios. This approach moves beyond declaring a single 'optimal' setting to dynamically identify which microstructure acts as the primary transmission driver under specific social, behavioral, and policy conditions. The outcome is a policy-testing tool that predicts where targeted interventions (e.g., workplace ventilation vs. household isolation) would be most effective for a given pathogen and social context.

ideas.title: AMR requires a singular, dedicated response system
ideas.description: Test if AMR is best tackled by integrating it into all primary care systems.
ideas.sourceTitle: Confronting antimicrobial resistance beyond the COVID-19 pandemic and the 2020 US election

ideas.title: Network Topology Mapping for Pathogen Transmission: A Shift from Household Nodes to High-Throughput Edges
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that households are the optimal transmission setting by drawing an analogy from STI epidemiology. In STI research, the field moved beyond studying monogamous partnerships to mapping entire sexual networks, identifying core groups and high-activity individuals that sustain epidemics. The adapted solution is to apply this network topology approach to respiratory pathogens. Research would shift from static household studies to dynamic infection chain tracing, using methods like digital contact tracing, serial interval estimation, and environmental surveillance to actively map where transmissions actually occur. The goal is to identify which settings—such as open-plan offices, public transport, or gyms—act as critical transmission hubs or bridges between otherwise isolated household clusters, thereby revealing the true high-throughput edges in the transmission network.

ideas.title: Scale up to national BIPOC media partnership campaign
ideas.description: Create a large-scale, coordinated vaccine confidence campaign across national media outlets trusted by BIPOC audiences.
ideas.sourceTitle: Addressing and Inspiring Vaccine Confidence in Black, Indigenous, and People of Color During the Coronavirus Disease 2019 Pandemic

ideas.title: Scale up to regional or national networks
ideas.description: Implement a coordinated stewardship program across large healthcare systems or payer networks.
ideas.sourceTitle: Antimicrobial stewardship in the outpatient setting: A review and proposed framework

ideas.title: A National Pediatric Equity Dashboard Using Ecological Monitoring Protocols
ideas.description: This research idea adapts the solution from national ecological monitoring networks to scale up equity tracking in pediatrics. In ecosystem science, projects like NEON successfully aggregate heterogeneous local data into a national dashboard by enforcing standardized measurement protocols and data quality tiers across all monitoring sites. We propose creating a similar infrastructure: a National Pediatric Society Equity Dashboard built on mandated, standardized equity metrics (demographics, climate, advancement) collected uniformly from all training programs. This data would be centrally aggregated, quality-controlled, and published via an interactive public portal. This approach transforms isolated program data into a comparable, national dataset, enabling systemic analysis of equity trends, identification of high-performing models and disparity hotspots, and fostering accountability at a scale previously impossible, much like how ecological dashboards track forest health or water quality across a continent.

ideas.title: Vaccinated Transmission as a Percolation Problem: Modeling the Impact of Unvaccinated Clusters
ideas.description: This idea applies the framework of percolation theory from statistical physics to the original problem of how the unvaccinated impact transmission among the vaccinated. The analogous solution studies network connectivity through the lens of node and bond percolation, defining thresholds for system-wide behavior. Adapted to epidemiology, the population is modeled as a network where nodes have states (vaccinated/unvaccinated) and bonds have weighted transmission probabilities. The research would computationally and analytically determine how the clustering and size of unvaccinated groups act as 'insulating barriers' or, conversely, as 'bridge clusters' that enable the pathogen to percolate through the vaccinated network, thereby defining a critical coverage topology beyond which vaccinated transmission becomes self-sustaining.

ideas.title: Apply pathogen genomics to transmission linkage
ideas.description: Use viral genome sequencing to accurately link infection cases and infer transmission chains.
ideas.sourceTitle: COVID-19 Vaccines and SARS-CoV-2 Transmission in the Era of New Variants: A Review and Perspective

ideas.title: Justification-Required Nudge Architecture in EHRs to Preserve Deliberative Prescribing
ideas.description: This system integrates behavioral nudges into EHR prescribing pathways but mandates a micro-justification step whenever a nudge is presented. Clinicians must actively engage with the nudge by selecting a reason for accepting or overriding it from a short, clinically relevant list. This data feeds into a learning system that provides personalized feedback on decision patterns and refines nudge algorithms based on override justifications. The approach aims to reduce thoughtless adherence to defaults, reinforce clinical reasoning, and create a transparent record of how nudges influence care.

ideas.title: COVID-19 inevitably diverted resources from AMR
ideas.description: Investigate if pandemic response created any net-positive synergies for AMR surveillance.
ideas.sourceTitle: Confronting antimicrobial resistance beyond the COVID-19 pandemic and the 2020 US election

ideas.title: Equity-First Design via Ecological Niche Construction Theory
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original inverse problem of designing an equitable pediatric department by drawing an analogy from theoretical ecology's niche construction theory. The analogous problem is how organisms build their own optimal environments rather than just fitting into existing ones. The solution involves defining a target 'equitable niche' with specific parameters for fairness, access, and outcomes. Adapted to healthcare, the research would involve first-principles modeling to define this equity niche mathematically and then reverse-engineering the core components—organizational structures, resource flow algorithms, decision-making protocols, and adaptive feedback mechanisms—that would necessarily emerge to construct and maintain it. This results in a theoretical blueprint for a pediatric system whose fundamental operating logic is the continuous production of equity as its primary stable state.

ideas.title: National Policy Simulation via Ecosystem Stability Modeling
ideas.description: This research idea adapts a solution from ecology—using computational ecosystem stability models to predict the system-wide impact of a major perturbation—to the problem of scaling a medical education policy change to the national level. The analogous solution involves simulating complex, interconnected systems to forecast regime shifts and cascading effects. The adapted idea is to model the national GME landscape as an ecosystem, where residency programs and applicants interact. By inputting the policy change (universal holistic review and Step 1 de-emphasis) as a simulated perturbation, the model would project outcomes on system stability, equity, and efficiency, providing a predictive tool for policymakers to anticipate and mitigate unintended consequences before nationwide rollout.

ideas.title: Causal Disentanglement of Entangled Structural Exposures on Life-Course Outcomes
ideas.description: This study uses a causal discovery approach to map the interdependent effects of racialized, economic, and geographic structures on key life outcomes (e.g., wealth, health, incarceration). It leverages granular administrative and survey data within a longitudinal framework to apply algorithmic causal structure learning, identifying potential directed relationships among factors. The core analysis then uses targeted counterfactual models to quantify the direct, indirect, and joint effects of each structural dimension, moving beyond testing 'sole primary drivers' to mapping the system of causal pathways.

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Restoration Principles to Organizational Systems
ideas.description: This idea inverts the focus from fixing women to fixing systems by drawing an analogy from ecological restoration. In ecology, solutions moved from adapting individual species to degraded environments to repairing the foundational ecosystem. The analogous solution involved restoring core abiotic and biotic conditions (soil, water, biodiversity) to support thriving communities. Adapted to the organizational context, the research shifts from women's adaptation strategies to actively redesigning the organizational 'habitat.' It treats promotion, compensation, and leadership systems as interdependent components of a social ecosystem. The adapted idea involves diagnosing systemic dysfunctions and using principles of iterative design and systemic health metrics—such as equity flows, feedback loop resilience, and diversity of pathways—to create environments where a diverse range of talent can succeed organically, without requiring constant individual adaptation to a flawed system.

ideas.title: A CRISPR-Cas-based paper strip test for early detection of multidrug-resistant bacterial infections
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from the field of point-of-care viral diagnostics, which solved the problem of rapid, instrument-free pathogen detection using CRISPR-Cas systems (e.g., SHERLOCK, DETECTR), this research adapts that solution to bacterial antibiotic resistance. The analogous solution uses programmable Cas enzymes to bind target nucleic acids and trigger a visual signal on a paper strip. The adapted idea is to develop a rapid, accessible diagnostic test that programs CRISPR-Cas machinery to detect genetic signatures of multidrug resistance (e.g., resistance genes or mutations) directly from clinical specimens. The outcome would be a simple, low-cost, multiplexed paper-strip test that provides a clear colorimetric readout within an hour at the point of care, enabling early identification of resistant infections without complex laboratory infrastructure.

ideas.title: Adversarial Equity Engineering: Proving Policy Neutrality Through Stress-Testing
ideas.description: This research proposes a framework where no policy is assumed to be effectively colorblind. Instead, policies must earn a designation of 'disparity-neutral' by surviving a rigorous, adversarial review process. The system combines predictive analytics, using real-world data on existing racial inequities, with structured red-teaming to uncover and correct for hidden bias in policy mechanics. The outcome is not a claim of colorblindness, but a demonstrable, stress-tested resilience against perpetuating racial inequities, shifting the burden of proof onto policy designers.

ideas.title: Transfer vaccine platform technology
ideas.description: Apply novel vaccine platforms (mRNA, viral vectors) from other diseases to CMV vaccine development.
ideas.sourceTitle: Cytomegalovirus infection in liver transplant recipients: updates on clinical management.

ideas.title: Integrated Genomic-Epidemiological Probabilistic Framework for Transmission Inference
ideas.description: This redesigned system moves beyond simple genomic clustering by formally integrating viral sequence data with detailed case-level epidemiological metadata (e.g., contact history, location, timing) within a unified Bayesian or likelihood-based inference framework. It explicitly models the possibilities of unsampled cases and common sources, generating probabilistic transmission networks rather than deterministic chains. The output is a set of statistically supported linkage hypotheses with quantified uncertainty, which is more robust for guiding public health interventions and understanding outbreak dynamics.

ideas.title: Apply social network analysis to disclosure patterns
ideas.description: Map how disclosure spreads through resident cohorts via peer influence.
ideas.sourceTitle: Barriers to Disclosure of Disability and Request for Accommodations Among First-Year Resident Physicians in the US

ideas.title: Study sustainability of diversity gains
ideas.description: Investigate whether increases in URM matriculants are maintained over multiple recruitment cycles.
ideas.sourceTitle: Interventions Associated With Racial and Ethnic Diversity in US Graduate Medical Education

ideas.title: Scale down to individual mucosal immunity measurement
ideas.description: Study transmission reduction by measuring vaccine-induced mucosal immunity at the individual level.
ideas.sourceTitle: COVID-19 Vaccines and SARS-CoV-2 Transmission in the Era of New Variants: A Review and Perspective

ideas.title: Adapt legal discovery for policy audit
ideas.description: Systematically audit healthcare policies using legal discovery methods to uncover discriminatory intent or effect.
ideas.sourceTitle: Racism, Not Race, Drives Inequity Across the COVID-19 Continuum.

ideas.title: Mapping the Informal Support Ecosystem: A Systems Biology Approach
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that all needed supports can be formally requested. Drawing an analogy from systems biology's approach to metabolic 'dark matter'—essential but uncatalogued biochemical components—this project adapts its solution. In biochemistry, techniques like metabolomics detect system outputs to map unknown, transient necessities. Adapted to social and institutional contexts, the research would employ ethnographic observation and qualitative analysis of environments where individuals thrive due to informal supports. By analyzing these successful functional outcomes, the study aims to reverse-engineer and characterize the taxonomy of supports that are inherently informal, tacit, relational, or contextually dynamic, thereby mapping the limits of formal accommodation systems.

ideas.title: Longitudinal study of structural interventions on health using ecological restoration monitoring frameworks
ideas.description: This research adapts the longitudinal monitoring frameworks used in ecological restoration science to study structural health interventions. In ecology, the recovery of a system after a root-cause intervention (like restoring a water flow) is tracked by measuring multiple interacting indicators over long periods against a reference state. The adapted study would implement this by treating a community targeted by a structural policy (e.g., a housing guarantee or minimum wage increase) as a complex system. It would establish defined cohorts as 'monitoring plots' to longitudinally track a matrix of structural ('abiotic') drivers (policy uptake, economic indicators, social cohesion) and health ('biotic') outcomes. The core analytical innovation is to model the recovery trajectories and lag times between structural change and population health improvement, using comparable 'reference' communities to benchmark success, thereby identifying the critical pathways and necessary conditions for systemic health equity to be restored.

ideas.title: Apply Traffic Flow Optimization Principles to EHR Prescribing Nudges
ideas.description: This idea adapts solutions from transportation engineering's traffic flow optimization to the problem of nudging clinicians toward appropriate prescribing in EHRs. In traffic theory, systems are designed to guide drivers efficiently using subtle, embedded cues like lane markings and timed signals, rather than rigid commands. The adapted research idea applies this by designing the EHR prescribing interface as an optimized 'decision roadway.' This involves implementing sequenced defaults, visual priority cues (such as highlighting guideline-concordant or formulary-preferred options), and strategically timed decision points that naturally and smoothly guide clinician choices. The goal is to reduce cognitive load and prescription errors by creating an information architecture that facilitates appropriate flow, minimizing friction and 'decision congestion' without compromising clinical autonomy.

ideas.title: Community AMR Sentinel Nodes with Distributed Ledger Verification
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from distributed environmental sensor networks to the challenge of scaling down AMR surveillance. In environmental science, networks of low-cost, localized sensors (e.g., for air quality) use cryptographic methods like blockchain hashing to verify data integrity from disparate sources. Adapted to AMR, community clinics would be equipped with simple, standardized point-of-care antimicrobial susceptibility test devices. Each test run generates a result that is immediately signed with a unique, secure clinic identifier and a precise timestamp. This 'data packet' is then appended to a distributed ledger or uses a similar verification protocol. The system creates a tamper-evident, decentralized record of local resistance patterns, enabling real-time, trustworthy community-level AMR data aggregation for regional and national surveillance without the delays and costs of shipping all samples to central reference labs.

ideas.title: Adaptive Screening: A Multi-Threshold Model from Conservation Biology
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that universal screening is the singular optimal goal by drawing an analogy from conservation biology, where universal species monitoring is impossible. The analogous solution is adaptive resource allocation, using dynamic risk factors (like population trends) to tier surveillance intensity. Adapted to screening, the idea is to investigate a dynamic, multi-threshold screening model. This system would continuously stratify a population into risk tiers based on evolving factors and adapt the screening protocol (method, frequency) for each tier, aiming to prove that such an adaptive model is more efficient and pragmatically optimal than a static universal or one-time targeted approach.

ideas.title: Adaptive Immunization Schedules Using Platform-Specific Reactogenicity Feedback to Minimize Vaccine Fatigue
ideas.description: This research investigates the design of adaptive, personalized CMV vaccination schedules by leveraging the distinct reactogenicity profiles of novel vaccine platforms (e.g., mRNA vs. viral vector). The core hypothesis is that systematic, individual-reported feedback on side effects (pain, fever, fatigue) after initial doses can inform algorithm-driven adjustments to the timing, dose, or even platform of subsequent booster immunizations. The goal is to maintain or improve vaccine regimen effectiveness and completion rates by minimizing the physical and psychological burden that leads to dropout, a form of 'vaccine fatigue.' This represents a novel convergence of vaccinology and human-factors engineering, moving beyond static schedules to create responsive, patient-centric immunization strategies.

ideas.title: Apply pandemic forecasting models to AMR spread
ideas.description: Use epidemiological models from virology to predict geographic spread of resistant bacteria.
ideas.sourceTitle: Confronting antimicrobial resistance beyond the COVID-19 pandemic and the 2020 US election

ideas.title: The US leadership is essential for global AMR
ideas.description: Examine global AMR progress scenarios without US political or financial leadership.
ideas.sourceTitle: Confronting antimicrobial resistance beyond the COVID-19 pandemic and the 2020 US election

ideas.title: Forensic Policy Audit via Multi-Source Evidence Triangulation and Causal Process Tracing
ideas.description: This system audits healthcare policies for discrimination by forensically reconstructing the policy-making process and its effects, eliminating dependency on compelled internal discovery. It triangulates evidence from three streams: computational text and version analysis of public policy documents, statistical audit of administrative outcome data to pinpoint disparate effects, and strategic use of open records laws. These inputs feed into a causal process-tracing framework that models the policy pathway, identifies critical decision nodes, and evaluates the plausibility of discriminatory mechanisms against alternative explanations, producing a robust, evidence-based audit report.

ideas.title: Adapting Plant Systemic Acquired Resistance for CMV Vaccine Adjuvant Design
ideas.description: The analogous problem from plant pathology is protecting a vulnerable organism (a crop) from a pervasive pathogen using a pre-emptive, systemically primed defense state (Systemic Acquired Resistance), rather than relying solely on direct pathogen confrontation. This was solved by applying chemical inducers of SAR, like salicylic acid analogs, to 'train' the plant's immune system for a heightened future response. Adapted to the CMV vaccine context, the research idea is to develop and test clinical vaccine strategies that incorporate adjuvant systems designed to chemically 'prime' the recipient's immune system. By identifying agents that safely induce a broad, durable state of antiviral readiness—enhancing innate sensors and promoting robust memory cell formation—this approach aims to create a more protective and resilient immune response in immunocompromised transplant patients receiving a CMV vaccine, potentially overcoming the limitations of conventional adjuvants.

ideas.title: Use predictive analytics from college admissions
ideas.description: Adapt validated non-cognitive success predictors from undergraduate admissions for holistic GME review.
ideas.sourceTitle: Interventions Associated With Racial and Ethnic Diversity in US Graduate Medical Education

ideas.title: Relax assumption that non-disclosure is always problematic
ideas.description: Examine scenarios where not requesting accommodations is a rational, strategic choice.
ideas.sourceTitle: Barriers to Disclosure of Disability and Request for Accommodations Among First-Year Resident Physicians in the US

ideas.title: Applying Structural Engineering's Load-Bearing Analysis to Vaccine Efficacy Against Severe Disease
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that vaccine impact on serious illness is fully understood by proposing a novel analytical framework borrowed from structural engineering. The analogous problem—predicting when a bridge will fail under changing loads and material fatigue—was solved through dynamic load testing and fatigue modeling focused on critical failure points. Adapting this solution, the research would re-evaluate vaccine protection against severe disease not as a static percentage but as a dynamic 'biological load-bearing capacity.' It would model how this capacity erodes due to viral evolution (changing the 'load') and waning immunity (degrading the 'material'), using high-resolution clinical and virological data to identify predictive thresholds for severe disease breakthrough. This approach aims to build failure prediction models that can inform the timing and design of booster strategies, moving from a reactive to a predictive understanding of severe disease protection.

ideas.title: Apply organizational psychology safety climate surveys
ideas.description: Use validated psychological safety scales to measure residency program supportiveness.
ideas.sourceTitle: Barriers to Disclosure of Disability and Request for Accommodations Among First-Year Resident Physicians in the US

ideas.title: Adapting Integrated Pest Management (IPM) to CMV Resistance
