ideas.description: This idea adapts the core principles of Integrated Pest Management (IPM) from agriculture to address the inevitability of drug-resistant CMV. In IPM, pesticide resistance is managed by combining biological controls, cultural practices, and mechanical methods, using chemicals only as a last resort within a monitored system. The adapted research idea proposes to design a comprehensive CMV management strategy that similarly de-emphasizes antiviral drugs as the frontline. This 'Integrated Pathogen Management' protocol would combine adoptive T-cell therapies (biological control), stringent infection control measures (cultural/physical control), and immunomodulation, using antiviral drugs in a targeted, sparing manner guided by continuous resistance and viral load monitoring. The goal is to develop a sustainable system where the pathogen's ability to develop resistance is minimized because drug pressure is no longer the dominant selective force.

ideas.title: Apply geological stratigraphy principles to identify policy discontinuities for causal inference
ideas.description: This idea adapts the core methodological principle from geological stratigraphy—using clear discontinuities to separate and compare adjacent temporal layers—to the problem of estimating causal effects of historical policies on health. In geology, sharp boundaries between rock strata signal major environmental shifts and allow for causal inference about past events. Analogously, we can identify sharp, date-specific implementations of historical policies (e.g., the 1855 Nuisances Removal Act in a specific city, or the 1963 introduction of measles vaccine in a country) as creating a clear temporal 'policy stratum.' By applying a regression discontinuity design (RDD) across this precise implementation date, we can estimate the local average treatment effect of the policy by comparing health outcomes (e.g., mortality rates, disease incidence) in time periods immediately preceding and following the policy's start, thereby controlling for slow-moving historical confounders in a manner analogous to how geologists control for gradual sedimentation processes.

ideas.title: Implement structured interviews from corporate HR
ideas.description: Apply standardized, competency-based interview rubrics used in corporate diversity hiring to residency interviews.
ideas.sourceTitle: Interventions Associated With Racial and Ethnic Diversity in US Graduate Medical Education

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Niche Modeling to Map Structural Risk Landscapes for Hypertension
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from ecology's Species Distribution Modeling (SDM) to the context of scaling a structural inequity framework to chronic diseases like hypertension. In ecology, SDMs integrate multiple environmental variables to define a species' niche and predict its geographic distribution. The adapted idea applies this by treating 'poor hypertension outcomes' analogously to a species, and the 'structural inequities' (e.g., policies, resource deserts, environmental stressors) as the environmental variables. By layering and analyzing these multi-source geospatial inequity data, the research will generate predictive maps of 'structural risk niches' for hypertension disparities, identifying high-risk areas where systemic factors converge. This provides a powerful, visual tool for targeting policy interventions and resource allocation to dismantle the root causes of health inequity.

ideas.title: Dynamic, model-informed closed-loop control for personalized antiviral prophylaxis
ideas.description: This system continuously optimizes antiviral prophylaxis using a real-time, closed-loop control framework. It employs frequent, passive monitoring of immune and inflammatory biomarkers via wearable or point-of-care devices, feeding this data into a patient-specific computational model of immune reconstitution dynamics. A reinforcement learning algorithm, trained on historical patient outcome data, uses the model's predictions to make weekly or daily dosage adjustments, minimizing the combined risk of breakthrough infection and drug-related adverse events. This approach manages uncertainty and biological noise by treating prophylaxis as a dynamic control problem rather than a one-time duration decision.

ideas.title: Comparative Multi-Scale Modeling of Consistent vs. Context-Specific Transmission Mechanisms in High-Density Neighborhoods
ideas.description: This research employs a multi-site, multi-scale modeling approach to overcome the non-generalizability of single-neighborhood studies. It constructs parallel agent-based models for several high-density Hispanic/Latinx neighborhoods in different metropolitan areas, using a shared core structure to ensure comparability. The system is designed to distinguish between fundamental micro-mechanisms of spread that are consistent across contexts and those that are heavily dependent on unique local factors. This comparative design produces findings that are both locally precise and inform broader public health strategies for similar community structures elsewhere.

ideas.title: Political attention is scarce for concurrent crises
ideas.description: Challenge the notion that political systems cannot focus on AMR and COVID-19.
ideas.sourceTitle: Confronting antimicrobial resistance beyond the COVID-19 pandemic and the 2020 US election

ideas.title: Applying Error-Correcting Code Architecture to Hospital Policy Design
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original idea by drawing an analogy from Information Theory. The analogous problem is data corruption during transmission, solved by embedding error-correcting codes into system architecture. We adapt this solution to hospital policy and space design. The core idea is to design institutional protocols and physical workflows (e.g., for triage, resource allocation, or care transitions) that inherently contain structured, redundant decision points and verification steps—akin to an error-correcting code. This architectural approach would automatically detect and correct for deviations that may stem from individual or systemic bias, making the reduction of biased outcomes a built-in, scalable feature of the hospital's operational 'code' rather than relying solely on training or post-audit correction.

ideas.title: Adapting Ozone Layer Governance for AMR Stewardship
ideas.description: This idea proposes adapting the successful governance framework of the Montreal Protocol on Substances that Deplete the Ozone Layer to manage the global environmental dimension of Antimicrobial Resistance (AMR). The analogous problem—a diffuse, long-term global threat caused by specific, identifiable chemical agents—was solved through a binding treaty targeting those specific agents with phased reduction mandates and financial mechanisms for equitable compliance. Adapted to AMR, this would involve creating an international treaty that identifies and lists priority antimicrobial compounds and resistance genes as 'controlled substances' for environmental release. The treaty would mandate verifiable, phased reductions in their discharge from pharmaceutical manufacturing, agriculture, and healthcare systems, supported by a multilateral fund to finance surveillance, wastewater treatment infrastructure, and the adoption of non-antimicrobial alternatives, particularly in developing nations. This moves beyond voluntary frameworks to create accountable, measurable, and financed global action against environmental AMR drivers.

ideas.title: Design policy to sustain AMR focus post-crisis
ideas.description: Create governance structures that maintain political attention on AMR after acute pandemics.
ideas.sourceTitle: Confronting antimicrobial resistance beyond the COVID-19 pandemic and the 2020 US election

ideas.title: From Recognition to Revision: Forcing the Application of Personalized Bias Feedback to Specific Judgments
ideas.description: This research tests whether behavioral correction occurs only when bias recognition is forced into a concrete, self-relevant revision task. Participants complete a decision-making exercise (e.g., resume screening) and receive algorithmic feedback highlighting a specific bias pattern in their own choices. Crucially, they must then re-justify each affected decision in writing, either adhering to or contradicting the bias feedback. The core measure is the change in their subsequent decisions compared to a control group that receives the same feedback but without the forced revision step, isolating the effect of mandatory application over passive recognition.

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Tracer Techniques to Map Systemic Bias Pathways
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from ecology to challenge the assumption that bias is always overt. In ecology, scientists use tracer techniques (e.g., stable isotopes) and network models to map the hidden pathways and amplification of pollutants or nutrients in complex systems, moving beyond simple endpoint measurements. The adapted idea applies this analogical solution by designing 'bias tracer' studies within organizational or algorithmic workflows. By introducing subtly varied elements (e.g., in credentials, work styles, or communication) and tracking their propagation through multiple evaluation and decision points, researchers can use systems analysis to map the latent pathways through which small, systemic biases accumulate and cause disproportionate harm, identifying critical leverage points for intervention that are invisible to analyses focused solely on overt discrimination or final outcomes.

ideas.title: Epidemiological Back-Calculation for Policy Attribution
ideas.description: This research adapts the cosmological inverse problem solution to infer historical policy failures from health data. In cosmology, the distribution of dark matter is inferred by back-calculating from the observed large-scale structure of the universe. Here, we treat spatial and demographic patterns of disease (e.g., mortality rates, morbidity clusters) as the observable 'structure.' The adapted method involves creating a simulation framework that generates a massive ensemble of possible policy histories—each with different combinations, timings, and scales of failures (e.g., underinvestment, misallocation, delayed implementation). These are fed into a detailed epidemiological model to produce simulated disease outcome maps. Using simulation-based inference techniques, we then compare these simulated maps to real-world health outcome data to statistically identify the policy failure 'parameters' (type, magnitude, location, timing) that have the highest posterior probability of causing the observed patterns, thereby solving the inverse problem from health outcomes to policy failure.

ideas.title: Platform-Antigen Pairing Optimization for CMV-Specific Protective Immunity
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond simple platform transfer by systematically pairing novel delivery platforms with CMV-specific antigenic targets to overcome viral immune evasion. A high-throughput immunogenicity screening pipeline tests multiple platform-antigen combinations in parallel to identify those that best induce durable, high-quality T-cell responses and neutralizing antibodies against key CMV vulnerabilities. The selected lead candidate is then engineered based on this empirical data, ensuring the vaccine platform is specifically optimized for the unique challenges of CMV immunobiology rather than merely adapted from other diseases.

ideas.title: Household studies are the optimal transmission setting
ideas.description: Challenge the focus on households by studying transmission in workplaces or communal settings.
ideas.sourceTitle: COVID-19 Vaccines and SARS-CoV-2 Transmission in the Era of New Variants: A Review and Perspective

ideas.title: Cognitive Tunneling in Debiasing: Isolating the Mechanisms and Load Profiles of Counterproductive Bias Awareness
ideas.description: This research program moves beyond documenting performance costs to experimentally dissect the causal cognitive pathways by which bias awareness can become detrimental. Using a combination of dual-task paradigms, cognitive load manipulation, and process-tracing measures, it aims to identify specific mechanisms (e.g., regulatory depletion, attentional narrowing, heuristic substitution) that mediate negative outcomes. The goal is to develop a predictive, mechanistic model that links intervention characteristics to cognitive load profiles and performance outcomes. This model will directly inform the design of next-generation debiasing tools that are cognitively efficient and avoid the pitfalls of current awareness-based approaches.

ideas.title: Causal Triangulation Framework for Prescribing Disparities Using Multi-Method Econometric Convergence
ideas.description: This research system applies a suite of econometric causal inference techniques in parallel—including instrumental variables, difference-in-differences, regression discontinuity, and matching estimators—to identify drivers of prescribing disparities. The core innovation is a formal triangulation protocol where results are only considered robust if multiple methods, each relying on different and largely non-overlapping identifying assumptions, converge on a consistent causal estimate. This approach directly mitigates the fatal flaw of any single method's untestable assumptions by demanding cross-validation from independent causal logic. The final output is a set of causal drivers validated through methodological convergence, accompanied by a quantitative assessment of robustness across the ensemble of models.

ideas.title: Cognitive Load Theory for Political Crisis Management
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that political systems cannot focus on both AMR and COVID-19 by drawing an analogy from Cognitive Psychology and Educational Science. The analogous problem is the limited capacity of working memory in learning complex topics, solved through Cognitive Load Theory (CLT) by managing intrinsic load, optimizing germane load, and minimizing extraneous load via techniques like segmentation and dual-modality presentation. The adapted research idea applies CLT principles to political crisis management, proposing the design of communication and policy frameworks that structure information and integrate messaging for concurrent health crises. By creating cognitive 'scaffolds' and reusable policy templates, the research aims to demonstrate that political attention can be effectively allocated to multiple complex issues simultaneously, thereby increasing systemic capacity beyond a presumed singular focus.

ideas.title: Use discrete choice experiments from health economics
ideas.description: Model residents' trade-offs between accommodation attributes using choice experiments.
ideas.sourceTitle: Barriers to Disclosure of Disability and Request for Accommodations Among First-Year Resident Physicians in the US

ideas.title: Adaptive Incentive & Surveillance Platforms for AMR: Agile Systems Over Slow Treaties
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond slow, top-down climate treaty models by designing agile, multi-level governance systems that generate immediate local action. It combines real-time, integrated genomic and prescription surveillance with transparent accounting for antimicrobial use and resistance, creating a verifiable 'AMR footprint'. This data infrastructure enables rapid policy experiments, direct financial incentives for stewardship (e.g., payments for reduced resistance rates), and dynamic regulatory responses, transforming AMR management into a continuously adaptive system rather than a static global framework.

ideas.title: Use geospatial mapping for HAI and resource disparity
ideas.description: Apply GIS mapping to visualize overlap between HAI hotspots, hospital resources, and community socioeconomic vulnerability.
ideas.sourceTitle: Racial/Ethnic Inequities in Healthcare-associated Infections Under the Shadow of Structural Racism: Narrative Review and Call to Action

ideas.title: Challenge pre-emptive therapy's reliance on monitoring
ideas.description: Question if available monitoring tools are sensitive and timely enough for optimal pre-emptive strategy.
ideas.sourceTitle: Cytomegalovirus infection in liver transplant recipients: updates on clinical management.

ideas.title: Identifying 'Transmission Breakpoints' in Vaccines Using Ecological Stability Theory
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that vaccines always reduce transmission compared to the unvaccinated. By drawing an analogy from ecology and population dynamics, it adapts the solution of modeling system stability to identify critical breakpoints. In ecology, models pinpoint parameter thresholds that cause regime shifts in disease prevalence. Adapted here, the idea is to use multi-parameter transmission dynamics modeling (incorporating variant-specific immune escape, transmissibility, and vaccine efficacy profiles) to theoretically predict and then empirically test for conditions—specific variant mutations or background immunity landscapes—that constitute a 'transmission breakpoint.' At this breakpoint, the net effect of vaccination on reducing *population-level* transmission of that variant becomes statistically indistinguishable from zero, even if the vaccine still protects against severe disease. This reframes the question from a binary 'does it reduce transmission?' to 'under what precise conditions does the transmission reduction benefit vanish?'

ideas.title: Scale up to national policy impact modeling
ideas.description: Model how national stewardship policies differentially affect subpopulations to predict and mitigate equity impacts.
ideas.sourceTitle: Health Equity and Antibiotic Prescribing in the United States: A Systematic Scoping Review.

ideas.title: Dynamic Mobility-Integrated Network for Real-Time Pathogen Exposure and Justice (D-MINER)
ideas.description: This system moves beyond static exposure mapping by fusing traditional environmental justice indicators with anonymized, aggregated human mobility data to model real-time exposure risk. It constructs a dynamic network where nodes represent neighborhoods characterized by EJ factors, and weighted edges represent population movement between them, simulating pathogen transmission pathways. The model outputs time-varying risk forecasts, identifying not only vulnerable residential areas but also critical mobility corridors and points of convergence that drive transmission. This enables proactive, justice-focused interventions that target both structural vulnerability and dynamic behavioral patterns.

ideas.title: Dynamic Network Mapping and Adaptive Sequencing of Gatekeeper Influence
ideas.description: This research first tests the validity of the four-group gatekeeper model within specific operational contexts, mapping the real-world network of influence and interdependencies among actors. It then employs adaptive experimental designs, such as SMART trials, to test intervention sequences not on predefined static groups, but on identified network roles and relationships. This allows the research to determine not only which gatekeepers are most critical, but under what conditions their influence shifts and how interventions on one node propagate through the system, creating a robust, context-aware model of necessary intervention sequences.

ideas.title: Dynamic Emergent Equity Modeling for National Policy (DEEPN-Policy)
ideas.description: This research system employs a large-scale, computationally agent-based model (ABM) to simulate how national stewardship policies create, exacerbate, or mitigate inequities within a synthetic population. Instead of pre-defining subpopulations, agents are endowed with a multidimensional attribute space (socioeconomic, geographic, health, etc.), and clustering algorithms identify emergent groups experiencing disproportionate impacts as policies unfold. The system is designed for iterative, scenario-based policy testing, integrating real-time data feeds to continuously calibrate the simulation, thereby predicting not just known but novel and intersectional equity failures before they manifest at scale.

ideas.title: Inappropriate use is primarily a knowledge deficit
ideas.description: Explore if time pressure, diagnostic uncertainty, or habit are stronger drivers than knowledge.
ideas.sourceTitle: Antimicrobial stewardship in the outpatient setting: A review and proposed framework

ideas.title: Phase-Locked Pilot Implementation with Controlled Feedback Loops
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from control systems engineering in Electrical Engineering, this idea adapts the 'hardware-in-the-loop' (HIL) simulation method. In HIL testing, a new component is connected to a real-time simulated model of the larger system in a lab, allowing safe performance validation and parameter tuning. The adapted research idea is a 'Phase-Locked Pilot Implementation with Controlled Feedback Loops.' A single intervention, like committee training, is tested in a small, real academic program. Crucially, the program's interface with the broader university system is not fully opened; instead, interactions with central administration, other departments, and institutional policies are simulated through designed scenarios and role-played inputs. This creates a 'program-in-the-loop' pilot, a high-fidelity, contained environment to study the intervention's behavior, identify unintended consequences, and optimize its design under realistic but safe conditions, thereby de-risking the subsequent system-wide scaling process.

ideas.title: Data-Assimilated, Adaptive Agent-Based Modeling for Epidemic Forecasting and Intervention Planning
ideas.description: This research system combines a high-fidelity, socially-structured agent-based model with a robust data assimilation engine. The model continuously ingests diverse, real-world data streams to dynamically calibrate network connectivity, intervention adherence, and disease transmission parameters. This closed-loop approach allows the simulation to adaptively reflect observed epidemic trajectories and behavioral feedback, moving beyond static assumptions. The result is a continuously validated digital twin capable of generating more reliable, scenario-specific forecasts for evaluating the potential impact of public health interventions.

ideas.title: Adapt network theory to model transmission dynamics
ideas.description: Model transmission using contact network analysis from social science and epidemiology.
ideas.sourceTitle: COVID-19 Vaccines and SARS-CoV-2 Transmission in the Era of New Variants: A Review and Perspective

ideas.title: Modeling Historical Inequities as Ecological Tipping Points in Pandemic Vulnerability
ideas.description: This research challenges the linear assumption by proposing that historical inequities impact present-day pandemic vulnerability through non-linear, threshold-based dynamics analogous to ecological regime shifts. Drawing from ecosystem ecology's solutions for modeling tipping points, we adapt regime shift theory and bifurcation analysis. The adapted idea treats historical policies as cumulative stressors on a community's 'health resilience ecosystem.' By modeling these stressors (e.g., decades of discriminatory housing, education, and economic policies) and their interactions with contemporary factors, we aim to identify critical thresholds where vulnerability spikes disproportionately and to find statistical early-warning signals (like increased autocorrelation in health indicators) that precede such shifts, offering a predictive, non-linear model of pandemic risk.

ideas.title: Adapting Insecticide Resistance Monitoring to Quantify Vaccine Efficacy Against Variant Transmission
ideas.description: This idea adapts the established solution from agricultural entomology for quantifying insecticide resistance to the problem of measuring vaccine-mediated transmission reduction against SARS-CoV-2 variants. In entomology, standardized bioassays generate dose-response curves for susceptible and resistant insect strains, allowing calculation of a resistance ratio (e.g., LC50 resistant/LC50 susceptible) to precisely quantify the magnitude of efficacy loss. Adapted to virology, the research would develop a controlled, reproducible challenge model (using engineered susceptible cell lines or standardized animal models) to test the potency of vaccine-elicited immune components (e.g., neutralizing antibodies from sera or mucosal surfaces) against both an ancestral reference virus and an emerging variant of concern. By establishing dose-response curves for viral replication or transmission proxies, the core output would be a calculated 'Transmission Reduction Resistance Ratio' (TRRR) or similar metric. This ratio would precisely quantify how much more immune effector concentration is required to achieve the same level of transmission blockade for the variant, providing a standardized, comparable measure of the magnitude of transmission reduction erosion caused by viral evolution, critical for informing vaccine updates and public health policy.

ideas.title: Adapting Environmental Sensor Network Principles for Clinical Screening Alerts
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from environmental sensor networks used for industrial safety, this idea adapts the principle of using distributed, simple sensors in non-primary locations to capture missed events. The analogous solution deploys reliable sensors at high-traffic or critical 'choke points' to feed a central alert system. Adapted to scaling screening alerts to emergency departments and urgent care centers, the research would design and implement lightweight, context-specific clinical 'sensor' prompts within these settings' workflows. These prompts, triggered by discrete data points like specific diagnoses or vital signs, would act as screening opportunity detectors. The aggregated data from these distributed points would then provide a system-wide view of captured and missed screenings, enabling targeted interventions without requiring a full-scale integration of the primary care alert logic.

ideas.title: Signal Detection in Accommodation Requests: Differentiating True Need from Strategic Noise
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution to the signal detection problem from communications engineering to the context of disability accommodations. In communications, statistical models differentiate true signals from noise to optimize system performance. The analogous solution involves using Signal Detection Theory to set decision thresholds that balance detection rates and false alarms. Adapted to this research, the idea is to model accommodation requests as 'signals' of genuine need amidst 'noise' from non-clinical motivators. The study would develop a framework to establish evidence-based evaluation thresholds, aiming to objectively identify when requests are likely unnecessary by quantifying and controlling for confounding factors like perceived advantage or policy gaming, thereby scaling the dual objectives of access and resource integrity.

ideas.title: Adaptive Mesh Refinement for Hyper-Local Disparity Mapping
ideas.description: This idea adapts the computational technique of Adaptive Mesh Refinement (AMR) from Computational Fluid Dynamics to the problem of mapping COVID-19 disparities and structural factors at a hyper-local scale. In CFD, AMR solves the problem of modeling complex systems by using a coarse simulation grid everywhere, then dynamically creating finer-resolution grids only in regions where critical changes are occurring (like turbulence or pressure gradients), optimizing the detail-to-resource ratio. Adapted to public health geography, this means developing an algorithmic framework that begins with a broad, coarse-grained map (e.g., county or ZIP code-level disparities). The system then automatically identifies 'regions of interest'—areas with steep gradients in case rates, mortality, vaccination uptake, or underlying structural factor indices—and recursively increases the analytical resolution down to the census tract or block group level specifically within those zones. The output is a multi-scale disparity map that provides neighborhood-level detail where it matters most for intervention, while efficiently summarizing less variable regions at a broader scale, thereby making hyper-local analysis computationally tractable and strategically focused.

ideas.title: Personalized Prophylaxis via Immune Reconstitution Biomarker Kinetics
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from structural engineering, where the curing duration of concrete is dynamically determined by real-time sensor data on material properties rather than a fixed timeline. The analogous problem in medicine is the fixed-duration antiviral prophylaxis given after procedures like hematopoietic stem cell transplantation. The adapted research idea is to tailor prophylaxis duration by monitoring the kinetics of individual patient immune reconstitution markers (e.g., TRECs, CD4+ subsets, TCR diversity). Prophylaxis would be discontinued only when a patient's biomarker profile reaches a validated threshold indicating sufficient immune reconstitution to prevent viral reactivation, thereby personalizing treatment and potentially shortening unnecessary drug exposure.

ideas.title: Closed-Loop Restorative Systems: Embedding Process Control in Community-Driven Trust Repair
ideas.description: This research integrates restorative justice circles with industrial statistical process control to create a dynamic, community-informed equity management system. Key relational and procedural metrics (e.g., perceived fairness, follow-through on commitments) derived from circle dialogues are tracked using control charts to establish baseline trust levels and detect statistically significant regressions or improvements. This allows institutions to move from episodic, reactive trust-building to a continuous, data-responsive practice, where alerts from the control charts trigger the reconvening of circles for dialogue and repair, closing the feedback loop. The synergy transforms qualitative restorative processes into a sustainable, measurable framework for institutional accountability and adaptive equity intervention.

ideas.title: Disentangling the Stigma of Needing Help: A Causal Test of Disability vs. Dependency Aversion
ideas.description: This research program employs controlled experiments to causally separate stigma attributed to a disability from stigma triggered by the act of requesting accommodation or assistance. Using vignette studies with randomized factors (disability type/visibility x help request type/none), it measures explicit attitudes, implicit associations, and behavioral intentions toward hypothetical individuals. The design is extended with field experiments measuring real-world helping behaviors and neuroimaging components to assess non-conscious threat or aversion responses, providing a robust, multi-level test of whether stigma is fundamentally driven by perceptions of dependency rather than impairment.

ideas.title: Community-Sovereign Accountability Circles with Binding Institutional Pre-Commitments
ideas.description: This redesigned system mandates that institutions undergo a transparent, community-led audit of policies and power structures prior to any dialogue. Based on audit findings, institutions must publicly commit to specific, measurable, and binding policy or resource reallocation actions. Restorative circles are then convened, not as initial trust-builders, but as community-facilitated spaces to review institutional progress, hold leadership accountable for commitments, and negotiate further steps. This shifts the model from symbolic dialogue to a structured process of verified institutional action and community-controlled oversight, ensuring tangible change precedes and underpins the relational repair process.

ideas.title: Apply environmental justice mapping to disease exposure
ideas.description: Use GIS and environmental justice mapping tools to spatially correlate pollution, redlining, and COVID-19 hotspots.
ideas.sourceTitle: Racial Disparity of Coronavirus Disease 2019 in African American Communities

ideas.title: Apply econometric causal inference to historical policies
ideas.description: Use instrumental variables or difference-in-differences to estimate causal effect of past policies on health.
ideas.sourceTitle: Racial Disparity of Coronavirus Disease 2019 in African American Communities

ideas.title: Apply seismic hazard mapping techniques to model COVID-19 risk
ideas.description: This idea adapts the probabilistic, multi-layer mapping techniques from seismic hazard assessment in seismology to model COVID-19 risk. In seismology, hazard maps integrate diverse data layers (fault activity, soil type, historical seismicity) to forecast the probability and intensity of ground shaking. The adapted solution applies this framework to pandemic risk by using GIS and exposure modeling to integrate analogous layers: epidemiological data (case rates, transmission dynamics), healthcare infrastructure vulnerability, and socio-economic exposure factors derived from environmental justice methodologies. The output is a dynamic, probabilistic risk map that forecasts not just where cases might occur, but the potential 'intensity' of outbreaks and associated healthcare system strain, providing a more nuanced tool for targeted public health intervention and resource allocation.

ideas.title: Stigma as a Signal of Social Cost: Testing the Request-for-Help Hypothesis
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from behavioral ecology—costly signaling theory—to challenge the assumption that stigma attaches primarily to a disability. In animal signaling, receivers (e.g., parent birds) evaluate the honesty of a signal (e.g., a begging call) based on its cost to the signaler. Applying this analogy, we hypothesize that human stigma functions similarly: it is a negative social response triggered not by a disability per se, but by the perception of a request for help as a 'cheap signal'—one that imposes a high burden on the helper while seeming to cost the requester little. The adapted research idea involves designing controlled experiments and vignette studies to dissect requests for help into variables of perceived cost (e.g., psychological vulnerability, offered reciprocity) and benefit. By measuring attitudes toward requesters with and without disabilities while holding these signaling variables constant, we can test if stigma is more accurately predicted by the economics of the help transaction than by the presence of a disability label.

ideas.title: Network-Level Intervention Design for the Gatekeeper Ecosystem
ideas.description: This research maps the interdependent incentive structures and resource flows connecting academic centers, societies, journals, and funding agencies as a coherent ecosystem. Using agent-based modeling informed by institutional data, it simulates how interventions targeting one node propagate or are resisted by the network. The core output is a suite of coordinated, multi-point intervention packages designed to shift the ecosystem's equilibrium by simultaneously altering key coupling mechanisms (e.g., linking funding criteria to journal review reforms and society endorsement policies), thereby overcoming the systemic inertia that defeats isolated reforms.

ideas.title: Assume all needed accommodations are formally requestable
ideas.description: Explore if some needed supports are inherently informal or impossible to formally request.
ideas.sourceTitle: Barriers to Disclosure of Disability and Request for Accommodations Among First-Year Resident Physicians in the US

ideas.title: Architectural Nudges: Using Behavioral Economics to Redesign Systemic Structures for Equity
ideas.description: This research direction inverts the traditional application of nudge theory by targeting system architects and policymakers, not just individual decision-makers. It investigates how behavioral economics can be used to design 'choice architectures' for the creation and reform of promotion, compensation, and leadership systems that are inherently equitable by default. The synergy lies in using nudges to steer the design process of systemic structures, ensuring they are built to counteract bias at an institutional level rather than relying on individuals to overcome biased systems.

ideas.title: Robust Causal Triangulation for Historical Policy Evaluation
ideas.description: This research system applies a suite of econometric causal inference methods to the same historical policy-health question to assess robustness. It formally conducts sensitivity analyses to quantify how results change with varying degrees of assumption violation, moving beyond a single point estimate. The approach prioritizes the digitization and harmonization of novel archival data sources to create multiple potential instruments and control units, enabling deliberate methodological triangulation. The final output is a distribution of plausible causal effects, explicitly acknowledging and bounding the uncertainty inherent in historical causal analysis.

ideas.title: Apply invasive species dispersal models to AMR spread
ideas.description: This idea adapts solution methods from the field of Ecology and Conservation Biology, where network and agent-based models are used to forecast the spread of invasive species by incorporating environmental suitability, human transportation vectors, and interaction dynamics. The analogous solution is adapted by modeling antimicrobial-resistant (AMR) bacteria as invasive species, using global travel and trade data (e.g., airline routes, shipping lanes) as dispersal networks, and integrating local 'environmental' factors like antibiotic selective pressure and WASH (Water, Sanitation, and Hygiene) infrastructure to predict global colonization pathways and hotspots of AMR emergence and establishment.

ideas.title: Apply Phylogenetic Network Methods from Paleoanthropology to Viral Transmission Inference
ideas.description: The original idea proposes using viral genome sequencing to link infection cases and infer transmission chains. By drawing an analogy from paleoanthropology—where scientists use phylogenetic network methods (e.g., SplitsTree) to reconstruct complex, reticulate human ancestry and migration patterns from ancient DNA—we can adapt a more sophisticated solution. The analogous solution involves moving beyond simple tree models to network models that account for admixture, multiple ancestors, and hybridization. Adapted to viral genomics, this research would develop and apply phylogenetic network and admixture graph analysis to outbreak sequence data. This approach would model complex transmission dynamics, such as those involving super-spreaders connected to multiple downstream cases, co-circulating strains, or recombinant viruses, thereby inferring a 'transmission web' and quantifying the support for different network-based linkage hypotheses.

ideas.title: Co-evolutionary Adaptive Networks for Behavioral-Transmission Dynamics
ideas.description: This research develops a dynamic network model where transmission dynamics and network structure co-evolve. It explicitly incorporates nodes (individuals or groups) that can adapt their connectivity based on local infection risk, perceived threat, or policy interventions, modeled via simple game-theoretic rules or threshold mechanisms. The system simulates feedback loops where disease spread alters the contact network, which in turn alters future spread, moving beyond static topological analysis. This allows for testing the impact of adaptive behaviors (like social distancing or clustering) on outbreak trajectories and the efficacy of dynamic intervention strategies.

ideas.title: Employ causal inference for policy impact analysis
ideas.description: Use difference-in-differences or instrumental variable methods to estimate causal effects of payment reforms on HAI disparities.
ideas.sourceTitle: Racial/Ethnic Inequities in Healthcare-associated Infections Under the Shadow of Structural Racism: Narrative Review and Call to Action

ideas.title: Bias recognition automatically leads to mitigation
ideas.description: Test if recognizing one's own bias is sufficient for behavioral correction without structured interventions.
ideas.sourceTitle: The Impact of Unconscious Bias in Healthcare: How to Recognize and Mitigate It.

ideas.title: Antibiotic Market Health and Supply Security (AMHSS) System
ideas.description: This system moves beyond simple supply chain resilience to address the root cause of antibiotic access failure: fragile and exiting markets. It establishes a global coordinating mechanism that guarantees sustainable demand to manufacturers through advance purchase commitments, stabilizing the production ecosystem. Concurrently, it implements a distributed, intelligence-led buffer stock for the most essential and vulnerable antibiotics, informed by real-time monitoring of API supplies and manufacturer viability. The integrated approach ensures long-term access by making antibiotic production economically sustainable, while the smart buffer provides immediate protection against acute supply shocks.

ideas.title: Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning for Coordinated CDSS-Patient Portal Screening
ideas.description: This research expands the seed idea of integrating provider CDSS alerts with patient portal prompts by drawing an analogy from distributed robotics. The analogous problem of coordinating heterogeneous agents (like drones) with partial information is solved using multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) with a shared reward. We adapt this by conceptualizing the CDSS and patient portal as two autonomous 'agents' in a healthcare system. The research idea is to design and test a MARL-inspired framework where a central algorithmic coordinator uses a shared reward function (e.g., screening adherence) to learn an optimal policy for triggering and tailoring the provider alert and the patient prompt. This creates an adaptive, multi-modal screening intervention that learns over time to improve the synergy and effectiveness of the combined human-digital system, moving beyond a simple, static integration.

ideas.title: Dynamic Immunotherapy: CMV-Specific T-Cell Therapy Guided by Real-Time Immunosuppression Biomarkers
ideas.description: This research direction integrates adoptive transfer of CMV-specific T cells (Idea A) with precision management of immunosuppression (Idea B) into a unified, biomarker-driven strategy. The core innovation is to use high-resolution immune monitoring (e.g., T-cell receptor repertoire sequencing, functional assays of drug effect on T cells) to define a patient-specific 'therapeutic window'—a level of net immunosuppression that permits engraftment of the transferred T cells while controlling alloreactivity. Therapy is then dynamically adjusted: immunosuppressive drugs are titrated to maintain this window, and CMV-specific T-cell doses are administered or boosted based on real-time signals of CMV reactivation and T-cell persistence. This synergistic approach moves beyond static prevention or treatment, enabling proactive, personalized immune calibration to simultaneously prevent CMV disease and graft rejection.

ideas.title: Decentralized Coordination Models for Global AMR in the Absence of a Single Hegemon
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that US leadership is essential for global progress on Antimicrobial Resistance (AMR) by using an analogy from ecology. The analogous problem is an ecosystem's response to the loss of a keystone species, which risks collapse without that central stabilizing force. Ecological solutions focus on building resilience through functional redundancy, modular networks, and enhanced local capacity. Adapted to the AMR context, the research would systematically examine scenarios for global AMR progress without predominant US political or financial leadership. It would map the existing global AMR governance and funding 'ecosystem' to identify and model the potential of alternative actors (e.g., the EU, China, philanthropic coalitions, regional bodies), financial instruments (e.g., pooled global funds, mandatory pharmaceutical levies), and decentralized governance structures (e.g., strengthened WHO regional offices, topic-specific multi-stakeholder partnerships) to provide coordination and momentum. The outcome would be a set of modeled scenarios and policy frameworks for a resilient, polycentric global AMR response system that does not rely on a single hegemon.

ideas.title: Use A/B testing from web design
ideas.description: Randomize providers to different alert designs to empirically determine the most effective version.
ideas.sourceTitle: Assessment and improvement of HIV screening rates in a Midwest primary care practice using an electronic clinical decision support system: a quality improvement study

ideas.title: Adapt restorative justice circles for trust-building
ideas.description: Implement restorative justice circle practices to facilitate dialogue and repair trust between institutions and BIPOC communities.
ideas.sourceTitle: Addressing and Inspiring Vaccine Confidence in Black, Indigenous, and People of Color During the Coronavirus Disease 2019 Pandemic

ideas.title: Modeling the Decay and Reinforcement of CDSS Alert Efficacy Using Ecological Succession Principles
ideas.description: This research adapts concepts from ecological succession and adaptive management to study the long-term sustainability of CDSS alert efficacy. The analogous problem in ecology is ensuring a restored ecosystem does not regress to a prior, degraded state over time, which is solved by modeling succession phases and applying targeted reinforcements. The adapted idea frames the post-CDS intervention period as a 'clinical practice succession,' monitoring key indicator metrics beyond simple screening rates to identify phases of efficacy decay. The study would then design and test timed, low-intensity reinforcement interventions (e.g., subtle interface reminders, peer-led refreshers) informed by this decay model, aiming to proactively engineer durable improvements rather than merely document their decline.

ideas.title: Adapting Phage Display for Pathogen-Specific T-Cell Receptor Discovery
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from antibody engineering, this idea adapts the phage display screening technique—used to discover high-affinity therapeutic antibodies—to the problem of generating CMV-specific T cells for transplant patients. The analogous solution involves panning a diverse phage-displayed library against a target to find the best binders. Adapted to this context, a synthetic library of human T-cell receptor (TCR) variable regions would be displayed on phages. This library would be panned against immobilized CMV peptide-MHC complexes to isolate TCR sequences with optimal affinity and specificity for CMV. The identified TCR genes can then be cloned and used to genetically engineer a patient's own or donor T cells, creating a potent, precisely targeted adoptive T-cell therapy to prevent or treat CMV reactivation post-transplant.

ideas.title: Adaptive Filtering for Clinical Alerts Using Signal Processing Principles
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution to noise cancellation from Digital Signal Processing to the problem of reducing unnecessary clinical alerts. In signal processing, adaptive filters use a reference input correlated with interference to subtract noise from a primary signal. The analogous solution involves systems like the Least Mean Squares (LMS) filter, which dynamically adjusts its parameters to suppress predictable noise. Adapted to the clinical context, we propose developing a system that treats the continuous stream of structured patient data (recent test results, active medications, documented diagnoses) as the reference input. This input is correlated with 'alert noise'—situations where an HIV screening alert would be inappropriate or redundant. An adaptive algorithmic filter would be trained to predict and suppress these unnecessary alerts in real-time, dynamically adjusting its 'coefficients' based on evolving clinical context and feedback, thereby scaling down alert fatigue while scaling up the salience of truly necessary notifications.

ideas.title: Adaptive Alerting Using a Predictive Salience Filter
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from auditory neuroscience—the predictive filtering model of the 'cocktail party effect'—to clinical alert fatigue. In noisy environments, the brain uses predictive models to focus only on salient auditory streams. The analogous computational solution involves attention-driven scene analysis that filters out predictable noise. Adapted to healthcare, the idea is to investigate an adaptive clinical alert system that functions as a predictive salience filter. By ingesting real-time contextual data (patient status trends, recent clinical actions, workload), the system would model which alerts are truly novel or critical for a given context and suppress those predicted to be low-priority or redundant. The research would focus on designing, testing, and refining this adaptive filtering algorithm to maintain diagnostic sensitivity for critical events while drastically reducing the total alert volume presented to the provider, thereby reducing fatigue.

ideas.title: The Feedback Gap: Testing if Bias Recognition Without Corrective Feedback Enables Self-Correction
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that recognizing one's own bias automatically leads to mitigation by drawing an analogy from motor learning. In that field, awareness of an error is not enough for correction without specific, augmented feedback. The adapted idea tests if a similar 'feedback gap' exists for cognitive bias. Participants would first undergo a task revealing a specific bias (e.g., anchoring). One group would only be informed of their bias, while another would receive structured feedback on corrective strategies. The core test is whether recognition alone in the first group is sufficient for behavioral correction in a follow-up task, or if explicit, actionable guidance is necessary, thereby refining models of bias intervention.

ideas.title: Apply crime prediction hotspot modeling to disease exposure vulnerability
ideas.description: This idea adapts the method of Risk Terrain Modeling (RTM) from the field of predictive policing and criminology to the context of environmental justice and disease exposure. In criminology, RTM analyzes and weights multiple spatial layers of environmental features (e.g., bars, lighting, zoning) to create a composite map predicting where crime is most likely to occur, identifying the underlying environmental drivers of risk. Adapted to public health, this involves using GIS to layer, statistically weight, and combine spatial data on socio-environmental determinants (e.g., PM2.5 levels, toxic release sites, historical redlining maps, access to healthcare, population density) to generate a dynamic 'vulnerability terrain' model. This model would predict geographic hotspots for vulnerability to diseases like COVID-19 based on the confluence of systemic environmental injustices, providing a proactive tool for targeting resource allocation and policy intervention beyond simple retrospective mapping of case clusters.

ideas.title: Uncertainty-Aware, Data-Assimilative Transmission Models with Dynamic Vaccine Parameter Learning
ideas.description: This research develops a new class of large-scale computational models that explicitly confront and manage the profound uncertainty in vaccine transmission parameters. The system integrates a continuous data assimilation engine that updates model states and parameters in near-real-time by fusing diverse, noisy surveillance data streams. It employs ensemble and probabilistic techniques to generate forecasts with quantified confidence intervals, highlighting where predictions are robust versus where they are data-limited. The outcome is a more trustworthy and operationally useful modeling framework that supports decision-making under uncertainty, rather than providing a false sense of precision.

ideas.title: Adapting Forensic Geology's Provenance Tracing to Map Policy Legacies on Health
ideas.description: This idea adapts the provenance-tracing methodology from forensic geology to historical policy analysis. In forensic geology, the origin and transport path of a material are identified by analyzing its unique compositional fingerprint and matching it to mapped source locations. Adapted here, the 'material' is a community's current health infrastructure landscape. The research method involves creating a multi-attribute 'fingerprint' for a given health infrastructure gap using archival data (zoning maps, budget allocations, public works records, property deeds). This fingerprint is then systematically matched against the 'source maps' of historical policy interventions—such as the specific boundaries of redlined districts or the implementation timelines of discriminatory urban renewal plans. By correlating the infrastructure fingerprint with the policy source, the method formally reconstructs the pathway from a specific historical policy decision to the present-day health infrastructure gap, providing forensically rigorous evidence of impact.

ideas.title: Apply pharmacokinetic modeling from other drugs
ideas.description: Use advanced PK/PD modeling from other antivirals to optimize dosing of new CMV drugs.
ideas.sourceTitle: Cytomegalovirus infection in liver transplant recipients: updates on clinical management.

ideas.title: Dual problem: Treat AMR as an information crisis
ideas.description: Frame undetected and undiagnosed AMR primarily as a failure of diagnostic information flow.
ideas.sourceTitle: Confronting antimicrobial resistance beyond the COVID-19 pandemic and the 2020 US election

ideas.title: Incorporate vaccine transmission data into large-scale disease models using agent-based frameworks from traffic engineering
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from urban traffic engineering, where agent-based models (ABMs) are scaled using real-world sensor data to simulate city-wide traffic dynamics. The analogous solution involves creating discrete agents (vehicles) with behavioral rules calibrated by aggregated traffic data to predict large-scale flow and congestion. Adapted to epidemiological modeling, the research idea is to build a large-scale, computationally efficient ABM for disease spread where each individual is an agent. Vaccine transmission data (e.g., on infectivity reduction post-vaccination) will be incorporated to define the core interaction rules between agents. This framework will allow for the simulation of national or city-level disease dynamics, directly testing how vaccination campaigns, informed by real-world transmission data, alter the course of an epidemic at scale.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismic Network Principles for Wastewater-Based AMR Surveillance
ideas.description: This idea proposes scaling up wastewater monitoring for antimicrobial resistance (AMR) genes by drawing a direct analogy from geophysics, specifically the scaling of global seismic monitoring networks. The analogous problem—detecting, locating, and characterizing rare, distributed events (earthquakes) through a permanent, standardized sensor network—was solved by deploying interoperable sensors, creating centralized data management and quality control hubs (e.g., the Incorporated Research Institutions for Seismology, IRIS), and developing automated processing algorithms. This solution is adapted to the AMR context by conceptualizing the emergence or spike of a resistance gene in a population as a 'biological seismic event.' The adapted research idea is to architect a national wastewater surveillance network for AMR by standardizing sensor (sampling and genomic analysis) technology across existing COVID-19 wastewater infrastructure, establishing a central bioinformatics repository and quality control framework, and developing automated, algorithmic pipelines to continuously analyze incoming sequence data. This system would detect, geolocate, and characterize trends in AMR gene abundance and diversity, providing a scalable, early-warning system for population-level AMR dynamics.

ideas.title: AMR requires a specialized, centralized surveillance and response network, analogous to earthquake early warning systems
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that AMR is best tackled by integrating surveillance into all primary care systems. It draws an analogy from geophysics, specifically earthquake early warning systems (EEWS). The analogous problem is detecting a diffuse, high-impact threat (seismic activity) early enough to enable a targeted response. The solution was not to embed seismometers in every building but to create a dedicated, centralized network of specialized sensors feeding data to a rapid analysis hub. Adapting this solution, the research would test if AMR requires a similar specialized architecture: a dedicated network of sentinel labs using genomic surveillance as 'sensors' for novel resistance, feeding data to a central analytical body. This body would function as an 'AMR early warning hub,' modeling outbreaks and coordinating rapid, targeted containment responses, rather than relying on the variable capacity and slow aggregation of data from integrated primary care reporting.

ideas.title: Causally-Informed Process Control for Equity System Monitoring
ideas.description: This system moves beyond passive monitoring by embedding causal reasoning within statistical process control for equity metrics. It begins by modeling the social system producing the metric to distinguish between random variation, expected systemic drift, and meaningful intervention effects. When a control chart flags a potential shift, the system automatically tests for causal impact against a synthetic counterfactual derived from leading indicators and correlated variables. This allows practitioners to differentiate between noise, regression, and true signal, ensuring that responses are data-informed and targeted at probable causes rather than statistical artifacts.

ideas.title: Policy Synthesis with Integrated Causal Auditing
ideas.description: This research proposes a hybrid system where large-scale data synthesis is used not as a final answer, but as a powerful engine for generating plausible policy models and hypotheses. Every synthesized model is then subjected to a mandatory 'causal audit' phase, employing established causal inference methods on appropriate validation data to test robustness, identify confounding, and estimate true effect bounds. The final output is a policy recommendation accompanied by a transparent causal credibility report, explicitly stating the limits of inference and remaining assumptions, ensuring decisions are informed by both data breadth and causal depth.

ideas.title: Challenge that USMLE Step 1 de-emphasis helps
ideas.description: Test if removing Step 1 filters inadvertently disadvantages some URM applicants with strong test scores.
ideas.sourceTitle: Interventions Associated With Racial and Ethnic Diversity in US Graduate Medical Education

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Succession Principles for Institutional-Community Trust Repair
ideas.description: This research adapts the ecological solution of 'succession'—the staged, sequential recovery of damaged ecosystems—to the problem of rebuilding trust between institutions and BIPOC communities. In ecology, full restoration requires pioneer species to first create stable conditions for more complex species to follow. Analogously, the adapted idea proposes a 'trust succession' model for restorative justice circles. The process would be deliberately phased: initial 'pioneer' circles would focus on low-stakes, procedural agreements and basic communication to stabilize the relationship. Subsequent 'intermediate' circles would tackle specific, bounded issues, building capacity for dialogue. Finally, 'climax' circles would engage with deep historical harms and systemic co-design. This structured, sequential approach acknowledges that trust is a complex social ecosystem that requires foundational conditions before full restorative work can succeed, preventing the collapse of dialogue by moving too quickly to high-stakes issues.

ideas.title: Data synthesis alone suffices for policy
ideas.description: Question if synthesizing available data is enough without new causal inference frameworks.
ideas.sourceTitle: COVID-19 Vaccines and SARS-CoV-2 Transmission in the Era of New Variants: A Review and Perspective

ideas.title: Scale down to neighborhood-level outbreak dynamics
ideas.description: Model hyper-local transmission dynamics within specific high-density Hispanic/Latinx neighborhoods to identify micro-mechanisms of spread.
ideas.sourceTitle: COVID-19 Pandemic: Disparate Health Impact on the Hispanic/Latinx Population in the United States

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Trophic Cascade Models for Antibiotic Supply Chain Resilience
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from ecology, this research adapts solutions from the study of trophic cascades—where the loss of a keystone species triggers ecosystem collapse—to secure essential antibiotic supplies. The analogous ecological solution involves mapping critical dependencies and building redundant, compensatory pathways to maintain system function. Adapted to the research context, the idea is to model antibiotic supply chains as ecological networks, identify vulnerable 'keystone' manufacturing or distribution nodes, and proactively design validated backup pathways (e.g., alternative suppliers or rapid-switch generics) to prevent access cascades from disruptions, thereby applying a resilience framework proven in complex natural systems to a critical pharmaceutical challenge.

ideas.title: Scale up wastewater monitoring for AMR genes
ideas.description: Expand COVID-19 wastewater surveillance infrastructure to track population-level AMR trends.
ideas.sourceTitle: Confronting antimicrobial resistance beyond the COVID-19 pandemic and the 2020 US election

ideas.title: Policy Synthesis via Data Compilation: A Paleontological Assemblage Approach
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that new causal inference frameworks are necessary for effective policy by arguing that synthesizing available data is sufficient. It draws an analogy from paleontology and stratigraphy, where scientists routinely synthesize existing fossil collections and site data into 'faunal assemblages' to reconstruct past environments without new excavations. Adapting this solution, the research proposes a 'policy assemblage' methodology: systematically compiling disparate existing administrative, survey, and operational datasets into a unified whole. Analyzing the co-occurrence, correlations, and structural gaps within this synthesized assemblage would be used to map system dynamics, model outcomes, and inform decisions, asserting that such rigorous synthesis can often substitute for novel causal identification frameworks in the policy domain.

ideas.title: Apply blind audition techniques from orchestras
ideas.description: Use anonymized application portfolios to reduce bias in initial GME screening phases.
ideas.sourceTitle: Interventions Associated With Racial and Ethnic Diversity in US Graduate Medical Education

ideas.title: Adaptive Sensor Fusion for Digital Epidemiology
ideas.description: This idea adapts the core solution from autonomous vehicle navigation—multi-sensor fusion—to the challenge of real-time data linkage in digital epidemiology. In autonomous systems, data from LiDAR, radar, cameras, and GPS are combined using probabilistic models to create a robust and accurate perception of the environment, overcoming the limitations of any single sensor. Similarly, this research proposes to develop an adaptive sensor fusion framework for health. By probabilistically linking and analyzing multiple concurrent, passive digital streams from wearables and smartphones (e.g., movement patterns, geolocation, acoustic biomarkers, physiological signals, and sparse active inputs), the system can generate a composite, real-time 'health state perception.' This would allow for more accurate, passive inference of individual exposure risks and early, subtle symptom onset, moving beyond single-metric thresholds to a dynamic, multi-factor risk assessment model.

ideas.title: Adapting Conjoint Analysis from Marketing to Model Resident Housing Trade-offs
ideas.description: This idea adapts the method of conjoint analysis, a staple in marketing and consumer research for modeling preferences for multi-attribute products, to the context of residential accommodation choice. In marketing, conjoint analysis presents respondents with hypothetical product profiles defined by combinations of attributes (e.g., brand, price, features) to statistically determine the utility or value they assign to each attribute level. This solution is adapted by designing a discrete choice experiment where the 'product' is a housing unit, and its 'features' are key accommodation attributes such as monthly cost, square footage, neighborhood safety, proximity to work/transit, included utilities, and building amenities. By analyzing the choices residents make between hypothetical accommodation bundles, the research can decompose their preferences, quantify trade-offs (e.g., how much extra rent they would accept for a shorter commute), and identify heterogeneous preference patterns across demographic or socioeconomic subgroups, providing a robust model of housing decision-making.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismic Early Warning Systems to Pre-emptive Therapy Monitoring
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that available monitoring tools are sufficiently sensitive and timely for optimal pre-emptive strategy by drawing an analogy from geophysics. In seismology, the problem of predicting damaging aftershocks or mainshock evolution was solved by moving beyond single-sensor, threshold-based alerts to integrated probabilistic early warning systems. These systems combine data from seismic, GPS, and strainmeter networks with real-time statistical models. Adapted to pre-emptive therapy, the research would develop a clinical decision framework that integrates multiple, complementary monitoring modalities (e.g., ctDNA, proteomics, functional imaging, wearable sensors) into a dynamic, probabilistic model of subclinical disease progression. Therapy initiation would then be based on a calculated, continuously updated risk probability exceeding a pre-defined threshold, rather than on a single biomarker crossing a static level, thereby enhancing the sensitivity and timeliness of intervention.

ideas.title: Use climate change policy frameworks for AMR
ideas.description: Adapt long-term, global governance models from climate policy to manage AMR.
ideas.sourceTitle: Confronting antimicrobial resistance beyond the COVID-19 pandemic and the 2020 US election

ideas.title: Adversarial System Redesign: Stress-Testing for Material Power Redistribution
ideas.description: This research moves beyond designing theoretically equitable systems to rigorously testing whether they dismantle existing power structures. It establishes a framework where any proposed change to promotion, compensation, or leadership systems must undergo simulated adversarial audits that model how entrenched interests could circumvent the new rules. The core outcome is a set of 'power-redistributive' system designs proven, through counterfactual and behavioral simulation, to alter actual resource flows and authority, moving past symbolic compliance to measurable shifts in organizational influence.

ideas.title: Apply industrial process control to equity interventions
ideas.description: Use statistical process control charts from manufacturing to monitor equity metrics and detect regressions.
ideas.sourceTitle: Women in Pediatrics: Progress, Barriers, and Opportunities for Equity, Diversity, and Inclusion

ideas.title: Apply critical race theory spatial analysis
ideas.description: Use GIS and CRT to map historical redlining onto modern COVID-19 hotspot maps.
ideas.sourceTitle: Racism, Not Race, Drives Inequity Across the COVID-19 Continuum.

ideas.title: Hybrid Revealed and Stated Preference Model with Behavioral Calibration for Housing Choice
ideas.description: This research system combines discrete choice experiments with revealed preference data from real housing transactions and residential mobility records to ground hypothetical trade-offs in observed behavior. It uses the revealed data to calibrate the choice experiment design, ensuring attribute levels and constraints reflect actual market conditions and budget limitations. The system incorporates behavioral modules that test for common decision heuristics and social influence effects within the choice tasks. Finally, it employs a validation loop where model predictions are compared against subsequent real-world moves, creating a robust, behaviorally-informed model of residential trade-offs.

ideas.title: Adapt Percolation Theory from Condensed Matter Physics to Model Critical Thresholds in Information Transmission
ideas.description: This idea adapts the core framework of percolation theory from condensed matter physics to the problem of transmission dynamics on contact networks. In physics, percolation theory solves the problem of predicting when a random network (like a porous material) will become globally connected, defining a sharp phase transition at a critical probability. The analogous solution involves analyzing bond or site percolation on a lattice to find this threshold. Adapted to the research context, we apply this to social or epidemiological contact networks to model the critical threshold for large-scale transmission cascades. By mapping transmission probability to bond occupancy and individual susceptibility to site occupancy, we can calculate the critical point where a localized transmission event percolates through the entire network, offering a powerful, fundamental metric for predicting outbreak potential and resilience.

ideas.title: Optimize alert design to reduce fatigue
ideas.description: Investigate different alert modalities or frequencies to maintain effectiveness while minimizing provider alert fatigue.
ideas.sourceTitle: Assessment and improvement of HIV screening rates in a Midwest primary care practice using an electronic clinical decision support system: a quality improvement study

papers.title: The Impact of Unconscious Bias in Healthcare: How to Recognize and Mitigate It.
papers.abstract: The increasing diversity in the US population is reflected in the patients who healthcare professionals treat. Unfortunately, this diversity is not always represented by the demographic characteristics of healthcare professionals themselves. Patients from underrepresented groups in the United States

papers.abstract: Abstract In December 2019, a novel coronavirus known as SARS-CoV-2, emerged in Wuhan, China, causing the Coronavirus disease 2019 we now refer to as COVID-19.
